topics.title: Properties of Concrete and Steel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Concrete and Steel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Concrete

**Concrete** is a composite construction material composed primarily of **cement**, **fine aggregate (sand)**, **coarse aggregate (gravel/crushed stone)**, **water**, and often **chemical/mineral admixtures**. When these ingredients are mixed in proper proportions and allowed to cure, the cement hydrates and binds the aggregates into a rock-like mass possessing significant compressive strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (IS 456:2000 Perspective):**
> Concrete is a mixture of cement, aggregates, and water with or without suitable admixtures. The strength, durability, and other characteristics of concrete depend upon the relative proportions and properties of its constituent materials.

**Designation of Concrete Grade:**
Concrete is classified by its characteristic compressive strength ($f_{ck}$) measured on **150 mm cubes** at **28 days** of curing. Common grades per **IS 456:2000** are:

- **Ordinary Concrete:** M10, M15, M20
- **Standard Concrete:** M25, M30, M35, M40, M45, M50, M55
- **High Strength Concrete:** M60, M65, M70, M75, M80 and above

For example, **M30** concrete has a characteristic cube compressive strength of **30 N/mm²** at 28 days.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** As per **IS 456:2000 (Clause 6.1.1)**, the minimum grade of concrete for **RCC (Reinforced Cement Concrete)** is **M20** for ordinary exposure and **M25** for moderate exposure conditions. For **prestressed concrete** and structures exposed to **severe/aggressive environments**, the minimum is **M30**.

### 1.2 Definition of Reinforcing Steel

**Reinforcing steel** is the high-strength steel bar (rebar) embedded within the concrete to provide the tensile strength that concrete inherently lacks. The steel and concrete act compositely due to bond and nearly equal thermal expansion coefficients.

The two principal categories per **IS 1786:2008** are:

1. **Mild Steel (MS) — Fe 250 grade:** Yield strength $f_y = 250$ N/mm² (now largely obsolete, replaced by HYSD)
2. **High Yield Strength Deformed (HYSD) bars:**
   - **Fe 415** — $f_y = 415$ N/mm²
   - **Fe 500** — $f_y = 500$ N/mm²
   - **Fe 550** — $f_y = 550$ N/mm²
   - **Fe 600** — $f_y = 600$ N/mm²

> [!NOTE]
> **TMT Bars (Thermo-Mechanically Treated):** TMT bars are the modern category of HYSD bars that undergo a special heat treatment process to give them a tough outer surface (martensite) and a ductile inner core (ferrite-pearlite). Most reinforcement supplied today in Kerala for KTU projects is TMT Fe 500 or Fe 500D (where 'D' denotes higher ductility).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Concrete as Artificial Stone:**
Imagine building a wall using a mixture of mud, straw, small pebbles, and water. As the water dries, the mud cements everything together, forming a hard, stone-like mass capable of carrying heavy vertical loads (compression) but crumbling when you try to pull it apart (tension). Modern concrete behaves identically — it is a man-made sedimentary rock.

**Analogy 2 — Steel as the Skeleton:**
Think of a concrete beam as your upper arm bone. The hard outer shell (compact bone) is the concrete that resists squeezing, while the marrow-rich inner core resists pulling. The steel rebar placed near the bottom of a beam acts exactly like the tension-side reinforcement of the bone, taking up all the pulling forces (tensile stresses) that the concrete cannot handle.

**Analogy 3 — Bond as Hook-and-Loop Fastener:**
For steel and concrete to act as one unit, the surface of the rebar is ribbed (deformed). These ribs act like microscopic hooks that grip the surrounding concrete, just as the hooks of a Velcro strip interlock with the loops of its counterpart, transferring stress across the interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why both materials?**
> - Concrete alone: Strong in **compression** ($f_{ck}$ = 20–80 N/mm²) but weak in **tension** (only ~10% of compressive strength).
> - Steel alone: Strong in both tension and compression, but expensive and prone to buckling in slender forms.
> - **Combined:** Concrete protects steel from corrosion and buckling, while steel carries the tension and confines the concrete. This is the genius of RCC.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Key Properties of Concrete

#### 2.1.1 Compressive Strength ($f_c$ / $f_{ck}$)
- **Cube Strength ($f_{ck}$):** Measured on a 150 mm cube, capped, and tested under uniaxial compression in a UTM at a loading rate of **140 kg/cm²/min (13.73 N/mm²/min)** per **IS 516:1959**.
- **Cylinder Strength ($f_{cy}$ or $f_c'$):** Measured on a 150 mm diameter × 300 mm height cylinder (used in ACI code).
- **Prism Strength:** Measured on standard prisms; lower than cube strength due to platen friction effects.

**Relationship between strengths:**

$$f_{cy} \approx 0.80 \times f_{ck}$$

$$f_{prism} \approx 0.67 \times f_{ck}$$

#### 2.1.2 Tensile Strength of Concrete
Concrete's tensile strength is small and is generally **ignored in design** except for:
- Shear and torsion calculations
- Crack-width estimation
- Deflection calculations (indirectly)

**Methods to measure tensile strength:**
1. **Direct Tension Test** — Difficult to conduct, gives erratic results
2. **Split Cylinder Test (Brazilian Test)** — Per **IS 5816:1999**
3. **Flexural Test (Modulus of Rupture)** — Per **IS 516:1959**

**Empirical formula for split tensile strength ($f_{ct,sp}$):**

$$f_{ct,sp} = 0.49 \sqrt{f_{ck}} \quad \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Modulus of Rupture ($f_{cr}$):**

$$f_{cr} = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}} \quad \text{N/mm}^2 \quad \text{(IS 456:2000, Cl. 6.1.5)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Favourite:** For M25 concrete: $f_{ct,sp} = 0.49 \times \sqrt{25} = 2.45$ N/mm². This frequently appears in 3-mark and 14-mark questions on shear design and crack-width.

#### 2.1.3 Modulus of Elasticity of Concrete ($E_c$)
The short-term static modulus of elasticity of concrete per **IS 456:2000 (Cl. 6.2.3.1)** is:

$$E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}} \quad \text{N/mm}^2$$

This is the **secant modulus** at approximately **one-third of the failure load**. For grades above **M60**, the formula needs modification per **IS 456:2000 (Note under Cl. 6.2.3.1)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Long-term Modulus of Elasticity:** Due to creep, the effective or sustained modulus is reduced:

$$E_{c,eff} = \frac{E_c}{1 + \theta}$$

where $\theta$ is the **creep coefficient** (typically 1.5 to 2.0 for normal concrete per IS 456).

#### 2.1.4 Poisson's Ratio of Concrete
The Poisson's ratio for concrete under short-term loading lies between **0.15 and 0.25**. For design, IS 456 recommends:

$$\mu = 0.20$$

#### 2.1.5 Workability
Workability is the ease with which fresh concrete can be mixed, transported, placed, compacted, and finished without segregation.

**Measurement Methods:**
| Test | Apparatus | Slump Range | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Slump Test | Slump Cone | 25–150 mm | Most common on site |
| Compaction Factor | Compaction Factor Apparatus | 0.78–0.95 | Low-workability mixes |
| Vee-Bee Test | Vee-Bee Consistometer | 2–10 sec (VB time) | Stiff mixes |
| Flow Test | Flow Table | 50–150% spread | Highly workable mixes |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Designation:** Per **IS 456:2000 (Table 4)**, minimum slump values are specified based on the type of construction:
> - Beams/Slabs: **50 mm** (compacted by vibration)
> - Columns/Walls: **75 mm**
> - Mass concrete foundations: **25–50 mm**

#### 2.1.6 Durability
Concrete must withstand the environmental conditions throughout its design life. **IS 456:2000 (Cl. 8.1)** specifies:
- **Minimum cement content** (kg/m³) based on exposure condition
- **Maximum water-cement ratio** based on exposure
- **Minimum cover** to reinforcement

**Exposure Conditions (IS 456:2000, Table 3):**
- **Mild** — Concrete protected from weather
- **Moderate** — Sheltered from rain, alternate wetting/drying
- **Severe** — Exposed to rain, coastal, alternate wetting/drying
- **Very Severe** — Coastal with salt spray, submerged
- **Extreme** — Tidal zone, chemical attack

#### 2.1.7 Shrinkage and Creep
- **Drying Shrinkage:** Long-term volumetric reduction due to moisture loss (≈ 0.04% to 0.10% strain)
- **Creep:** Time-dependent increase in strain under sustained stress (creep strain ≈ 2–4× elastic strain for normal concrete)
- **Creep Coefficient ($\theta$):** Ratio of creep strain to elastic strain = **1.5 to 2.0** (per IS 456)

### 2.2 Key Properties of Reinforcing Steel

#### 2.2.1 Stress-Strain Behaviour
The behaviour is fundamentally different for mild steel versus HYSD:

**Mild Steel (Fe 250):**
- Clearly defined **yield point** ($f_y$)
- Long plastic plateau
- Strain at yield ≈ 0.12%, strain at ultimate ≈ 20%

**HYSD Bars (Fe 415, 500, 550):**
- **No sharp yield point** — yield is defined at the **0.2% proof stress** (residual strain method)
- Steeper elastic region
- Strain at 0.2% proof stress ≈ 0.2%, strain at ultimate ≈ 12–14%

#### 2.2.2 Modulus of Elasticity of Steel ($E_s$)
Per **IS 456:2000 (Cl. 5.6.3.1)** and **IS 1786:2008**:

$$E_s = 2.0 \times 10^5 \quad \text{N/mm}^2 = 200 \text{ GPa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The value $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² is **fixed** and used in ALL KTU design problems. It is the most repeated constant in the syllabus.

#### 2.2.3 Modular Ratio ($m$)
The modular ratio is the ratio of the elastic modulus of steel to the effective elastic modulus of concrete. Per **IS 456:2000 (Cl. B-1.3, Annex B)**:

$$m = \frac{E_s}{E_c} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}}} = \frac{40}{\sqrt{f_{ck}}}$$

For sustained loading (considering creep):

$$m = \frac{280}{3 \sqrt{f_{ck}}}$$

#### 2.2.4 Other Properties of Steel
| Property | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Density | 7850 kg/m³ |
| Coefficient of thermal expansion | $12 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C |
| Yield strength (Fe 415) | 415 N/mm² |
| Yield strength (Fe 500) | 500 N/mm² |
| Ultimate tensile strength | 1.08 to 1.20 × $f_y$ |
| Minimum elongation | 14.5% (Fe 415) / 12% (Fe 500) |
| Poisson's ratio | 0.30 |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Reference)

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **IS Code Reference** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Characteristic cube strength | $f_{ck}$ | N/mm² | IS 456 Cl. 6.1 |
| Cylinder strength | $f_{cy} = 0.8 f_{ck}$ | N/mm² | Empirical |
| Prism strength | $f_p = 0.67 f_{ck}$ | N/mm² | Empirical |
| Split tensile strength | $f_{ct,sp} = 0.49\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | N/mm² | IS 5816 |
| Modulus of rupture | $f_{cr} = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | N/mm² | IS 456 Cl. 6.1.5 |
| Modulus of elasticity (concrete) | $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | N/mm² | IS 456 Cl. 6.2.3.1 |
| Effective modulus (long-term) | $E_{ce} = E_c / (1+\theta)$ | N/mm² | IS 456 |
| Modulus of elasticity (steel) | $E_s = 2.0 \times 10^5$ | N/mm² | IS 456 Cl. 5.6.3.1 |
| Modular ratio (short-term) | $m = E_s / E_c = 40/\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | — | IS 456 Annex B |
| Modular ratio (long-term) | $m = 280 / (3\sqrt{f_{ck}})$ | — | IS 456 Annex B |
| Poisson's ratio (concrete) | $\mu_c = 0.20$ | — | IS 456 |
| Poisson's ratio (steel) | $\mu_s = 0.30$ | — | Standard |
| Creep coefficient (concrete) | $\theta = 1.5$ to $2.0$ | — | IS 456 |
| Density of concrete (plain) | $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³ | kN/m³ | IS 456 Cl. 5.4 |
| Density of concrete (RCC) | $\gamma_c = 25$ kN/m³ | kN/m³ | IS 456 Cl. 5.4 |
| Density of steel | $\gamma_s = 78.5$ kN/m³ | kN/m³ | Standard |
| Coefficient of thermal expansion (concrete) | $9.9 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C | /°C | IS 456 |
| Coefficient of thermal expansion (steel) | $12 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C | /°C | IS 456 |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Material Selection in Coastal Kerala:** Engineers use $f_{ck} \geq 30$ N/mm² with low W/C ratio and proper cover to resist chloride-induced corrosion in marine environments (Kochi, Calicut, Trivandrum coasts).
2. **High-Rise Buildings:** High-grade concrete (M50 to M80) reduces column cross-section, increasing usable floor area. The modified $E_c$ formula for higher grades becomes critical.
3. **Seismic Design (Kerala Zone III):** Ductile HYSD bars (Fe 500D) are mandated for earthquake resistance, ensuring the structure absorbs energy without brittle failure.
4. **Long-Span Bridges:** Pre-stressed concrete uses $f_{ck} \geq 40$ N/mm² with high-tensile steel strands ($f_y$ up to 1860 N/mm²) for efficient load transfer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Empirical Relation $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$

**Background:** The empirical relationship between elastic modulus and compressive strength of concrete is one of the most-used formulae in KTU design problems. It is based on experimental data collected worldwide and adopted in **IS 456:2000** as a simplified safe formula.

**Starting Assumptions (based on experimental studies):**
- For normal-weight concrete, the empirical relationship is of the form:
$$E_c = K \cdot (f_{ck})^n$$
- Empirical data (from ACI 318 and adopted by IS 456) suggests $n = 0.5$ (square root relation).
- The constant $K$ is calibrated so that for $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm² (a common grade), $E_c \approx 25{,}000$ N/mm².

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$\text{Step 1: General form} \implies E_c = K \cdot (f_{ck})^{0.5}$$

$$\text{Step 2: Substitute the calibration point } (f_{ck} = 25, \, E_c = 25000)$$

$$25000 = K \cdot \sqrt{25} = K \cdot 5$$

$$\text{Step 3: Solve for } K$$

$$K = \frac{25000}{5} = 5000$$

$$\text{Step 4: Substitute back into the general equation}$$

$$\boxed{E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}} \quad \text{N/mm}^2}$$

**Verification for M30 concrete:**

$$E_c = 5000 \times \sqrt{30} = 5000 \times 5.477 = 27{,}386 \approx 27{,}386 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 27.4 \text{ GPa}$$

This is consistent with experimental values reported in the literature (≈ 27–29 GPa for M30).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Important Note:** Per **IS 456:2000**, for grades above M60, the empirical formula may not be accurate. The code recommends using **$E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ for $f_{ck} \leq 60$ N/mm²** and consulting specialist literature for higher grades.

### 3.2 Derivation of Modular Ratio $m = 40 / \sqrt{f_{ck}}$

**Concept:** The modular ratio $m$ is defined as the ratio of the elastic modulus of steel to that of concrete. It is used in the working stress method (LSM) to transform a composite section into an equivalent homogeneous section.

**Starting Definition:**

$$m = \frac{E_s}{E_c}$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$\text{Step 1: Substitute the standard value } E_s = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$m = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{E_c}$$

$$\text{Step 2: Substitute } E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$$

$$m = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}}$$

$$\text{Step 3: Simplify the numerator}$

$$m = \frac{200{,}000}{5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}} = \frac{40}{\sqrt{f_{ck}}}$$

$$\text{Step 4: Final boxed result}$$

$$\boxed{m = \frac{40}{\sqrt{f_{ck}}}}$$

**Derivation of Long-Term Modular Ratio (including creep):**

The long-term modular ratio accounts for sustained loading, where concrete undergoes additional creep strain. The effective concrete modulus becomes:

$$E_{ce} = \frac{E_c}{1 + \theta}$$

where $\theta$ = creep coefficient (typically 1.5 to 2.0; per IS 456, often taken as **1.5** for design).

$$m_{long} = \frac{E_s}{E_{ce}} = \frac{E_s(1+\theta)}{E_c} = m \cdot (1 + \theta)$$

$$\text{Substituting } m = 40/\sqrt{f_{ck}} \text{ and } \theta = 1.5:$$

$$m_{long} = \frac{40}{\sqrt{f_{ck}}} \times 2.5 = \frac{100}{\sqrt{f_{ck}}}$$

$$\text{Per IS 456, the long-term modular ratio is given as:}$$

$$\boxed{m_{long} = \frac{280}{3\sqrt{f_{ck}}}}$$

**Verification:** For $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm²:

$$m_{long} = \frac{280}{3\sqrt{25}} = \frac{280}{15} = 18.67$$

And from the simple formula with $\theta = 1.5$:

$$m_{long} = \frac{40}{\sqrt{25}} \times 2.5 = 8 \times 2.5 = 20.0$$

The slight difference (18.67 vs. 20.0) arises because the IS 456 formula uses a slightly different effective modulus formulation.

### 3.3 Derivation of Stress in Steel Using Modular Ratio (Composite Action)

**Problem Setup:** A reinforced concrete beam is subjected to a bending moment $M$. The beam has a steel area $A_{st}$ at a depth $d$ from the extreme compression fibre. We need to derive the relationship between stress in steel and stress in concrete.

**Assumptions (Working Stress Method):**
1. Plane sections remain plane (Bernoulli's hypothesis)
2. Perfect bond between steel and concrete (no slip)
3. Modular ratio $m$ is constant
4. Concrete is elastic (Hooke's law applicable)

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$\text{Step 1: Consider a small element of length } dx. \text{ Due to bending, the top fibre compresses by } \delta_c \text{ and steel stretches by } \delta_s.$$

$$\text{Step 2: Strain compatibility (plane sections remain plane)}$$

$$\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_c \cdot \frac{d}{x}$$

$$\text{where } x = \text{ depth of neutral axis}, \, d = \text{ effective depth}$$

$$\text{Step 3: Hooke's law for each material}$$

$$\sigma_c = E_c \cdot \varepsilon_c, \quad \sigma_s = E_s \cdot \varepsilon_s$$

$$\text{Step 4: Take the ratio of stresses}$$

$$\frac{\sigma_s}{\sigma_c} = \frac{E_s \cdot \varepsilon_s}{E_c \cdot \varepsilon_c} = \frac{E_s}{E_c} \cdot \frac{d}{x} = m \cdot \frac{d}{x}$$

$$\text{Step 5: Solve for steel stress}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_s = m \cdot \sigma_c \cdot \frac{d}{x}}$$

**Special Case (for balanced design, $x = k \cdot d$):**

$$\sigma_s = \frac{m}{k} \cdot \sigma_c$$

where $k = x/d$ is the **neutral axis depth factor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Application:** This derived relation is the **foundation of the working stress method (WSM)** for design of RCC members. In Limit State Method (LSM), the same relation is replaced by partial safety factors and the use of design strength (i.e., $f_d = f_y / 1.15$ for steel and $f_d = 0.446 f_{ck}$ for concrete bending stress in LSM).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of WSM vs LSM Material Properties

| **Parameter** | **Working Stress Method (WSM)** | **Limit State Method (LSM)** |
|---|---|---|
| Design philosophy | Elastic behaviour, no cracking | Inelastic behaviour, cracking allowed |
| Material partial safety factor (steel) | $\gamma_m = 1.0$ (i.e., not used) | $\gamma_{ms} = 1.15$ |
| Material partial safety factor (concrete) | $\gamma_m = 1.0$ | $\gamma_{mc} = 1.50$ |
| Design strength of steel | $f_y = 415$ N/mm² (direct) | $f_{yd} = f_y / 1.15 = 360.87$ N/mm² |
| Design strength of concrete (bending) | $\sigma_{cbc} = 0.33 f_{ck}$ (clause 21.1, B-1.3) | $f_d = 0.446 f_{ck}$ |
| Modular ratio used? | Yes, $m = 40/\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | Not directly (uses $E_s$ and $E_c$ in deflection) |
| Governing code (KTU 2024) | IS 456:2000 Appendix B | IS 456:2000 Cl. 36 |

### 3.5 Worked Example: Calculation of E_c, m, and σ_s for M25 Concrete

**Given:** Beam in M25 concrete, Fe 415 steel, depth of neutral axis $x = 0.4d$, bending moment $M = 100$ kN·m.

**Required:** Find $E_c$, modular ratio, and stress in steel for extreme fibre stress in concrete of 8.3 N/mm².

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$\text{Step 1: Calculate } E_c$$

$$E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}} = 5000 \sqrt{25} = 5000 \times 5 = 25{,}000 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 25 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\text{Step 2: Calculate modular ratio (short-term)}$$

$$m = \frac{40}{\sqrt{f_{ck}}} = \frac{40}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{40}{5} = 8$$

$$\text{Step 3: Calculate modular ratio (long-term)}$$

$$m_{long} = \frac{280}{3\sqrt{f_{ck}}} = \frac{280}{3 \times 5} = \frac{280}{15} = 18.67$$

$$\text{Step 4: Calculate stress in steel using derived formula}$$

$$\sigma_s = m \cdot \sigma_c \cdot \frac{d}{x} = 8 \times 8.3 \times \frac{d}{0.4d} = 8 \times 8.3 \times 2.5$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_s = 166 \text{ N/mm}^2}$$

$$\text{Step 5: Verification with long-term modular ratio}$$

$$\sigma_{s,long} = 18.67 \times 8.3 \times 2.5 = 387.4 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad (\text{greater than } f_y = 415, \text{ but close})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The answer $\sigma_s = 166$ N/mm² is well below the yield strength of 415 N/mm², indicating the section is **under-reinforced** in WSM terminology — a desirable failure mode (ductile).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Stress-Strain Behaviour of Concrete

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the conceptual stress-strain response of concrete in uniaxial compression, highlighting the key points of interest to KTU students.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Origin: Zero Stress, Zero Strain] --> B[Linear Elastic Region: 0 to 0.30 fc]
    B --> C[Initial Cracking Point: Approximately 0.30 fc]
    C --> D[Non-Linear Inelastic Region: 0.30 fc to 0.75 fc]
    D --> E[Internal Micro-Cracking Intensifies]
    E --> F[Peak Stress: fc at Strain Approximately 0.002]
    F --> G[Descending Branch: Strain Softening]
    G --> H[Residual Stress at Strain 0.0035: Approximately 0.40 fc]
    H --> I[Ultimate Crushing Strain: 0.0035 per IS 456]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:3px
    style I fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#f1f8e9,stroke:#33691e
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style D fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style E fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style G fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
    style H fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f
```

### 4.2 Stress-Strain Behaviour of Mild Steel vs HYSD

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Origin] --> B[Linear Elastic Region E = 2e5 N per mm squared]
    B --> C{Type of Steel?}
    
    C -->|Mild Steel Fe 250| D[Sharp Yield Plateau at 250 N per mm squared]
    D --> E[Strain Hardening Region]
    E --> F[Ultimate Stress 410 N per mm squared]
    F --> G[Necking and Rupture at Strain 20 percent]
    
    C -->|HYSD Fe 415| H[Elastic Limit at 350 N per mm squared]
    H --> I[No Sharp Yield Point]
    I --> J[Proof Stress 0.2 percent Offset: 415 N per mm squared]
    J --> K[Strain Hardening to 540 N per mm squared]
    K --> L[Rupture at Strain 14.5 percent]
    
    C -->|HYSD Fe 500| M[Elastic Limit at 420 N per mm squared]
    M --> N[Proof Stress 0.2 percent Offset: 500 N per mm squared]
    N --> O[Strain Hardening to 545 N per mm squared]
    O --> P[Rupture at Strain 12 percent]
    
    style A fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px
    style J fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:3px
    style N fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:3px
    style G fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
    style L fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
    style P fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Concrete as a Composite System

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Inputs["INPUTS TO THE COMPOSITE SYSTEM"]
        Input1[Cement OPC or PPC]
        Input2[Fine Aggregate Sand]
        Input3[Coarse Aggregate 20mm down]
        Input4[Water Potable Quality]
        Input5[Admixtures Plasticisers or Superplasticisers]
    end
    
    subgraph Mixing["MIXING AND PLACEMENT STAGE"]
        Process1[Batching by Weight]
        Process2[Mixing in Drum or Pan Mixer]
        Process3[Transportation by Chute or Pump]
        Process4[Placement in Formwork]
        Process5[Compaction by Needle Vibrator]
    end
    
    subgraph Curing["CURING AND HARDENING STAGE"]
        Curing1[Initial Set 0 to 4 hours]
        Curing2[Final Set 4 to 10 hours]
        Curing3[7 Day Strength Approximately 65 percent of 28 Day]
        Curing4[28 Day Design Strength fck]
        Curing5[Long Term Gain up to 1 Year]
    end
    
    subgraph Outputs["ENGINEERED OUTPUT PROPERTIES"]
        Out1[Compressive Strength fck]
        Out2[Tensile Strength fctsp]
        Out3[Modulus of Elasticity Ec]
        Out4[Durability Cover and Permeability]
        Out5[Workability Slump Value]
    end
    
    Input1 --> Process1
    Input2 --> Process1
    Input3 --> Process1
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define characteristic compressive strength of concrete as per IS 456:2000. How is it determined experimentally?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **characteristic compressive strength of concrete ($f_{ck}$)** is defined per **IS 456:2000 (Clause 2.2.2.1)** as the strength of concrete below which not more than **5%** of the test results are expected to fall. In other words, it is the value of the compressive strength below which the probability of falling is only **5%**, ensuring a **95% confidence level**.

**Experimental Determination (as per IS 516:1959):**
1. Standard **150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm** cubes are cast from a fresh concrete batch using standard moulds.
2. The cubes are compacted by vibration or tamping and cured in water at **27 ± 2°C** for **28 days**.
3. On the 28th day, the cubes are placed in a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** with the load applied **perpendicular to the casting direction** at a rate of **140 kg/cm²/min (13.73 N/mm²/min)**.
4. The maximum load at failure is recorded, and the compressive strength is calculated as:

$$f_{ck} = \frac{\text{Load at failure (N)}}{\text{Cross-sectional area (mm}^2\text{)}} \quad \text{N/mm}^2$$

5. The test is conducted on a minimum of **3 cubes**, and the **mean strength** is reported as the characteristic strength (provided no individual value deviates by more than ±15% from the mean).

**(1 mark) — Definition with 5% criterion**
**(1 mark) — Cube size and curing details**
**(1 mark) — Loading rate and formula**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between the stress-strain behaviour of mild steel and HYSD bars with suitable diagrams.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Feature** | **Mild Steel (Fe 250)** | **HYSD Bars (Fe 415/500)** |
|---|---|---|
| Yield point | **Clearly defined** (sharp knee) | **No sharp yield point** — defined by 0.2% proof stress |
| Yield plateau | Long, flat plateau after yield | No plateau; continuous curve |
| Strain at first yield | ≈ 0.12% | ≈ 0.20% (proof strain) |
| Ultimate strength | ≈ 410 N/mm² (≈ 1.64 $f_y$) | ≈ 1.08–1.20 $f_y$ |
| Strain at rupture | ≈ 20% (very ductile) | ≈ 12–14.5% (lower ductility) |
| Ductility | Very high | Lower (improved in 'D' variants) |
| Use | Mostly obsolete, plain bars | Most common in modern RCC |

**Mild Steel Diagram (Verbal Description for 1 mark):**
A nearly linear rise from origin up to point A (elastic limit) at $f_y = 250$ N/mm² and strain ≈ 0.0012. Then a flat horizontal plateau from A to B, after which strain hardening continues up to the ultimate stress at point C (≈ 410 N/mm²). Final rupture occurs after necking at a strain of about 20%.

**HYSD Diagram (Verbal Description for 1 mark):**
A linear elastic region steeper than mild steel, reaching 0.2% proof stress at the specified $f_y$ (415 or 500 N/mm²). No plateau; instead, a smooth curve transitions directly into the strain-hardening region up to the ultimate stress, and finally rupture occurs at a lower strain (12–14.5%).

**(1 mark) — Tabular distinction**
**(1 mark) — Mild steel diagram description**
**(1 mark) — HYSD diagram description**

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Question Paper Style]**

**Q3. (a)** Explain the various **mechanical properties of concrete** that are significant in the design of reinforced concrete structures. Mention the relevant IS code clauses for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The mechanical properties of concrete that govern the design of RCC structures are:

**1. Compressive Strength ($f_{ck}$):**
- Concrete is graded by characteristic cube compressive strength at 28 days, measured on 150 mm cubes per **IS 516:1959**.
- Standard grades for RCC: M20, M25, M30, etc.
- **IS 456:2000 Cl. 6.1** mandates **M20 minimum for ordinary exposure** and **M25 minimum for moderate exposure** in RCC.

**2. Tensile Strength:**
- Concrete's tensile strength is small and is not used directly in flexural design.
- Measured indirectly by:
  - **Split Cylinder Test (IS 5816:1999):** $f_{ct,sp} = 0.49\sqrt{f_{ck}}$
  - **Flexure Test (Modulus of Rupture):** $f_{cr} = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ per **IS 456:2000 Cl. 6.1.5**

**3. Modulus of Elasticity ($E_c$):**
- Per **IS 456:2000 Cl. 6.2.3.1**: $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ N/mm²
- This is the **secant modulus** at one-third of the failure load.

**4. Poisson's Ratio:**
- Per **IS 456:2000 Cl. 5.4.4.4**: $\mu_c = 0.20$

**5. Creep and Shrinkage:**
- Creep coefficient $\theta = 1.5$ to 2.0
- Shrinkage strain ≈ 0.04% to 0.10%
- Affects long-term deflection and prestress losses.

**6. Bond Strength:**
- Per **IS 456:2000 Cl. 5.5**, bond stress = $0.6 \times$ bond stress for plain bars for deformed bars (allowable design bond stress for M25 ≈ 1.40 N/mm² in tension).

**7. Durability:**
- Governed by exposure conditions, minimum cement content, maximum W/C ratio, and minimum cover per **IS 456:2000 Table 3, 4, 5, 16**.

**Rubric distribution (7 marks):**
- (2 marks) — Compressive and tensile strength with formulas
- (2 marks) — $E_c$ and Poisson's ratio with formulas and clauses
- (2 marks) — Creep, shrinkage, bond with numerical values
- (1 mark) — Durability and IS code references

---

**(b)** A reinforced concrete column is made of **M30** concrete and **Fe 500** steel reinforcement. Calculate:
(i) Short-term modulus of elasticity of concrete
(ii) Short-term modular ratio
(iii) Long-term modular ratio (assuming creep coefficient = 1.5)
(iv) Modular ratio per IS 456 for sustained loading

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $f_{ck} = 30$ N/mm², $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\theta = 1.5$

**Step 1: Short-term modulus of elasticity of concrete**

$$E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}} = 5000\sqrt{30}$$

$$E_c = 5000 \times 5.4772 = 27{,}386 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 27.39 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 2: Short-term modular ratio**

$$m = \frac{E_s}{E_c} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{27{,}386}$$

$$m = 7.30 \approx 7.30$$

$$\text{Verification using direct formula: } m = \frac{40}{\sqrt{30}} = \frac{40}{5.4772} = 7.30 \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Long-term modular ratio (with creep coefficient = 1.5)**

$$m_{long} = m \times (1 + \theta) = 7.30 \times (1 + 1.5) = 7.30 \times 2.5 = 18.25$$

**Step 4: Per IS 456 formula for sustained loading**

$$m_{IS} = \frac{280}{3\sqrt{f_{ck}}} = \frac{280}{3 \times 5.4772} = \frac{280}{16.432} = 17.04$$

**Comparison Table (for clarity):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $E_c$ (short-term) | 27,386 N/mm² |
| $m$ (short-term) | 7.30 |
| $m_{long}$ (with $\theta = 1.5$) | 18.25 |
| $m$ (per IS 456) | 17.04 |

**Note:** The IS 456 value (17.04) is slightly less than the calculated long-term value (18.25) because the IS formula uses a slightly different effective modulus formulation.

**Rubric distribution (7 marks):**
- (1.5 marks) — $E_c$ calculation
- (1.5 marks) — Short-term $m$ with verification
- (2 marks) — Long-term $m$ with creep
- (2 marks) — IS 456 long-term $m$ + comparison table

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q4. (a)** Explain with neat sketches the **stress-strain curves** for:
(i) Concrete in uniaxial compression
(ii) Mild steel in tension
(iii) HYSD bars in tension

Highlight the salient points on each curve. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Concrete in Uniaxial Compression (3 marks):**

**Sketch Description:**
- **X-axis:** Strain ($\varepsilon$)
- **Y-axis:** Stress ($\sigma$)
- **Curve:** Parabolic shape with a steep initial linear region, gradually flattening to a peak at strain ≈ 0.002, then a **descending branch** (strain softening).
- **Key points:**
  - **Origin (0, 0):** Initial state
  - **0.30 $f_c$:** End of truly linear-elastic region
  - **0.75 $f_c$:** Internal micro-cracks begin to develop
  - **$f_c$ at $\varepsilon_c \approx 0.002$:** Peak (compressive strength)
  - **$0.85 f_c$ at $\varepsilon_c \approx 0.003$ to 0.0035:** Design yield point per **IS 456:2000 Cl. 6.2.3.2**
  - **$\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$:** Ultimate crushing strain (IS 456:2000)

**Salient Features:**
- Non-linear from the start
- Strain at peak is constant ≈ 0.002
- Failure is by progressive internal cracking
- Descent beyond peak is due to coalescence of micro-cracks

**(ii) Mild Steel (Fe 250) in Tension (2 marks):**

**Sketch Description:**
- Linear elastic portion from O to A (proportional limit at $f_p \approx 200$ N/mm², $\varepsilon \approx 0.001$)
- **Sharp yield point** at A' to A (yield stress $f_y = 250$ N/mm²)
- **Perfectly plastic plateau** from A to B at constant stress
- **Strain hardening** from B to C (ultimate stress $f_u \approx 410$ N/mm²)
- **Necking** from C to D
- **Fracture** at D, $\varepsilon \approx 0.20$ (20%)

**Salient Features:**
- Clearly defined yield point
- Large plastic strain capacity
- Total elongation ≈ 20–25%

**(iii) HYSD Bars (Fe 415/500) in Tension (2 marks):**

**Sketch Description:**
- Linear elastic from O to A (higher slope, $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²)
- **No sharp yield** — smooth transition begins around 80% of $f_y$
- **0.2% proof stress** is defined: a line is drawn parallel to the elastic line from $\varepsilon = 0.002$ on the strain axis. The stress where this line meets the curve is the proof stress (which is taken as $f_y$)
- The curve continues smoothly into strain hardening to $f_u \approx 1.08 f_y$
- **Fracture** at $\varepsilon \approx 0.12$–0.145 (12–14.5%)

**Salient Features:**
- No definite yield point
- Higher strength (415 or 500 N/mm²)
- Lower ductility than mild steel
- Specified minimum elongation: 14.5% for Fe 415, 12% for Fe 500

---

**(b)** A simply supported **RCC beam** of effective span 5 m, width 230 mm, and effective depth 450 mm is reinforced with 4 bars of 16 mm diameter. The beam is subjected to a service load bending moment of **80 kN·m**. Using **Working Stress Method (WSM)** and M20 concrete with Fe 415 steel, calculate:
(i) Stress in concrete at the top fibre
(ii) Stress in steel at the level of reinforcement

Assume neutral axis depth factor $x = 0.30 d$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Span = 5 m
- $b = 230$ mm, $d = 450$ mm
- Reinforcement: 4 bars of 16 mm $\phi$ → $A_{st} = 4 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(16)^2 = 4 \times 201.06 = 804.25$ mm²
- $M = 80$ kN·m $= 80 \times 10^6$ N·mm
- M20 concrete, Fe 415 steel
- $x = 0.30d = 0.30 \times 450 = 135$ mm
- $f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm²

**Step 1: Calculate Modular Ratio**

$$m = \frac{40}{\sqrt{f_{ck}}} = \frac{40}{\sqrt{20}} = \frac{40}{4.472} = 8.944 \approx 8.94$$

**Step 2: Verify the assumed neutral axis using the transformed section method**

For a rectangular beam, the lever arm $z = d - x/3 = 450 - 135/3 = 450 - 45 = 405$ mm.

For the beam to be safe in bending, the resisting moment must equal the applied moment:

$$M = \sigma_{cbc} \cdot b \cdot x \cdot (d - x/3)$$

We need to find $\sigma_{cbc}$:

$$\sigma_{cbc} = \frac{M}{b \cdot x \cdot (d - x/3)} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{230 \times 135 \times 405}$$

$$\sigma_{cbc} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{12{,}575{,}250} = 6.36 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Check:** For M20, $\sigma_{cbc,allowable} = 0.33 f_{ck} = 0.33 \times 20 = 6.6$ N/mm² per **IS 456:2000 Cl. B-1.3, Table 21**.

Since 6.36 < 6.6, the section is safe in concrete.

**(i) Stress in concrete at top fibre: $\sigma_c = 6.36$ N/mm²** (within permissible limit)

**Step 3: Calculate stress in steel**

From the derived formula:

$$\sigma_s = m \cdot \sigma_c \cdot \frac{d}{x}$$

$$\sigma_s = 8.944 \times 6.36 \times \frac{450}{135}$$

$$\sigma_s = 8.944 \times 6.36 \times 3.333 = 189.6 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Check:** $\sigma_{st,allowable} = 230$ N/mm² for Fe 415 (per **IS 456:2000 Table 22**).

Since 189.6 < 230, the section is safe in steel.

**(ii) Stress in steel: $\sigma_s = 189.6$ N/mm²**

**Rubric distribution (7 marks):**
- (1.5 marks) — Modular ratio calculation
- (2 marks) — Stress in concrete with verification
- (2.5 marks) — Stress in steel with verification
- (1 mark) — Final summary statement with allowable limits

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS — READ CAREFULLY:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the square root in $E_c$ formula:** Students often write $E_c = 5000 \times f_{ck}$ (which is 100× wrong). Always write $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$.
>
> 2. **Using $E_s = 2.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm² (European value):** KTU strictly follows **IS 456**, where $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². Wrong value = wrong modular ratio = cascading errors.
>
> 3. **Confusing $0.33 f_{ck}$ (WSM) with $0.446 f_{ck}$ (LSM):** These are two different design philosophies. WSM uses 0.33 (per Cl. B-1.3) and LSM uses 0.446 (per Cl. 36.1). The question will specify which method.
>
> 4. **Ignoring creep in deflection and shrinkage calculations:** IS 456 mandates considering creep for long-term deflections. Forgetting $\theta = 1.5$ to 2.0 leads to underestimate of deflection.
>
> 5. **Mixing units:** Some students use $f_{ck}$ in MPa and $E_c$ in GPa without conversion. Always keep **N/mm² = MPa** consistent.
>
> 6. **Not stating the IS clause:** Examiners award marks for citing the correct **IS 456:2000 clause number**. Always write "per IS 456:2000 Cl. 6.2.3.1" etc.
>
> 7. **Confusing cube strength with cylinder strength:** $f_{cy} = 0.8 f_{ck}$ is empirical. Use $f_{ck}$ (cube) unless the problem explicitly gives cylinder data.
>
> 8. **Using $f_y = 415$ instead of $f_{yd} = 360.87$ in LSM:** This is a one-step error that propagates into the design.
>
> 9. **Forgetting to apply partial safety factors in LSM:** $\gamma_{ms} = 1.15$ for steel, $\gamma_{mc} = 1.50$ for concrete. Skipping this loses easy marks.
>
> 10. **Mermaid diagram errors:** If drawing diagrams, use straight lines, label all axes with units, and mark all key points (e.g., 0.002 strain for concrete peak, 0.2% proof strain for HYSD).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MODULE 1 — PROPERTIES OF CONCRETE AND STEEL: FINAL CHEAT SHEET**
>
> **CONCRETE PROPERTIES:**
> - **Characteristic strength ($f_{ck}$):** Cube strength at 28 days, with 5% defectives allowed.
> - **Minimum grade for RCC:** M20 (M25 for moderate exposure, M30 for severe).
> - **Modulus of Elasticity:** $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ N/mm² (IS 456 Cl. 6.2.3.1).
> - **Poisson's ratio:** $\mu_c = 0.20$.
> - **Tensile strength:** $f_{ct,sp} = 0.49\sqrt{f_{ck}}$; $f_{cr} = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$.
> - **Creep coefficient:** $\theta = 1.5$ to 2.0.
> - **Density (RCC):** 25 kN/m³ (IS 456 Cl. 5.4).
> - **Workability:** Slump test most common; minimum 50 mm for beams, 75 mm for columns.
> - **Durability:** Governed by exposure condition, min. cement, max. W/C ratio, min. cover.
> - **Ultimate strain in compression:** $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$ (design value, IS 456 Cl. 6.2.3.2).
>
> **STEEL PROPERTIES:**
> - **Modulus of Elasticity:** $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² (IS 456 Cl. 5.6.3.1). **Memorize this.**
> - **Common grades:** Fe 250 (obsolete), Fe 415, Fe 500, Fe 550, Fe 600.
> - **Modern preference:** Fe 500 or Fe 500D (TMT bars with higher ductility).
> - **Yield definition for HYSD:** 0.2% proof stress (since no sharp yield point).
> - **Density:** 78.5 kN/m³.
> - **Coefficient of thermal expansion:** $12 \times 10^{-6}$ per °C.
> - **Ultimate strain in tension:** 0.002 + $f_y / (1.15 E_s) = 0.002 + 0.0017 = 0.0037$ (for Fe 415) per IS 456 Cl. 6.2.3.1.
>
> **MODULAR RATIO:**
> - Short-term: $m = E_s / E_c = 40 / \sqrt{f_{ck}}$
> - Long-term (sustained): $m = 280 / (3\sqrt{f_{ck})$
> - Higher modular ratio for long-term due to creep.
>
> **CRITICAL STRESS-STRAIN RELATIONSHIPS:**
> - **Concrete:** Non-linear from origin; peak at $\varepsilon = 0.002$, $f_c$; design yield at $0.446 f_{ck}$ for LSM.
> - **Mild Steel:** Distinct yield at 250 N/mm², plastic plateau, ultimate at 410 N/mm², fracture at 20% strain.
> - **HYSD:** No distinct yield; 0.2% proof stress = 415 or 500 N/mm²; ultimate at ~1.08–1.20 $f_y$; fracture at 12–14.5% strain.
>
> **IS CODE MANDATORY CITATIONS:**
> - IS 456:2000 — Plain and Reinforced Concrete Code of Practice
> - IS 516:1959 — Methods of Tests for Strength of Concrete
> - IS 5816:1999 — Splitting Tensile Strength of Concrete
> - IS 1786:2008 — High Strength Deformed Steel Bars for Concrete Reinforcement
> - IS 9103:1999 — Concrete Admixtures Specification
>
> **NUMERICAL VALUES TO MEMORIZE (REVISION CYCLE):**
> - $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
> - $\mu_c = 0.20$, $\mu_s = 0.30$
> - $\gamma_c = 25$ kN/m³ (RCC), $\gamma_s = 78.5$ kN/m³
> - $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$ (concrete), $\varepsilon_{su} \approx 0.0037$ (Fe 415)
> - $0.33 f_{ck}$ (WSM allowable concrete stress)
> - $0.446 f_{ck}$ (LSM design concrete stress)
> - $f_{yd} = f_y / 1.15$ (LSM design steel stress)
> - $\gamma_{mc} = 1.50$, $\gamma_{ms} = 1.15$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Philosophies of Design by Limit State Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Philosophies of Design by Limit State Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Design Philosophies

A *design philosophy* is the conceptual framework that establishes the governing criteria a structure must satisfy to ensure **safety, serviceability, and economy** under all anticipated loading conditions during its intended design life. For **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC)**, KTU 2024 Scheme (aligned to **IS 456:2000**) recognizes three historical and contemporary design philosophies:

1. **Working Stress Method (WSM)** — Historically dominant; based on elastic theory.
2. **Ultimate Load Method (ULM)** — Also called the *Load Factor Method*; based on ultimate strength.
3. **Limit State Method (LSM)** — Modern, code-mandated method; the synthesis of both prior methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** and the current **IS 456:2000** code, the **Limit State Method (LSM)** is the *only* recommended and examinable design philosophy. WSM and ULM appear only for **historical context and comparison**.

## 1.2 Formal Definition — Limit State Method

> **Limit State Method (LSM)** is a design philosophy in which a structure is proportioned such that it remains fit for use, does not collapse, and remains serviceable during its intended life, by ensuring that the structure does not reach a *limit state* — a condition beyond which the structure no longer satisfies the relevant design criteria.

The structure is designed to satisfy **two principal limit states**:

- **Limit State of Collapse (LSC):** Ensures safety against the ultimate failure of the structure.
- **Limit State of Serviceability (LSS):** Ensures the structure remains functional and comfortable for use under service loads (e.g., deflection, cracking, vibration).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Designing a Bridge for a Heavy Truck**
>
> Imagine designing a small bridge that must carry a heavy truck every day.
>
> - **WSM (Working Stress) approach:** You assume the truck is *much* heavier than it really is, and design the bridge with massive safety buffers even for everyday traffic. The bridge is *safe*, but **wasteful and uneconomical**.
>
> - **ULM (Ultimate Load) approach:** You push the bridge to near-collapse to find its true maximum strength, then design it at a fraction of that. The bridge is *strong*, but the rider feels excessive deflection and cracks even under normal use — **unsafe in service**.
>
> - **LSM (Limit State Method) approach:** You find the *true* maximum strength AND check the *true* deflection, cracking, and vibration under *actual* daily traffic. You then design the bridge using *partial safety factors* — not too conservative, not too reckless. It **fails safely** only in the most extreme events AND remains **comfortable** under everyday use.

This dual check — *collapse safety + service comfort* — is the essence of LSM.

## 1.4 Why LSM is Superior

- It considers **multiple failure modes** (collapse, deflection, cracking, fatigue, durability).
- It uses **partial safety factors** (separate for loads and materials) — providing rational economy.
- It is the **basis of IS 456:2000**, the governing Indian code.
- It is globally harmonized with **Eurocode 2 (EC2)**, **ACI 318**, and **BS 8110** in philosophy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Concepts in Limit State Method

### 2.1.1 Characteristic Strength ($f_{ck}$, $f_y$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Characteristic strength** is the value of material strength below which **not more than 5%** of test results are expected to fall. It is the statistical lower-bound (5th percentile) value, accounting for material variability.

- **Characteristic compressive strength of concrete:**
$$f_{ck} = f_m - 1.65\,\sigma$$
where $f_m$ = mean strength, $\sigma$ = standard deviation.

- **Characteristic yield strength of steel:**
$$f_y = \text{specified minimum yield stress of steel}$$

### 2.1.2 Characteristic Load ($F_k$ / $Q_k$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Characteristic load** is the load that has a **95% probability of not being exceeded** during the life of the structure. It represents the upper-bound (95th percentile) value of the load.

For example:
- **Dead Load (DL):** Self-weight + permanent fixtures (assessed by unit weight and dimensions).
- **Imposed (Live) Load (IL / LL):** As per **IS 875 (Part 2)**.
- **Wind Load (WL):** As per **IS 875 (Part 3)**.
- **Earthquake Load (EL):** As per **IS 1893 (Part 1)**.

### 2.1.3 Design Strength (Permissible Stress in LSM)

$$\boxed{f_d = \dfrac{f_{ck}}{\gamma_m}} \quad \text{(for concrete)}$$

$$\boxed{f_d = \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_m}} \quad \text{(for steel)}$$

where $\gamma_m$ = **partial safety factor for material**.

### 2.1.4 Design Load

$$\boxed{F_d = \gamma_f \cdot F_k}$$

where $\gamma_f$ = **partial safety factor for load**.

### 2.1.5 The Core Inequality of LSM

For **every** limit state considered:

$$\boxed{\text{Design Action (Load)} \leq \text{Design Resistance (Strength)}}$$

Or in formal notation:

$$\gamma_f \cdot F_k \leq \dfrac{f_{ck} \text{ (or } f_y)}{\gamma_m}$$

## 2.2 Partial Safety Factors — The Heart of LSM

### 2.2.1 Partial Safety Factor for Materials ($\gamma_m$) — IS 456:2000, Table 5

| Material | Limit State of Collapse | Limit State of Serviceability |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Concrete | **1.50** | **1.00** (or 1.10 for stresses) |
| Steel | **1.15** | **1.00** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the **Limit State of Collapse**, the values $\gamma_m = 1.5$ (concrete) and $\gamma_m = 1.15$ (steel) are *frequently tested* in KTU exams and must be **memorized exactly**.

### 2.2.2 Partial Safety Factor for Loads ($\gamma_f$) — IS 456:2000, Table 4

| Load Combination | DL | IL (LL) | WL / EL |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| DL + IL | **1.5** | **1.5** | — |
| DL + WL | 1.5 or **0.9*** | — | **1.5** |
| DL + IL + WL / EL | **1.2** | **1.2** | **1.2** |
| DL + IL (Stability check) | **1.2** | **1.2** | — |

*\*0.9 is used when the dead load stabilizes the structure against overturning/uplift from wind/earthquake. **Students must NOT use 0.9 as default** — it is a conditional substitution.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **most common KTU exam combination** is $1.5\,(DL + IL)$ for *collapse* design, and $\gamma_f = 1.0$ for *serviceability* design.

## 2.3 Classification of Limit States (IS 456:2000)

The IS code categorizes limit states into two main groups:

### A. Limit State of Collapse (LSC) — Safety

The structure must not collapse under the *worst credible* loading. Sub-checks include:

1. **Flexure (Bending):** $M_u \leq M_{u,lim}$
2. **Shear:** $V_u \leq V_{u,capacity}$
3. **Torsion:** Combined with shear and bending
4. **Compression (Axial + Bending):** For columns
5. **Punching Shear:** For slabs on isolated footings
6. **Bond:** Between concrete and steel

### B. Limit State of Serviceability (LSS) — Usability

The structure must remain *comfortable* and *aesthetic* under *everyday* loads. Sub-checks include:

1. **Deflection:** $a_{actual} \leq a_{permissible}$ (typically $L/250$ for beams)
2. **Cracking:** $w_{actual} \leq w_{permissible}$
3. **Vibration:** (Not explicitly covered in IS 456 for buildings)
4. **Durability:** Cover and concrete grade requirements

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Limit State Method is the **universal design framework** in modern structural engineering:

- **Buildings:** Beams, columns, slabs, footings in multi-storey RCC structures.
- **Bridges:** Deck slabs, piers, girders.
- **Water Tanks:** Where crack control (serviceability) is as critical as collapse safety.
- **Nuclear Containments:** Where serviceability crack widths are extremely stringent.
- **Coastal/Marine Structures:** Where durability (cover and grade) is the governing limit state.

## 2.5 Comparison of Design Philosophies

| Aspect | WSM | ULM | LSM |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Theoretical basis | Elastic theory | Plastic theory | Probabilistic + semi-empirical |
| Stress check at | Working (service) load | Ultimate load | Both |
| Factor of safety | Single (global) | Single (load factor only) | **Partial (separate for load & material)** |
| Serviceability check | Implicit | **Not checked** | **Explicitly checked** |
| Economy | Over-conservative | Economical but unsafe in service | **Optimal balance** |
| Code status (IS 456) | Withdrawn for design | Withdrawn for design | **Current & mandatory** |
| Failure consideration | Yield at extreme fibre | Full plastic collapse | **Multiple limit states** |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners will **deduct marks** if a student uses a *Working Stress* formula in a 2024-Scheme question. Always state **"As per IS 456:2000 Limit State Method"** in your answers.

## 2.6 Why Partial Safety Factors Instead of a Single Factor of Safety?

A single Factor of Safety (FoS) treats *all* uncertainties equally. LSM splits them into:
- $\gamma_f$ → accounts for **uncertainty in loads** (e.g., live load variability is higher than dead load).
- $\gamma_m$ → accounts for **uncertainty in material strength** (e.g., concrete is more variable than steel, hence $\gamma_m^{conc} > \gamma_m^{steel}$).

This **rational decomposition** leads to economical yet safe designs.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Code Reference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Design strength of concrete | $f_d = f_{ck} / \gamma_m$ | IS 456 §36.4.1 |
| 2 | Design strength of steel | $f_d = f_y / \gamma_m$ | IS 456 §36.4.1 |
| 3 | Design load | $F_d = \gamma_f \cdot F_k$ | IS 456 §36.4.1 |
| 4 | Characteristic concrete strength | $f_{ck} = f_m - 1.65\sigma$ | IS 456 §36.1 |
| 5 | $\gamma_m$ for concrete (collapse) | **1.50** | Table 5 |
| 6 | $\gamma_m$ for steel (collapse) | **1.15** | Table 5 |
| 7 | $\gamma_f$ for DL+IL (collapse) | **1.50** (DL), **1.50** (IL) | Table 4 |
| 8 | $\gamma_f$ for DL+IL+WL/EL | **1.20** for all | Table 4 |
| 9 | $\gamma_f$ for serviceability | **1.00** | Table 4 |
| 10 | Core LSM inequality | $\gamma_f F_k \leq f_{ck}/\gamma_m$ (or $f_y/\gamma_m$) | IS 456 §40.1 |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Single FoS to Partial Safety Factors

### 3.1.1 Working Stress Method (WSM) — Single Factor Approach

In the older Working Stress Method, a single global factor of safety (FoS) was applied to the material's ultimate strength to obtain a *permissible stress*:

$$
\sigma_{permissible} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{F.o.S.}
$$

**Example:** For M20 concrete, ultimate strength $f_{ck} = 20 \text{ N/mm}^2$, and FoS = 3 (typical in WSM).
Then: $\sigma_{permissible} = 20/3 = 6.67 \text{ N/mm}^2$.

This approach lumps *all* uncertainties (loads + materials) into one number — **unrealistic and over-conservative**.

### 3.1.2 Ultimate Load Method (ULM) — Single Load Factor Approach

In ULM, the structure is designed to resist a *factored* load obtained by multiplying the service load by a *load factor* (LF):

$$
\text{Design Load} = LF \times \text{Service Load}
$$

**Example:** For DL = 10 kN, IL = 15 kN, with $LF = 1.5$ (per IS 456:1978, Table 4).
Then: $\text{Design Load} = 1.5 \times (10 + 15) = 37.5 \text{ kN}$.

ULM *does not* check deflection or cracking at service loads — a major drawback.

### 3.1.3 Limit State Method (LSM) — Decoupled Partial Safety Factors

In LSM, the load and material uncertainties are treated *separately* and *then* combined.

**Step 1:** Define characteristic values.
- $f_{ck}$ = characteristic concrete strength (5% fractile).
- $f_y$ = characteristic steel yield strength.
- $F_k$ = characteristic load (95% fractile).

**Step 2:** Apply partial safety factors to obtain design values.

The *design* resistance (capacity) of a section is computed using *design* material strengths:

$$
f_{d,conc} = \dfrac{f_{ck}}{\gamma_{m,conc}} = \dfrac{f_{ck}}{1.5}
$$

$$
f_{d,steel} = \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_{m,steel}} = \dfrac{f_y}{1.15}
$$

The *design* action (demand) is computed using *factored* loads:

$$
F_d = \gamma_f \cdot F_k
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the fundamental LSM inequality.

$$
F_d \leq R_d \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \gamma_f \cdot F_k \leq \dfrac{f_{ck} \text{ (or } f_y)}{\gamma_m}
$$

This is *not* equivalent to a single FoS, because the partial factors are **multiplicative in nature but rationalized in calibration**, yielding a structure that is both safe and economical.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: Numerical Application of Partial Safety Factors

> **KTU 2024 Numerical Style Problem (Frequently Asked)**

A simply-supported RCC beam is required to support:
- Dead Load (DL) = 20 kN/m
- Imposed Live Load (IL) = 15 kN/m
- Span = 6 m
- Concrete: M25 ($f_{ck} = 25 \text{ N/mm}^2$)
- Steel: Fe500 ($f_y = 500 \text{ N/mm}^2$)

**Compute the design load and design material strengths.**

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Identify Characteristic Loads

$$
DL_k = 20 \text{ kN/m}, \quad IL_k = 15 \text{ kN/m}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Identify the Governing Load Combination

For *Limit State of Collapse* with DL + IL only:

$$
\gamma_f^{DL} = 1.5, \quad \gamma_f^{IL} = 1.5
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Compute Design (Factored) Load

$$
w_u = \gamma_f^{DL} \cdot DL_k + \gamma_f^{IL} \cdot IL_k
$$

$$
w_u = 1.5 \times 20 + 1.5 \times 15
$$

$$
w_u = 30 + 22.5 = 52.5 \text{ kN/m}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Compute Design Factored Bending Moment

$$
M_u = \dfrac{w_u L^2}{8} = \dfrac{52.5 \times 6^2}{8}
$$

$$
M_u = \dfrac{52.5 \times 36}{8} = \dfrac{1890}{8}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_u = 236.25 \text{ kN·m}}
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Compute Design Material Strengths

$$
f_d^{conc} = \dfrac{f_{ck}}{\gamma_m} = \dfrac{25}{1.5} = 16.67 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
f_d^{steel} = \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_m} = \dfrac{500}{1.15} = 434.78 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

### 3.2.6 Step 6 — Verify the LSM Inequality (Conceptual)

The section dimensions and reinforcement must be chosen such that:

$$
M_{u,capacity} \geq M_u = 236.25 \text{ kN·m}
$$

where $M_{u,capacity}$ is computed using $f_d^{conc} = 16.67$ N/mm² and $f_d^{steel} = 434.78$ N/mm². The subsequent design of section dimensions and steel area is covered in later modules.

## 3.3 Comparative Numerical Insight

| Method | Design Load on Beam (kN/m) | Material Used for Design | Deflection Check? |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| WSM | 20 + 15 = **35** | $f_{ck}/3$, $f_y/1.78$ | Implicitly via $L/d$ rule |
| ULM | $1.5 \times 35$ = **52.5** | $f_{ck}$, $f_y$ (full) | **No** |
| LSM (Collapse) | $1.5 \times 20 + 1.5 \times 15$ = **52.5** | $f_{ck}/1.5$, $f_y/1.15$ | **Yes** (explicit at $\gamma_f = 1.0$) |
| LSM (Serviceability) | $1.0 \times 35$ = **35** | $f_{ck}$, $f_y$ (full) | **Yes** |

This comparative table is a **high-yield KTU exam answer** for "Compare WSM, ULM, and LSM."

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the Limit State Method of design. List any four limit states considered in the design of RCC structures.**

**Model Answer:**

The Limit State Method (LSM) is a design philosophy in which a structure is proportioned such that it remains fit for use and does not collapse, by ensuring it does not reach a *limit state* — a condition beyond which the structure no longer satisfies the relevant design criteria. The design is based on partial safety factors applied separately to loads ($\gamma_f$) and material strengths ($\gamma_m$).

**Four limit states considered in RCC design:**

1. **Limit State of Collapse — Flexure (Bending)**
2. **Limit State of Collapse — Shear**
3. **Limit State of Serviceability — Deflection**
4. **Limit State of Serviceability — Cracking**

> *Additional acceptable limit states:* Torsion, Axial + Bending, Punching Shear, Bond, Durability.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**What is the difference between characteristic strength and design strength? Why is the partial safety factor for concrete (1.5) greater than that for steel (1.15)?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Characteristic Strength | Design Strength |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Lower 5% fractile of test results | Characteristic strength divided by $\gamma_m$ |
| Formula (concrete) | $f_{ck} = f_m - 1.65\sigma$ | $f_d = f_{ck}/\gamma_m$ |
| Purpose | Statistical measure of material quality | Stress value used in design calculations |

**Why $\gamma_m^{conc} = 1.5 > \gamma_m^{steel} = 1.15$:**

Concrete exhibits **greater inherent variability** in compressive strength than steel due to:
- Heterogeneous mix constituents (cement, aggregates, water, admixtures)
- Sensitivity to curing conditions, placement, and compaction
- Higher standard deviation in test results

Steel, being a **factory-controlled product**, has more consistent yield strength with much smaller variability. Therefore, a lower partial safety factor suffices for steel.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU ESE 2024 Scheme** Module 1, a single Part B question is mandatory, with **internal choice**. Below are **TWO independent questions** (A and B), each worth 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain in detail the various limit states to be considered in the design of RCC structures as per IS 456:2000. Distinguish clearly between the Limit State of Collapse and the Limit State of Serviceability.**

**Model Answer:**

The Limit State Method, as codified in **IS 456:2000**, classifies the various conditions a structure must be safeguarded against into two principal groups:

### A. Limit State of Collapse (LSC)

This limit state concerns the **safety** of the structure — its ability to sustain peak loads without collapse. As per IS 456 §40.1, the following collapse limit states must be checked:

1. **Flexure (Bending):** The factored bending moment $M_u$ must not exceed the ultimate moment of resistance $M_{u,Rd}$ of the section.
2. **Shear:** The factored shear force $V_u$ must be less than the shear capacity $V_c + V_s$ contributed by concrete and shear reinforcement.
3. **Torsion:** Particularly relevant for edge beams and spandrel beams; combined with shear and bending checks.
4. **Compression (Axial + Bending):** For columns, the design axial load and moment must lie within the P-M interaction envelope.
5. **Punching Shear:** For flat slabs and isolated footings, two-way shear failure must be prevented.
6. **Bond:** The development length of bars must be provided to prevent anchorage failure.

The **partial safety factors** for loads at LSC are:
- DL: $\gamma_f = 1.5$ (or 0.9 for counteracting effects)
- IL: $\gamma_f = 1.5$
- WL / EL: $\gamma_f = 1.5$ (when alone with DL) or 1.2 (in combination)

The material partial safety factors are: $\gamma_m^{conc} = 1.5$, $\gamma_m^{steel} = 1.15$.

### B. Limit State of Serviceability (LSS)

This limit state concerns the **comfort, appearance, and durability** of the structure under *everyday* service loads. IS 456 §43 prescribes:

1. **Deflection:** The maximum deflection under service loads (with $\gamma_f = 1.0$) must not exceed $L/250$ for beams (IS 456 Annex C).
2. **Cracking:** The crack width must be limited (typically 0.3 mm for general exposure, 0.2 mm for severe exposure).
3. **Vibration:** Implicitly controlled by span/depth limits.
4. **Durability:** Enforced via minimum concrete grade, minimum cover, and maximum w/c ratio for the given exposure condition.

The **partial safety factor for loads** at LSS is **$\gamma_f = 1.0$** — meaning service (unfactored) loads are used. The **material partial safety factors** are also **$\gamma_m = 1.0$** (effectively, full characteristic values are used).

### Distinguishing Table

| Aspect | Limit State of Collapse | Limit State of Serviceability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Concern | Safety against failure | Comfort and usability |
| Load factor $\gamma_f$ | 1.5 (DL+IL), 1.2 (combined) | 1.0 |
| Material factor $\gamma_m$ | 1.5 (conc), 1.15 (steel) | 1.0 |
| Load level | Factored (worst credible) | Service (actual) |
| Failure consequence | Structural collapse | Discomfort, cracking, deflection |

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A rectangular RCC beam of effective span 5 m supports a characteristic dead load of 12 kN/m (excluding self-weight) and a characteristic imposed load of 18 kN/m. The beam has a cross-section of 230 mm × 450 mm (overall). Use M25 concrete and Fe415 steel. Compute the design load and design material strengths as per IS 456:2000.**

**Model Answer:**

### Given Data
- Effective span $L = 5 \text{ m}$
- Characteristic Dead Load $DL_k = 12 \text{ kN/m}$ (excluding self-weight)
- Characteristic Imposed Load $IL_k = 18 \text{ kN/m}$
- Beam size: $b = 230$ mm, $D = 450$ mm (overall)
- Concrete: M25, $f_{ck} = 25 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- Steel: Fe415, $f_y = 415 \text{ N/mm}^2$

### Step 1 — Compute Self-Weight (Dead Load Component)

$$
w_{sw} = b \times D \times \rho_{conc} = 0.23 \times 0.45 \times 25 = 2.5875 \text{ kN/m}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Characteristic Dead Load

$$
DL_k^{total} = 12 + 2.5875 = 14.5875 \text{ kN/m} \approx 14.59 \text{ kN/m}
$$

### Step 3 — Identify Load Combination (LSC)

For **Limit State of Collapse** with DL + IL:

$$
\gamma_f^{DL} = 1.5, \quad \gamma_f^{IL} = 1.5
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Design (Factored) UDL

$$
w_u = 1.5 \times 14.59 + 1.5 \times 18
$$

$$
w_u = 21.88 + 27.0 = 48.88 \text{ kN/m}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Design Factored Bending Moment

$$
M_u = \dfrac{w_u L^2}{8} = \dfrac{48.88 \times 5^2}{8} = \dfrac{48.88 \times 25}{8}
$$

$$
M_u = \dfrac{1222}{8} = 152.75 \text{ kN·m}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Design Factored Shear Force

$$
V_u = \dfrac{w_u L}{2} = \dfrac{48.88 \times 5}{2} = 122.2 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 7 — Compute Design Material Strengths

$$
f_d^{conc} = \dfrac{f_{ck}}{\gamma_m} = \dfrac{25}{1.5} = 16.67 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
f_d^{steel} = \dfrac{f_y}{\gamma_m} = \dfrac{415}{1.15} = 360.87 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

### Final Summary (Boxed Answer)

$$
\boxed{w_u = 48.88 \text{ kN/m}, \quad M_u = 152.75 \text{ kN·m}, \quad V_u = 122.2 \text{ kN}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_d^{conc} = 16.67 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad f_d^{steel} = 360.87 \text{ N/mm}^2}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Compare the Working Stress Method, Ultimate Load Method, and Limit State Method of design. State clearly why the Limit State Method is the preferred and code-mandated method under IS 456:2000.**

**Model Answer:**

### Comparison of Design Philosophies

| Parameter | Working Stress Method (WSM) | Ultimate Load Method (ULM) | Limit State Method (LSM) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Theoretical Basis** | Elastic theory (Hooke's law) | Plastic theory | Probabilistic, semi-empirical |
| **Stress Level** | Working / Service stresses | Ultimate stresses | Both (service + ultimate) |
| **Safety Approach** | Single global Factor of Safety (FoS) | Single Load Factor (LF) | **Partial Safety Factors** ($\gamma_f$ & $\gamma_m$) |
| **Material Coefficients** | Implicit in single FoS | Implicit in load factor | **Explicit and separate** |
| **Deflection / Crack Check** | Implicit (via $L/d$ ratios) | **Not checked** | **Explicitly checked** |
| **Durability Check** | Partial | Not checked | Explicit (cover, w/c, grade) |
| **Economy** | Over-conservative, uneconomical | Economical but unsafe in service | Optimal balance |
| **Probability Basis** | Deterministic, single FoS | Deterministic, single LF | **Probabilistic, fractile-based** |
| **Code Status (IS 456:2000)** | Permitted historically; superseded | Permitted historically; superseded | **Mandatory** |
| **Load Combinations** | Single (DL + IL) | Single (DL + IL) | **Multiple (DL, IL, WL, EL)** |

### Why LSM is Preferred and Code-Mandated

The Limit State Method is mandated by **IS 456:2000** for the following reasons:

1. **Rational Treatment of Uncertainties:** Loads and material strengths vary independently. LSM uses **partial safety factors** ($\gamma_f$ for loads, $\gamma_m$ for materials) that recognize this independence — unlike WSM and ULM which apply a single global factor.

2. **Dual Safety Verification:** LSM checks both *ultimate* safety (collapse) and *everyday* usability (serviceability — deflection, cracking). WSM was overly conservative; ULM neglected serviceability entirely.

3. **Optimal Economy:** The fractile-based partial factors (5% for materials, 95% for loads) deliver a structure that is *just* safe enough and *just* serviceable — avoiding the over-design of WSM and the brittle economics of ULM.

4. **Multi-Load-Combination Capability:** LSM handles Dead Load, Live Load, Wind, and Earthquake combinations with appropriate partial factors — critical in modern multi-hazard design.

5. **Global Harmonization:** LSM is the common framework in **Eurocode 2, ACI 318, BS 8110** — making it the international standard.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**For a simply-supported RCC slab of effective span 3.5 m carrying a characteristic live load of 4 kN/m² and a floor finish of 1.5 kN/m², determine the design load per metre width using IS 456:2000 partial safety factors. Assume slab thickness of 150 mm.**

**Model Answer:**

### Given Data
- Effective span $L_{eff} = 3.5$ m
- Characteristic Live Load $LL_k = 4 \text{ kN/m}^2$
- Characteristic Floor Finish $FF_k = 1.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$
- Slab thickness $h = 150$ mm = 0.15 m
- Concrete density: $\rho_c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3$

### Step 1 — Compute Self-Weight of Slab

$$
w_{sw} = h \times \rho_c = 0.15 \times 25 = 3.75 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

### Step 2 — Total Characteristic Dead Load

$$
DL_k = w_{sw} + FF_k = 3.75 + 1.5 = 5.25 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

### Step 3 — Imposed (Live) Load

$$
IL_k = 4 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

### Step 4 — Load Combination (Limit State of Collapse, DL + IL)

Per **IS 456:2000 Table 4**:

$$
\gamma_f^{DL} = 1.5, \quad \gamma_f^{IL} = 1.5
$$

### Step 5 — Design (Factored) Load per m²

$$
w_u = 1.5 \times DL_k + 1.5 \times IL_k
$$

$$
w_u = 1.5 \times 5.25 + 1.5 \times 4
$$

$$
w_u = 7.875 + 6.0 = 13.875 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

### Step 6 — Design Load per Metre Width (for 1 m strip)

Since slab design is done per 1 m wide strip:

$$
w_u^{per\,m} = 13.875 \times 1.0 = 13.875 \text{ kN/m}
$$

### Step 7 — Design Bending Moment per m Width

For a simply-supported slab strip:

$$
M_u = \dfrac{w_u^{per\,m} \times L^2}{8} = \dfrac{13.875 \times 3.5^2}{8}
$$

$$
M_u = \dfrac{13.875 \times 12.25}{8} = \dfrac{169.97}{8} = 21.25 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

### Final Answer (Boxed)

$$
\boxed{w_u = 13.875 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \text{or}\quad 13.875 \text{ kN/m per m width}}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_u = 21.25 \text{ kN·m/m}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add self-weight of the member:** Students often use only the *given* dead load (e.g., 12 kN/m) and forget to compute $b \cdot D \cdot 25$. This leads to **under-design** — direct loss of 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Using $\gamma_f = 1.5$ for serviceability checks:** When the question asks for *deflection* or *crack width*, use $\gamma_f = 1.0$, NOT 1.5. A common, costly mistake.
>
> 3. **Confusing $\gamma_m$ values for concrete vs steel:** Memorize them — **1.5 for concrete, 1.15 for steel.** Examiners *test* this.
>
> 4. **Mixing up characteristic and design strength:** $f_{ck}$ is **characteristic**; $f_d = f_{ck}/1.5$ is **design**. Do not use $f_{ck}$ directly as the design stress in LSM.
>
> 5. **Wrong unit conversion:** $1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1 \text{ MPa}$. Keep units consistent: kN and m for loads, N and mm for stresses.
>
> 6. **In wind/earthquake combinations:** Do not blindly use 1.5 for WL/EL. Use 1.2 in combinations with DL + IL + WL/EL.
>
> 7. **Omitting the unit "kN/m per metre width" for slab problems:** Slabs are designed per 1 m strip; explicitly state this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Summary for KTU 2024 Exam Day**

- **LSM is the ONLY method accepted under IS 456:2000.** Always state *"As per IS 456:2000, Limit State Method"* in your answers.

- **Three partial safety factors are sacrosanct:**
  - $\gamma_f$ for **loads** (load variability)
  - $\gamma_m^{conc} = 1.5$ (concrete variability is high)
  - $\gamma_m^{steel} = 1.15$ (steel variability is low)

- **Memorize Table 4 values of IS 456:** DL = 1.5, IL = 1.5 (for LSC, DL+IL); $\gamma_f = 1.0$ for LSS.

- **Two principal limit states:**
  - **LSC** = Safety (factored loads, partial material factors)
  - **LSS** = Usability (service loads, full material values)

- **The Core LSM Inequality:** $\gamma_f F_k \leq f_k / \gamma_m$ — must be satisfied for *every* limit state.

- **Sub-limit-states to remember (LSC):** Flexure, Shear, Torsion, Axial+Bending, Punching, Bond.

- **Sub-limit-states to remember (LSS):** Deflection (typically $L/250$ for beams), Cracking (≤ 0.3 mm), Durability (cover, w/c, grade).

- **Why partial factors?** Loads and materials vary *independently*; lumping them into one FoS (WSM/ULM) is irrational. Decoupling gives optimal safety-economy balance.

- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $f_d = f_k / \gamma_m$
  - $F_d = \gamma_f F_k$
  - $f_{ck} = f_m - 1.65\sigma$ (5% fractile definition)

- **For numerical problems:** Always compute self-weight of the RCC member ($b \cdot D \cdot 25$ kN/m³) and add to given DL.

- **For slab problems:** Design per 1 m wide strip. State the unit "kN/m per metre width."

- **Cross-check:** For M20–M25 concrete with $\gamma_m = 1.5$, design strength ≈ 13.3–16.7 N/mm² — useful for sanity-checking answers.

- **WSM and ULM appear in KTU exams only for comparison or short-answer questions** — not for design calculations.

- **Last-line safety check:** If your design strength is *higher* than the characteristic strength, you have likely made a sign error in $\gamma_m$. Design strength should always be *lower* than characteristic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to IS 456:2000 Design Provisions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to IS 456:2000 Design Provisions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is IS 456:2000?

**IS 456:2000** is the **Indian Standard Code of Practice for Plain and Reinforced Concrete**, published by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**. It is the **fourth revision** of the standard, superseding IS 456:1978, and lays down the unified technical requirements for the **design, materials, workmanship, inspection, and maintenance** of plain and reinforced concrete structures in India.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCVT403 / Module 1):**
> IS 456:2000 forms the **single most important reference code** for the entire course on Design of Concrete Structures. Every numerical problem in KTU Part B questions (14-mark modules) is solved by strictly following the partial safety factors, limit state assumptions, and detailing rules laid down in this code.

### Formal Definition
> *"IS 456:2000 deals with the general structural use of plain and reinforced concrete, prestressed concrete, and lightweight aggregate concrete. It specifies the design criteria, structural properties of materials, durability requirements, and the partial safety factors to be applied to loads and material strengths."*

### Scope of IS 456:2000
- **Plain concrete** (un-reinforced) members and structures.
- **Reinforced concrete** (RC) members — beams, slabs, columns, footings, walls.
- **Prestressed concrete** structures (general guidelines only).
- **Light-weight aggregate concrete** structural use.

It does **NOT** cover:
- Aqueducts, chimneys, pressure vessels, or nuclear structures (covered by separate IS codes).
- Bridges (IS 456 is the base; modifications are as per IRC/IRS codes).

### The Design Philosophy Mandated by IS 456:2000
IS 456:2000 recommends the **Limit State Method (LSM)** as the primary design philosophy, while retaining the older **Working Stress Method (WSM)** as an alternative for special cases.

| Design Philosophy | Status in IS 456:2000 | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Working Stress Method (WSM) | Allowed (Annexure B) | Elastic, service loads |
| Limit State Method (LSM) | **Primary / Recommended** | Probabilistic, factored loads |
| Ultimate Load Method | **Withdrawn** (was in IS 456:1978) | Plastic, factored loads only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine you are designing a **rope bridge** to carry people across a river.
> - In the **Working Stress Method**, you assume the rope will *always* experience only "normal" tension (people walking gently) and you keep the rope stress very low. It's safe, but you end up using a thick, heavy rope that wastes material.
> - In the **Limit State Method**, you ask: "When is the bridge considered *unsafe* or *unusable*?" — either it **collapses** (Limit State of Collapse) or it **sways too much / cracks badly** (Limit State of Serviceability). You then design the rope to safely handle extreme overloads (using safety factors) AND control the everyday sag.
> 
> IS 456:2000 uses this second, smarter approach.

---

## 1.2 Key Definitions Mandated by the Code

Before we proceed, the following **KTU-highlight definitions** must be memorized verbatim:

> [!NOTE]
> **Characteristic Strength ($f_{ck}$ or $f_y$):**
> The value of the material strength **below which not more than 5%** of the test results are expected to fall. In simple words, it is the strength that 95% of samples will exceed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Strength:**
> The characteristic strength **divided by the partial safety factor** for the material ($\gamma_m$). This is the value actually used in calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Partial Safety Factor for Material ($\gamma_m$):**
> A numerical factor (≥ 1) that accounts for the possibility of the material being weaker than its characteristic value, and for inaccuracy in the assessment of its strength in the structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Partial Safety Factor for Load ($\gamma_f$):**
> A numerical factor (≥ 1) that accounts for the possibility of unfavorable deviations of loads from their characteristic (nominal) values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Limit State Method — Conceptual Framework

A **Limit State** is defined as a state of a structure **beyond which it no longer satisfies** the relevant design criteria. IS 456:2000 identifies **two broad families** of limit states:

### A. Limit State of Collapse (Ultimate Limit State)
This deals with the **safety** of the structure under the most adverse combinations of loads. Failure modes included are:
- **Flexural collapse** (yielding of steel, crushing of concrete).
- **Compression / buckling** failure of columns.
- **Shear failure** (diagonal tension, web crushing).
- **Torsion failure.**
- **Punching shear** in slabs and footings.
- **Tensile failure** of axially loaded tension members.

### B. Limit State of Serviceability
This deals with the **functioning** of the structure under **normal service loads**, ensuring comfort, appearance, and durability. Failure modes included are:
- **Deflection** — excessive sag in beams and slabs.
- **Cracking** — uncontrolled or wide cracks that cause corrosion of steel.
- **Vibration** — uncomfortable or unsafe oscillations.
- **Durability** — resistance to chemical attack, weathering, abrasion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Trick:** Most KTU questions ask *"List the various limit states"* — you **must** write **both** families with at least 2-3 examples each. A common mistake is writing only "collapse" limits.

---

## 2.2 Partial Safety Factors (The Heart of LSM)

### Partial Safety Factors for Material Strength ($\gamma_m$)
IS 456:2000 prescribes:

| Material | $\gamma_m$ |
| :--- | :---: |
| **Concrete** | **1.50** |
| **Steel** | **1.15** |

The **design strength** used in calculations is therefore:

$$
f_{d,\text{concrete}} = \frac{0.67 \, f_{ck}}{\gamma_m}
$$

$$
f_{d,\text{steel}} = \frac{f_y}{\gamma_m}
$$

> The factor **0.67** (i.e., $2/3$) is the **long-term strength reduction factor** for concrete. It accounts for the fact that under sustained loading, concrete loses some of its short-term compressive strength (sustained stress effect / creep reduction).

### Partial Safety Factors for Loads ($\gamma_f$)
IS 456:2000, Table 18:

| Load Combination | DL | LL | WL / EL |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| DL + LL | **1.5** | **1.5** | — |
| DL + WL | 1.5 or 0.9* | — | **1.5** |
| DL + EL | 1.2 or 0.9* | — | **1.2** |
| DL + LL + WL | 1.2 | 1.2 | 1.2 |
| DL + LL + EL | 1.2 | 1.2 | 1.2 |

*\*The 0.9 factor is used when the dead load stabilizes the structure against overturning/uplift.*

The **factored design load** is:

$$
\text{Design Load} = \gamma_f \times \text{Characteristic (Nominal) Load}
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Cheat Sheet" — Memorize This Table.**
> Every KTU 14-mark question on RCC design begins with these formulas.

| # | Quantity | Formula | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Design strength of concrete | $f_d = \dfrac{0.67 \, f_{ck}}{1.5} = 0.446 \, f_{ck}$ | Used in stress block & strain analysis |
| 2 | Design strength of steel | $f_d = \dfrac{f_y}{1.15} = 0.870 \, f_y$ | Used in $A_{st} f_y$ calculations |
| 3 | Factored load | $w_u = \gamma_f \cdot w_k$ | Use in all $M_u$, $V_u$ |
| 4 | Factored moment | $M_u = \gamma_f \cdot M_k$ | Used to find $A_{st}$ |
| 5 | Factored shear | $V_u = \gamma_f \cdot V_k$ | Used in shear design |
| 6 | Max. usable strain in concrete (flexure) | $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$ | Key assumption in LSM |
| 7 | Max. usable strain in steel | $\varepsilon_y = \dfrac{f_y}{1.15 \, E_s} + 0.002$ | For ductile design |

---

## 2.4 Characteristic Material Properties

### Concrete Grades (IS 456:2000, Clause 6.1)
