For typical ratios ($a/D \approx 0.2$ to $0.4$), the total bursting tension is:

$$T_{burst} = \int_0^D \sigma_{br}(y) \cdot \text{(perimeter)} \, dy$$

**Step 3: Simplified Empirical Form**

Through integration and curve-fitting, Guyon obtained the practical design formula:

$$T_{burst} = 0.30 \cdot P \cdot \left(1 - \frac{a}{D}\right)$$

For a square or circular section where $D = b$:

$$\boxed{T_{burst} = 0.30 \cdot P \cdot \left(1 - \frac{a}{b}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most widely used form** of the bursting tension formula in KTU-style problems.

**Step 4: Verification by Limiting Cases**

Case 1: When $a = D$ (anchor fills the entire section), there is **no bursting** because the force is distributed uniformly. The formula gives $T_{burst} = 0$. ✓

Case 2: When $a = 0$ (point load), bursting is maximum:
$$T_{burst} = 0.30 P$$
This means 30% of the prestress force tries to split the concrete transversely — a critical design scenario. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation: Required Bursting Reinforcement

**Step 1: Force to be Resisted**

The bursting reinforcement must carry the bursting tensile force $T_{burst}$ computed above.

**Step 2: Allowable Steel Stress**

For high-yield deformed bars (Fe 500) as per IS 1343:

$$f_{sv} = 0.55 f_y = 0.55 \times 500 = 275 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Required Steel Area**

$$A_{sv} = \frac{T_{burst}}{f_{sv}}$$

**Step 4: Number and Spacing of Stirrups**

For 2-legged 8 mm diameter stirrups:
- Area of one stirrup = $2 \times \dfrac{\pi \times 8^2}{4} = 100.5 \text{ mm}^2$

Number of stirrups required:

$$n = \frac{A_{sv}}{A_{stirrup}}$$

**Step 5: Distribution of Stirrups**

The stirrups should be distributed over a length of $2D$ from the loaded face, with closer spacing (e.g., 50 mm) near the anchor and progressively wider spacing (e.g., 100 mm) further away.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 1343 Clause 19.5.2** requires at least 2% of the longitudinal prestressing reinforcement to be present as **bursting reinforcement** in the anchorage zone, distributed within a distance of $2D$ from the loaded face.

## 3.4 Comprehensive Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A post-tensioned concrete beam is 600 mm deep and 300 mm wide. A Freyssinet anchorage of size 200 mm × 200 mm applies a prestress force of 2000 kN. Design the bursting reinforcement using Fe 500 steel.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 600$ mm
- $b = 300$ mm
- $a = 200$ mm
- $P = 2000 \text{ kN} = 2000 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$
- $f_y = 500$ MPa
- $f_{sv} = 0.55 f_y = 275$ MPa

**Step 1: Compute Bursting Tension (Guyon)**

$$T_{burst} = 0.30 \cdot P \cdot \left(1 - \frac{a}{D}\right)$$

$$T_{burst} = 0.30 \times 2000 \times 10^3 \times \left(1 - \frac{200}{600}\right)$$

$$T_{burst} = 600 \times 10^3 \times \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)$$

$$T_{burst} = 400 \times 10^3 \text{ N} = 400 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Compute Required Steel Area**

$$A_{sv} = \frac{T_{burst}}{f_{sv}} = \frac{400 \times 10^3}{275} = 1454.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Choose Stirrup Configuration**

Try 2-legged 10 mm diameter stirrups:
- Area of one stirrup = $2 \times \dfrac{\pi \times 10^2}{4} = 157.08 \text{ mm}^2$

Number of stirrups:
$$n = \frac{1454.5}{157.08} = 9.26 \approx 10 \text{ stirrups}$$

**Step 4: Distribution**

Distribute 10 stirrups over a length of $2D = 1200$ mm from the loaded face.

Spacing = $1200 / 10 = 120$ mm c/c

**Step 5: Check with IS 1343 Minimum**

2% of $A_p$ — assuming $A_p = 2000 \text{ kN} / 1300 \text{ MPa} = 1538.5 \text{ mm}^2$

2% of $A_p$ = $30.77 \text{ mm}^2$ — this is far less than 1454.5 mm², so bursting governs. ✓

**Step 6: Final Design**

Use **2-legged 10 mm Ø stirrups @ 120 mm c/c** over a length of 1200 mm from the anchor face.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Judgment:** In practice, the first 3–4 stirrups nearest the anchor are placed at 50 mm c/c to resist the **localized spalling stresses**, followed by progressively wider spacing for the bursting zone.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Classification of Anchorage Systems — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ANCHORAGE SYSTEMS] --> B[BOND ANCHORAGE]
    A --> C[BEARING ANCHORAGE]
    
    B --> B1[Pretensioned Members]
    B --> B2[Adhesion plus Hoyer Effect]
    B --> B3[Transfer Length Lt]
    
    C --> C1[Post Tensioned Members]
    C --> C2[Wedge Type]
    C --> C3[Plate and Nut Type]
    C --> C4[Threaded Bar Type]
    C --> C5[Strand Grip Type]
    
    C2 --> C2A[Freyssinet System]
    C2 --> C2B[6 to 37 strands per tendon]
    C3 --> C3A[BBRV System]
    C3 --> C3B[Button Head Bars]
    C4 --> C4A[Lee McCall System]
    C4 --> C4B[Single High Tensile Bar]
    C5 --> C5A[Magnel Blaton System]
    C5 --> C5B[Multiple Strand Wedges]
```

## 4.2 Anchorage Zone Stress Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FRONT[Loaded Face]
        direction TB
        F1[Concrete at loaded face]
    end
    
    subgraph ZONE1[Bursting Zone 0 to 2D]
        direction TB
        B1[Spalling Stress at surface]
        B2[Maximum Bursting Stress at yp equals 0.4 times 2D minus a]
        B3[Decay to zero at depth D]
    end
    
    subgraph ZONE2[Beyond Anchorage Zone]
        direction TB
        P1[Uniform Compression P over A]
    end
    
    F1 --> ZONE1 --> ZONE2
    
    B2 -. Tension perpendicular to axis .-> REINF[Closed Stirrups or Helix]
```

## 4.3 Force Transfer Mechanism in Bond Anchorage

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Tendon Tensioned Against Abutment] --> B[Concrete Cast Around Tendon]
    B --> C[Concrete Cures and Gains Strength]
    C --> D[Cut Tendon at End]
    D --> E[Tendon Contracts Longitudinally]
    E --> F[Hoyer's Effect: Tendon Tries to Regain Original Diameter]
    F --> G[Radial Pressure on Concrete]
    G --> H[Frictional Bond Force Develops]
    H --> I[Full Prestress Transferred Over Transfer Length Lt]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Anchorage Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph EXTERNAL[External Prestressing Jack]
        J1[Hydraulic Cylinder]
        J2[Strain Gauge]
    end
    
    subgraph ANCHOR[Anchorage Assembly]
        direction TB
        A1[Anchor Plate Bearing on Concrete]
        A2[Cheese Plate for Stress Distribution]
        A3[Trumpet Transition Duct]
        A4[Wedge or Nut Locking Element]
        A5[Strand or Bar Tendon]
    end
    
    subgraph CONCRETE[Concrete Member]
        C1[Local Bearing Zone]
        C2[Bursting Zone with Stirrups]
        C3[General Compression Zone]
    end
    
    J1 --> A5
    A5 --> A4
    A4 --> A1
    A1 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    J2 -. Monitors .-> A5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The prestress force enters the structure from the external jack, travels through the tendon, gets locked by the wedge/nut, is distributed by the anchor plate into the concrete, and triggers bursting stresses in the local zone that must be resisted by special anchorage zone reinforcement.

## 4.5 Cross-Sectional View of Typical Freyssinet Anchorage

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ABOVE[At Anchorage]
        T1[7 wire strands cut flush with wedges]
        T2[Conical wedges 2 per strand in pair]
        T3[Anchor plate with tapered holes]
        T4[Cast iron or concrete block recess]
        T5[Helical bursting reinforcement]
    end
    
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T4 --> T5
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Direct, conceptual short-answer questions requiring precise definitions and brief reasoning. Each answer should be **3–4 sentences** with a clear statement.

---

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Define anchorage in prestressed concrete and distinguish between bond anchorage and bearing anchorage.**

**Model Answer:**

Anchorage in prestressed concrete is the mechanism by which the prestressing force is transferred from the high-tensile steel tendon to the surrounding concrete member, ensuring that the induced compressive stress is maintained throughout the service life of the structure.

**Bond anchorage** relies on the chemical adhesion and frictional resistance (enhanced by the Hoyer Effect) between the tendon and the hardened concrete, and is used in **pretensioned members** where the tendon is stressed before the concrete is cast.

**Bearing anchorage** uses **mechanical devices** such as wedges, nuts, or button heads to lock the tendon against a bearing plate, transferring the force through direct compression into the concrete; it is used in **post-tensioned members** where the tendon is stressed after the concrete hardens.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**What is meant by transfer length? State IS 1343 recommendation for transfer length of 7-wire strands.**

**Model Answer:**

**Transfer length ($L_t$)** is the length of the tendon required at the end of a pretensioned member over which the full prestressing force is gradually transferred from the steel tendon to the surrounding concrete through bond action.

Beyond the transfer length, the prestress in the tendon is fully effective; within it, the stress varies from zero at the free end to the full initial prestress $f_{pi}$ at a distance $L_t$.

As per **IS 1343:2012**, the transfer length for 7-wire strands is approximately **$L_t = 100 \phi$** (one hundred times the strand diameter), where $\phi$ is the nominal strand diameter.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts worth 7 marks each. The student must answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** from the choice provided.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A.** (a) Explain with neat sketches the working of a **Freyssinet anchorage system**. List its advantages and limitations.

**A.** (b) A post-tensioned beam of rectangular cross-section, 400 mm wide and 800 mm deep, is prestressed by a Freyssinet anchorage of size 250 mm × 250 mm with a prestressing force of 3000 kN. Design the bursting reinforcement using 8 mm diameter two-legged stirrups of Fe 500 steel.

---

#### Model Solution for A(a):

**Freyssinet Anchorage System:**

The Freyssinet anchorage is a **wedge-type anchorage** developed by Eugène Freyssinet in the 1930s. It is used for post-tensioning with multiple 7-wire strands.

**Components:**
1. **Anchor Plate** — a square or rectangular cast-iron or steel plate with tapered (conical) holes
2. **Conical Wedges** — two per strand, made of high-strength steel, that grip the strand by wedging action
3. **Cheese Plate / Distribution Plate** — distributes the concentrated force over the concrete bearing area
4. **Trumpet** — a flared duct at the end of the cable duct to provide smooth stress transfer

**Working Principle:**

Each strand is passed through a tapered hole in the anchor plate. Two conical wedges (split into two halves) are then driven into the hole, gripping the strand by **friction** as the wedges are pulled inward by the tension in the strand after the jack releases. The wedges seat firmly against the tapered walls of the hole, transferring the tendon force to the anchor plate, which bears against the concrete through the cheese plate.

**Block-Level Flow:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Strand Tensioned by Jack] --> B[Jack Released]
    B --> C[Wedges Pulled into Tapered Hole]
    C --> D[Frictional Grip on Strand]
    D --> E[Force to Anchor Plate]
    E --> F[Force to Concrete via Bearing]
```

**Advantages:**
- Accommodates **6 to 37 strands** in a single anchorage (high capacity)
- Compact and economical for large tendons
- Self-locking wedges — no need for separate locking devices
- Suitable for repetitive use in segmental construction

**Limitations:**
- Wedge slip can cause **loss of prestress**
- Sensitive to **concrete compressive strength** at the bearing face
- Requires precise manufacturing tolerances
- Less suitable for very short tendons due to wedge seating losses

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Sketch (2), Components & Working (2), Advantages (1.5), Limitations (1.5).

---

#### Model Solution for A(b):

**Given:**
- $b = 400$ mm (width)
- $D = 800$ mm (depth)
- $a = 250$ mm (anchorage size)
- $P = 3000$ kN
- Stirrups: 2-legged 8 mm Ø, Fe 500

**Step 1: Compute Bursting Tension (Guyon's Formula)**

$$T_{burst} = 0.30 \cdot P \cdot \left(1 - \frac{a}{D}\right)$$

$$T_{burst} = 0.30 \times 3000 \times \left(1 - \frac{250}{800}\right)$$

$$T_{burst} = 900 \times \left(\frac{550}{800}\right) = 900 \times 0.6875$$

$$T_{burst} = 618.75 \text{ kN} = 618.75 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Allowable Stress in Stirrups**

For Fe 500 HYSD bars as per IS 1343:

$$f_{sv} = 0.55 f_y = 0.55 \times 500 = 275 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Required Area of Bursting Reinforcement**

$$A_{sv} = \frac{T_{burst}}{f_{sv}} = \frac{618.75 \times 10^3}{275} = 2250 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Area of One 2-legged 8 mm Stirrup**

$$A_{\phi8} = 2 \times \frac{\pi \times 8^2}{4} = 100.53 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Number of Stirrups Required**

$$n = \frac{2250}{100.53} = 22.38 \approx 23 \text{ stirrups}$$

**Step 6: Spacing and Distribution**

Length of distribution = $2D = 2 \times 800 = 1600$ mm

Provide 23 stirrups at a uniform spacing of:

$$s = \frac{1600}{23} = 69.6 \text{ mm} \approx 70 \text{ mm c/c}$$

Or, alternatively, vary the spacing:
- First 6 stirrups at 50 mm c/c = 300 mm
- Next 6 stirrups at 75 mm c/c = 450 mm
- Next 6 stirrups at 100 mm c/c = 600 mm
- Last 5 stirrups at 50 mm c/c = 250 mm
- Total = 23 stirrups over 1600 mm

**Step 7: Final Design**

Provide **2-legged 8 mm Ø stirrups @ 70 mm c/c** distributed over 1600 mm from the loaded face. Close spacing near the anchor (within $y_p$) is recommended.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Bursting tension (2), Required area (1.5), Number of stirrups (1.5), Spacing/distribution (2).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**B.** (a) With a neat sketch, explain the **Hoyer Effect** and its role in bond anchorage of pretensioned members.

**B.** (b) A pretensioned concrete member uses a 7-wire strand of 15.2 mm diameter stressed to an initial prestress of 1100 MPa. The average bond stress at transfer is 3.5 MPa. Compute the **transfer length** and verify with the IS 1343 recommendation.

---

#### Model Solution for B(a):

**Hoyer Effect — Detailed Explanation:**

The **Hoyer Effect** is named after the German engineer **E. Hoyer**, who first documented the phenomenon in 1938. It is the **principal mechanism** that makes bond anchorage possible in pretensioned concrete.

**Physical Origin (the "Why"):**

When a tendon is held in a stretched state (e.g., between the abutments of a pretensioning bed), its diameter is slightly **reduced** due to the **Poisson Effect** — the tensile stress causes lateral contraction of the steel.

Once the concrete hardens around the tendon and the external tension is released, the tendon attempts to **contract longitudinally** (relieving its elastic strain) and **expand laterally** (regaining its original diameter). This lateral expansion is **restrained by the surrounding concrete**, generating a **radial pressure** at the steel-concrete interface.

**Role in Bond Anchorage:**

This radial pressure (clamping effect) dramatically increases the **frictional resistance** between the tendon and the concrete, supplementing the chemical adhesion. The result is a very efficient bond, with transfer lengths typically only **50 to 100 times the strand diameter**.

**Sketch (Conceptual):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Tendon Under Tension in Mould] -->|Reduced Diameter phi_1| B[Concrete Cast Around Tendon]
    B --> C[Concrete Hardens]
    C -->|Tendon Cut at Ends| D[Tendon Tries to Expand to phi_2 greater than phi_1]
    D --> E[Concrete Restrains Expansion]
    E --> F[Radial Pressure Develops]
    F --> G[Frictional Bond Force increases]
    G --> H[Efficient Force Transfer]
```

**Quantitative Note:**

The lateral expansion is given by:
$$\Delta \phi = \nu \cdot \epsilon \cdot \phi = \nu \cdot \frac{f_{pi}}{E_s} \cdot \phi$$

Where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio ($\approx 0.3$), $E_s$ is the Young's modulus of steel ($\approx 2 \times 10^5$ MPa), and $\epsilon$ is the tensile strain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Phenomenon description (2), Physical origin (2), Role in bond (2), Sketch (1).

---

#### Model Solution for B(b):

**Given:**
- $\phi = 15.2$ mm
- $f_{pi} = 1100$ MPa
- $\tau_{bd} = 3.5$ MPa

**Step 1: Transfer Length from Equilibrium Formula**

$$L_t = \frac{\phi \cdot f_{pi}}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}$$

$$L_t = \frac{15.2 \times 1100}{4 \times 3.5} = \frac{16720}{14} = 1194.3 \text{ mm}$$

$$L_t \approx 1195 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute $L_t / \phi$ Ratio**

$$\frac{L_t}{\phi} = \frac{1195}{15.2} = 78.6$$

**Step 3: Verify with IS 1343 Recommendation**

IS 1343 recommends $L_t = 100 \phi$ for design purposes:
$$L_t^{IS} = 100 \times 15.2 = 1520 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Design Recommendation**

For safe design, use the **higher value**:

$$\boxed{L_t = 1520 \text{ mm} = 100\phi}$$

This is because:
- The IS 1343 value accounts for **scatter in bond strength**
- It provides a **conservative design** that ensures full prestress transfer
- It accommodates **lower bound** values of $\tau_{bd}$

**Step 5: Design Length for End Zones**

- **Transfer zone** = 0 to $L_t$ = 0 to 1520 mm
- **Development length** beyond the critical section = $2 L_t$ = 3040 mm
- No flexural tensile stress allowed in transfer zone (no debonding without special anchorage)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Formula application (3), Numerical result (2), IS 1343 verification (2).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Anchorage Problems — Read Carefully Before Exam:**
>
> 1. **Using $f_y$ instead of $0.55 f_y$ for $f_{sv}$:** Many students use the full yield stress for bursting reinforcement. **Correct: $f_{sv} = 0.55 f_y$** as per IS 1343 to limit crack width and ensure serviceability.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the distribution length $2D$:** Students often place all stirrups within $L_t$ only. The bursting zone extends over **$2D$ from the loaded face**, not just $L_t$.
>
> 3. **Confusing transfer length with development length:** $L_t$ is the length to **transfer the prestress**; development length is the length to **develop the ultimate tendon stress** ($f_{pd}$) and is **larger** (typically 2 to 3 times $L_t$).
>
> 4. **Wrong formula in bursting tension:** Using $T_{burst} = 0.3P$ always is incorrect. The correct Guyon formula is $T_{burst} = 0.3 P (1 - a/D)$ which **reduces** as $a/D$ increases.
>
> 5. **Ignoring edge distance:** Anchorage zones near the edge of the member experience **additional spalling stresses** that may govern over bursting stresses if edge distance is less than $D$.
>
> 6. **Sign convention for bond stress:** $\tau_{bd}$ is the **average bond stress at the steel-concrete interface**, not a shear stress in the concrete. Confusing these leads to gross errors.
>
> 7. **Using cube strength $f_{ck}$ for $f_{ci}$:** At transfer, only **$f_{ci} \geq 0.8 f_{ck}$** has been achieved. Using $f_{ck}$ in transfer-zone checks is unconservative.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — ANCHORAGE**
>
> - **Anchorage** = mechanism to transfer prestress from tendon to concrete; **bond** for pretension, **bearing** for post-tension.
> - **Bond anchorage** uses **adhesion + friction (Hoyer Effect) + mechanical interlock**.
> - **Hoyer Effect** = radial expansion of tendon at release → clamping pressure → frictional bond.
> - **Transfer length** $L_t = \dfrac{\phi \cdot f_{pi}}{4 \tau_{bd}}$; IS 1343 recommends $L_t = 100 \phi$ for 7-wire strands.
> - **Development length** $\approx 2 L_t$ to $3 L_t$ to develop $f_{pd}$ at the critical section.
> - **Bearing anchorage** uses wedges (Freyssinet), button heads (BBRV), threaded bars (Lee-McCall), or strand grips (Magnel).
> - **Bursting tension** (Guyon) $T_{burst} = 0.30 P \left(1 - \dfrac{a}{D}\right)$; **maximum when $a \to 0$**; **zero when $a = D$**.
> - **Bursting reinforcement** $A_{sv} = \dfrac{T_{burst}}{f_{sv}}$; $f_{sv} = 0.55 f_y$; distributed over **length $2D$** from loaded face.
> - **IS 1343 minimum** bursting reinforcement = **2% of longitudinal prestressing steel** in the anchorage zone.
> - **Location of bursting centroid** $y_p = 0.4(2D - a)$ — closer spacing of stirrups within this region.
> - **Local bearing stress** under anchor: $\sigma_{br} \leq 0.4 f_{ci}$ (IS 1343); typical cheese plate distributes the load.
> - **Concrete strength at transfer** $f_{ci} \geq 0.8 f_{ck}$ for pretensioned members.
> - **Hoyer Effect** is the reason bond anchorage works in **strands with helical wire geometry**; less effective in smooth wires.
> - **Pretensioned** = anchorage is at the **mould ends** (external abutments), then released into concrete.
> - **Post-tensioned** = anchorage is the **end hardware** (cones, plates, wedges) at the **member face**.
> - **End block design** in post-tensioned members uses Guyon/Magnel for bursting and **cable equivalent prism** (IS 1343 Annex C) for refined analysis.
> - **Hoyer** is for transfer, **Guyon** is for bursting — don't mix them up.
> - **Spalling stresses** are at the **surface** of the member near the anchor; need separate reinforcement.
> - **Bursting reinforcement** is usually **closed stirrups or helix**; lap splicing is **not permitted** within the bursting zone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Development Length and Splicing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Development Length — The IS 456 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Development Length ($L_d$) is the embedded length of a reinforcement bar required to be embedded into concrete such that the bar can develop its full design tensile (or compressive) stress through bond at the steel–concrete interface, without any slip or pull-out failure.**

As per **IS 456 : 2000, Clause 26.2.1**, the development length of a bar of diameter $\phi$ is given by:

$$L_d = \dfrac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}$$

where:
- $\phi$ = nominal diameter of the bar (in **mm**)
- $\sigma_s$ = stress in the bar at the section under consideration (in **N/mm²**)
- $\tau_{bd}$ = design bond stress (in **N/mm²**), as per Table in Clause 26.2.1.1

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Development Length"?** The bar has to "develop" its full strength gradually through the bond with surrounding concrete. If the available length is less than $L_d$, the bar will simply pull out before yielding — a brittle and catastrophic failure.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Embedded Hook"

Imagine you push a smooth steel rod into a block of soft clay and try to pull it out. You will feel some resistance. Now, picture a **screw** being screwed into wood — it is much harder to pull out because of the thread. The minimum length the screw must go into the wood to resist a given pull-out force is its **development length**.

| Analogy Element | Structural Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Wood / clay | Concrete matrix |
| Screw thread | Ribs on a deformed bar |
| Depth of insertion | Development length ($L_d$) |
| Pull-out force | Tensile force in the bar |
| Resistance at the interface | Bond stress ($\tau_{bd}$) |

> [!TIP]
> If a bar cannot be extended through the full $L_d$ (e.g., at a beam support), we must either provide a **hook, bend, or anchorage** to make up the deficit — this is called **"shortfall in development length"**.

---

## 1.3 Splicing (Lapping) — The Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Splicing** is the method of joining two reinforcement bars end-to-end so that the load is transferred from one bar to the other through the surrounding concrete via bond, thereby maintaining continuity of reinforcement.

Splicing becomes necessary when:
1. The required bar length exceeds the available stock length (typically **12 m**).
2. The bar diameter in the design changes along the length.
3. Construction joints or staged concreting demand bar termination.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Rule (IS 456, Clause 26.2.5):** Splices should preferably be **avoided in regions of maximum tensile stress**. Wherever possible, laps should be **staggered** and not located in the same cross-section.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Bond

The bond between steel and concrete is not a single phenomenon — it is the **sum of three distinct mechanisms**:

1. **Chemical Adhesion (Grip)** — Initial gripping force due to the setting of cement paste on the steel surface. Vanishes as soon as micro-slip occurs.
2. **Frictional Resistance** — Friction between the steel surface and the surrounding concrete, enhanced by shrinkage-induced normal pressure.
3. **Mechanical Interlock** — The ribs/indentations of **deformed (HYSD) bars** physically bear against the concrete lugs between the ribs. This contributes **70–80%** of total bond in deformed bars.

> [!NOTE]
> Plain mild-steel bars rely almost entirely on adhesion and friction. This is why **deformed bars** are assigned a **1.6 times higher bond stress** in IS 456 (Clause 26.2.1.1).

---

## 2.2 Design Bond Stress (IS 456, Table under Clause 26.2.1.1)

The values below are for **plain bars in tension**. Apply the multipliers shown to obtain values for deformed bars or for bars in compression.

| Concrete Grade | $\tau_{bd}$ for Plain Bars in Tension (N/mm²) | For Deformed Bars (× 1.6) | For Compression (× 1.25) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **M 20** | **0.8** | **1.28** | **1.00** |
| **M 25** | **0.9** | **1.44** | **1.125** |
| **M 30** | **1.0** | **1.60** | **1.25** |
| **M 35** | **1.1** | **1.76** | **1.375** |
| **M 40** | **1.2** | **1.92** | **1.50** |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** For M20 plain, $\tau_{bd} = 0.8$. For every grade above, add **0.1 N/mm²** per step.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination Must-Knows)

| # | Formula | Description / Condition |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $L_d = \dfrac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}$ | Base development length (IS 456, Cl. 26.2.1) |
| 2 | $\sigma_s = 0.87 \, f_y$ | Working stress in HYSD bar at limit state |
| 3 | $L_d' = 1.25 \, L_d$ | For bars in **compression** |
| 4 | $\tau_{bd,\text{def}} = 1.6 \, \tau_{bd,\text{plain}}$ | For **deformed bars** |
| 5 | $L_d = 47\phi$ | Simplified (Fe415, deformed, M20 min.) |
| 6 | $L_d = 57\phi$ | Simplified (Fe500, deformed, M20 min.) |
| 7 | Hook length $= 9\phi$ | For 180° hook (Clause 26.2.2.1) |
| 8 | Hook length $= 12\phi$ | For 90° bend (Clause 26.2.2.1) |
| 9 | Lap length in tension $= L_d$ or $30\phi$ (max) | IS 456, Cl. 26.2.5.1 |
| 10 | Lap length in compression $= L_d$ or $24\phi$ (max) | IS 456, Cl. 26.2.5.2 |
| 11 | Staggered lap spacing $\geq 1.3 \, L_d$ | To avoid concentration in one section |
| 12 | Max $\% $ bars lapped in one section $\leq 50\%$ | Avoids progressive failure |

> [!NOTE]
> The simplified values $47\phi$ and $57\phi$ are **not absolute** — they assume M20 concrete minimum. For higher grades, the calculated $L_d$ from Formula 1 may govern. **Always adopt the maximum of the two.**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Significance

- **Beam-Column Joints:** At a cantilever support, the negative-moment bars must be developed within the column width. If $L_d$ is unavailable, **mechanical anchorage** (headed bars or welded plates) is used.
- **Continuous Slabs:** Top bars at supports must be extended into the span by a length $\geq L_d/3$. This is a direct application of development length.
- **Precast Construction:** Splicing by **laps, mechanical couplers, or welding** is essential since precast units are short.
- **Earthquake-Resistant Design (IS 13920):** Splice locations are strictly controlled — splices in plastic hinge regions are forbidden; $L_d$ is increased by **60%** for critical members.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Development Length Formula

Consider a bar of diameter $\phi$ embedded in concrete for a length $L_d$. Let the bar be subjected to an axial tensile force $T$ at one end. Equilibrium at the steel–concrete interface gives:

### Step 1 — Tensile force in the bar
The cross-sectional area of the bar is:
$$A_s = \dfrac{\pi \, \phi^2}{4}$$
The tensile force the bar can resist at its working stress $\sigma_s$ is:
$$T = A_s \cdot \sigma_s = \dfrac{\pi \, \phi^2}{4} \cdot \sigma_s$$

### Step 2 — Bond (shear) force on the bar surface
The surface area of the bar in contact with concrete over length $L_d$ is:
$$A_b = \pi \, \phi \cdot L_d$$
The bond resistance developed at this surface, with average design bond stress $\tau_{bd}$, is:
$$F_b = A_b \cdot \tau_{bd} = \pi \, \phi \cdot L_d \cdot \tau_{bd}$$

### Step 3 — Equilibrium (no slip condition)
For the bar not to slip, the bond force must equal the applied tensile force:
$$\pi \, \phi \cdot L_d \cdot \tau_{bd} = \dfrac{\pi \, \phi^2}{4} \cdot \sigma_s$$

### Step 4 — Simplification
Cancel $\pi$ and $\phi$ on both sides:
$$L_d \cdot \tau_{bd} = \dfrac{\phi}{4} \cdot \sigma_s$$

Rearranging:
$$L_d = \dfrac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **fundamental IS 456 development length formula**. Every variation (compression, deformed, lightweight concrete) is built upon this single equation.

---

## 3.2 Modification for Bars in Compression

For a compression bar, the surrounding concrete can **spall** at the loaded end, reducing confinement. IS 456 increases the development length by **25%** (Clause 26.2.1.2):

$$L_{d,\text{comp}} = 1.25 \, L_d = \dfrac{1.25 \cdot \phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}$$

Equivalently, the **bond stress in compression** is taken as:
$$\tau_{bd,\text{comp}} = \tau_{bd,\text{tension}} \, / \, 1.25$$

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Analytical Example (Exam-Caliber)

> **Problem:** A simply supported RC beam of effective span 5.5 m, width 230 mm, and effective depth 480 mm is reinforced at the bottom with **4 bars of 20 mm diameter, Fe415 grade**, in M25 concrete. The design moment at the mid-span is 180 kN·m. Calculate the **development length** required for the tension reinforcement at the simply supported end. Verify whether the bar is fully developed within the span.

### Step 1 — Stress in the bar ($\sigma_s$)

$$\sigma_s = 0.87 \cdot f_y = 0.87 \cdot 415 = 360.75 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

### Step 2 — Design bond stress ($\tau_{bd}$)

For **M25 concrete** with **deformed (HYSD) bars in tension**:
$$\tau_{bd} = 0.9 \times 1.6 = 1.44 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

### Step 3 — Development length (calculated)

$$L_d = \dfrac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}} = \dfrac{20 \cdot 360.75}{4 \cdot 1.44} = \dfrac{7215}{5.76}$$

$$L_d = 1252.6 \; \text{mm} \approx 1253 \; \text{mm}$$

### Step 4 — Verification with simplified formula

For Fe415 deformed bars, simplified value:
$$L_{d,\text{simplified}} = 47\phi = 47 \times 20 = 940 \; \text{mm}$$

### Step 5 — Adopt the governing value

$$L_d = \max(1253, \; 940) = 1253 \; \text{mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The calculated value (1253 mm) governs**, because M25 concrete has a higher characteristic strength that the simplified $47\phi$ is conservative for.

### Step 6 — Check if $L_d$ fits within the available embedment length

For a simply supported beam, the bottom bars extend into the support. Assume a bearing of 230 mm and the bars continue into the support by the same amount:
$$L_{\text{available}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{eff}}}{2} + \text{projection into support} = \dfrac{5500}{2} + 115 = 2865 \; \text{mm}$$

Since $L_{\text{available}} = 2865 \; \text{mm} > L_d = 1253 \; \text{mm}$:

> **Bar is fully developed. ✓ No anchorage required.**

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Lap Length Calculation

> **Problem:** Determine the **lap length in tension and compression** for 25 mm diameter Fe500 bars in M30 concrete.

### Step 1 — Working stress
$$\sigma_s = 0.87 \cdot f_y = 0.87 \cdot 500 = 435 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

### Step 2 — Design bond stress for deformed bars in tension (M30)
$$\tau_{bd} = 1.0 \times 1.6 = 1.6 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

### Step 3 — Development length
$$L_d = \dfrac{25 \cdot 435}{4 \cdot 1.6} = \dfrac{10875}{6.4} = 1699.2 \; \text{mm}$$

### Step 4 — Lap length in tension
$$L_{\text{lap,tension}} = \max(L_d, \; 30\phi) = \max(1699.2, \; 750) = 1699.2 \; \text{mm}$$

### Step 5 — Lap length in compression (increase $L_d$ by 25%)
$$L_{d,\text{comp}} = 1.25 \times 1699.2 = 2124 \; \text{mm}$$
$$L_{\text{lap,comp}} = \max(2124, \; 24\phi) = \max(2124, \; 600) = 2124 \; \text{mm}$$

**Result:** $L_{\text{lap,tension}} = 1699.2 \; \text{mm}$ and $L_{\text{lap,compression}} = 2124 \; \text{mm}$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Development Length Mechanism

The following block-level architecture illustrates the force-transfer mechanism across the steel–concrete interface:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Steel Bar Diameter phi] -->|Axial Tension T| B[Bond Transfer Zone]
    B -->|Bond Stress tau_bd| C[Concrete Matrix]
    C -->|Shear Resistance| D[Full Development Length Ld]
    D -->|Stress sigma_s Achieved| E[Design Strength Reached]
    B -.->|Insufficient Length| F[Bond Failure or Pull Out]
    F -.->|Mitigation| G[Use Hooks or Mechanical Anchorage]
```

---

## 4.2 Bond Mechanism — Decomposition Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Bond Between Steel and Concrete] --> B[Adhesion Chemical Grip]
    A --> C[Friction Surface Roughness]
    A --> D[Mechanical Interlock Bar Ribs]
    B -->|Vanishes at Slip| E[Total Bond Capacity tau_bd]
    C -->|Slip Resistance| E
    D -->|Dominant for HYSD Bars| E
    E -->|Plain Bars| F[tau_bd from IS 456 Table]
    E -->|Deformed Bars x 1.6| G[Enhanced tau_bd]
```

---

## 4.3 Splicing Architecture — Contact vs Staggered

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Splicing Methods in RCC] --> B[Contact Splice]
    A --> C[Staggered Splice]
    B -->|Bars Adjacent| D[All Laps at One Section]
    C -->|Bars Distributed| E[Laps at Different Sections]
    D -->|Lap Length Equals Ld| F[Stress Continuity]
    E -->|Stagger Greater Than 1.3 Ld| F
    F --> G[Continuous Bar Behavior]
    H[Restrictions per IS 456] --> I[Avoid in Max Tension Zone]
    H --> J[Max 50 percent Bars Lapped per Section]
    H --> K[No Lap for Bars Larger Than 36 mm]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Computation Flow — Development Length

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Bar Diameter phi] --> B[Compute sigma_s = 0.87 fy]
    C[Input Concrete Grade] --> D[Select tau_bd for Plain Tension]
    D --> E[Multiply by 1.6 if Deformed]
    B --> F[Compute Ld = phi x sigma_s / 4 x tau_bd]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Compare with Simplified 47 phi or 57 phi}
    G -->|Calculated Greater| H[Adopt Calculated Ld]
    G -->|Simplified Greater| I[Adopt Simplified Ld]
    H --> J[Final Development Length for Design]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Verify Anchorage Within Available Length]
    K -->|Sufficient| L[Design OK]
    K -->|Insufficient| M[Provide Hooks or Couplers]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above provide a **block-level functional architecture** of the development length and splicing concepts. In a physical beam, the steel bar is embedded in concrete over a length $L_d$ from the critical section (point of maximum stress), with bond stress $\tau_{bd}$ acting uniformly over the cylindrical contact surface of the bar.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define development length as per IS 456 : 2000. State the formula for calculating it.** `[3 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Development length ($L_d$) is defined as the **length of embedment of a reinforcement bar required in concrete to develop the full working stress in the bar** through bond at the interface, ensuring no slip or pull-out occurs.

As per **IS 456 : 2000, Clause 26.2.1**:

$$L_d = \dfrac{\phi \, \sigma_s}{4 \, \tau_{bd}}$$

where:
- $\phi$ = nominal diameter of bar
- $\sigma_s$ = stress in the bar at the section
- $\tau_{bd}$ = design bond stress (Table 26.2.1.1)

---

### **Q2. State the IS 456 provisions for the minimum lap length in tension and compression.** `[3 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

As per **IS 456 : 2000, Clause 26.2.5**:

1. **Lap length in tension** = $L_d$ **or** $30\phi$, whichever is **greater**.
2. **Lap length in compression** = $L_d$ **or** $24\phi$, whichever is **greater**.

Additional provisions:
- Laps should be **staggered** with a clear spacing of at least **$1.3 \, L_d$**.
- **Not more than 50%** of bars shall be lapped at any one section.
- Splices should be **avoided in regions of maximum tensile stress**.

---

## 📐 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **ESE Module Pattern:** Attempt **Q1 OR Q2** (full 14 marks), with each split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Q1. (a) Explain the bond mechanism between steel reinforcement and concrete in detail. How is it modified for deformed bars and compression bars?** `[7 Marks]`

#### Model Answer:

The bond between steel and concrete is a **complex interaction** that transfers stress from the bar to the surrounding concrete, ensuring **composite action**. It comprises three components:

**1. Chemical Adhesion**
- Initial grip provided by the setting of cement paste against the steel surface.
- It is a **surface phenomenon** governed by van der Waals forces and chemical bonding.
- It vanishes once micro-slip occurs between bar and concrete.

**2. Frictional Resistance**
- Develops as the bar tries to slip; surface roughness and the **shrinkage-induced normal pressure** of concrete generate friction.
- Significant for plain bars; contributes throughout the slip range.

**3. Mechanical Interlock**
- The **transverse ribs** of deformed (HYSD) bars bear directly against the concrete keys formed between the ribs.
- This contributes **70–80% of total bond** in deformed bars and is the most reliable mechanism.
- Concrete in front of the ribs may crush, but this is resisted by the helical confinement from the surrounding concrete.

**Modifications per IS 456:**

| Condition | Modification |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Deformed bars** (IS 1786) | $\tau_{bd}$ increased by a factor of **1.6** |
| **Bars in compression** | $L_d$ increased by **25%** (equivalently, $\tau_{bd}$ divided by 1.25) |
| **Lightweight aggregate concrete** | $\tau_{bd}$ reduced by **factor depending on density** (Cl. 26.2.1.1) |

> **Conclusion:** For modern RCC design using Fe415/Fe500 deformed bars in normal-weight concrete, the **mechanical interlock** is the dominant bond mechanism.

---

### **Q1. (b) A simply supported RC beam of effective span 6 m, width 250 mm, and effective depth 500 mm is reinforced with 4 bars of 20 mm diameter (Fe415) at the bottom in M20 concrete. Check whether the tension reinforcement is fully developed at the support end.** `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Step 1 — Working stress in the bar

$$\sigma_s = 0.87 \times 415 = 360.75 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

#### Step 2 — Design bond stress (M20, deformed, tension)

$$\tau_{bd} = 0.8 \times 1.6 = 1.28 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

#### Step 3 — Development length (calculated)

$$L_d = \dfrac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}} = \dfrac{20 \times 360.75}{4 \times 1.28} = \dfrac{7215}{5.12} = 1409.2 \; \text{mm}$$

#### Step 4 — Simplified value check (Fe415)

$$L_{d,\text{simplified}} = 47\phi = 47 \times 20 = 940 \; \text{mm}$$

#### Step 5 — Governing value

$$L_d = \max(1409.2, \; 940) = 1409.2 \; \text{mm} \approx 1410 \; \text{mm}$$

#### Step 6 — Check available embedment length

For a simply supported beam, the bars extend half the effective span into the support plus the bearing:
$$L_{\text{available}} = \dfrac{6000}{2} + 230/2 = 3000 + 115 = 3115 \; \text{mm}$$

#### Step 7 — Verdict

$$L_{\text{available}} (3115 \; \text{mm}) > L_d (1410 \; \text{mm})$$

> **The bars are fully developed at the support. No additional anchorage is required. ✓**

---

### **Q2. (a) Explain the IS 456 : 2000 provisions for splicing of reinforcement bars. Differentiate between contact splice and staggered splice.** `[7 Marks]`

#### Model Answer:

**Splicing (Lapping)** is the overlapping of two bars to transfer stress through the surrounding concrete via bond. It is necessary when:
- Bar length exceeds available stock (typically 12 m).
- Bar diameter changes along the length.
- Construction joints are present.

**IS 456 Provisions (Clause 26.2.5):**

| S.No. | Provision |
| :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Lap length in **tension** = $L_d$ or $30\phi$ (whichever is greater) |
| 2 | Lap length in **compression** = $L_d$ or $24\phi$ (whichever is greater) |
| 3 | **Not more than 50%** of bars shall be lapped at any one section |
| 4 | Laps should be **staggered** with a minimum distance of **$1.3 \, L_d$** between adjacent lap pairs |
| 5 | Splices should preferably be **avoided in zones of maximum tensile stress** |
| 6 | **Bars larger than 36 mm** shall **not** be lapped — use welding or mechanical couplers |
| 7 | Laps in flexural members should be located **away from regions of high bending moment** |

**Contact Splice vs Staggered Splice:**

| Aspect | Contact Splice | Staggered Splice |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | All bars lapped at the **same cross-section** | Bars lapped at **different sections** along the length |
| **Concentration** | High stress concentration in one section | Stress spread over a larger zone |
| **Bond efficiency** | Lower (cracking risk) | Higher (distributed load transfer) |
| **Use** | Where staggering is impractical (e.g., column splices) | Preferred for beams in flexural tension zones |
| **Spacing** | All laps coincide | Adjacent lap pairs separated by $\geq 1.3 \, L_d$ |

> **Conclusion:** Staggered splicing is **always preferred** in flexural members. Contact splicing is acceptable only when staggering is structurally or practically impossible.

---

### **Q2. (b) Calculate the development length and lap length (in tension and compression) for 25 mm diameter Fe500 bars in M25 concrete.** `[7 Marks]`

#### Step 1 — Working stress in the bar

$$\sigma_s = 0.87 \times 500 = 435 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

#### Step 2 — Design bond stress (M25, deformed, tension)

$$\tau_{bd} = 0.9 \times 1.6 = 1.44 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

#### Step 3 — Development length

$$L_d = \dfrac{25 \times 435}{4 \times 1.44} = \dfrac{10875}{5.76} = 1888.0 \; \text{mm}$$

#### Step 4 — Simplified value check (Fe500)

$$L_{d,\text{simplified}} = 57\phi = 57 \times 25 = 1425 \; \text{mm}$$

#### Step 5 — Governing development length

$$L_d = \max(1888, \; 1425) = 1888 \; \text{mm}$$

#### Step 6 — Lap length in tension

$$L_{\text{lap,tension}} = \max(L_d, \; 30\phi) = \max(1888, \; 750) = \mathbf{1888 \; mm}$$

#### Step 7 — Development length in compression (25% increase)

$$L_{d,\text{comp}} = 1.25 \times 1888 = 2360 \; \text{mm}$$

#### Step 8 — Lap length in compression

$$L_{\text{lap,comp}} = \max(L_{d,\text{comp}}, \; 24\phi) = \max(2360, \; 600) = \mathbf{2360 \; mm}$$

> **Final Results:**
> - Development length $L_d = 1888 \; \text{mm}$
> - Lap length in tension = $\mathbf{1888 \; mm}$
> - Lap length in compression = $\mathbf{2360 \; mm}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Points**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.6 factor** for deformed bars — if you use $\tau_{bd} = 0.8$ directly for HYSD bars in M20, your $L_d$ will be **underestimated by 60%**, leading to unsafe design. (Lose 2–3 marks)
> 2. **Not adding 25%** for compression bars — a frequent error that yields an unsafe lap length. (Lose 2 marks)
> 3. **Ignoring the 30φ / 24φ minimum** in lap length — many students compute $L_d$ and stop. The IS code mandates the additional minimum check. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 4. **Confusing 47φ (Fe415) with 57φ (Fe500)** — the simplified formulas are steel-grade specific. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **Wrong σs value** — using $f_y$ instead of $0.87 f_y$ overestimates the stress, increasing $L_d$ unnecessarily. (Lose 1 mark)

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Development length** = embedded bar length needed to develop full design stress via bond.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $L_d = \dfrac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}$ (IS 456, Cl. 26.2.1).
- ✅ **Steel working stress:** $\sigma_s = 0.87 \, f_y$.
- ✅ **Design bond stress table** starts at **0.8 N/mm² (M20)** and increases by **0.1 N/mm²** per grade.
- ✅ **Deformed bars (HYSD):** multiply $\tau_{bd}$ by **1.6**.
- ✅ **Compression bars:** multiply $L_d$ by **1.25** (or divide $\tau_{bd}$ by 1.25).
- ✅ **Simplified IS 456 values:** $L_d = 47\phi$ (Fe415), $L_d = 57\phi$ (Fe500).
- ✅ **Hook lengths:** $9\phi$ for 180° hook, $12\phi$ for 90° bend (Cl. 26.2.2.1).
- ✅ **Lap length in tension:** $L_d$ or $30\phi$ (whichever is greater).
- ✅ **Lap length in compression:** $L_d$ or $24\phi$ (whichever is greater).
- ✅ **Stagger spacing** between adjacent laps: $\geq 1.3 \, L_d$.
- ✅ **Maximum 50%** of bars can be lapped at a single section.
- ✅ **No lap splicing for bars $\geq 36 \; \text{mm}$** diameter — use welding or mechanical couplers.
- ✅ Always adopt $L_d = \max(\text{calculated},\; \text{simplified})$.
- ✅ **Three bond mechanisms:** adhesion (chemical), friction (slip-resistance), mechanical interlock (dominant for deformed bars).
- ✅ For **IS 13920 (seismic design)**, $L_d$ in critical members is increased by **60%**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion in Beams - Limit State of Collapse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Torsion in Beams - Limit State of Collapse

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU / IS 456:2000 Terminology)

**Torsion in a reinforced concrete beam** is the twisting moment acting about the longitudinal axis of the member, which produces a *shear flow* in the cross-section. In the **Limit State of Collapse** (Limit State of Collapse in Flexure, Shear & Torsion – governed by **IS 456:2000, Clause 41**), torsion is treated as a *combined-stress* state because torsional cracking spirals around the beam, and reinforcement must intercept these 45° diagonal cracks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 456:2000, Clause 41.1** mandates a **minimum concrete grade of M20** and **Fe415 (or higher) longitudinal steel** for any member designed for torsion. The design follows the *space-truss analogy* in which the beam is idealised as a thin-walled tube with longitudinal bars acting as tension ties and transverse stirrups as vertical ties.

The two fundamental types are:

| Type | Source | Action |
|------|--------|--------|
| **Equilibrium Torsion (Primary)** | Loading geometry | Cannot be ignored; required for structural equilibrium |
| **Compatibility Torsion (Secondary)** | Continuity at supports / spandrel beams | Can be redistributed if ductility is ensured |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Driveshaft" Analogy:**
> Imagine a car driveshaft transmitting power to the wheels. The engine applies a *twisting moment*; the shaft walls resist it through a *circulating shear flow* around its cross-section (think of small forces flowing around a ring). In a reinforced concrete beam, the concrete forms the "ring" and the steel bars (stirrups + longitudinal bars at the corners) act as the "wall thickness" of that ring. If you take a hollow cardboard tube and twist it, the cracks form a *spiral pattern* — exactly the 45° helix that IS 456 expects you to reinforce against.

## 1.3 Key Design Constants to Memorise

- **Design torsional moment, T<sub>u</sub> = 1.2 T<sub>g</sub> + 1.2 V<sub>g</sub>** (load factor 1.5 × characteristic; for KTU simplification, T<sub>u</sub> ≈ 1.5 T)
- **τ<sub>c,max</sub>** (Table 20, IS 456) is a *hard limit* — section must be enlarged if exceeded
- **Equivalent shear concept** reduces torsion to an *augmented shear* — a brilliant simplification
- **b<sub>1</sub> = centre-to-centre distance between corner bars (stirrup width)**
- **d<sub>1</sub> = centre-to-centre distance between corner bars (stirrup depth)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Equivalent Shear? — The Underlying Logic

A closed thin-walled tube subjected to pure torque *T* develops a constant *shear flow* **q = T / (2 A<sub>k</sub>)**, where A<sub>k</sub> is the area enclosed by the centreline of the tube wall. In a solid rectangular beam, IS 456 idealises the section as an equivalent thin-walled tube of wall thickness *t* = b/4 or D/4 (whichever is greater, but capped), and the total transverse (vertical) force in the stirrups must resist this shear flow.

The result is the **equivalent shear force equation** (Cl. 41.3.1):

$$V_e = V_u + \dfrac{1.6\, T_u}{b}$$

This is **not** an approximation — it is the *exact* equivalence for a rectangular section treated as a thin-walled tube, where the *1.6* factor arises from the geometry (2 × 0.8, with 0.8 being the lever arm ratio).

## 2.2 The Complete KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Equivalent Shear | $V_e = V_u + \dfrac{1.6\, T_u}{b}$ | N, b = breadth in mm |
| 2 | Equivalent Shear Stress | $\tau_{ve} = \dfrac{V_e}{b\,d}$ | N/mm² |
| 3 | Section Adequacy Check | $\tau_{ve} \le \tau_{c,\max}$ | Table 20 of IS 456 |
| 4 | Stirrup Area (2-legged) | $\dfrac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \dfrac{0.4\,(\tau_{ve} - \tau_c)\,b}{0.87\,f_y} + \dfrac{T_u\,p_v}{1.32\,b_1\,d_1\,f_y}$ | For vertical stirrups p<sub>v</sub> = 1 |
| 5 | Longitudinal Steel (Torsion) | $A_{l} = \dfrac{T_u\,p_v}{1.32\,f_y}$ | Distributed to corner bars |
| 6 | Total Longitudinal per face | $A_{l,\text{face}} = \dfrac{A_l}{2} + \dfrac{A_{st,\text{extra}}}{2}$ | Al is total; split between top & bottom |
| 7 | Min. Shear Reinforcement (Cl. 26.5.1.6) | $\dfrac{A_{sv,\min}}{b\,s_v} = \dfrac{0.4}{0.87\,f_y}$ | Always govern over calculated if section is large |
| 8 | Max. Spacing of Stirrups (Cl. 26.5.1.5) | $s_v \le \min\!\left(0.75\,d,\ 300\,\text{mm}\right)$ | For τ<sub>ve</sub> > τ<sub>c</sub> |

### τ<sub>c,max</sub> Values (Table 20, IS 456)

| Concrete Grade | M20 | M25 | M30 | M35 | M40+ |
|----------------|-----|-----|-----|-----|------|
| **τ<sub>c,max</sub> (N/mm²)** | **2.8** | **3.1** | **3.5** | **3.7** | **4.0** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is τ<sub>ve</sub> capped by τ<sub>c,max</sub>?** Because beyond this stress, the concrete struts of the space truss would crush *before* the steel yields. This is a *ductility* requirement — we want steel to yield first (giving warning) before brittle concrete failure.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Spandrel beams** in building frames (supporting masonry walls + slab) — the slab's eccentricity creates torsional moment.
- **Curved bridge girders** and **interchange ramps** — centrifugal loads plus asymmetric live load create torsion.
- **Beams supporting cantilever slabs** (balcony beams, canopy beams) — the slab's load does not pass through the beam's centroidal axis.
- **Peripheral beams of floor systems** carrying partial UDL on one side only.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why "1.6 T/b" in the Equivalent Shear

Consider a rectangular beam of breadth *b* and overall depth *D* idealised as a thin-walled closed tube. The shear flow at any point is:

$$q = \dfrac{T_u}{2 A_k}$$

where $A_k = b_1 \cdot d_1$ (enclosed area by centreline of stirrups).

The portion of shear flow resisted by *vertical stirrups* (per unit length of beam) is:

$$q_v = q \cdot \dfrac{d_1}{b_1 + d_1} = \dfrac{T_u}{2 b_1 d_1} \cdot \dfrac{d_1}{b_1 + d_1} = \dfrac{T_u}{2(b_1 + d_1)}$$

The vertical force per unit length on each face of the stirrup is then $\dfrac{T_u}{2(b_1 + d_1)}$. Multiplying by the lever arm to convert this distributed force into an *equivalent concentrated shear at a section*:

$$V_{e,T} = q_v \cdot (b_1 + d_1) \cdot 0.8 = \dfrac{0.8\, T_u}{b_1}$$

For solid rectangular sections, IS 456 conservatively replaces $b_1$ by the **full breadth b** (since $b_1 \le b$):

$$V_{e,T} = \dfrac{0.8\, T_u}{b}$$

Adding the contribution from both faces of the equivalent tube (since shear flow circulates), we get the factor **1.6**:

$$\boxed{V_e = V_u + \dfrac{1.6\, T_u}{b}}$$

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Design Procedure (KTU Board Format)

> [!NOTE]
> The following 8-step procedure is the **canonical KTU ESE 14-mark answer structure** for combined bending + shear + torsion problems.

**Step 1 — Section & Material Assumptions**
Given: b, D, cover, f<sub>ck</sub>, f<sub>y</sub>, M<sub>u</sub>, V<sub>u</sub>, T<sub>u</sub>. Compute effective d and d′.

**Step 2 — Compute Equivalent Shear**

$$V_e = V_u + \dfrac{1.6\, T_u}{b} \quad \text{(in N, with b in mm)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Equivalent Shear Stress**

$$\tau_{ve} = \dfrac{V_e}{b\,d} \le \tau_{c,\max}$$

If τ<sub>ve</sub> > τ<sub>c,max</sub>, **enlarge section**.

**Step 4 — Flexural Design (Bending)**
From M<sub>u</sub>, compute A<sub>st</sub> using limit state of flexure. Choose bars (number, diameter). Distribute at top and bottom.

**Step 5 — Shear Capacity of Concrete**
For assumed p<sub>t</sub> = (100 A<sub>st</sub>/(b d)), read τ<sub>c</sub> from Table 19 of IS 456. If τ<sub>ve</sub> ≤ τ<sub>c</sub>, only minimum shear reinforcement is required, but torsion stirrups are still needed.

**Step 6 — Design of Stirrups (Combined Shear + Torsion)**

$$\dfrac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \dfrac{0.4\,(\tau_{ve} - \tau_c)\,b}{0.87\,f_y} + \dfrac{T_u}{1.32\,b_1\,d_1\,f_y}$$

Compute s<sub>v</sub> for chosen stirrup diameter (say 2-legged 8 mm Ø: A<sub>sv</sub> = 100.5 mm²). Adopt the **lesser** of calculated s<sub>v</sub> and **s<sub>v,max</sub> = 0.75 d (≤ 300 mm)**.

**Step 7 — Design of Longitudinal Steel for Torsion**

$$A_l = \dfrac{T_u}{1.32\,f_y} \quad \text{(total, mm²)}$$

Distribute A<sub>l</sub> equally to **all four corners** (i.e., add to all flexural bars). The top and bottom longitudinal steel at the corners must each have an additional:

$$A_{l,\text{corner}} = \dfrac{A_l}{4}$$

If the existing flexural steel at a face (A<sub>st</sub> for tension, A<sub>sc</sub> for compression) is **less than** A<sub>l</sub>/2, additional bars must be added at the sides of the section.

**Step 8 — Side-Face Reinforcement (Cl. 26.5.1.3)**
If D > 450 mm, provide side-face reinforcement of **0.1 % of web area** distributed equally on both side faces.

## 3.3 Worked Example Outline (Logic Trace)

**Example:** b = 230 mm, D = 500 mm, d = 450 mm, d′ = 50 mm, M<sub>u</sub> = 80 kN·m, V<sub>u</sub> = 45 kN, T<sub>u</sub> = 18 kN·m, M20, Fe415.

1. $V_e = 45\,000 + \dfrac{1.6 \times 18 \times 10^6}{230} = 45\,000 + 125\,217 = 170.22$ kN
2. $\tau_{ve} = \dfrac{170\,217}{230 \times 450} = 1.645$ N/mm²
3. $\tau_{c,\max} = 2.8$ N/mm² (M20) → section **adequate**
4. Assuming p<sub>t</sub> ≈ 1.0 %, $\tau_c \approx 0.62$ N/mm² (from Table 19)
5. $b_1 = 230 - 2(50) + 2(8) = 196$ mm, $d_1 = 450 - 2(50) + 2(8) = 416$ mm (assuming 8 mm stirrups, 50 mm cover)
6. $\dfrac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \dfrac{0.4 \times (1.645 - 0.62) \times 230}{0.87 \times 415} + \dfrac{18 \times 10^6}{1.32 \times 196 \times 416 \times 415}$
   $= \dfrac{94.34}{361.05} + \dfrac{18 \times 10^6}{4.46 \times 10^7} = 0.2613 + 0.4036 = 0.6649$ mm
7. Using 2-legged 8 mm Ø stirrups (A<sub>sv</sub> = 100.5 mm²): $s_v = 100.5 / 0.6649 = 151.2$ mm
   Adopt **s<sub>v</sub> = 150 mm** ≤ 0.75 × 450 = 337.5 mm ✓
8. $A_l = \dfrac{18 \times 10^6}{1.32 \times 415} = 328.4$ mm² (total) → **82.1 mm² per corner** → add 8 mm Ø bar at each corner.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Crack Pattern & Space Truss Analogy

Since a physical 3D rendering of the helical crack pattern is impractical in Mermaid, the following block-level schematic captures the **functional architecture** of the design logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Mu Vu Tu b D fck fy] --> B[Compute effective d and dprime]
    B --> C[Compute Ve = Vu + 1.6 Tu per b]
    C --> D[Compute tauve = Ve per b d]
    D --> E{tauve le taucmax}
    E -- No --> F[Enlarge Section and Return to Step B]
    E -- Yes --> G[Flexural Design Ast from Mu]
    G --> H[Read tau c from Table 19 for pt]
    H --> I[Compute Stirrups Asv per sv]
    I --> J[Check sv min and sv max limits]
    J --> K[Compute Longitudinal Steel Al]
    K --> L[Distribute Al to four corner bars]
    L --> M[Check Side Face Reinforcement if D greater than 450 mm]
    M --> N[Final Detailing: spacing cover anchorage]
    N --> O[Output Sketches and Bar Schedule]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of Equivalent Shear Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Original Loads]
        A1[Vu]
        A2[Tu]
    end
    subgraph S2[IS 456 Transformation]
        A1 --> B1[Direct Shear]
        A2 --> B2[Convert Tu to VeT equals 1.6 Tu per b]
    end
    subgraph S3[Unified Shear]
        B1 --> C1[Ve total equals Vu plus VeT]
        B2 --> C1
        C1 --> D1[tauve equals Ve per b d]
    end
    D1 --> E1[Design Stirrups]
    D1 --> E2[Check taucmax]
    D1 --> E3[Read tau c]
```

## 4.3 Torsional Reinforcement Layout (Schematic)

```
        Top Longitudinal Bars (with extra Al/4 each at corners)
   ┌────●────────────────────────────●────────────●────┐
   │    │            b1              │            │    │   ↑
   │    │←───────────────────────────→│            │    │   │
   │    │              ↑             │            │    │   │
   │    │              │ d1          │            │    │   D
   │    │              │             │            │    │   │
   │    │              ↓             │            │    │   │
   │    │            Stirrups         │           │    │   │
   │    │            (2-legged)       │           │    │   │
   │    │                            │           │    │   ↓
   └────●────────────────────────────●────────────●────┘
        Bottom Longitudinal Bars (with extra Al/4 each at corners)

   ● = Corner bars (must be tied to stirrup hooks)
   Stirrup spacing = sv (≤ 0.75d, ≤ 300 mm)
   Al/4 added to each of the 4 corner bars
```

> [!NOTE]
> The figure above is a **text-rendered schematic** showing the four corner bars that receive the distributed longitudinal torsion steel. In an actual site drawing, the stirrups would be drawn as continuous closed loops with 135° hooks at top and 135° hooks at bottom (seismic detailing requires this even for non-seismic zones per IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.2(b)).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Differentiate between equilibrium torsion and compatibility torsion. State the IS 456 clause that governs torsion design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S. No. | Equilibrium Torsion | Compatibility Torsion |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | Also called **primary torsion** | Also called **secondary torsion** |
| 2 | Required for the **static equilibrium** of the structure | Induced by **continuity / compatibility of deformation** |
| 3 | **Cannot be neglected**; member must resist full T | **Can be redistributed** if ductility ensured |
| 4 | Example: cantilever beam supporting a slab on one side | Example: spandrel beam in a frame |

IS 456:2000 — **Clause 41** governs the design of RCC members for torsion, with **Cl. 41.1** mandating minimum **M20** concrete and **Fe415** steel.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for differentiation, 1 mark for IS clause)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Explain the concept of equivalent shear in the design of RC beams for torsion as per IS 456.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In the design of RCC beams subjected to combined shear and torsion, IS 456:2000 (Cl. 41.3.1) simplifies the analysis by replacing the torsional moment with an **equivalent additional shear force**. The rationale is based on the **thin-walled tube analogy** in which the beam's cross-section is idealised as a closed tube; the torsional shear flow can be represented as an additional vertical shear.

The equivalent shear is given by:

$$V_e = V_u + \dfrac{1.6\, T_u}{b}$$

where $b$ is the breadth of the beam. The resulting equivalent shear stress is:

$$\tau_{ve} = \dfrac{V_e}{b\,d} \le \tau_{c,\max}$$

This approach allows the combined action of shear and torsion to be handled using a single set of equations and design charts, leading to a uniform and economical design.

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for tube analogy, 1 mark for τve condition)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply load factors** (γ<sub>f</sub> = 1.5) on T and V — leads to under-design and full marks deducted.
> 2. **Confusing τ<sub>c</sub> (Table 19) with τ<sub>c,max</sub> (Table 20)** — Table 19 is for design strength (depends on p<sub>t</sub>); Table 20 is the absolute upper limit.
> 3. **Using full *b* and *D* for b<sub>1</sub>, d<sub>1</sub>** — must use **centre-to-centre of corner bars**, otherwise stirrup design is wrong.
> 4. **Forgetting to add A<sub>l</sub>/4 to all four corners** — torsion requires longitudinal steel at *all* four corners, not just tension face.
> 5. **Spacing check missed** — many students compute s<sub>v</sub> but forget s<sub>v</sub> ≤ 0.75 d and ≤ 300 mm limit.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

A reinforced concrete beam of size **b = 300 mm**, **D = 600 mm** is subjected to:
- Factored bending moment, **M<sub>u</sub> = 180 kN·m**
- Factored shear force, **V<sub>u</sub> = 90 kN**
- Factored torsional moment, **T<sub>u</sub> = 30 kN·m**

Materials: **M25** concrete and **Fe415** steel. Effective cover = 50 mm. Design the section for combined bending, shear, and torsion. Provide reinforcement details.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Effective dimensions**
$$d = 600 - 50 = 550\,\text{mm}, \quad d' = 50\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent Shear**

$$V_e = 90 \times 10^3 + \dfrac{1.6 \times 30 \times 10^6}{300} = 90\,000 + 160\,000 = 250\,000\,\text{N} = 250\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent Shear Stress**

$$\tau_{ve} = \dfrac{250\,000}{300 \times 550} = 1.515\,\text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Check Section Adequacy**
From Table 20, M25 → $\tau_{c,\max} = 3.1\,\text{N/mm}^2$.
$\tau_{ve} = 1.515 < 3.1$ ✓ **Section is adequate.**

**Step 5 — Flexural Design (Bending)**

For M25, Fe415, the limiting moment (without compression steel):
$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.138\,f_{ck}\,b\,d^2 = 0.138 \times 25 \times 300 \times 550^2 = 313.1\,\text{kN·m}$$

Since M<sub>u</sub> = 180 kN·m < 313.1 kN·m, the section is **singly reinforced**.

$$\frac{M_u}{bd^2} = \frac{180 \times 10^6}{300 \times 550^2} = 1.984 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_t = 0.523\% \text{ (from SP-16)}$$
$$A_{st} = \frac{0.523 \times 300 \times 550}{100} = 863\,\text{mm}^2$$

Provide **4 bars of 18 mm Ø** (A<sub>st</sub> = 4 × 254.5 = 1018 mm²) at the bottom.

For the top, provide 2 bars of 12 mm Ø as hanger bars (A<sub>st</sub> = 226 mm²).

**Step 6 — Shear Capacity of Concrete**
For p<sub>t</sub> = 0.523 %, M25 → $\tau_c = 0.62\,\text{N/mm}^2$ (Table 19, IS 456).

**Step 7 — Stirrup Design (Combined)**

Assume 10 mm Ø 2-legged stirrups, cover = 50 mm, stirrup diameter = 10 mm.
$$b_1 = 300 - 2(50) + 2(10) = 230\,\text{mm}$$
$$d_1 = 550 - 2(50) + 2(10) = 470\,\text{mm}$$

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \frac{0.4 \times (1.515 - 0.62) \times 300}{0.87 \times 415} + \frac{30 \times 10^6}{1.32 \times 230 \times 470 \times 415}$$

$$= \frac{107.4}{361.05} + \frac{30 \times 10^6}{5.93 \times 10^7} = 0.2975 + 0.5060 = 0.8035\,\text{mm}$$

For 2-legged 10 mm Ø stirrup: $A_{sv} = 2 \times 78.5 = 157\,\text{mm}^2$.
$$s_v = \frac{157}{0.8035} = 195.4\,\text{mm}$$

Maximum spacing: $s_{v,\max} = 0.75 \times 550 = 412.5\,\text{mm}$, but also ≤ 300 mm. So adopt:

$$\boxed{s_v = 190\,\text{mm c/c}}$$

**Step 8 — Longitudinal Steel for Torsion**

$$A_l = \frac{T_u \cdot p_v}{1.32 \cdot f_y} = \frac{30 \times 10^6 \times 1.0}{1.32 \times 415} = 547.7\,\text{mm}^2$$

**Distribution (4 corner bars):**
$$A_{l,\text{corner}} = \frac{547.7}{4} = 136.9\,\text{mm}^2 \text{ per corner}$$

Add 12 mm Ø bar at each corner (area = 113 mm²). The 2 existing 18 mm Ø bottom bars already provide 509 mm² at bottom, exceeding A<sub>l</sub>/2 = 273.8 mm² ✓. Add two 12 mm Ø corner bars (one each at top corners) to meet corner requirement.

**Step 9 — Side-Face Reinforcement**
Since D = 600 mm > 450 mm, side-face reinforcement is required:
$$A_{\text{side}} = 0.001 \times b \times (d - d') = 0.001 \times 300 \times 500 = 150\,\text{mm}^2 \text{ per face}$$

Provide 2 bars of 10 mm Ø (A = 157 mm²) on each side face.

**Step 10 — Final Detailing Summary**
- **Bottom (tension):** 4 bars of 18 mm Ø + 2 bars of 12 mm Ø at corners
- **Top (compression):** 2 bars of 12 mm Ø at corners + 2 bars of 10 mm Ø (hanger)
- **Side faces:** 2 bars of 10 mm Ø each face
- **Stirrups:** 10 mm Ø 2-legged @ 190 mm c/c throughout

*(Marks distribution: 3+3 marks for Steps 1–5; 4 marks for Step 7; 2 marks for Step 8; 2 marks for Step 9–10)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

A rectangular beam of size **b = 250 mm**, **D = 500 mm** (effective d = 450 mm) is subjected at a particular section to:
- Factored bending moment, **M<sub>u</sub> = 90 kN·m**
- Factored shear force, **V<sub>u</sub> = 60 kN**
- Factored torsional moment, **T<sub>u</sub> = 22 kN·m**

Materials: **M20** concrete, **Fe415** steel. Effective cover = 50 mm.
Check the adequacy of the section and design shear and torsional reinforcement.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Effective dimensions and material**
d = 450 mm, d′ = 50 mm, b = 250 mm, f<sub>ck</sub> = 20 N/mm², f<sub>y</sub> = 415 N/mm².

**Step 2 — Equivalent Shear**

$$V_e = 60\,000 + \frac{1.6 \times 22 \times 10^6}{250} = 60\,000 + 140\,800 = 200\,800\,\text{N} = 200.8\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent Shear Stress**

$$\tau_{ve} = \frac{200\,800}{250 \times 450} = 1.785\,\text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Section Adequacy**
$\tau_{c,\max}$ for M20 = 2.8 N/mm². $\tau_{ve} = 1.785 < 2.8$ ✓ **Section is adequate.**

**Step 5 — Flexural Design**

$$\frac{M_u}{b d^2} = \frac{90 \times 10^6}{250 \times 450^2} = 1.778$$

For M20, Fe415: $p_t = 0.467\%$ (from SP-16).
$$A_{st} = \frac{0.467 \times 250 \times 450}{100} = 525.4\,\text{mm}^2$$

Provide **3 bars of 16 mm Ø** (A<sub>st</sub> = 3 × 201.1 = 603.2 mm²) at the bottom.
Provide 2 bars of 12 mm Ø at the top (A<sub>st</sub> = 226 mm²).

**Step 6 — τ<sub>c</sub> from Table 19**
For p<sub>t</sub> = 0.467 %, M20 → $\tau_c = 0.56\,\text{N/mm}^2$.

**Step 7 — Stirrup Design**

Assume 8 mm Ø 2-legged stirrups:
$$b_1 = 250 - 100 + 16 = 166\,\text{mm}, \quad d_1 = 450 - 100 + 16 = 366\,\text{mm}$$

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \frac{0.4 \times (1.785 - 0.56) \times 250}{0.87 \times 415} + \frac{22 \times 10^6}{1.32 \times 166 \times 366 \times 415}$$

$$= \frac{122.5}{361.05} + \frac{22 \times 10^6}{3.32 \times 10^7} = 0.3393 + 0.6623 = 1.0016\,\text{mm}$$

For 2-legged 8 mm Ø: $A_{sv} = 100.5\,\text{mm}^2$.
$$s_v = \frac{100.5}{1.0016} = 100.3\,\text{mm}$$

Check limits: $s_{v,\max} = 0.75 \times 450 = 337.5\,\text{mm}$ and ≤ 300 mm. Adopt:

$$\boxed{s_v = 100\,\text{mm c/c}}$$

**Step 8 — Longitudinal Steel for Torsion**

$$A_l = \frac{22 \times 10^6 \times 1.0}{1.32 \times 415} = 401.6\,\text{mm}^2 \quad \text{(total)}$$

Per corner: $A_{l,\text{corner}} = 100.4\,\text{mm}^2$.

**Step 9 — Add Corner Bars**
Add 10 mm Ø bar at each of 4 corners (A = 78.5 mm²). The bottom flexural steel (3 × 16 mm = 603.2 mm²) easily exceeds A<sub>l</sub>/2 = 200.8 mm² ✓. Add 2 top corner bars of 10 mm Ø (in addition to the 2 hanger 12 mm Ø bars).

**Step 10 — Side Face Reinforcement**
D = 500 mm > 450 mm → side-face steel needed.
$$A_{\text{side}} = 0.001 \times 250 \times 400 = 100\,\text{mm}^2 \text{ per face}$$
Provide 2 bars of 8 mm Ø (A = 100.5 mm²) on each side.

**Step 11 — Final Detailing**
- **Bottom (tension):** 3 bars of 16 mm Ø + 2 corner bars of 10 mm Ø
- **Top:** 2 bars of 12 mm Ø (hanger) + 2 corner bars of 10 mm Ø
- **Side faces:** 2 bars of 8 mm Ø each face
- **Stirrups:** 8 mm Ø 2-legged @ 100 mm c/c

*(Marks distribution: 3+3 marks for Steps 1–5; 4 marks for Step 7; 2 marks for Step 8; 2 marks for Steps 9–11)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Memory Anchors for KTU Board Exam:**

- ☐ **Torsion + Shear → Equivalent Shear:** $V_e = V_u + \dfrac{1.6\, T_u}{b}$ — *memorise this FIRST*.
- ☐ **τ<sub>c,max</sub> limits** (M20: 2.8, M25: 3.1, M30: 3.5, M35: 3.7, M40+: 4.0) — Table 20.
- ☐ **τ<sub>c</sub>** is from Table 19 — depends on p<sub>t</sub> and grade — used to *reduce* stirrup requirement if concrete is strong.
- ☐ **b<sub>1</sub>, d<sub>1</sub>** = centre-to-centre of corner bars (NOT overall b, D).
- ☐ **Stirrups (vertical):** $A_{sv}/s_v = \dfrac{0.4(\tau_{ve} - \tau_c)b}{0.87 f_y} + \dfrac{T_u}{1.32 b_1 d_1 f_y}$.
- ☐ **Longitudinal torsion steel:** $A_l = \dfrac{T_u \cdot p_v}{1.32 f_y}$ — distributed **equally to all 4 corner bars** (A<sub>l</sub>/4 each).
- ☐ **p<sub>v</sub> = 1** for vertical stirrups, **p<sub>v</sub> = 2** for 45° inclined stirrups (rarely used).
- ☐ **Spacing limits:** s<sub>v</sub> ≤ 0.75 d AND s<sub>v</sub> ≤ 300 mm.
- ☐ **Minimum grade M20, Fe415** is mandatory for torsion design (Cl. 41.1).
- ☐ **Equilibrium torsion** cannot be ignored; **compatibility torsion** can be reduced by 50 % if member is designed for ductility (Cl. 41.2).
- ☐ **Side-face reinforcement** mandatory when D > 450 mm or b > 300 mm — area = 0.1 % of web area per face.
- ☐ **Stirrup hooks:** 135° bend + 10d extension at both ends (seismic-grade anchorage) to prevent brittle shear failure.
- ☐ **Critical section for torsion design:** at distance d from face of support (same as shear).
- ☐ **Don't mix** characteristic loads with factored loads — always apply γ<sub>f</sub> = 1.5 first.
- ☐ **Common ratio trick:** for similar rectangular sections, the equivalent shear contribution from torsion scales as T/b — doubling torsion or halving breadth doubles V<sub>e,T</sub>.
- ☐ **Final check before submission:** verify A<sub>st,provided</sub> ≥ A<sub>st,required</sub> AND corner bars present AND stirrup spacing as per calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problems on Design and Analysis of Singly Reinforced Rectangular Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems on Design and Analysis of Singly Reinforced Rectangular Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (IS 456:2000 Terminology)

A **Singly Reinforced Rectangular Beam** is a flexural member whose cross-section is rectangular, provided with **tension reinforcement only** in the zone below the neutral axis, and designed to resist the ultimate bending moment (factored load moment) using the combined action of concrete in the compression zone and steel in the tension zone.

Per **IS 456:2000 (Cl. G-1.1)**, the design of such beams shall be carried out by the **Limit State Method (LSM)**, satisfying the **Limit State of Collapse (Flexure)**. The section is considered "singly reinforced" when the depth of the actual neutral axis $x_u$ is **less than or equal to the limiting value $x_{u,\max}$**, beyond which compression steel becomes mandatory (doubly reinforced section).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Sectional Categories (Critical for KTU Numericals):**
> - **Balanced Section:** $x_u = x_{u,\max}$ → Concrete and steel reach ultimate strain simultaneously.
> - **Under-Reinforced Section:** $x_u < x_{u,\max}$ → Steel yields first (ductile failure – **IS 456 mandates this**).
> - **Over-Reinforced Section:** $x_u > x_{u,\max}$ → Concrete crushes first (brittle, sudden failure – **NOT permitted**).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a beam like a seesaw carrying a heavy load at its center:**
> - The **top fibers** are squeezed (compression) — concrete handles this excellently, just like our spine compresses when we lift a weight.
> - The **bottom fibers** are stretched (tension) — concrete is weak here, so we embed **steel rebars** (which behave like strong rubber bands) to carry the tension.
> - The beam is **"singly reinforced"** because we only need steel at the bottom (tension side); the top concrete is strong enough to handle compression on its own. If the load becomes too large, we'd need steel on top too — making it **"doubly reinforced"**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in IS 456

- **Effective Span ($l_{eff}$):** As per IS 456 (Cl. 22.2), the lesser of (a) clear span + effective depth, or (b) center-to-center of supports.
- **Effective Cover:** Clear cover + half the bar diameter. Typically **50 mm** for beams.
- **Partial Safety Factors (LSM):** $\gamma_f = 1.5$ for DL+LL, $\gamma_m = 1.5$ for concrete, $\gamma_m = 1.15$ for steel.
- **Grade Limits (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** M20–M40 for concrete; Fe415 / Fe500 for HYSD bars.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Limit State of Collapse (Flexure)

**Assumptions (IS 456:2000, Cl. 38.1):**
- Plane sections remain plane after bending (Bernoulli's hypothesis).
- Maximum strain in concrete at the extreme compression fiber = **0.0035**.
- Tensile strength of concrete is ignored.
- Perfect bond exists between steel and concrete.
- The **rectangular stress block** (Whitney) replaces the actual parabolic stress distribution.

### 2.2 The Two Pillars of LSM Design

**(A) Equilibrium Equation (Force):** The total compressive force equals the total tensile force.

$$C = T$$

$$0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}$$

**(B) Moment Equation (Equilibrium):** The ultimate moment of resistance equals the couple of forces times the lever arm.

$$M_u = T \cdot z = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \cdot (d - 0.42 \, x_u)$$

### 2.3 Derivation of the Limiting Moment (Why $M_{u,\text{lim}}$ Exists)

The **limiting depth of neutral axis** ensures steel yields before concrete crushes:

$$x_{u,\max} = k_1 \cdot d \quad \text{where} \quad k_1 = \begin{cases} 0.48 & \text{for Fe 415} \\ 0.46 & \text{for Fe 500} \end{cases}$$

Substituting into the moment equation:

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_{u,\max} \, (d - 0.42 \, x_{u,\max}) = Q_{\text{lim}} \, b \, d^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Constants ($Q_{\text{lim}}$) — KTU Frequently Asked Values:**
> - For **Fe 415**: $Q_{\text{lim}} = 0.138 \, f_{ck}$
> - For **Fe 500**: $Q_{\text{lim}} = 0.133 \, f_{ck}$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Parameter / Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|:------|:-------------------------|:------------|:----------:|
| 1 | Factored Bending Moment | $M_u = 1.5 \cdot \dfrac{w \cdot l_{eff}^2}{8}$ (simply supported) | kN·m |
| 2 | Force Equilibrium | $0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}$ | N |
| 3 | Neutral Axis Depth | $x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b}$ | mm |
| 4 | Limiting NA Depth | $x_{u,\max} = 0.48 \, d$ (Fe415) or $0.46 \, d$ (Fe500) | mm |
| 5 | Limiting Moment | $M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_{u,\max} \, (d - 0.42 \, x_{u,\max})$ | N·mm |
| 6 | Simplified $M_{u,\text{lim}}$ | $M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$ (Fe415) | N·mm |
| 7 | Lever Arm | $z = d \left(1 - \dfrac{0.42 \, x_u}{d}\right) = d - 0.42 \, x_u$ | mm |
| 8 | Moment of Resistance (Under-Reinforced) | $M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \cdot z$ | N·mm |
| 9 | Steel Area (Under-Reinforced) | $A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5 \, f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6 \, M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2}}\right] b \, d$ | mm² |
| 10 | Min. Tension Steel (Cl. 26.5.1.1) | $A_{st,\min} = \dfrac{0.85}{f_y} \cdot b \, d$ | mm² |
| 11 | Max. Tension Steel (Cl. 26.5.1.1) | $A_{st,\max} = 0.04 \, b \, D$ | mm² |
| 12 | Required Effective Depth | $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{M_u}{Q_{\text{lim}} \cdot b}}$ | mm |
| 13 | % of Steel Provided | $p = \dfrac{A_{st}}{b \, d} \times 100$ | % |
| 14 | Limiting % Steel (Fe415) | $p_{\text{lim}} \approx 0.85 \,\%$ | % |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

This design framework is the **backbone of every RC structure** — from residential building beams, bridge girders, to industrial rafters. The principle ensures **safe, ductile, and economical** structural members that warn before failure (visible cracking/deflection) rather than collapsing suddenly.
