## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Master Derivation: From Strain to Design Equation

**Starting Point (Strain Compatibility):**

The strain distribution is linear (plane sections remain plane). The depth of NA, $x_u$, separates compression (above) and tension (below).

**Strain in steel at level of reinforcement:**

$$\varepsilon_s = 0.0035 \cdot \frac{d - x_u}{x_u}$$

**Stress in steel (Elastic–Perfectly Plastic):**

$$f_s = \begin{cases} E_s \cdot \varepsilon_s & \text{if } \varepsilon_s < \varepsilon_{y} \\ f_y & \text{if } \varepsilon_s \geq \varepsilon_{y} \end{cases}$$

**Equilibrium of Forces (Tension = Compression):**

$$0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u$$

**Solving for $x_u$:**

$$x_u = \frac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b}$$

**Moment of Resistance (Taking moment about compression zone centroid):**

$$M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \cdot \left(d - \frac{x_u}{2} \cdot \frac{0.42}{0.5}\right) = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \cdot (d - 0.42 \, x_u)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of the 0.42 factor:** The centroid of the rectangular stress block (depth $0.42 \, x_u$ from the extreme compression fiber) gives the lever arm $z = d - 0.42 \, x_u$. This is **not** an empirical fudge — it is derived from equating areas of the actual and equivalent rectangular blocks.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example #1 — ANALYSIS PROBLEM (Finding Moment of Resistance)

> **Problem Statement:**
> *A singly reinforced rectangular beam has width $b = 250$ mm and effective depth $d = 500$ mm. It is reinforced with 4 bars of 20 mm diameter. Determine the moment of resistance of the section using M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. (Per IS 456:2000)*

**Given Data:**
- $b = 250$ mm, $d = 500$ mm
- Bars: 4 nos. × 20 mm $\Rightarrow A_{st} = 4 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 4 \times 314.159 = 1256.64$ mm²
- $f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm²
- Fe415 $\Rightarrow x_{u,\max} = 0.48 \, d = 0.48 \times 500 = 240$ mm

**Step 1: Calculate actual depth of neutral axis from force equilibrium.**

$$x_u = \frac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b} = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 1256.64}{0.36 \times 20 \times 250}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 0.87 \times 415 \times 1256.64 = 453,994.7 \text{ N} \\
\text{Denominator} &= 0.36 \times 20 \times 250 = 1800 \text{ N/mm} \\
x_u &= \frac{453,994.7}{1800} = 252.22 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Classify the section.**

$$x_u = 252.22 \text{ mm} > x_{u,\max} = 240 \text{ mm}$$

$$\therefore \text{Section is OVER-REINFORCED} \quad (\text{Not permitted by IS 456})$$

**Step 3: Restrict moment to limiting value (IS 456:2000 Cl. G-1.1).**

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_{u,\max} \, (d - 0.42 \, x_{u,\max})$$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{u,\text{lim}} &= 0.36 \times 20 \times 250 \times 240 \times (500 - 0.42 \times 240) \\
&= 1800 \times 240 \times (500 - 100.8) \\
&= 1800 \times 240 \times 399.2 \\
&= 172,454,400 \text{ N·mm} \\
&= 172.45 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{M_R = 172.45 \text{ kN·m}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Many students report $M_R = 0.87 \times 415 \times 1256.64 \times (500 - 0.42 \times 252.22) = 174.8$ kN·m by **directly** substituting $x_u$ even when the section is over-reinforced. This **loses full marks** because IS 456 prohibits over-reinforced sections — you must cap the moment at $M_{u,\text{lim}}$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example #2 — DESIGN PROBLEM (Finding $A_{st}$ Given $M_u$)

> **Problem Statement:**
> *Design a singly reinforced concrete beam to resist a factored bending moment of 200 kN·m. Use M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. Take $b = 300$ mm, effective cover = 50 mm.*

**Step 1: Compute limiting moment for given section (assume trial $d$).**

For an initial estimate, we set $M_{u,\text{lim}} \geq M_u$ and solve for $d$:

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{M_u}{0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b}} = \sqrt{\frac{200 \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 20 \times 300}} = \sqrt{\frac{200 \times 10^6}{828}}$$

$$d = \sqrt{241,546} = 491.47 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Provide practical dimensions.**

Adopt $d = 500$ mm; thus overall depth:

$$D = d + \text{effective cover} = 500 + 50 = 550 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow \text{Adopt } D = 575 \text{ mm (rounded)}$$

Revised $d = 575 - 50 = 525$ mm.

**Step 3: Verify section qualifies as singly reinforced.**

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 300 \times 525^2 = 0.138 \times 20 \times 300 \times 275,625$$

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 228,217,500 \text{ N·mm} = 228.22 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $M_u$ (200 kN·m) $< M_{u,\text{lim}}$ (228.22 kN·m), **singly reinforced design is valid.** ✓

**Step 4: Calculate required steel area.**

$$A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \, f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \, M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2}}\right] b \, d$$

Compute the discriminant term:

$$\frac{4.6 \, M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2} = \frac{4.6 \times 200 \times 10^6}{20 \times 300 \times 525^2} = \frac{920 \times 10^6}{1.654 \times 10^9} = 0.5562$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{1 - 0.5562} &= \sqrt{0.4438} = 0.6662 \\
1 - 0.6662 &= 0.3338
\end{aligned}$$

Coefficient: $\frac{0.5 \times 20}{415} = 0.02410$

$$A_{st} = 0.02410 \times 0.3338 \times 300 \times 525 = 0.02410 \times 0.3338 \times 157,500$$

$$A_{st} = 1266.7 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Check minimum steel requirement (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1).**

$$A_{st,\min} = \frac{0.85}{f_y} \cdot b \cdot d = \frac{0.85}{415} \times 300 \times 525 = 322.65 \text{ mm}^2$$

Since $A_{st}$ (1266.7) $> A_{st,\min}$ (322.65) ✓

**Step 6: Choose bar arrangement.**

Provide **4 bars of 20 mm** $\Rightarrow A_{st,\text{provided}} = 4 \times 314.16 = 1256.64$ mm²

This is **slightly less** than required. Try **5 bars of 20 mm** $\Rightarrow A_{st,\text{provided}} = 1570.8$ mm² ✓

**Final Design Summary:**
- $b = 300$ mm, $D = 575$ mm, $d = 525$ mm
- Tension Reinforcement: **5 bars of 20 mm $\phi$** (2 layers if required, else 1 layer of 5 bars with adequate spacing)
- Effective cover: **50 mm**
- Shear stirrups: 8 mm $\phi$ @ appropriate spacing (to be checked in shear design)

---

### 3.4 Worked Example #3 — DESIGN PROBLEM (Finding Section Dimensions)

> **Problem Statement:**
> *A simply supported beam of effective span 6 m carries a superimposed (dead + live) load of 20 kN/m. Design the beam section using M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. Take $b = 250$ mm.*

**Step 1: Estimate self-weight (assume trial $D$).**

Try $D = 500$ mm $\Rightarrow$ self-weight = $0.25 \times 0.5 \times 25 = 3.125$ kN/m

Total working load: $w = 20 + 3.125 = 23.125$ kN/m

Factored load: $w_u = 1.5 \times 23.125 = 34.69$ kN/m

**Step 2: Compute factored moment.**

$$M_u = \frac{w_u \cdot l_{eff}^2}{8} = \frac{34.69 \times 6^2}{8} = \frac{34.69 \times 36}{8} = 156.1 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3: Compute required effective depth.**

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{M_u}{0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b}} = \sqrt{\frac{156.1 \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 20 \times 250}} = \sqrt{\frac{156.1 \times 10^6}{690}}$$

$$d = \sqrt{226,231.9} = 475.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Provide dimensions.**

Adopt $d = 480$ mm. With effective cover 50 mm: $D = 480 + 50 = 530$ mm $\Rightarrow$ adopt $D = 550$ mm.

Revised $d = 550 - 50 = 500$ mm.

**Step 5: Recalculate $M_u$ with new self-weight.**

Self-weight = $0.25 \times 0.55 \times 25 = 3.4375$ kN/m
$w = 20 + 3.4375 = 23.4375$ kN/m
$w_u = 35.16$ kN/m
$M_u = \dfrac{35.16 \times 36}{8} = 158.2$ kN·m

**Step 6: Check singly reinforced validity.**

$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times 500^2 = 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times 250,000 = 172,500,000 \text{ N·mm} = 172.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $M_u$ (158.2) $< M_{u,\text{lim}}$ (172.5) ✓

**Step 7: Calculate $A_{st}$.**

$$\frac{4.6 \, M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2} = \frac{4.6 \times 158.2 \times 10^6}{20 \times 250 \times 500^2} = \frac{727.72 \times 10^6}{1.25 \times 10^9} = 0.5822$$

$$\sqrt{1 - 0.5822} = \sqrt{0.4178} = 0.6464 \Rightarrow 1 - 0.6464 = 0.3536$$

$$A_{st} = 0.02410 \times 0.3536 \times 250 \times 500 = 1065.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

Provide **4 bars of 20 mm $\phi$** $\Rightarrow A_{st,\text{provided}} = 1256.64$ mm² ✓

**Final Design:**
- $b = 250$ mm, $D = 550$ mm, $d = 500$ mm
- **4 bars of 20 mm $\phi$** as tension reinforcement
- Effective cover: 50 mm
- Minimum steel check: $A_{st,\min} = (0.85/415) \times 250 \times 500 = 256$ mm² $<$ 1256.64 ✓

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Design Flow for Singly Reinforced Beam (LSM)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Mu, b, fck, fy] --> B[Compute Mu,lim = 0.138 fck b d^2]
    B --> C{Is d known?}
    C -- No --> D[Compute d required = sqrt(Mu / 0.138 fck b)]
    D --> E[Adopt practical D and revise d]
    E --> F[Recompute Mu with self weight]
    C -- Yes --> F
    F --> G{Mu ≤ Mu,lim?}
    G -- No --> H[Increase d OR Use Doubly Reinforced Section]
    G -- Yes --> I[Compute Ast using quadratic formula]
    I --> J[Check Ast,min = 0.85 b d / fy]
    J --> K{Ast,provided ≥ Ast,required?}
    K -- No --> L[Increase bar size / number]
    L --> M[Provide reinforcement details]
    K -- Yes --> M[Provide reinforcement details]
    M --> N[Check Ast,max = 0.04 b D]
    N --> O[Design shear stirrups]
    O --> P[End: Final Design Summary]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Analysis Flow for Singly Reinforced Beam

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Given b, d, Ast, fck, fy] --> B1[Compute xu = 0.87 fy Ast / 0.36 fck b]
    B1 --> C1[Compute xu,max based on grade]
    C1 --> D1{Compare xu vs xu,max}
    D1 -- xu ≤ xu,max --> E1[Under Reinforced Section]
    D1 -- xu > xu,max --> F1[Over Reinforced: Not Allowed]
    E1 --> G1[Compute z = d - 0.42 xu]
    G1 --> H1[Compute Mu = 0.87 fy Ast z]
    H1 --> I1[Report Mu as Moment of Resistance]
    F1 --> J1[Restrict Mu to Mu,lim]
    J1 --> K1[Report Mu,lim as Moment of Resistance]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Internal Force Couple in a Singly Reinforced Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SCC[Compression Zone: Top of Beam]
        C1["C = 0.36 fck b xu"]
    end
    subgraph NAZ[Neutral Axis: xu from top]
        NA1["Strain = 0 at NA"]
    end
    subgraph STZ[Tension Zone: Bottom of Beam]
        T1["T = 0.87 fy Ast"]
    end
    SCC -- Force C --> NAZ
    NAZ -- Force T --> STZ
    NAZ -- Lever Arm z --> L1["z = d - 0.42 xu"]
    L1 --> M1["Mu = T x z = 0.87 fy Ast (d - 0.42 xu)"]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Section Reinforcement Layout (Schematic Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CRS[Beam Cross-Section: b x D]
        S1["Clear Cover: 25 mm"]
        S2["Effective Cover: 50 mm"]
        S3["Stirrup: 8 mm dia."]
        S4["Main Bars: 4-5 nos of 20 mm at bottom"]
        S5["Top Bar: 2 nos of 12 mm (side face / hanger bars)"]
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
    S3 --> S5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note on Detailing:** The above Mermaid block diagram abstracts the actual cross-section. In your exam answer sheet, you must **draw the cross-section to scale** showing (i) clear cover, (ii) effective cover to centroid of steel, (iii) bar arrangement, and (iv) stirrups. Refer to **SP-34** for standard detailing practices.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] (3 Marks)**
*Define the term "Limit State of Collapse (Flexure)" as per IS 456:2000. List the partial safety factors for loads and materials used in this method.*

**Model Answer:**
The Limit State of Collapse (Flexure) is defined as the state at which the structural member reaches its **maximum load-carrying capacity** in flexure, just before failure due to yielding of steel or crushing of concrete.

Partial Safety Factors (IS 456, Table 18 & 21):
- For Loads ($\gamma_f$): **1.5** (for DL + LL combinations)
- For Concrete ($\gamma_m$): **1.5**
- For Steel ($\gamma_m$): **1.15**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (3 Marks)**
*Distinguish between "Balanced", "Under-reinforced", and "Over-reinforced" sections in terms of neutral axis depth and failure mode.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Section Type** | **NA Depth** | **Failure Mode** | **Permitted?** |
|:-----------------|:-------------|:-----------------|:---------------|
| Balanced | $x_u = x_{u,\max}$ | Both fail simultaneously | Theoretically (rare) |
| Under-reinforced | $x_u < x_{u,\max}$ | Steel yields first → ductile | **Yes (preferred)** |
| Over-reinforced | $x_u > x_{u,\max}$ | Concrete crushes first → brittle, sudden | **No (prohibited)** |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **INSTRUCTIONS:** Answer **ONE** full question. Each carries **14 marks** split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — DESIGN PROBLEM

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q1] (14 Marks)**

> *(a) [7 Marks]* A simply supported beam of effective span **5 m** carries a superimposed (dead + live) load of **18 kN/m** (excluding self-weight). Design the cross-section dimensions. Use **M20 concrete** and **Fe415 steel**. Take $b = 250$ mm and effective cover = 50 mm.
>
> *(b) [7 Marks]* For the section designed in (a), determine the **area of tension steel** required. Provide a suitable bar arrangement and check minimum steel provisions of IS 456.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Section Sizing**

Step 1: Assume self-weight. Try $D = 500$ mm.
$$w_{sw} = 0.25 \times 0.5 \times 25 = 3.125 \text{ kN/m}$$
$$w = 18 + 3.125 = 21.125 \text{ kN/m}$$
$$w_u = 1.5 \times 21.125 = 31.69 \text{ kN/m}$$
$$M_u = \frac{w_u \cdot l^2}{8} = \frac{31.69 \times 25}{8} = 99.03 \text{ kN·m}$$

Step 2: Required effective depth:
$$d = \sqrt{\frac{M_u}{0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b}} = \sqrt{\frac{99.03 \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 20 \times 250}} = \sqrt{359,565} = 599.6 \text{ mm}$$

Step 3: Adopt $d = 600$ mm. Overall depth $D = 600 + 50 = 650$ mm.

Step 4: Revise self-weight: $w_{sw} = 0.25 \times 0.65 \times 25 = 4.0625$ kN/m
$$w = 22.0625 \text{ kN/m}, \quad w_u = 33.09 \text{ kN/m}$$
$$M_u = \frac{33.09 \times 25}{8} = 103.4 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Final Section: $b = 250$ mm, $D = 650$ mm, $d = 600$ mm**

**Part (b) – Steel Design**

Step 1: Check $M_{u,\text{lim}}$:
$$M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times 600^2 = 248.4 \text{ kN·m} > M_u = 103.4 \text{ kN·m} \quad ✓$$

Step 2: Calculate $A_{st}$:
$$\frac{4.6 \, M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2} = \frac{4.6 \times 103.4 \times 10^6}{20 \times 250 \times 600^2} = 0.2643$$
$$\sqrt{1 - 0.2643} = \sqrt{0.7357} = 0.8578 \Rightarrow 1 - 0.8578 = 0.1422$$
$$A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415} \times 0.1422 \times 250 \times 600 = 0.02410 \times 0.1422 \times 150,000 = 514.1 \text{ mm}^2$$

Step 3: Check minimum steel:
$$A_{st,\min} = \frac{0.85}{415} \times 250 \times 600 = 307.2 \text{ mm}^2 < 514.1 \quad ✓$$

Step 4: Provide **3 bars of 16 mm $\phi$** $\Rightarrow A_{st,\text{prov}} = 3 \times 201.06 = 603.2$ mm² $> 514.1$ mm² ✓

**Final Answer:**
- Section: **250 mm × 650 mm** (b × D)
- Tension steel: **3 bars of 16 mm $\phi$** at bottom
- Effective cover: 50 mm

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — ANALYSIS PROBLEM (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2] (14 Marks)**

> *(a) [7 Marks]* A singly reinforced rectangular beam has $b = 300$ mm, $d = 550$ mm, and is reinforced with **5 bars of 20 mm diameter**. Determine the **depth of neutral axis** and classify the section. Use M20 concrete and Fe415 steel.
>
> *(b) [7 Marks]* For the same beam, calculate the **ultimate moment of resistance** as per IS 456:2000. Also, compute the **percentage of steel** provided.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – NA Depth and Section Classification**

Given: $b = 300$ mm, $d = 550$ mm, $A_{st} = 5 \times 314.16 = 1570.8$ mm²

Step 1: $x_{u,\max} = 0.48 \times 550 = 264$ mm

Step 2: Equilibrium:
$$x_u = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 1570.8}{0.36 \times 20 \times 300} = \frac{567,924.3}{2160} = 262.93 \text{ mm}$$

Step 3: Since $x_u = 262.93$ mm $< x_{u,\max} = 264$ mm → **UNDER-REINFORCED Section** ✓

**Part (b) – Moment of Resistance**

Step 1: Lever arm:
$$z = d - 0.42 \, x_u = 550 - 0.42 \times 262.93 = 550 - 110.43 = 439.57 \text{ mm}$$

Step 2: Moment of resistance:
$$M_u = 0.87 \times 415 \times 1570.8 \times 439.57 = 249,547,829 \text{ N·mm} = 249.55 \text{ kN·m}$$

Step 3: Percentage of steel:
$$p = \frac{A_{st}}{b \cdot d} \times 100 = \frac{1570.8}{300 \times 550} \times 100 = \frac{1570.8}{165,000} \times 100 = 0.952\%$$

**Comparison:** $p = 0.952\% < p_{\text{lim}} = 0.85\%$? No, $p > p_{\text{lim}}$ would normally be a concern, but we must verify: Actually $p_{\text{lim}}$ for Fe415 from $x_{u,\max}$ corresponds to a particular value. Since $x_u < x_{u,\max}$, the section is **under-reinforced and SAFE**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check $A_{st,\min}$:** IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1 mandates a minimum of $\frac{0.85}{f_y} b d$. Omission = **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 2. **Failing to restrict $M_R$ for over-reinforced sections:** Direct substitution of $x_u$ into the moment equation for an over-reinforced section is **conceptually wrong** and will lead to **−3 to −4 marks**.
> 3. **Ignoring self-weight iteration:** In a design problem, once you compute $d$, the new self-weight changes $M_u$. Skipping this iteration loses **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing Fe415 ($x_{u,\max} = 0.48d$) with Fe500 ($x_{u,\max} = 0.46d$):** Always cross-check the steel grade. **−2 marks** for using the wrong limiting value.
> 5. **Not drawing the reinforcement detail:** KTU board examiners expect a labeled cross-section diagram in Part (b). **−1 to −2 marks** for omission.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 QUICK REVISION BULLET POINTS:**
> - **Working Stress Method (WSM)** uses $\sigma_{cbc}$ and $\sigma_{st}$ allowable stresses with a global factor of safety ≈ 1.78.
> - **Limit State Method (LSM)** uses partial safety factors ($\gamma_f = 1.5$, $\gamma_m = 1.5/1.15$) and is the **mandatory** method per IS 456:2000.
> - **Limiting NA depth ($x_{u,\max}$):** $0.48d$ (Fe415) | $0.46d$ (Fe500).
> - **Limiting moment $M_{u,\text{lim}}$:** $= 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ (Fe415) | $= 0.133 f_{ck} b d^2$ (Fe500).
> - **Singly reinforced** design is valid **only if** $M_u \leq M_{u,\text{lim}}$; otherwise go to **doubly reinforced**.
> - **Always classify** the section in **analysis problems**: $x_u \leq x_{u,\max}$ (under-reinforced, OK) vs $x_u > x_{u,\max}$ (over-reinforced, restrict to $M_{u,\text{lim}}$).
> - **Minimum tension steel** = $\frac{0.85 b d}{f_y}$ (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1).
> - **Maximum tension steel** = $0.04 b D$ (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1).
> - **Lever arm** $z = d - 0.42 x_u$ is the **standard** formulation for rectangular stress block.
> - **Master formula** for $A_{st}$: $A_{st} = \frac{0.5 f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2}}\right] b d$ (derived from equilibrium + moment equations).
> - **Effective cover** (50 mm typical) must be added to clear cover + half bar diameter to convert $D$ to $d$.
> - **Rounding rules:** Beam dimensions in multiples of **50 mm**; bar diameters from standard sizes (8, 10, 12, 16, 20, 25, 32 mm).
> - **Draw a properly labeled cross-section** in every numerical — KTU examiners reward clear diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Doubly Reinforced Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Doubly Reinforced Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (IS 456:2000 Terminology)

A **Doubly Reinforced Concrete Beam** is a flexural member in which reinforcement is provided in **both the tension zone (bottom steel, $A_{st}$) and the compression zone (top steel, $A_{sc}$)**. The compression steel is formally termed the *top reinforcement* or *hanger steel*, while the tension steel at the bottom is the *main reinforcement*.

According to **IS 456:2000, Clause 5.1.2.2 and 38.1**, doubly reinforced sections are adopted when:

- The applied design bending moment ($M_u$) **exceeds** the limiting moment of resistance ($M_{u,lim}$) of a singly reinforced section of the given cross-section.
- Architectural/headroom constraints restrict the **overall depth ($D$)** such that the effective depth $d$ cannot be increased.
- The cross-section must resist both **positive and negative moments** of comparable magnitude (e.g., continuous beams, portal frames, seismic load cases).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to (i) identify situations warranting a doubly reinforced design, (ii) apply the *split-section philosophy* (isolating the additional moment $M_{u2}$ carried by the compression–tension steel couple), and (iii) check for deflection and cracking serviceability using IS 456 Annexure C and Clause 23.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Person See-Saw"

Imagine a heavy adult (representing the **applied moment $M_u$**) sitting on one end of a see-saw, while a small child (the **concrete compression block**) tries to balance the load. The see-saw is the **lever arm** ($d - 0.42 x_u$). If the adult is too heavy, the small child alone cannot prevent the beam from failing in flexure.

**Solution?** Add a second helper — a teenager (representing the **compression steel $A_{sc}$**) — who sits on the same end as the child, gripping the lever from above. Now the combined "compression force group" (concrete + top steel) balances the heavier adult, and an **extra tension counterweight** (additional bottom steel $A_{st2}$) is tied to the adult's side to keep equilibrium.

The see-saw has *two* pairs working together: (i) the **original child–adult pair** (singly reinforced balanced design, $M_{u,lim}$), and (ii) the **teenager–extra adult pair** (the additional steel couple, $M_{u2}$). The total load carried is the **sum of the two pairs**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Key standard values mandated by IS 456:2000 (Table for LSM):
> - **Strain in concrete at collapse, $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$** (Clause 38.1.1)
> - **Modulus of elasticity of steel, $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²** (Clause 2.2.4.1)
> - **Partial safety factor for steel, $\gamma_m = 1.15$** (Clause 36.4.2.1)
> - **Design yield strength of steel, $f_{yd} = f_y / 1.15$**
> - **Limiting depth of neutral axis, $x_{u,max}/d = 0.48$ for Fe415, $0.46$ for Fe500, $0.53$ for Fe250** (Clause 38.1.1)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Split-Section Philosophy (Core Logic)

The **limit-state design** of a doubly reinforced section is based on a **superposition principle**. The design moment is divided into two components:

$$M_u = M_{u1} + M_{u2}$$

where:
- $M_{u1}$ = the moment carried by a **singly reinforced balanced section** of width $b$ and effective depth $d$, where the neutral axis depth is held at $x_{u,max}$. This is the *limiting moment*, $M_{u,lim}$.
- $M_{u2}$ = the **additional moment** above $M_{u,lim}$, carried entirely by an **equal and opposite couple** of additional steel forces — compression steel $A_{sc}$ at stress $f_{sc}$ and additional tension steel $A_{st2}$ at stress $f_{yd}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind splitting:** The concrete compression block is *already at its design strength* ($0.446 f_{ck}$) when $x_u = x_{u,max}$. Adding more concrete cannot help. To carry *more moment*, we must increase the **lever-arm action** by adding a *second* force couple in the web region — this is the compression–tension steel couple with lever arm $(d - d')$.

### 2.2 Stress and Strain Compatibility (LSM)

The stresses at the ultimate limit state are derived from the **rectangular-parabolic stress block** (IS 456, Clause 38.1, Fig. 4). The strain in the compression steel $A_{sc}$ located at depth $d'$ is:

$$\varepsilon_{sc} = 0.0035 \times \left(1 - \frac{d'}{x_u}\right) \quad \text{(for any } x_u\text{)}$$

For the *limiting case* ($x_u = x_{u,max}$), the stress in compression steel is:

$$f_{sc} = E_s \times \varepsilon_{sc} = 2 \times 10^5 \times 0.0035 \times \left(1 - \frac{d'}{x_{u,max}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{f_{sc} = 700 \times \left(1 - \frac{d'}{x_{u,max}}\right) \;\; \text{N/mm}^2 \leq f_{yd}}$$

The design strength of tension steel is **assumed to have yielded** at $f_{yd} = f_y / 1.15$ (since the strain at $A_{st}$ far exceeds the yield strain, IS 456, Cl. 38.1.1.1).

### 2.3 Forces in the Section (Equilibrium)

At the limit state, the **force in the compression steel** is:

$$C_{sc} = A_{sc} \times f_{sc}$$

The **force in the additional tension steel** $A_{st2}$ is:

$$T_2 = A_{st2} \times f_{yd}$$

The couple between them has a lever arm of $(d - d')$, so:

$$M_{u2} = A_{sc} \times f_{sc} \times (d - d') = A_{st2} \times f_{yd} \times (d - d')$$

### 2.4 Steel Areas (Direct Formulas)

**Step 1:** Calculate $M_{u,lim}$ for the given $b$, $d$, $f_{ck}$, $f_y$. If $M_u > M_{u,lim}$, compute:

$$M_{u2} = M_u - M_{u,lim}$$

**Step 2:** Steel area to resist $M_{u1}$ (singly reinforced balanced portion):

$$A_{st1} = 0.5 \times \frac{f_{ck}}{f_y} \times x_{u,max} \times b$$

**Step 3:** Steel area to resist $M_{u2}$ (couple portion):

$$A_{sc} = \frac{M_{u2}}{f_{sc} \times (d - d')}$$

$$A_{st2} = \frac{M_{u2}}{f_{yd} \times (d - d')}$$

**Step 4:** Total tension steel:

$$\boxed{A_{st} = A_{st1} + A_{st2}}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the *exam-critical* formulas. Note: All values in **N and mm** unless stated. Use $\vert$ only for absolute value, not inside tables.

| Quantity | Formula | Application |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| Design yield of steel | $f_{yd} = f_y / 1.15$ | Always in LSM |
| Limiting $x_u/d$ (Fe415) | $0.48$ | Per IS 456 Cl. 38.1.1 |
| Limiting $x_u/d$ (Fe500) | $0.46$ | Per IS 456 Cl. 38.1.1 |
| Limiting $x_u/d$ (Fe250) | $0.53$ | Per IS 456 Cl. 38.1.1 |
| Limiting moment | $M_{u,lim} = 0.36 \,(x_{u,max}/d) \,[1 - 0.42(x_{u,max}/d)] \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$ | For Q-table (SP-16): $M_{u,lim} = Q_{lim}\, b\, d^2$ |
| $Q_{lim}$ (Fe415, M20) | $2.76$ N/mm² | SP-16 Design Aids |
| $Q_{lim}$ (Fe415, M25) | $3.45$ N/mm² | SP-16 Design Aids |
| $Q_{lim}$ (Fe500, M20) | $2.33$ N/mm² | SP-16 Design Aids |
| Compression steel stress | $f_{sc} = 700\,(1 - d'/x_{u,max})$, $\leq f_{yd}$ | Beam $d' \leq 0.05 \times$ to $0.10 \times d$ typically |
| Tension steel — first part | $A_{st1} = 0.5\,(f_{ck}/f_y)\, x_{u,max}\, b$ | Singly reinforced portion |
| Tension steel — second part | $A_{st2} = M_{u2} / [f_{yd}\,(d - d')]$ | Additional moment |
| Compression steel area | $A_{sc} = M_{u2} / [f_{sc}\,(d - d')]$ | Compression zone |
| Max. tension steel (Clause 26.5.1.1) | $A_{st,max} = 0.04\, b\, D$ | Overall limit on $A_{st}$ |
| Min. tension steel (Fe415) | $A_{st,min} = 0.85\, b\, d / f_y$ | Clause 26.5.1.1 |
| Max. compression steel | $A_{sc,max} = 0.04\, b\, D$ | Same as tension |
| Min. compression steel | $A_{sc,min} = 0.0012\, b\, D$ | Side-face, IS 13920 |
| Effective flange for T-beam | per IS 456, Cl. 23.1.2 | For flanged beams only |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Doubly reinforced beams are essential in:
1. **Seismic Design (IS 13920):** Beams must resist reversals of moment; top steel is mandatory at supports.
2. **Continuous Beams & Portal Frames:** Negative moment at supports requires *top steel* in continuous regions.
3. **Cantilever Beams (Roof Slabs, Balconies):** Top steel is the *primary* tension reinforcement.
4. **Deep Beams & Transfer Girders:** Restricted depth forces engineers to strengthen the compression zone.
5. **Beams Supporting Heavy Point Loads:** Local bending demands may require additional compression reinforcement.

### 2.7 Conceptual Stress-Strain Logic (Step-by-Step Bullets)

- **Step A — Section is loaded beyond $M_{u,lim}$:** Concrete at top reaches $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$ first, but tension steel *also* yields.
- **Step B — The neutral axis cannot move further down** (bounded by $x_{u,max}/d$ ratio).
- **Step C — Additional moment $M_{u2}$ must be resisted** by a *secondary force couple*.
- **Step D — Compression steel is added** at depth $d'$ to provide $C_{sc} = A_{sc} f_{sc}$.
- **Step E — Additional tension steel $A_{st2}$** balances this force, giving lever arm $(d - d')$.
- **Step F — Result:** $M_u = M_{u1} + M_{u2}$, with all materials at their design strengths.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Limiting Moment of Resistance $M_{u,lim}$ for a Singly Reinforced Section

Starting from the rectangular stress block, the **compressive force in concrete** is:

$$C = 0.446 \times f_{ck} \times b \times x_u$$

*(where $0.446$ is the average stress coefficient for a rectangular stress block, derived from $0.67 \times f_{ck}/\gamma_m = 0.67 f_{ck}/1.5$ which equals $0.4467 f_{ck}$)*

The **tensile force in steel** is:

$$T = 0.87 \times f_y \times A_{st}$$

Equating $C = T$ for equilibrium:

$$0.446 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}$$

Solving for $A_{st}$:

$$A_{st} = \frac{0.446 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u}{0.87 \, f_y} = 0.513 \, \frac{f_{ck}}{f_y} \, b \, x_u$$

But using the **balance condition** (Clause 38.1.1: both concrete crushes and steel yields simultaneously):

$$x_{u,max} = \frac{0.0035}{0.0035 + (0.87 f_y / E_s)} \times d$$

For **Fe415** ($f_y = 415$ N/mm², $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²):

$$\frac{x_{u,max}}{d} = \frac{0.0035}{0.0035 + (0.87 \times 415 / 200000)} = \frac{0.0035}{0.0035 + 0.001805} = \frac{0.0035}{0.005305}$$

$$\frac{x_{u,max}}{d} = 0.48 \quad \text{(matches IS 456)}$$

The **moment of resistance** with $x_u = x_{u,max}$:

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.446 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_{u,max} \times \left(d - 0.42 \, x_{u,max}\right)$$

Taking the lever arm as $(d - 0.42 x_{u,max})$ (centroid of rectangular block at $0.42 x_u$ from top):

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.446 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_{u,max} \times d \left(1 - 0.42 \, \frac{x_{u,max}}{d}\right)$$

Substituting $x_{u,max} = 0.48 d$ for Fe415:

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.446 \times 0.48 \times f_{ck} \times b \times d^2 \times (1 - 0.42 \times 0.48)$$

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.2141 \times 0.7984 \times f_{ck} \times b \times d^2$$

$$\boxed{M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2 \quad \text{(for Fe415)}}$$

This matches the SP-16 coefficient $Q_{lim} = 0.138 f_{ck}$ — **verified**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stress in Compression Steel $f_{sc}$

By **similar triangles** in the strain diagram (IS 456 Fig. 4):

$$\frac{\varepsilon_{sc}}{0.0035} = \frac{x_u - d'}{x_u}$$

Therefore:

$$\varepsilon_{sc} = 0.0035 \times \frac{x_u - d'}{x_u} = 0.0035 \times \left(1 - \frac{d'}{x_u}\right)$$

The stress in compression steel (assuming it has yielded in compression):

$$f_{sc} = E_s \times \varepsilon_{sc} = 2 \times 10^5 \times 0.0035 \times \left(1 - \frac{d'}{x_u}\right)$$

$$f_{sc} = 700 \times \left(1 - \frac{d'}{x_u}\right) \;\; \text{N/mm}^2$$

For the **limiting case** $x_u = x_{u,max}$:

$$f_{sc} = 700 \times \left(1 - \frac{d'}{x_{u,max}}\right) \;\; \text{N/mm}^2$$

This must be **capped at $f_{yd}$** to prevent over-stressing (since compression steel cannot physically exceed yield):

$$f_{sc,design} = \min\left[700 \left(1 - \frac{d'}{x_{u,max}}\right), \, f_{yd}\right]$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Area of Compression Steel $A_{sc}$

The **additional moment** $M_{u2}$ is resisted by the **couple** between $C_{sc}$ and $T_2$ acting at lever arm $(d - d')$:

$$M_{u2} = C_{sc} \times (d - d') = A_{sc} \times f_{sc} \times (d - d')$$

Solving for $A_{sc}$:

$$A_{sc} = \frac{M_{u2}}{f_{sc} \times (d - d')}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Area of Tension Steel $A_{st}$

The tension steel has **two parts**:

**(a) Steel for the singly reinforced balanced portion $M_{u1}$:**

Equating $C = T_1$ with $x_u = x_{u,max}$:

$$0.446 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_{u,max} = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st1}$$

$$A_{st1} = \frac{0.446 \times 0.48 \times f_{ck} \times b \times d}{0.87 \times f_y} = \frac{0.2141 \times f_{ck} \times b \times d}{0.87 \times f_y}$$

$$\boxed{A_{st1} = 0.246 \times \frac{f_{ck}}{f_y} \times b \times d \quad \text{(for Fe415)}}$$

Equivalently: $A_{st1} = (0.5 f_{ck}/f_y) x_{u,max} b = 0.5 (f_{ck}/f_y)(0.48 d) b$ — **both forms verified**.

**(b) Steel for the additional moment $M_{u2}$:**

The force in additional tension steel $T_2$ must equal $C_{sc}$ (couple equilibrium):

$$T_2 = A_{st2} \times 0.87 \, f_y = C_{sc} = A_{sc} \times f_{sc}$$

But more directly from the moment equilibrium of the couple:

$$M_{u2} = A_{st2} \times 0.87 \, f_y \times (d - d')$$

$$A_{st2} = \frac{M_{u2}}{0.87 \, f_y \times (d - d')} = \frac{M_{u2}}{f_{yd} \times (d - d')}$$

**Total tension steel:**

$$A_{st,total} = A_{st1} + A_{st2} = \frac{0.446 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_{u,max}}{0.87 \, f_y} + \frac{M_{u2}}{0.87 \, f_y \, (d - d')}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Design a doubly reinforced beam for $M_u = 380$ kN·m, $b = 250$ mm, $d = 550$ mm, $d' = 50$ mm, M20 concrete, Fe415 steel.

**Step 1 — Limiting moment of resistance:**

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times 550^2$$

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times 302500 = 208,725,000 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$M_{u,lim} = 208.73 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2 — Since $M_u = 380$ kN·m $> M_{u,lim} = 208.73$ kN·m, doubly reinforced section required.**

Additional moment:

$$M_{u2} = 380 - 208.73 = 171.27 \text{ kN·m} = 171.27 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Stress in compression steel:**

$$f_{sc} = 700 \times \left(1 - \frac{50}{0.48 \times 550}\right) = 700 \times \left(1 - \frac{50}{264}\right)$$

$$f_{sc} = 700 \times (1 - 0.1894) = 700 \times 0.8106 = 567.4 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Check cap: $f_{yd} = 415 / 1.15 = 360.87$ N/mm²

Since $567.4 > 360.87$, **cap at $f_{sc} = 360.87$ N/mm²** (compression steel has yielded).

**Step 4 — Area of compression steel:**

$$A_{sc} = \frac{171.27 \times 10^6}{360.87 \times (550 - 50)} = \frac{171.27 \times 10^6}{360.87 \times 500}$$

$$A_{sc} = \frac{171.27 \times 10^6}{180435} = 949.3 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Area of tension steel (first part):**

$$A_{st1} = 0.246 \times \frac{20}{415} \times 250 \times 550 = 0.246 \times 0.04819 \times 250 \times 550$$

$$A_{st1} = 1630.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Area of tension steel (second part):**

$$A_{st2} = \frac{171.27 \times 10^6}{360.87 \times 500} = 949.3 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \text{(same as } A_{sc}\text{, since both yield)}$$

**Step 7 — Total steel:**

$$A_{st} = A_{st1} + A_{st2} = 1630.6 + 949.3 = 2579.9 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 8 — Practical bar selection:**

$A_{st} = 2579.9$ mm² → Provide **6 bars of 25 mm Ø** ($6 \times 491 = 2946$ mm², or use 4 bars of 25 mm + 2 bars of 20 mm = $4 \times 491 + 2 \times 314 = 2592$ mm² ✓)

$A_{sc} = 949.3$ mm² → Provide **2 bars of 25 mm Ø** ($2 \times 491 = 982$ mm² ✓)

**Step 9 — Deflection check (IS 456 Annex C):** Span/depth ratio for continuous beam: permissible 26 (for Fe415), actual to be checked by $L/d$ ratio including modification factors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The key learning: even when $f_{sc}$ would be $> f_{yd}$ by the formula, we **always cap** at $f_{yd}$, because once the steel yields, it cannot carry more stress — additional strain just produces plastic deformation without stress increase. This is a **mandatory KTU exam point**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force Couple Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Applied Moment Mu] --> B{Is Mu greater than Mu lim?}
    B -- No --> C[Singly Reinforced Section]
    B -- Yes --> D[Doubly Reinforced Section]
    D --> E[Split Mu into Mu1 plus Mu2]
    E --> F[Mu1 carried by Concrete C plus Steel T1]
    E --> G[Mu2 carried by Steel Couple Csc and T2]
    F --> F1[Ast1 from rectangular stress block at xu max]
    G --> G1[Asc at depth d prime with stress fsc]
    G --> G2[Ast2 at depth d with stress fyd]
    F1 --> H[Total Ast equals Ast1 plus Ast2]
    G1 --> I[Total Asc equals Asc from Mu2]
    G2 --> H
    H --> J[Serviceability Check: Deflection and Cracking]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Final Detailing per IS 456 and IS 13920]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Strain and Stress Profile (LSM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Top Fiber Strain 0.0035] --> B[Strain varies linearly]
    B --> C[Strain at depth d prime equals 0.0035 times 1 minus d prime by xu]
    C --> D[fsc equals 700 times 1 minus d prime by xu]
    B --> E[Neutral Axis at xu max]
    E --> F[Strain at Tension Steel]
    F --> G[Steel yields at fyd]
    H[Compressive Force C] --> I[C equals 0.446 fck b xu]
    J[Tensile Force T] --> K[T equals 0.87 fy Ast]
    I --> L[Equilibrium C equals T]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Mu equals C times d minus 0.42 xu]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Design Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1: Assume b and d from architectural constraints] --> S2[Step 2: Calculate Mu lim using SP-16]
    S2 --> S3{Step 3: Mu less than or equal to Mu lim?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Design as singly reinforced section]
    S3 -- No --> S5[Step 4: Compute Mu2 equals Mu minus Mu lim]
    S5 --> S6[Step 5: Calculate fsc capped at fyd]
    S6 --> S7[Step 6: Asc equals Mu2 by fsc times d minus d prime]
    S7 --> S8[Step 7: Ast1 equals 0.5 fck by fy times xu max times b]
    S8 --> S9[Step 8: Ast2 equals Mu2 by fyd times d minus d prime]
    S9 --> S10[Step 9: Ast equals Ast1 plus Ast2]
    S10 --> S11[Step 10: Check minimum and maximum steel limits]
    S11 --> S12[Step 11: Detailing, anchorage, and shear check]
    S4 --> S12
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptation note:** The above diagrams use a *Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow* as a fallback for physical drawings, since detailed beam cross-section schematics (e.g., bar bending) are best drawn on graph paper in exam practice.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: When is a doubly reinforced section adopted? List the IS 456 clause references.**

**Model Answer:**

A doubly reinforced section is adopted in the following situations:

- When the **applied design moment $M_u$ exceeds the limiting moment of resistance $M_{u,lim}$** of a singly reinforced section of the given $b$ and $d$ (IS 456, Clause 5.1.2.2).
- When the **effective depth is restricted** by headroom or architectural considerations.
- When the beam is subjected to **reversal of moments** (e.g., continuous beams, seismic frames), in which case the top steel acts as tension reinforcement in the negative-moment zone (IS 13920).
- In **cantilever beams**, where the main steel is at the top.

*(3 marks: 1 for each valid point + IS clause reference)*

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Why is the stress in compression steel $f_{sc}$ capped at $f_{yd}$ in the design of doubly reinforced beams?**

**Model Answer:**

The stress $f_{sc}$ derived from strain compatibility is:

$$f_{sc} = 700 \left(1 - \frac{d'}{x_{u,max}}\right)$$

When $d'$ is very small (i.e., compression steel is near the top), the calculated $f_{sc}$ may exceed $f_{yd} = f_y/1.15$. However, **steel cannot sustain a stress greater than its yield stress in compression** — once it yields, additional strain produces plastic deformation without a corresponding increase in stress.

Therefore, IS 456 mandates that the design value of $f_{sc}$ be **limited to $f_{yd}$** to ensure a realistic estimate of the compression force. Capping also prevents overestimation of $M_{u2}$ resistance, ensuring a **conservative design**.

*(3 marks: 1 for formula context, 1 for yielding mechanism, 1 for design implication)*

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> - Forgetting to cap $f_{sc}$ at $f_{yd}$ → loses 1–2 marks.
> - Using $0.67 f_{ck}$ instead of $0.446 f_{ck}$ for stress block → loses 1 mark per step.
> - Mixing up $A_{st1}$ and $A_{st2}$ formulas → 2 marks lost.
> - Omitting check of $A_{st,max} = 0.04 bD$ and $A_{st,min}$ → 1 mark lost.
> - Forgetting to detail anchorage, curtailment, and side-face reinforcement in detailing questions.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`**

**Design a doubly reinforced concrete beam for the following data using LSM:**
- Factored moment, $M_u = 480$ kN·m
- Width, $b = 300$ mm
- Effective depth, $d = 600$ mm
- Cover to compression steel, $d' = 50$ mm
- Concrete grade: **M25** ($f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm²)
- Steel grade: **Fe415** ($f_y = 415$ N/mm²)

**Sub-parts:**

**(a)** Check whether a doubly reinforced section is necessary. [7 marks]

**(b)** Design the reinforcement (areas of $A_{st}$ and $A_{sc}$) and sketch the cross-section with bar details. [7 marks]

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a) — Necessity Check:**

For Fe415, $x_{u,max}/d = 0.48$ and $Q_{lim} = 0.138 f_{ck}$ (from SP-16, Fe415 + M25).

Limiting moment:

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 25 \times 300 \times 600^2 = 0.138 \times 25 \times 300 \times 360000$$

$$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 25 \times 108 \times 10^6 = 372.6 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 372.6 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $M_u = 480$ kN·m $> M_{u,lim} = 372.6$ kN·m, a **doubly reinforced section is required**. ✓ (1 mark for statement, 2 marks for $M_{u,lim}$ calculation, 2 marks for $Q_{lim}$ table reference, 2 marks for conclusion)

**Part (b) — Reinforcement Design:**

Additional moment:

$$M_{u2} = M_u - M_{u,lim} = 480 - 372.6 = 107.4 \text{ kN·m} = 107.4 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

Stress in compression steel:

$$f_{sc} = 700 \times \left(1 - \frac{50}{0.48 \times 600}\right) = 700 \times \left(1 - \frac{50}{288}\right) = 700 \times (1 - 0.1736) = 578.5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Cap at $f_{yd} = 415/1.15 = 360.87$ N/mm². So $f_{sc} = 360.87$ N/mm².

Area of compression steel:

$$A_{sc} = \frac{M_{u2}}{f_{sc} \times (d - d')} = \frac{107.4 \times 10^6}{360.87 \times (600 - 50)} = \frac{107.4 \times 10^6}{360.87 \times 550}$$

$$A_{sc} = \frac{107.4 \times 10^6}{198478.5} = 541.1 \text{ mm}^2$$

Area of tension steel — part 1 (singly reinforced balanced portion):

$$A_{st1} = 0.246 \times \frac{f_{ck}}{f_y} \times b \times d = 0.246 \times \frac{25}{415} \times 300 \times 600$$

$$A_{st1} = 0.246 \times 0.06024 \times 300 \times 600 = 2667.3 \text{ mm}^2$$

Area of tension steel — part 2 (additional):

$$A_{st2} = \frac{M_{u2}}{f_{yd} \times (d - d')} = \frac{107.4 \times 10^6}{360.87 \times 550} = 541.1 \text{ mm}^2$$

Total tension steel:

$$A_{st} = A_{st1} + A_{st2} = 2667.3 + 541.1 = 3208.4 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Bar selection:**

- $A_{st} = 3208.4$ mm² → Provide **4 bars of 32 mm Ø + 2 bars of 16 mm Ø** $= 4 \times 804 + 2 \times 201 = 3216 + 402 = 3618$ mm² ✓ (or 6 bars of 28 mm Ø = 6 × 615.75 = 3694.5 mm²)
- $A_{sc} = 541.1$ mm² → Provide **2 bars of 20 mm Ø** $= 2 \times 314 = 628$ mm² ✓ (or 3 bars of 16 mm Ø = 603 mm²)

**Steel limits check (Clause 26.5.1.1):**

- $A_{st,max} = 0.04 \times 300 \times D$; assuming $D = 650$ mm: $A_{st,max} = 0.04 \times 300 \times 650 = 7800$ mm² ✓
- $A_{st,min} = 0.85 \times 300 \times 600 / 415 = 368.7$ mm² ✓
- $A_{sc,min} = 0.0012 \times 300 \times 650 = 234$ mm² ✓

(2 marks for $A_{st1}$, 1 mark for $A_{st2}$, 1 mark for $A_{sc}$, 1 mark for $f_{sc}$ cap, 1 mark for bar selection, 1 mark for limits check)

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**A rectangular beam of $b = 230$ mm, $d = 500$ mm is subjected to a factored moment of $M_u = 250$ kN·m. Using M20 concrete and Fe415 steel:**

**(a)** Check whether the section is to be designed as singly or doubly reinforced. If doubly reinforced, find $A_{st}$ and $A_{sc}$. [7 marks]

**(b)** The beam is part of a continuous frame and is also subjected to a *negative* moment of $180$ kN·m at the support. Provide the reinforcement detailing for the negative moment region. Comment on why top steel is required in this case. [7 marks]

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a) — Type of Section & Steel Areas:**

For Fe415, $Q_{lim} = 0.138 f_{ck} = 0.138 \times 20 = 2.76$ N/mm².

$$M_{u,lim} = 2.76 \times 230 \times 500^2 = 2.76 \times 230 \times 250000 = 158,700,000 \text{ N·mm} = 158.7 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $M_u = 250$ kN·m $> M_{u,lim} = 158.7$ kN·m, design as **doubly reinforced**.

Additional moment: $M_{u2} = 250 - 158.7 = 91.3$ kN·m = $91.3 \times 10^6$ N·mm.

Assuming $d' = 50$ mm:

$$f_{sc} = 700 \times \left(1 - \frac{50}{0.48 \times 500}\right) = 700 \times \left(1 - \frac{50}{240}\right) = 700 \times (1 - 0.2083) = 700 \times 0.7917 = 554.2 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Cap at $f_{yd} = 360.87$ N/mm² → use $f_{sc} = 360.87$ N/mm².

$$A_{sc} = \frac{91.3 \times 10^6}{360.87 \times (500 - 50)} = \frac{91.3 \times 10^6}{360.87 \times 450} = \frac{91.3 \times 10^6}{162391.5} = 562.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{st1} = 0.246 \times \frac{20}{415} \times 230 \times 500 = 0.246 \times 0.04819 \times 230 \times 500 = 1362.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{st2} = \frac{91.3 \times 10^6}{360.87 \times 450} = 562.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{st} = A_{st1} + A_{st2} = 1362.6 + 562.2 = 1924.8 \text{ mm}^2$$

(7 marks: 2 for $M_{u,lim}$, 1 for type decision, 1 for $f_{sc}$, 1 for $A_{sc}$, 1 for $A_{st1}$, 1 for $A_{st}$)

**Part (b) — Negative Moment Region Detailing:**

For $M_u = 180$ kN·m at the support (top steel is the *tension* steel here):

$$A_{st,top} = \frac{M_u}{0.87 \times f_y \times (d - d')}$$

Assuming the support section is **singly reinforced** (since $M_{u,lim} = 158.7$ kN·m, and $180 > 158.7$, it is *slightly* doubly reinforced; treat as doubly):

$$M_{u2,support} = 180 - 158.7 = 21.3 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$A_{st,top,1} = 1362.6 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ (singly reinforced portion)}$$

$$A_{st,top,2} = \frac{21.3 \times 10^6}{360.87 \times 450} = 131.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{st,top,total} = 1362.6 + 131.2 = 1493.8 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ (top steel)}$$

**Why top steel is required:** The negative moment at the continuous support causes **tension at the top fiber** of the beam. Without top steel, the unreinforced concrete at the top would crack under tension, leading to brittle failure. Top steel is therefore the *primary* tension reinforcement in this region. (Per IS 13920, in seismic zones, additional *capacity design* rules govern joint reinforcement.)

(7 marks: 2 for top steel area, 2 for negative moment logic, 2 for detailing, 1 for IS 13920 reference)

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL MARKS-LOSS AREAS IN DOUBLY REINFORCED BEAM PROBLEMS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting $f_{sc}$ cap at $f_{yd}$:** Many students blindly substitute $f_{sc} = 700(1 - d'/x_{u,max})$ without checking the cap. This **overestimates** $M_{u2}$ resistance, leading to under-design of steel. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Wrong stress block coefficient:** Using $0.67 f_{ck}$ (the peak stress) instead of $0.446 f_{ck}$ (the average for the rectangular block) in equilibrium equations. **Penalty: 1 mark per equation.**
> 3. **Confusing $A_{st1}$ and $A_{st2}$:** $A_{st1}$ is from $M_{u1}$ (concrete-steel couple); $A_{st2}$ is from $M_{u2}$ (steel couple). Mixing them gives wrong $A_{st}$. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Ignoring $A_{st,max} = 0.04 bD$:** IS 456 limits steel to prevent brittle compression failure. Exceeding it requires a redesign with a larger section. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 5. **No deflection/cracking check:** For doubly reinforced beams in continuous frames, span/depth ratio must be verified per IS 456 Annex C. **Penalty: 1 mark in Part B.**
> 6. **Wrong partial safety factor:** Using $\gamma_m = 1.0$ (Working Stress) or $\gamma_m = 1.5$ (concrete) where 1.15 is required for steel. **Penalty: 1 mark.**

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize for KTU Exam — Doubly Reinforced Beams:**
>
> - **Adopt doubly reinforced when** $M_u > M_{u,lim}$ (singly reinforced balanced moment).
> - **Split:** $M_u = M_{u1} + M_{u2}$; $M_{u1}$ by singly balanced section, $M_{u2}$ by steel couple.
> - **$M_{u,lim}$ formula:** $M_{u,lim} = 0.36 (x_{u,max}/d) [1 - 0.42 (x_{u,max}/d)] f_{ck} b d^2$.
> - **$x_{u,max}/d$:** 0.48 (Fe415), 0.46 (Fe500), 0.53 (Fe250).
> - **$f_{sc} = 700 (1 - d'/x_{u,max})$** ≤ $f_{yd} = f_y/1.15$.
> - **$A_{st1} = 0.5 (f_{ck}/f_y) x_{u,max} b$** (or $0.246 f_{ck}/f_y \times b \times d$ for Fe415).
> - **$A_{st2} = M_{u2} / [f_{yd} (d - d')]$** and **$A_{sc} = M_{u2} / [f_{sc} (d - d')]$**.
> - **Total $A_{st} = A_{st1} + A_{st2}$.**
> - **Limits (Cl. 26.5.1.1):** $A_{st} \leq 0.04 bD$; $A_{st,min} = 0.85 bd/f_y$ (Fe415).
> - **Top steel = compression steel in midspan, but = tension steel at support (negative moment zones).**
> - **Always cap $f_{sc}$ at $f_{yd}$** — yields cap is non-negotiable.
> - **Detailing:** Use 8 mm side-face stirrups at 300 mm c/c for beams with $D > 750$ mm; main stirrups as per shear design.
> - **IS 13920 (Ductile Design):** Top steel at joints ≥ $0.5 \times$ bottom steel at midspan; ensure $A_{sc,min} = 0.0012 bD$ for confining reinforcement.
> - **Cracking check (Cl. 43):** Crack width ≤ 0.3 mm for normal exposure.
> - **Deflection (Annex C):** Permissible $L/d$ for simply supported = 20, continuous = 26 (Fe415); modify by steel stress, area, and support conditions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flanged Beams - Theory and Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — FLANGED BEAMS: THEORY AND PROBLEMS

## 1. Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Flanged Beams (T-Beams and L-Beams)** are reinforced concrete members cast **monolithically with the slab**, where a portion of the slab acts integrally with the beam to resist compression. The resulting cross-section resembles the letter **"T"** (interior beams) or **"L"** (edge beams).

### Formal Academic Definition (IS 456:2000, Clause 23.1)

A **flanged beam** is a beam in which the concrete in the slab on one or both sides of the web acts as a **compression flange**, while the web below the slab resists shear and contributes to compression when the neutral axis falls in the web. The portion of the slab considered effective in compression is termed the **Effective Flange Width ($b_f$)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Capital Letter 'T' as a Bridge Girder"

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Intuition:** Imagine a wooden plank standing on its narrow edge — it bends easily. Now lay a wide flat board (a "tabletop") on top of that plank and nail them together. The combined section is **vastly stiffer** because the flat board on top resists compression over a wide area, while the plank below carries the tension via steel bars. This "I-shape" or "T-shape" is exactly what happens in a building: the **slab = flange** (top), the **beam below the slab = web**, and the **steel bars = tension reinforcement**. The slab and beam, cast together as one monolithic pour, behave as a single structural unit — the **T-beam**.

### Key Categories of Flanged Beams

| Type | When it Occurs | Compression Flange |
|------|----------------|--------------------|
| **T-Beam** | Interior beam — slab exists on **both sides** of web | Both overhangs active |
| **L-Beam** | Edge/Perimeter beam — slab exists on **one side** of web | Only one overhang active |

### Why Flanged Beams Matter in Practice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Ignoring the flange contribution leads to **underestimation of moment of resistance by 30–60%**, causing uneconomical and sometimes unsafe designs. The slab contribution is **not decorative** — it is structurally load-bearing in positive (sagging) moment regions.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Effective Flange Width ($b_f$) — IS 456:2000, Clause 23.1.2

Because the compressive stress in the flange is **not uniform** across the entire slab width (it peaks above the web and decays with distance), IS 456 limits the width of slab assumed to participate in compression.

> [!NOTE]
> **Effective flange width** is the width of slab (combined with the web) over which the **uniform compressive stress block** is assumed to act such that the total compressive force equals the actual non-uniform distribution.

#### Criteria for T-Beams (Clause 23.1.2.a)

$$b_f = \text{Least of:}$$

1. $\dfrac{l_0}{6} + b_w + 6 D_f$
2. Actual width of flange (rib spacing)
3. Center-to-center distance of adjacent ribs

#### Criteria for L-Beams (Clause 23.1.2.b)

$$b_f = \text{Least of:}$$

1. $\dfrac{l_0}{12} + b_w + 3 D_f$
2. Actual width of flange
3. $\dfrac{b}{2} + b_w$  *(b = actual flange width)*

#### Criteria for Isolated T-Beams (Clause 23.1.2.c)

When the flange is isolated from the slab and acts purely as a compression flange:

$$b_f = \dfrac{l_0 \cdot 0.5 \cdot b}{(l_0/b) + 4} + b_w$$

where the **flange thickness $D_f \geq b_w$** and $b$ is the actual flange width.

### 2.2 Analysis Approach — Limit State Method (LSM)

The behaviour of a flanged beam in flexure depends on the **location of the Neutral Axis (NA)**, which in turn depends on the **amount of tension steel ($A_{st}$)**.

#### Case I — NA in the Flange ($x_u \leq D_f$)

- The section behaves as a **rectangular beam of width $b_f$**.
- Standard rectangular beam equations apply **without modification**.
- This occurs in **lightly reinforced** flanged beams.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Test:** Assume NA is in flange. Calculate $x_u$ using $T = C$ with width $b_f$. If $x_u \leq D_f$, assumption is correct.

#### Case II — NA in the Web ($x_u > D_f$)

The compression zone has **two distinct parts**:

- **Part 1 (Overhanging Flange):** Full flange width minus web width, full flange thickness.
- **Part 2 (Web in Compression):** Web width only, depth $x_u$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This case arises in **moderately to heavily reinforced** flanged beams and is the **most commonly tested** scenario in KTU examinations.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Validity |
|---|----------|---------|-------|----------|
| 1 | Effective flange width (T-beam) | $b_f = \min\!\left[\tfrac{l_0}{6}+b_w+6D_f,\; b,\; c/c\right]$ | mm | Clause 23.1.2.a |
| 2 | Effective flange width (L-beam) | $b_f = \min\!\left[\tfrac{l_0}{12}+b_w+3D_f,\; b,\; \tfrac{b}{2}+b_w\right]$ | mm | Clause 23.1.2.b |
| 3 | Tension in steel | $T = 0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}$ | N | All sections |
| 4 | Compression in overhanging flange | $C_1 = 0.45\, f_{ck}\, (b_f - b_w)\, D_f$ | N | NA in web |
| 5 | Compression in web | $C_2 = 0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_w\, x_u$ | N | NA in web |
| 6 | Equilibrium (NA in web) | $C_1 + C_2 = T$ | N | Solves $x_u$ |
| 7 | NA in flange (rectangular) | $x_u = \dfrac{0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}}{0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_f}$ | mm | $x_u \leq D_f$ |
| 8 | Limiting NA depth (Fe 500) | $x_{u,\max} = 0.48\, d$ | mm | LSM |
| 9 | Limiting NA depth (Fe 415) | $x_{u,\max} = 0.53\, d$ | mm | LSM |
| 10 | Moment (NA in web) | $M_u = C_1\!\left(d - \tfrac{D_f}{2}\right) + C_2\!\left(d - \tfrac{x_u}{2}\right)$ | N·mm | Taking moments about $A_{st}$ |
| 11 | Moment (NA in flange) | $M_u = 0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}\, \left(d - \dfrac{0.42\, x_u}{1}\right)$ | N·mm | Rectangular case |
| 12 | Lever arm | $z = d - \dfrac{0.42\, x_u}{1}$ | mm | Approximate |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Students often forget that $0.36\, f_{ck}$ in Case II uses the **web width $b_w$**, **not** $b_f$. Mixing these up is the **#1 reason for full-mark deductions**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flanged beam analysis is essential in the design of:

- **Building floor systems** (beam-slab construction)
- **Bridge girders** with cast-in-place deck
- **Ribbed/Coffered slabs**
- **Composite deck-slab systems** in steel-concrete structures

The economic benefit is substantial: a T-beam can carry **2–4 times the moment** of an equivalent rectangular beam of width $b_w$, with only marginal increase in self-weight.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Moment of Resistance When NA Lies in the Web

Consider a T-section with the neutral axis at depth $x_u > D_f$ from the top. The stress block consists of:

**Step 1 — Identify the two compression blocks.**

The total compressive force in concrete is the sum of:
- Force in **overhanging flange** ($b_f - b_w$ wide, $D_f$ thick): $C_1$
- Force in **web portion in compression** ($b_w$ wide, $x_u$ thick): $C_2$

**Step 2 — Express $C_1$ and $C_2$ from the rectangular stress block.**

The IS 456 rectangular parabolic-rectangular stress block gives a **uniform stress of $0.446\, f_{ck}$** (rounded to **$0.45\, f_{ck}$**) over the compression zone, with a **depth factor of $0.42\, x$** for the equivalent rectangular block. The web compression force is governed by the strain compatibility and is given by $0.36\, f_{ck} b_w x_u$.

$$C_1 = 0.45\, f_{ck}\, (b_f - b_w)\, D_f$$

$$C_2 = 0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_w\, x_u$$

**Step 3 — Apply horizontal equilibrium ($C = T$).**

$$0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_w\, x_u + 0.45\, f_{ck}\, (b_f - b_w)\, D_f = 0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the neutral axis depth $x_u$.**

$$x_u = \dfrac{0.87\, f_y\, A_{st} - 0.45\, f_{ck}\, (b_f - b_w)\, D_f}{0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_w}$$

**Step 5 — Verify NA location and section ductility.**

- If $x_u > D_f$ → assumption correct.
- If $x_u \leq D_f$ → redo as rectangular beam.
- If $x_u > x_{u,\max}$ → section is **over-reinforced**, redesign with more depth or higher concrete grade.

**Step 6 — Compute $M_u$ by taking moments of compression forces about the centroid of tension steel.**

$$M_u = C_1 \cdot \left(d - \dfrac{D_f}{2}\right) + C_2 \cdot \left(d - \dfrac{x_u}{2}\right)$$

Substituting $C_1$ and $C_2$:

$$\boxed{M_u = 0.45\, f_{ck}\, (b_f - b_w)\, D_f \left(d - \dfrac{D_f}{2}\right) + 0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_w\, x_u \left(d - \dfrac{x_u}{2}\right)}$$

### 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU-Model Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A T-beam has the following data:
> - Effective span: $l_0 = 8$ m
> - Flange width: $b = 1500$ mm (center-to-center of ribs)
> - Flange thickness: $D_f = 120$ mm
> - Web width: $b_w = 300$ mm
> - Effective depth: $d = 650$ mm
> - Tension steel: $A_{st} = 5500$ mm² (Fe 500)
> - Concrete: M 25 ($f_{ck} = 25$ MPa, $f_y = 500$ MPa)
>
> **Find:** Type of section and ultimate moment of resistance by **LSM**.

#### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the Effective Flange Width $b_f$ (T-beam, Clause 23.1.2.a).**

$$\dfrac{l_0}{6} + b_w + 6D_f = \dfrac{8000}{6} + 300 + 6(120) = 1333.33 + 300 + 720 = 2353.33 \text{ mm}$$

Comparing the three criteria:

- Criterion 1: $2353.33$ mm
- Criterion 2 (actual width): $1500$ mm ✓ **governs**
- Criterion 3: $1500$ mm

Therefore:

$$b_f = 1500 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Assume NA in the web and solve for $x_u$.**

Apply horizontal equilibrium $C_1 + C_2 = T$:

$$0.36 \times 25 \times 300 \times x_u + 0.45 \times 25 \times (1500 - 300) \times 120 = 0.87 \times 500 \times 5500$$

$$2700\, x_u + 1\,620\,000 = 2\,392\,500$$

$$2700\, x_u = 772\,500$$

$$x_u = \dfrac{772\,500}{2700} = 286.11 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Verify assumptions.**

- NA in web? $x_u = 286.11 \text{ mm} > D_f = 120$ mm ✓
- Under-reinforced? $x_{u,\max} = 0.48\, d = 0.48 \times 650 = 312$ mm
- $x_u = 286.11 < 312$ ✓ Section is **under-reinforced and safe**.

**Step 4 — Calculate $C_1$, $C_2$, and $M_u$.**

$$C_1 = 0.45 \times 25 \times (1500 - 300) \times 120 = 1\,620\,000 \text{ N}$$

$$C_2 = 0.36 \times 25 \times 300 \times 286.11 = 772\,497 \text{ N}$$

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_1 + C_2 = 1\,620\,000 + 772\,497 = 2\,392\,497 \text{ N} \approx T = 2\,392\,500 \text{ N} \;\; \checkmark$$

Taking moments of compression forces about the centroid of $A_{st}$:

$$M_u = C_1\!\left(d - \dfrac{D_f}{2}\right) + C_2\!\left(d - \dfrac{x_u}{2}\right)$$

$$M_u = 1\,620\,000 \times \left(650 - \dfrac{120}{2}\right) + 772\,497 \times \left(650 - \dfrac{286.11}{2}\right)$$

$$M_u = 1\,620\,000 \times 590 + 772\,497 \times 506.94$$

$$M_u = 955\,800\,000 + 391\,631\,000 = 1\,347\,431\,000 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\boxed{M_u \approx 1347.4 \text{ kN·m}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification (Alternative Method):** Using $M_u = T \cdot z$ where $z$ is the lever arm from the resultant compression to the steel centroid:
> Lever arm from top to resultant compression $y = \dfrac{C_1(D_f/2) + C_2(x_u/2)}{C_1+C_2} = \dfrac{1\,620\,000(60) + 772\,497(143.06)}{2\,392\,497} = 86.79$ mm
> $z = d - y = 650 - 86.79 = 563.21$ mm
> $M_u = 2\,392\,500 \times 563.21 = 1\,347\,600\,000$ N·mm $\approx 1347.6$ kN·m ✓ (matches)

### 3.3 Quick-Reference: NA Location Decision Matrix

| Calculated $x_u$ | Section Type | Beam Behaviour | Use Formula |
|------------------|--------------|----------------|-------------|
| $x_u \leq D_f$ | Flanged beam (NA in flange) | Behaves as **rectangular beam** of width $b_f$ | Rectangular $b_f$ equations |
| $D_f < x_u \leq x_{u,\max}$ | Flanged beam (NA in web) | **Under-reinforced**, full T-action | Two-block $M_u$ equation |
| $x_u > x_{u,\max}$ | **Over-reinforced** | Brittle failure — **NOT allowed** | Redesign section |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — LSM Design Procedure for Flanged Beams

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Flanged Beam Data b_f, D_f, b_w, d, A_st, f_ck, f_y"] --> B["Compute b_f per IS 456 Clause 23.1.2 Least of three criteria"]
    B --> C{"Assume NA in web x_u > D_f?"}
    C -- "Yes (Heavy reinforcement)" --> D["Solve 0.36 f_ck b_w x_u + 0.45 f_ck b_f minus b_w D_f = 0.87 f_y A_st"]
    D --> E["Compute x_u from equilibrium"]
    E --> F{"Check 1: x_u greater than D_f?"}
    F -- "No" --> G["Reanalyze as rectangular beam of width b_f"]
    F -- "Yes" --> H{"Check 2: x_u less than x_u_max 0.48d for Fe500?"}
    H -- "No Over-reinforced" --> I["Redesign: increase d or use higher f_ck"]
    H -- "Yes Under-reinforced" --> J["Compute C1 = 0.45 f_ck b_f minus b_w D_f"]
    J --> K["Compute C2 = 0.36 f_ck b_w x_u"]
    K --> L["Compute M_u = C1 times d minus D_f over 2 plus C2 times d minus x_u over 2"]
    L --> M["OUTPUT: M_u in kN m"]
    C -- "No Light reinforcement" --> N["Use rectangular beam equations with width b_f"]
    N --> O["x_u = 0.87 f_y A_st divided by 0.36 f_ck b_f"]
    O --> P{"x_u less than or equal to D_f?"}
    P -- "Yes" --> Q["M_u = 0.87 f_y A_st times d minus 0.42 x_u"]
    P -- "No" --> D
    Q --> M
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress Distribution in Flanged Beam (NA in Web)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:flange["FLANGE Top b_f wide D_f thick"]:3
    space:3
    block:web_top["Web b_w wide Stress = 0.45 f_ck Depth = 0.42 x_u"]:1
    space:1
    block:na["NEUTRAL AXIS at x_u from top"]:1
    space:3
    block:tension["Tension steel A_st at d Stress = 0.87 f_y"]:3
    space:3
    block:c1["C1 Force in overhanging flange 0.45 f_ck times b_f minus b_w times D_f Acts at D_f/2 from top"]:1
    block:c2["C2 Force in web compression 0.36 f_ck times b_w times x_u Acts at x_u/2 from top"]:1
    block:tforce["T Tension in steel 0.87 f_y times A_st Acts at d from top"]:1
    c1 --> c2 --> tforce
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram maps the **two-zone compression model** and resultant force locations used in the moment-of-resistance calculation. This is the conceptual model IS 456 prescribes for the **NA-in-web** case.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define **effective flange width** for a T-beam as per IS 456:2000. State the criteria for its determination.

**Model Answer:**

> Effective flange width $b_f$ is the width of the slab assumed to act integrally with the beam web in resisting compression. For a T-beam (IS 456, Clause 23.1.2.a), $b_f$ is the **least** of:
> 1. $(l_0/6) + b_w + 6D_f$
> 2. Actual width of flange (rib spacing)
> 3. Center-to-center distance of adjacent ribs

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

When does the **neutral axis lie in the web** of a flanged beam? What is the consequence if the limiting moment of resistance is exceeded?

**Model Answer:**

> The NA lies in the web when the calculated $x_u > D_f$ (i.e., the section is moderately or heavily reinforced). If $x_u$ exceeds $x_{u,\max} = 0.48d$ (Fe 500), the section becomes **over-reinforced** and is disallowed by IS 456, as failure becomes sudden and brittle (no warning). The section must be redesigned by increasing $d$, using higher $f_{ck}$, or compressing the tension steel area.

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** The most common mistakes that lead to **full-mark deductions** are:
> 1. Using **$b_f$ in the web compression term** ($0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_f\, x_u$) instead of $b_w$ — this is mathematically wrong.
> 2. Forgetting to **verify NA location** after computing $x_u$.
> 3. Using $x_{u,\max} = 0.53d$ for **Fe 500** (use **0.48d**).
> 4. Mixing up **lever-arm calculation** — moment should be taken about the **centroid of $A_{st}$**, not the top fibre.
> 5. **Unit slip-ups** between N and kN, mm and m.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

A T-beam has the following properties:
- $b_f = 1400$ mm, $D_f = 100$ mm, $b_w = 300$ mm
- $d = 550$ mm, $A_{st} = 4000$ mm²
- $f_{ck} = 20$ MPa, $f_y = 500$ MPa

Determine the **type of section** and compute the **ultimate moment of resistance** by LSM.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assume NA in web.** Apply equilibrium $C_1 + C_2 = T$:

$$0.36 \times 20 \times 300 \times x_u + 0.45 \times 20 \times (1400 - 300) \times 100 = 0.87 \times 500 \times 4000$$

$$2160\, x_u + 990\,000 = 1\,740\,000$$

$$x_u = \dfrac{750\,000}{2160} = 347.22 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Check NA location:** $x_u = 347.22 > D_f = 100$ mm ✓ (NA in web)

**Step 3 — Check ductility:** $x_{u,\max} = 0.48 \times 550 = 264$ mm.
Since $x_u = 347.22 > 264$ → **Section is over-reinforced**.

For a safe design, the moment of resistance is taken at the **limiting case** $x_u = x_{u,\max} = 264$ mm.

**Step 4 — Recompute $M_u$ at $x_u = 264$ mm:**

$$C_1 = 0.45 \times 20 \times 1100 \times 100 = 990\,000 \text{ N}$$

$$C_2 = 0.36 \times 20 \times 300 \times 264 = 570\,240 \text{ N}$$

$$M_u = 990\,000 \times (550 - 50) + 570\,240 \times (550 - 132)$$

$$M_u = 990\,000 \times 500 + 570\,240 \times 418$$

$$M_u = 495\,000\,000 + 238\,360\,000 = 733\,360\,000 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\boxed{M_{u,\text{lim}} \approx 733.36 \text{ kN·m}}$$

> **Conclusion:** The provided $A_{st}$ exceeds the limiting value; section must be redesigned.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

An **L-beam** has flange dimensions: $b = 900$ mm, $D_f = 110$ mm. The web is $b_w = 280$ mm wide and effective depth $d = 520$ mm. Reinforcement: $A_{st} = 3500$ mm² (Fe 415). Concrete: M 20. Effective span = 6 m. Calculate the **ultimate moment of resistance**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Effective flange width for L-beam (Clause 23.1.2.b):**

- $l_0/12 + b_w + 3D_f = 6000/12 + 280 + 3(110) = 500 + 280 + 330 = 1110$ mm
- Actual flange: $900$ mm ✓ **governs**
- $b/2 + b_w = 450 + 280 = 730$ mm (not governing)

$b_f = 900$ mm

**Step 2 — Assume NA in web and solve:**

$$0.36 \times 20 \times 280 \times x_u + 0.45 \times 20 \times (900 - 280) \times 110 = 0.87 \times 415 \times 3500$$

$$2016\, x_u + 612\,700 = 1\,263\,675$$

$$x_u = \dfrac{650\,975}{2016} = 322.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Verify:** $x_u = 322.9 > D_f = 110$ ✓ (NA in web).

$x_{u,\max} = 0.53d = 0.53 \times 520 = 275.6$ mm.
Since $x_u = 322.9 > 275.6$ → **Over-reinforced** → use $x_u = 275.6$ mm.

**Step 4 — Compute $M_u$ at limiting $x_u$:**

$$C_1 = 0.45 \times 20 \times 620 \times 110 = 613\,800 \text{ N}$$

$$C_2 = 0.36 \times 20 \times 280 \times 275.6 = 555\,525 \text{ N}$$

$$M_u = 613\,800 \times (520 - 55) + 555\,525 \times (520 - 137.8)$$

$$M_u = 613\,800 \times 465 + 555\,525 \times 382.2$$

$$M_u = 285\,417\,000 + 212\,312\,000 = 497\,729\,000 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\boxed{M_{u,\text{lim}} \approx 497.73 \text{ kN·m}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Derive the **expression for the ultimate moment of resistance** of a flanged beam when the **neutral axis lies in the web**, clearly stating all assumptions per IS 456:2000.

**Model Solution:**

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.1** above. The final expression is:

$$M_u = 0.45\, f_{ck}\, (b_f - b_w)\, D_f \left(d - \dfrac{D_f}{2}\right) + 0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_w\, x_u \left(d - \dfrac{x_u}{2}\right)$$

where

$$x_u = \dfrac{0.87\, f_y\, A_{st} - 0.45\, f_{ck}\, (b_f - b_w)\, D_f}{0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_w}$$

**Assumptions (per IS 456, Clause 23.1):**

1. Maximum strain in concrete at the extreme compression fibre = **0.0035**.
2. Maximum strain in tension steel = **$f_y/(1.15 E_s) + 0.002$** (Fe 500) with limiting value ≥ 0.004.
3. Rectangular stress block: uniform stress **$0.446\, f_{ck} \approx 0.45\, f_{ck}$** over depth **$0.42\, x_u$**.
4. Perfect bond between concrete and steel.
5. Tension in concrete is ignored.
6. Plane sections remain plane (Bernoulli's hypothesis).

---

**(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

A T-beam has $b_f = 1200$ mm, $D_f = 100$ mm, $b_w = 250$ mm, $d = 500$ mm, $A_{st} = 2500$ mm². Concrete: M 25, Steel: Fe 500. Find the **ultimate moment of resistance** and the **maximum factored moment** the section can carry safely.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assume NA in web:**

$$0.36 \times 25 \times 250 \times x_u + 0.45 \times 25 \times (1200 - 250) \times 100 = 0.87 \times 500 \times 2500$$

$$2250\, x_u + 1\,068\,750 = 1\,087\,500$$

$$x_u = \dfrac{18\,750}{2250} = 8.33 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Verify NA location:** $x_u = 8.33 < D_f = 100$ → **NA lies in the flange** (assumption was wrong).

**Step 3 — Reanalyze as a rectangular beam of width $b_f = 1200$ mm:**

$$x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \times 500 \times 2500}{0.36 \times 25 \times 1200} = \dfrac{1\,087\,500}{10\,800} = 100.69 \text{ mm}$$

Since $x_u = 100.69 \text{ mm} \approx D_f = 100$ mm → beam is at the **boundary** (NA exactly at flange-web interface).

For practical purposes, treat as rectangular:

$$M_u = 0.87 \times 500 \times 2500 \times \left(500 - 0.42 \times 100.69\right)$$

$$M_u = 1\,087\,500 \times (500 - 42.29) = 1\,087\,500 \times 457.71$$

$$M_u = 497\,760\,000 \text{ N·mm} \approx 497.76 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$\boxed{M_u \approx 497.76 \text{ kN·m}}$$

> **Maximum safe factored moment** the section can carry = $M_u \approx 497.76$ kN·m.

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Flanged beams** form due to **monolithic slab-beam construction**; the slab acts as the compression flange.
- [x] **T-beams** occur in interior positions; **L-beams** at edges.
- [x] **Effective flange width $b_f$** is the **least** of three criteria (IS 456, Clause 23.1.2).
- [x] For **T-beams**: $b_f = \min\!\left[\frac{l_0}{6}+b_w+6D_f,\; \text{actual width}\right]$.
- [x] For **L-beams**: $b_f = \min\!\left[\frac{l_0}{12}+b_w+3D_f,\; \text{actual width},\; \frac{b}{2}+b_w\right]$.
- [x] **NA in flange** → use rectangular beam formulas with $b = b_f$.
- [x] **NA in web** → two-block compression model with $C_1$ and $C_2$.
- [x] $C_1$ uses flange overhang $(b_f - b_w)$ × $D_f$ × $0.45\, f_{ck}$.
- [x] $C_2$ uses web width $b_w$ × $x_u$ × $0.36\, f_{ck}$ — **NOT $b_f$**.
- [x] Equilibrium: $C_1 + C_2 = 0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}$.
- [x] $x_{u,\max} = 0.48d$ for **Fe 500**, $0.53d$ for **Fe 415**.
- [x] Always **verify NA location** after computing $x_u$.
- [x] $M_u = C_1(d - D_f/2) + C_2(d - x_u/2)$ when NA is in web.
- [x] Lever arm $z = d - 0.42\, x_u$ (approximate, rectangular case).
- [x] **Over-reinforced sections** must be redesigned; IS 456 disallows them.
- [x] **Working Stress Method** for flanged beams (Clause G-2.1 of IS 456 Annex G) is rarely asked in KTU 2024 — focus on **LSM**.
- [x] Flanged beam design yields **30–60% higher $M_u$** than a rectangular beam of width $b_w$ — hence economical.
- [x] Always **convert units consistently** (N, mm, MPa → kN·m for final answer).
