# MODULE 2 — FLANGED BEAMS: THEORY AND PROBLEMS

## 1. Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Flanged Beams (T-Beams and L-Beams)** are reinforced concrete members cast **monolithically with the slab**, where a portion of the slab acts integrally with the beam to resist compression. The resulting cross-section resembles the letter **"T"** (interior beams) or **"L"** (edge beams).

### Formal Academic Definition (IS 456:2000, Clause 23.1)

A **flanged beam** is a beam in which the concrete in the slab on one or both sides of the web acts as a **compression flange**, while the web below the slab resists shear and contributes to compression when the neutral axis falls in the web. The portion of the slab considered effective in compression is termed the **Effective Flange Width ($b_f$)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Capital Letter 'T' as a Bridge Girder"

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Intuition:** Imagine a wooden plank standing on its narrow edge — it bends easily. Now lay a wide flat board (a "tabletop") on top of that plank and nail them together. The combined section is **vastly stiffer** because the flat board on top resists compression over a wide area, while the plank below carries the tension via steel bars. This "I-shape" or "T-shape" is exactly what happens in a building: the **slab = flange** (top), the **beam below the slab = web**, and the **steel bars = tension reinforcement**. The slab and beam, cast together as one monolithic pour, behave as a single structural unit — the **T-beam**.

### Key Categories of Flanged Beams

| Type | When it Occurs | Compression Flange |
|------|----------------|--------------------|
| **T-Beam** | Interior beam — slab exists on **both sides** of web | Both overhangs active |
| **L-Beam** | Edge/Perimeter beam — slab exists on **one side** of web | Only one overhang active |

### Why Flanged Beams Matter in Practice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Ignoring the flange contribution leads to **underestimation of moment of resistance by 30–60%**, causing uneconomical and sometimes unsafe designs. The slab contribution is **not decorative** — it is structurally load-bearing in positive (sagging) moment regions.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Effective Flange Width ($b_f$) — IS 456:2000, Clause 23.1.2

Because the compressive stress in the flange is **not uniform** across the entire slab width (it peaks above the web and decays with distance), IS 456 limits the width of slab assumed to participate in compression.

> [!NOTE]
> **Effective flange width** is the width of slab (combined with the web) over which the **uniform compressive stress block** is assumed to act such that the total compressive force equals the actual non-uniform distribution.

#### Criteria for T-Beams (Clause 23.1.2.a)

$$b_f = \text{Least of:}$$

1. $\dfrac{l_0}{6} + b_w + 6 D_f$
2. Actual width of flange (rib spacing)
3. Center-to-center distance of adjacent ribs

#### Criteria for L-Beams (Clause 23.1.2.b)

$$b_f = \text{Least of:}$$

1. $\dfrac{l_0}{12} + b_w + 3 D_f$
2. Actual width of flange
3. $\dfrac{b}{2} + b_w$  *(b = actual flange width)*

#### Criteria for Isolated T-Beams (Clause 23.1.2.c)

When the flange is isolated from the slab and acts purely as a compression flange:

$$b_f = \dfrac{l_0 \cdot 0.5 \cdot b}{(l_0/b) + 4} + b_w$$

where the **flange thickness $D_f \geq b_w$** and $b$ is the actual flange width.

### 2.2 Analysis Approach — Limit State Method (LSM)

The behaviour of a flanged beam in flexure depends on the **location of the Neutral Axis (NA)**, which in turn depends on the **amount of tension steel ($A_{st}$)**.

#### Case I — NA in the Flange ($x_u \leq D_f$)

- The section behaves as a **rectangular beam of width $b_f$**.
- Standard rectangular beam equations apply **without modification**.
- This occurs in **lightly reinforced** flanged beams.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Test:** Assume NA is in flange. Calculate $x_u$ using $T = C$ with width $b_f$. If $x_u \leq D_f$, assumption is correct.

#### Case II — NA in the Web ($x_u > D_f$)

The compression zone has **two distinct parts**:

- **Part 1 (Overhanging Flange):** Full flange width minus web width, full flange thickness.
- **Part 2 (Web in Compression):** Web width only, depth $x_u$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This case arises in **moderately to heavily reinforced** flanged beams and is the **most commonly tested** scenario in KTU examinations.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Validity |
|---|----------|---------|-------|----------|
| 1 | Effective flange width (T-beam) | $b_f = \min\!\left[\tfrac{l_0}{6}+b_w+6D_f,\; b,\; c/c\right]$ | mm | Clause 23.1.2.a |
| 2 | Effective flange width (L-beam) | $b_f = \min\!\left[\tfrac{l_0}{12}+b_w+3D_f,\; b,\; \tfrac{b}{2}+b_w\right]$ | mm | Clause 23.1.2.b |
| 3 | Tension in steel | $T = 0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}$ | N | All sections |
| 4 | Compression in overhanging flange | $C_1 = 0.45\, f_{ck}\, (b_f - b_w)\, D_f$ | N | NA in web |
| 5 | Compression in web | $C_2 = 0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_w\, x_u$ | N | NA in web |
| 6 | Equilibrium (NA in web) | $C_1 + C_2 = T$ | N | Solves $x_u$ |
| 7 | NA in flange (rectangular) | $x_u = \dfrac{0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}}{0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_f}$ | mm | $x_u \leq D_f$ |
| 8 | Limiting NA depth (Fe 500) | $x_{u,\max} = 0.48\, d$ | mm | LSM |
| 9 | Limiting NA depth (Fe 415) | $x_{u,\max} = 0.53\, d$ | mm | LSM |
| 10 | Moment (NA in web) | $M_u = C_1\!\left(d - \tfrac{D_f}{2}\right) + C_2\!\left(d - \tfrac{x_u}{2}\right)$ | N·mm | Taking moments about $A_{st}$ |
| 11 | Moment (NA in flange) | $M_u = 0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}\, \left(d - \dfrac{0.42\, x_u}{1}\right)$ | N·mm | Rectangular case |
| 12 | Lever arm | $z = d - \dfrac{0.42\, x_u}{1}$ | mm | Approximate |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Students often forget that $0.36\, f_{ck}$ in Case II uses the **web width $b_w$**, **not** $b_f$. Mixing these up is the **#1 reason for full-mark deductions**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flanged beam analysis is essential in the design of:

- **Building floor systems** (beam-slab construction)
- **Bridge girders** with cast-in-place deck
- **Ribbed/Coffered slabs**
- **Composite deck-slab systems** in steel-concrete structures

The economic benefit is substantial: a T-beam can carry **2–4 times the moment** of an equivalent rectangular beam of width $b_w$, with only marginal increase in self-weight.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Moment of Resistance When NA Lies in the Web

Consider a T-section with the neutral axis at depth $x_u > D_f$ from the top. The stress block consists of:

**Step 1 — Identify the two compression blocks.**

The total compressive force in concrete is the sum of:
- Force in **overhanging flange** ($b_f - b_w$ wide, $D_f$ thick): $C_1$
- Force in **web portion in compression** ($b_w$ wide, $x_u$ thick): $C_2$

**Step 2 — Express $C_1$ and $C_2$ from the rectangular stress block.**

The IS 456 rectangular parabolic-rectangular stress block gives a **uniform stress of $0.446\, f_{ck}$** (rounded to **$0.45\, f_{ck}$**) over the compression zone, with a **depth factor of $0.42\, x$** for the equivalent rectangular block. The web compression force is governed by the strain compatibility and is given by $0.36\, f_{ck} b_w x_u$.

$$C_1 = 0.45\, f_{ck}\, (b_f - b_w)\, D_f$$

$$C_2 = 0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_w\, x_u$$

**Step 3 — Apply horizontal equilibrium ($C = T$).**

$$0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_w\, x_u + 0.45\, f_{ck}\, (b_f - b_w)\, D_f = 0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the neutral axis depth $x_u$.**

$$x_u = \dfrac{0.87\, f_y\, A_{st} - 0.45\, f_{ck}\, (b_f - b_w)\, D_f}{0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_w}$$

**Step 5 — Verify NA location and section ductility.**

- If $x_u > D_f$ → assumption correct.
- If $x_u \leq D_f$ → redo as rectangular beam.
- If $x_u > x_{u,\max}$ → section is **over-reinforced**, redesign with more depth or higher concrete grade.

**Step 6 — Compute $M_u$ by taking moments of compression forces about the centroid of tension steel.**

$$M_u = C_1 \cdot \left(d - \dfrac{D_f}{2}\right) + C_2 \cdot \left(d - \dfrac{x_u}{2}\right)$$

Substituting $C_1$ and $C_2$:

$$\boxed{M_u = 0.45\, f_{ck}\, (b_f - b_w)\, D_f \left(d - \dfrac{D_f}{2}\right) + 0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_w\, x_u \left(d - \dfrac{x_u}{2}\right)}$$

### 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU-Model Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A T-beam has the following data:
> - Effective span: $l_0 = 8$ m
> - Flange width: $b = 1500$ mm (center-to-center of ribs)
> - Flange thickness: $D_f = 120$ mm
> - Web width: $b_w = 300$ mm
> - Effective depth: $d = 650$ mm
> - Tension steel: $A_{st} = 5500$ mm² (Fe 500)
> - Concrete: M 25 ($f_{ck} = 25$ MPa, $f_y = 500$ MPa)
>
> **Find:** Type of section and ultimate moment of resistance by **LSM**.

#### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the Effective Flange Width $b_f$ (T-beam, Clause 23.1.2.a).**

$$\dfrac{l_0}{6} + b_w + 6D_f = \dfrac{8000}{6} + 300 + 6(120) = 1333.33 + 300 + 720 = 2353.33 \text{ mm}$$

Comparing the three criteria:

- Criterion 1: $2353.33$ mm
- Criterion 2 (actual width): $1500$ mm ✓ **governs**
- Criterion 3: $1500$ mm

Therefore:

$$b_f = 1500 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Assume NA in the web and solve for $x_u$.**

Apply horizontal equilibrium $C_1 + C_2 = T$:

$$0.36 \times 25 \times 300 \times x_u + 0.45 \times 25 \times (1500 - 300) \times 120 = 0.87 \times 500 \times 5500$$

$$2700\, x_u + 1\,620\,000 = 2\,392\,500$$

$$2700\, x_u = 772\,500$$

$$x_u = \dfrac{772\,500}{2700} = 286.11 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Verify assumptions.**

- NA in web? $x_u = 286.11 \text{ mm} > D_f = 120$ mm ✓
- Under-reinforced? $x_{u,\max} = 0.48\, d = 0.48 \times 650 = 312$ mm
- $x_u = 286.11 < 312$ ✓ Section is **under-reinforced and safe**.

**Step 4 — Calculate $C_1$, $C_2$, and $M_u$.**

$$C_1 = 0.45 \times 25 \times (1500 - 300) \times 120 = 1\,620\,000 \text{ N}$$

$$C_2 = 0.36 \times 25 \times 300 \times 286.11 = 772\,497 \text{ N}$$

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_1 + C_2 = 1\,620\,000 + 772\,497 = 2\,392\,497 \text{ N} \approx T = 2\,392\,500 \text{ N} \;\; \checkmark$$

Taking moments of compression forces about the centroid of $A_{st}$:

$$M_u = C_1\!\left(d - \dfrac{D_f}{2}\right) + C_2\!\left(d - \dfrac{x_u}{2}\right)$$

$$M_u = 1\,620\,000 \times \left(650 - \dfrac{120}{2}\right) + 772\,497 \times \left(650 - \dfrac{286.11}{2}\right)$$

$$M_u = 1\,620\,000 \times 590 + 772\,497 \times 506.94$$

$$M_u = 955\,800\,000 + 391\,631\,000 = 1\,347\,431\,000 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\boxed{M_u \approx 1347.4 \text{ kN·m}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification (Alternative Method):** Using $M_u = T \cdot z$ where $z$ is the lever arm from the resultant compression to the steel centroid:
> Lever arm from top to resultant compression $y = \dfrac{C_1(D_f/2) + C_2(x_u/2)}{C_1+C_2} = \dfrac{1\,620\,000(60) + 772\,497(143.06)}{2\,392\,497} = 86.79$ mm
> $z = d - y = 650 - 86.79 = 563.21$ mm
> $M_u = 2\,392\,500 \times 563.21 = 1\,347\,600\,000$ N·mm $\approx 1347.6$ kN·m ✓ (matches)

### 3.3 Quick-Reference: NA Location Decision Matrix

| Calculated $x_u$ | Section Type | Beam Behaviour | Use Formula |
|------------------|--------------|----------------|-------------|
| $x_u \leq D_f$ | Flanged beam (NA in flange) | Behaves as **rectangular beam** of width $b_f$ | Rectangular $b_f$ equations |
| $D_f < x_u \leq x_{u,\max}$ | Flanged beam (NA in web) | **Under-reinforced**, full T-action | Two-block $M_u$ equation |
| $x_u > x_{u,\max}$ | **Over-reinforced** | Brittle failure — **NOT allowed** | Redesign section |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — LSM Design Procedure for Flanged Beams

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Flanged Beam Data b_f, D_f, b_w, d, A_st, f_ck, f_y"] --> B["Compute b_f per IS 456 Clause 23.1.2 Least of three criteria"]
    B --> C{"Assume NA in web x_u > D_f?"}
    C -- "Yes (Heavy reinforcement)" --> D["Solve 0.36 f_ck b_w x_u + 0.45 f_ck b_f minus b_w D_f = 0.87 f_y A_st"]
    D --> E["Compute x_u from equilibrium"]
    E --> F{"Check 1: x_u greater than D_f?"}
    F -- "No" --> G["Reanalyze as rectangular beam of width b_f"]
    F -- "Yes" --> H{"Check 2: x_u less than x_u_max 0.48d for Fe500?"}
    H -- "No Over-reinforced" --> I["Redesign: increase d or use higher f_ck"]
    H -- "Yes Under-reinforced" --> J["Compute C1 = 0.45 f_ck b_f minus b_w D_f"]
    J --> K["Compute C2 = 0.36 f_ck b_w x_u"]
    K --> L["Compute M_u = C1 times d minus D_f over 2 plus C2 times d minus x_u over 2"]
    L --> M["OUTPUT: M_u in kN m"]
    C -- "No Light reinforcement" --> N["Use rectangular beam equations with width b_f"]
    N --> O["x_u = 0.87 f_y A_st divided by 0.36 f_ck b_f"]
    O --> P{"x_u less than or equal to D_f?"}
    P -- "Yes" --> Q["M_u = 0.87 f_y A_st times d minus 0.42 x_u"]
    P -- "No" --> D
    Q --> M
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress Distribution in Flanged Beam (NA in Web)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:flange["FLANGE Top b_f wide D_f thick"]:3
    space:3
    block:web_top["Web b_w wide Stress = 0.45 f_ck Depth = 0.42 x_u"]:1
    space:1
    block:na["NEUTRAL AXIS at x_u from top"]:1
    space:3
    block:tension["Tension steel A_st at d Stress = 0.87 f_y"]:3
    space:3
    block:c1["C1 Force in overhanging flange 0.45 f_ck times b_f minus b_w times D_f Acts at D_f/2 from top"]:1
    block:c2["C2 Force in web compression 0.36 f_ck times b_w times x_u Acts at x_u/2 from top"]:1
    block:tforce["T Tension in steel 0.87 f_y times A_st Acts at d from top"]:1
    c1 --> c2 --> tforce
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram maps the **two-zone compression model** and resultant force locations used in the moment-of-resistance calculation. This is the conceptual model IS 456 prescribes for the **NA-in-web** case.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define **effective flange width** for a T-beam as per IS 456:2000. State the criteria for its determination.

**Model Answer:**

> Effective flange width $b_f$ is the width of the slab assumed to act integrally with the beam web in resisting compression. For a T-beam (IS 456, Clause 23.1.2.a), $b_f$ is the **least** of:
> 1. $(l_0/6) + b_w + 6D_f$
> 2. Actual width of flange (rib spacing)
> 3. Center-to-center distance of adjacent ribs

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

When does the **neutral axis lie in the web** of a flanged beam? What is the consequence if the limiting moment of resistance is exceeded?

**Model Answer:**

> The NA lies in the web when the calculated $x_u > D_f$ (i.e., the section is moderately or heavily reinforced). If $x_u$ exceeds $x_{u,\max} = 0.48d$ (Fe 500), the section becomes **over-reinforced** and is disallowed by IS 456, as failure becomes sudden and brittle (no warning). The section must be redesigned by increasing $d$, using higher $f_{ck}$, or compressing the tension steel area.

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** The most common mistakes that lead to **full-mark deductions** are:
> 1. Using **$b_f$ in the web compression term** ($0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_f\, x_u$) instead of $b_w$ — this is mathematically wrong.
> 2. Forgetting to **verify NA location** after computing $x_u$.
> 3. Using $x_{u,\max} = 0.53d$ for **Fe 500** (use **0.48d**).
> 4. Mixing up **lever-arm calculation** — moment should be taken about the **centroid of $A_{st}$**, not the top fibre.
> 5. **Unit slip-ups** between N and kN, mm and m.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

A T-beam has the following properties:
- $b_f = 1400$ mm, $D_f = 100$ mm, $b_w = 300$ mm
- $d = 550$ mm, $A_{st} = 4000$ mm²
- $f_{ck} = 20$ MPa, $f_y = 500$ MPa

Determine the **type of section** and compute the **ultimate moment of resistance** by LSM.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assume NA in web.** Apply equilibrium $C_1 + C_2 = T$:

$$0.36 \times 20 \times 300 \times x_u + 0.45 \times 20 \times (1400 - 300) \times 100 = 0.87 \times 500 \times 4000$$

$$2160\, x_u + 990\,000 = 1\,740\,000$$

$$x_u = \dfrac{750\,000}{2160} = 347.22 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Check NA location:** $x_u = 347.22 > D_f = 100$ mm ✓ (NA in web)

**Step 3 — Check ductility:** $x_{u,\max} = 0.48 \times 550 = 264$ mm.
Since $x_u = 347.22 > 264$ → **Section is over-reinforced**.

For a safe design, the moment of resistance is taken at the **limiting case** $x_u = x_{u,\max} = 264$ mm.

**Step 4 — Recompute $M_u$ at $x_u = 264$ mm:**

$$C_1 = 0.45 \times 20 \times 1100 \times 100 = 990\,000 \text{ N}$$

$$C_2 = 0.36 \times 20 \times 300 \times 264 = 570\,240 \text{ N}$$

$$M_u = 990\,000 \times (550 - 50) + 570\,240 \times (550 - 132)$$

$$M_u = 990\,000 \times 500 + 570\,240 \times 418$$

$$M_u = 495\,000\,000 + 238\,360\,000 = 733\,360\,000 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\boxed{M_{u,\text{lim}} \approx 733.36 \text{ kN·m}}$$

> **Conclusion:** The provided $A_{st}$ exceeds the limiting value; section must be redesigned.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

An **L-beam** has flange dimensions: $b = 900$ mm, $D_f = 110$ mm. The web is $b_w = 280$ mm wide and effective depth $d = 520$ mm. Reinforcement: $A_{st} = 3500$ mm² (Fe 415). Concrete: M 20. Effective span = 6 m. Calculate the **ultimate moment of resistance**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Effective flange width for L-beam (Clause 23.1.2.b):**

- $l_0/12 + b_w + 3D_f = 6000/12 + 280 + 3(110) = 500 + 280 + 330 = 1110$ mm
- Actual flange: $900$ mm ✓ **governs**
- $b/2 + b_w = 450 + 280 = 730$ mm (not governing)

$b_f = 900$ mm

**Step 2 — Assume NA in web and solve:**

$$0.36 \times 20 \times 280 \times x_u + 0.45 \times 20 \times (900 - 280) \times 110 = 0.87 \times 415 \times 3500$$

$$2016\, x_u + 612\,700 = 1\,263\,675$$

$$x_u = \dfrac{650\,975}{2016} = 322.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Verify:** $x_u = 322.9 > D_f = 110$ ✓ (NA in web).

$x_{u,\max} = 0.53d = 0.53 \times 520 = 275.6$ mm.
Since $x_u = 322.9 > 275.6$ → **Over-reinforced** → use $x_u = 275.6$ mm.

**Step 4 — Compute $M_u$ at limiting $x_u$:**

$$C_1 = 0.45 \times 20 \times 620 \times 110 = 613\,800 \text{ N}$$

$$C_2 = 0.36 \times 20 \times 280 \times 275.6 = 555\,525 \text{ N}$$

$$M_u = 613\,800 \times (520 - 55) + 555\,525 \times (520 - 137.8)$$

$$M_u = 613\,800 \times 465 + 555\,525 \times 382.2$$

$$M_u = 285\,417\,000 + 212\,312\,000 = 497\,729\,000 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\boxed{M_{u,\text{lim}} \approx 497.73 \text{ kN·m}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Derive the **expression for the ultimate moment of resistance** of a flanged beam when the **neutral axis lies in the web**, clearly stating all assumptions per IS 456:2000.

**Model Solution:**

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.1** above. The final expression is:

$$M_u = 0.45\, f_{ck}\, (b_f - b_w)\, D_f \left(d - \dfrac{D_f}{2}\right) + 0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_w\, x_u \left(d - \dfrac{x_u}{2}\right)$$

where

$$x_u = \dfrac{0.87\, f_y\, A_{st} - 0.45\, f_{ck}\, (b_f - b_w)\, D_f}{0.36\, f_{ck}\, b_w}$$

**Assumptions (per IS 456, Clause 23.1):**

1. Maximum strain in concrete at the extreme compression fibre = **0.0035**.
2. Maximum strain in tension steel = **$f_y/(1.15 E_s) + 0.002$** (Fe 500) with limiting value ≥ 0.004.
3. Rectangular stress block: uniform stress **$0.446\, f_{ck} \approx 0.45\, f_{ck}$** over depth **$0.42\, x_u$**.
4. Perfect bond between concrete and steel.
5. Tension in concrete is ignored.
6. Plane sections remain plane (Bernoulli's hypothesis).

---

**(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

A T-beam has $b_f = 1200$ mm, $D_f = 100$ mm, $b_w = 250$ mm, $d = 500$ mm, $A_{st} = 2500$ mm². Concrete: M 25, Steel: Fe 500. Find the **ultimate moment of resistance** and the **maximum factored moment** the section can carry safely.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assume NA in web:**

$$0.36 \times 25 \times 250 \times x_u + 0.45 \times 25 \times (1200 - 250) \times 100 = 0.87 \times 500 \times 2500$$

$$2250\, x_u + 1\,068\,750 = 1\,087\,500$$

$$x_u = \dfrac{18\,750}{2250} = 8.33 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Verify NA location:** $x_u = 8.33 < D_f = 100$ → **NA lies in the flange** (assumption was wrong).

**Step 3 — Reanalyze as a rectangular beam of width $b_f = 1200$ mm:**

$$x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \times 500 \times 2500}{0.36 \times 25 \times 1200} = \dfrac{1\,087\,500}{10\,800} = 100.69 \text{ mm}$$

Since $x_u = 100.69 \text{ mm} \approx D_f = 100$ mm → beam is at the **boundary** (NA exactly at flange-web interface).

For practical purposes, treat as rectangular:

$$M_u = 0.87 \times 500 \times 2500 \times \left(500 - 0.42 \times 100.69\right)$$

$$M_u = 1\,087\,500 \times (500 - 42.29) = 1\,087\,500 \times 457.71$$

$$M_u = 497\,760\,000 \text{ N·mm} \approx 497.76 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$\boxed{M_u \approx 497.76 \text{ kN·m}}$$

> **Maximum safe factored moment** the section can carry = $M_u \approx 497.76$ kN·m.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Flanged beams** form due to **monolithic slab-beam construction**; the slab acts as the compression flange.
- [x] **T-beams** occur in interior positions; **L-beams** at edges.
- [x] **Effective flange width $b_f$** is the **least** of three criteria (IS 456, Clause 23.1.2).
- [x] For **T-beams**: $b_f = \min\!\left[\frac{l_0}{6}+b_w+6D_f,\; \text{actual width}\right]$.
- [x] For **L-beams**: $b_f = \min\!\left[\frac{l_0}{12}+b_w+3D_f,\; \text{actual width},\; \frac{b}{2}+b_w\right]$.
- [x] **NA in flange** → use rectangular beam formulas with $b = b_f$.
- [x] **NA in web** → two-block compression model with $C_1$ and $C_2$.
- [x] $C_1$ uses flange overhang $(b_f - b_w)$ × $D_f$ × $0.45\, f_{ck}$.
- [x] $C_2$ uses web width $b_w$ × $x_u$ × $0.36\, f_{ck}$ — **NOT $b_f$**.
- [x] Equilibrium: $C_1 + C_2 = 0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}$.
- [x] $x_{u,\max} = 0.48d$ for **Fe 500**, $0.53d$ for **Fe 415**.
- [x] Always **verify NA location** after computing $x_u$.
- [x] $M_u = C_1(d - D_f/2) + C_2(d - x_u/2)$ when NA is in web.
- [x] Lever arm $z = d - 0.42\, x_u$ (approximate, rectangular case).
- [x] **Over-reinforced sections** must be redesigned; IS 456 disallows them.
- [x] **Working Stress Method** for flanged beams (Clause G-2.1 of IS 456 Annex G) is rarely asked in KTU 2024 — focus on **LSM**.
- [x] Flanged beam design yields **30–60% higher $M_u$** than a rectangular beam of width $b_w$ — hence economical.
- [x] Always **convert units consistently** (N, mm, MPa → kN·m for final answer).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: One-way Slabs - Theory and IS Code Provisions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** One-way Slabs - Theory and IS Code Provisions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **One-Way Slab** is a structural reinforced concrete slab panel in which the ratio of the longer span to the shorter span (Ly/Lx) is **greater than or equal to 2 (Ly/Lx ≥ 2)**. Under gravity loading, the load is transferred predominantly along the **shorter direction** to the supporting beams or walls, causing bending to occur primarily in **one direction only**. The slab behaves essentially as a wide beam of unit width (typically **1 m**), and its design follows the **Limit State Method (LSM)** as per **IS 456:2000** clauses.

The **shorter span** is termed the **effective span (lex)** and the **longer span** is termed **ly**. The slab is supported along its **two opposite longer edges** by load-bearing walls, beams, or girders, while the **two shorter edges** are essentially free or un-supported structurally (they may have nominal continuity through distribution steel only).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** IS 456:2000 explicitly states that slabs with **Ly/Lx < 2** are designed as **Two-Way Slabs** (covered in Module 3). The boundary case **Ly/Lx = 2** lies at the cusp; conservative design practice at KTU treats this as **one-way**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **wooden plank** (about 1 m wide and 4 m long) placed across **two chairs** placed exactly beneath the short ends. When you stand on the middle of the plank, it bends *across* the short direction (the chairs are right there to support it), but it does *not* bend much along its long direction — because nothing is trying to support it along that long axis.

> A one-way slab is the same idea: a long, flat concrete panel that "leans on" its two short sides (the **main beams**). The steel reinforcement is therefore placed running **parallel to the short span** (perpendicular to the supports) so it can resist the bending that develops along that short span.

## 1.3 Key Identifiers at a Glance

| Property | Value | Reference |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| Span ratio criterion | **Ly/Lx ≥ 2** | IS 456:2000 (cl. 24.1, general principle) |
| Design width (b) | **1000 mm = 1 m** | Standard convention |
| Minimum grade of concrete (M) | **M20** | IS 456:2000, cl. 6.1.2 |
| Minimum grade of steel (Fe) | **Fe 415** (HYSD) | IS 456:2000, cl. 5.6 |
| Min. partial safety factor (DL) | **1.5** | IS 456:2000, Table 18 |
| Min. partial safety factor (LL) | **1.5** | IS 456:2000, Table 18 |
| Concrete safety factor (γm) | **1.5** | IS 456:2000, Table 18 |
| Steel safety factor (γm) | **1.15** | IS 456:2000, Table 18 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Note:** Because the longer edges are unrestrained (no support), the slab cannot develop negative moments or shear continuity in that direction. Hence, **only the short span is engineered**; the long-span direction gets only **distribution steel** (shrinkage and temperature reinforcement, NOT structural).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Behaviour of a One-Way Slab

1. **Load Path**: The vertical load (self-weight + live load) acts on the top surface. The slab deflects, generating flexural stresses.
2. **Bending Direction**: Because the shorter span is **2× stiffer** (in the sense that it is much shorter), nearly **all** the curvature happens across the short span.
3. **Tension Zone**: At midspan, the bottom fibre is in tension → **main steel is placed at the bottom**, parallel to the short span.
4. **Secondary Steel**: The top of the slab is also under modest tension due to shrinkage and temperature → **distribution steel** is placed transversely to control cracking.
5. **Supports Transfer**: Reactions are transferred to the two edge beams/walls, which then transfer the load to columns.

## 2.2 Design Assumptions (IS 456:2000 Compliant)

- Plane sections remain plane after bending (Bernoulli hypothesis).
- Perfect bond exists between concrete and steel.
- Tensile strength of concrete is ignored.
- Maximum strain in concrete at the extreme compression fibre = **0.0035** (cl. 38.1).
- Maximum strain in steel = **0.002 + fy/(1.15Es)** (cl. 38.1) → for Fe 415 = **0.002 + 415/(1.15 × 2×10⁵)** = **0.0038**.
- Stress block: equivalent rectangular (Whitney block) with depth **0.42x** and stress **0.67 fck** (cl. 38.1, Fig. 4).

## 2.3 IS 456:2000 Key Clauses for One-Way Slabs

| Clause | Title | Key Provision |
|--------|-------|----------------|
| **22.2** | Effective Span | $l_{eff} = \text{clear span} + d$ OR center-to-center of supports, **whichever is less** |
| **23.2.1** | Slab thickness (deflection) | Basic $L/d$ ratio × modification factor $\alpha$ (Table 26 read with Fig. 4 of IS 456) |
| **24.4.1** | Cover to main reinforcement | **20 mm** for mild exposure; **30 mm** for moderate; **45 mm** for severe |
| **26.5.2.1** | Minimum reinforcement | $A_{s,min} = \dfrac{0.12\,bD}{100}$ (for Fe 415/HYSD) |
| **26.5.2.2** | Maximum spacing of main bars | **3d** or **300 mm**, whichever is **lesser** |
| **26.5.2.2** | Maximum dia of main bars | $\phi \leq \dfrac{D}{8}$ |
| **26.5.3.2** | Distribution reinforcement | $A_{sd} = \dfrac{0.12\,A_g}{100}$ (for Fe 415) |
| **Table 12** | Bending moment coefficients | For continuous one-way slabs (simply supported, fixed, continuous cases) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas. These are the heart of every KTU ESE problem on one-way slabs.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|----------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $\text{Effective span } l_{ex} = \min(\text{clear span} + d,\ \text{c/c of supports})$ | IS 456 cl. 22.2 |
| 2 | $w_u = 1.5(w_D + w_L)$ | Factored (ultimate) load per m² |
| 3 | $M_u = \alpha_w \cdot w_u \cdot l_{ex}^2$ | Factored design moment; $\alpha_w$ from Table 12 |
| 4 | $M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.138\,f_{ck}\,b\,d^2$ | Limiting moment of resistance (for Fe 415) |
| 5 | $A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5\,f_{ck}}{f_y}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6\,M_u}{f_{ck}\,b\,d^2}}\right]\,b\,d$ | Steel area (LSM, rectangular section) |
| 6 | $A_{st,\text{min}} = \dfrac{0.12\,bD}{100}$ | Min. tension steel (Fe 415 HYSD, cl. 26.5.2.1) |
| 7 | $\text{Max spacing} = \min(3d,\ 300\text{ mm})$ | cl. 26.5.2.2 |
| 8 | Max bar dia $\phi \leq D/8$ | cl. 26.5.2.2 |
| 9 | Basic $L/d$ for simply supported = **20** (Fe 415) | cl. 23.2.1, Table 26 |
| 10 | Basic $L/d$ for continuous = **26** (Fe 415) | cl. 23.2.1, Table 26 |
| 11 | Modification factor $\alpha_s$ (depends on $p_t$ and span) | IS 456 Fig. 4 (read against $p_t = \dfrac{A_{st}}{bd} \times 100$) |
| 12 | $L/d$ allowable = $\alpha_s \times \text{basic } L/d$ | Effective design limit |
| 13 | $A_{sd} = \dfrac{0.12}{100}\,A_g$ | Distribution steel (cl. 26.5.3.2) |
| 14 | $V_u = w_u \cdot l_{ex} / 2$ (simply supported) | Factored shear force |
| 15 | $\tau_v = V_u / (b \cdot d)$ and $\tau_c$ from Table 19 | Shear design check (slabs generally OK) |
| 16 | $L_d = \dfrac{\phi\,\sigma_s}{4\,\tau_{bd}}$ | Development length (cl. 26.2.1) |
| 17 | $L_d \geq L_0 = 12\phi$ or $d$ (whichever is greater) | Minimum required $L_d$ |

## 2.5 Bending Moment Coefficients (Table 12, IS 456) — High-Yield Subset

| Support Condition | Position | Coefficient ($\alpha_w$) |
|--------------------|----------|--------------------------|
| **Simply Supported** | Mid-span | $+1/8 = 0.125$ |
| **Fixed at both ends** | Mid-span | $+1/24 \approx 0.042$ |
| **Fixed at both ends** | Support | $-1/12 \approx -0.083$ |
| **Continuous (interior span)** | Mid-span | $+1/16 = 0.0625$ (approx) |
| **Continuous (interior span)** | Support | $-1/10 = -0.100$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU board exams, the most common cases tested are: **simply supported** (use $w_u L^2/8$) and **continuous slabs** (use Table 12 coefficients). Always state the coefficient used clearly in your answer script.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

One-way slabs are the **most economical** slab type for rectangular building plans. They are extensively used in:
- **Staircase landings and mid-landings**
- **Verandahs and corridor slabs**
- **Sunshades and chajjas**
- **Cantilever portions of buildings** (single-direction projection)
- **Spanning slabs of long narrow rooms** (e.g., dormitories, halls, hospital wards)

Their design is faster and more straightforward than two-way slabs, and they require **less steel** in the secondary direction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Limiting Moment of Resistance (Fe 415, LSM)

The **Whitney Stress Block** (cl. 38.1) gives:
- Depth of neutral axis: $x_u$
- Equivalent rectangular stress block depth: $a = 0.42\,x_u$
- Compressive force: $C = 0.67\,f_{ck}\,b\,a$
- Lever arm: $z = d - a/2$

**Step 1: Force Equilibrium**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= C \\[4pt]
0.87\,f_y\,A_{st} &= 0.67\,f_{ck}\,b\,(0.42\,x_u) \\[4pt]
0.87\,f_y\,A_{st} &= 0.282\,f_{ck}\,b\,x_u
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: At the limit, tension steel reaches its maximum allowable strain.**

For Fe 415, the **limiting depth of neutral axis** is $x_{u,\text{max}} = 0.48\,d$ (from strain compatibility, IS 456 Fig. 1).

**Step 3: Lever arm at limit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_{\text{lim}} &= d - 0.42\,x_{u,\text{max}} / \text{? No} - 0.5 \times 0.42\,x_{u,\text{max}} \\
&= d - 0.5 \times 0.42 \times 0.48\,d \\
&= d\,(1 - 0.5 \times 0.42 \times 0.48) \\
&= d\,(1 - 0.1008) \\
&= 0.8992\,d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the standard limit values for Fe 415 (pioneered by SP-16 / IS 456 Fig. 4):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,\text{lim}} &= 0.36 \dfrac{x_{u,\text{max}}}{d} \left(1 - 0.42 \dfrac{x_{u,\text{max}}}{d}\right) f_{ck}\,b\,d^2 \\[4pt]
M_{u,\text{lim}} &= 0.36 \times 0.48 \times (1 - 0.42 \times 0.48) \times f_{ck}\,b\,d^2 \\[4pt]
M_{u,\text{lim}} &= 0.1728 \times 0.7984 \times f_{ck}\,b\,d^2 \\[4pt]
\therefore\ M_{u,\text{lim}} &= 0.138\,f_{ck}\,b\,d^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **magic number 0.138** that appears in every KTU slab problem. **Memorize it.**

## 3.2 Derivation of Steel Area Formula

**Step 1: Moment equilibrium (general case)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_u &= 0.87\,f_y\,A_{st}\,z \\[4pt]
\text{where } z &= d\,(1 - 0.42\,x_u/d) \text{ and } x_u/d = \dfrac{0.87\,f_y\,A_{st}}{0.36\,f_{ck}\,b\,d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute and simplify**

After algebraic manipulation (as derived in SP-16), we get:

$$
\boxed{\,A_{st} = \frac{0.5\,f_{ck}}{f_y}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6\,M_u}{f_{ck}\,b\,d^2}}\right]\,b\,d\,}
$$

**Step 3: Validity check**

If $\dfrac{M_u}{f_{ck}\,b\,d^2} \leq 0.219$ (Fe 415) → singly reinforced section, formula valid.
If $> 0.219$ → need to redesign with greater depth.

## 3.3 Complete Step-by-Step Design Procedure (Analytical Matrix)

The following table is the **golden workflow** for a KTU 14-mark one-way slab design problem:

| Step No. | Action | Formula / Reference |
|----------|--------|---------------------|
| **1** | Identify spans and support conditions | Given data |
| **2** | Decide effective span (lex) | IS 456 cl. 22.2: $l_{ex} = \min(\text{clear} + d,\ \text{c/c})$ |
| **3** | Estimate slab thickness (D) | Use $L/d = 20$ to 26 rule + cover + half bar dia |
| **4** | Compute dead load (DL) | $w_D = D \times 25$ kN/m² (concrete density **25 kN/m³**); add floor finish (typ. **1 to 1.5 kN/m²**) |
| **5** | Apply live load (LL) | IS 875 Part 2: **2 to 3 kN/m²** for residential; **3 to 5 kN/m²** for offices |
| **6** | Factored load $w_u$ | $1.5 (w_D + w_L)$ |
| **7** | Design moment $M_u$ | $M_u = \alpha_w \cdot w_u \cdot l_{ex}^2$ (Table 12) |
| **8** | Check section as singly reinforced | If $M_u \leq 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ → OK |
| **9** | Compute $A_{st}$ | Use Eq. (5) above |
| **10** | Check $A_{st} \geq A_{st,\text{min}}$ | $A_{st,\text{min}} = 0.12 bD / 100$ |
| **11** | Select bar dia & spacing | 8 mm, 10 mm, 12 mm @ spacing ≤ min(3d, 300 mm) |
| **12** | Check max bar dia | $\phi \leq D/8$ |
| **13** | Check deflection (L/d) | Actual $L/d \leq \alpha_s \times \text{basic } L/d$ |
| **14** | Check shear | $V_u = w_u \cdot l_{ex}/2$; $\tau_v \leq \tau_{c,\text{max}}$ (Table 20); $\tau_v \leq \tau_c$ (Table 19) |
| **15** | Distribution steel | $A_{sd} = 0.12 A_g / 100$; max spacing **5d** or **450 mm** |
| **16** | Check development length | $L_d$ provided ≥ $L_d$ required (cl. 26.2.1) |
| **17** | Detailing & curtailment | 50% bars may be curtailed at $L/4$ from support for simply supported |
| **18** | Detailing (anchorage) | Hooks / bends at simple supports if $L_d$ not available |

## 3.4 Illustrative Worked Example Outline (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Design a simply supported one-way slab for a room of clear dimensions **3.5 m × 8 m**. Live load = **3 kN/m²**, floor finish = **1.2 kN/m²**. Use M20 concrete and Fe 415 steel.

**Solution Outline:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_y / L_x &= 8 / 3.5 = 2.286 \geq 2 \quad \text{→ ONE-WAY SLAB}\\[4pt]
l_{ex} &\approx 3.5 + 0.15 \text{ (assumed }d\text{) } = 3.65 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\text{Assume } D &= 150 \text{ mm},\ d = 150 - 20 - 0.5 \times 12 = 124 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
w_D &= 0.15 \times 25 + 1.2 = 3.75 + 1.2 = 4.95 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\[4pt]
w_L &= 3.0 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\[4pt]
w_u &= 1.5 \times (4.95 + 3.0) = 11.925 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\[4pt]
M_u &= \frac{1}{8}\,w_u\,l_{ex}^2 = 0.125 \times 11.925 \times 3.65^2 = 19.85 \text{ kN·m/m} \\[4pt]
M_{u,\text{lim}} &= 0.138 \times 20 \times 1000 \times 124^2 \times 10^{-6} = 42.46 \text{ kN·m} \\[4pt]
A_{st} &= \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 19.85 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 124^2}}\right] \times 1000 \times 124 \\[4pt]
A_{st} &\approx 463 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ per m width} \\[4pt]
A_{st,\text{min}} &= 0.12 \times 1000 \times 150 / 100 = 180 \text{ mm}^2 \\[4pt]
A_{st} &> A_{st,\text{min}} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Bar Selection:** Provide **10 mm @ 170 mm c/c** ($A_{st,provided} = 462$ mm²) at the bottom along the short span. Distribution steel: **8 mm @ 250 mm c/c** along the long span.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between a one-way slab and a two-way slab. Under what conditions is a slab designed as one-way?**

**Model Answer:**

A **one-way slab** is a slab in which the load is transferred predominantly along **one direction** (the shorter span), and the ratio of the longer span to shorter span is **Ly/Lx ≥ 2**. The slab behaves as a wide beam of unit width.

A **two-way slab** is one with **Ly/Lx < 2**, where the load is transferred to supports along **both directions** and bending occurs in two principal directions.

**Conditions for one-way design (IS 456 cl. 24.1):**
1. The slab is supported on **all four sides** with Ly/Lx ≥ 2.
2. The slab is supported on **two opposite sides only** (regardless of ratio).
3. The slab is provided with adequate **edge stiffening beams** to ensure Ly/Lx ≥ 2 behaviour.

The design of one-way slabs follows the **coefficient method** (Table 12, IS 456) and a **strip of unit width** is considered for analysis.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State the IS 456:2000 provisions for (a) minimum reinforcement, and (b) maximum spacing of main bars in a one-way slab.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Minimum Reinforcement (cl. 26.5.2.1):**
The minimum area of tension reinforcement in a slab shall be:

$$
A_{st,\text{min}} = \frac{0.12\,b\,D}{100} \quad \text{(for Fe 415/HYSD bars)}
$$

where $b$ = width of section (1000 mm) and $D$ = overall depth of slab.

For mild steel bars: $A_{st,\text{min}} = \frac{0.15\,bD}{100}$.

**(b) Maximum Spacing of Main Bars (cl. 26.5.2.2):**
The horizontal distance between parallel main reinforcement bars shall be **not more than 3 times the effective depth of the slab**, or **300 mm**, whichever is **lesser**.

For distribution bars: maximum spacing = **5 times the effective depth** or **450 mm**, whichever is less.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
#### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2 Choice Question]`

**(a) Design a simply supported one-way slab for a room with clear dimensions 3.0 m × 7.0 m. The slab carries a live load of 3 kN/m² and a floor finish of 1.0 kN/m². Use M20 concrete and Fe 415 steel. Assume mild exposure. (7 marks)**

**(b) Check the slab designed in (a) for deflection, shear, and development length. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Design:**

**Step 1:** Span ratio = 7.0 / 3.0 = 2.33 ≥ 2 → **One-way slab**.

**Step 2:** Assume $D = 150$ mm; effective cover = 20 + 0.5(10) = 25 mm (assuming 10 mm bar); $d = 125$ mm.

**Step 3:** Effective span: $l_{ex} = \min(3.0 + 0.125, 3.0 + \text{half of support widths})$. Take $l_{ex} \approx 3.125$ m (using IS 456 cl. 22.2).

**Step 4:** Loads:
- Self-weight = 0.15 × 25 = 3.75 kN/m²
- Floor finish = 1.00 kN/m²
- Live load = 3.00 kN/m²
- **Total w = 7.75 kN/m²**
- **Factored load: $w_u$ = 1.5 × 7.75 = 11.625 kN/m²**

**Step 5:** Maximum bending moment (simply supported, Table 12):
$$
M_u = \frac{w_u\,l_{ex}^2}{8} = \frac{11.625 \times 3.125^2}{8} = 14.19 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

**Step 6:** Limiting moment (Fe 415, M20):
$$
M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 1000 \times 125^2 \times 10^{-6} = 43.13 \text{ kN·m/m} \gg 14.19 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 7:** Steel area:
$$
A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 14.19 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 125^2}}\right]\,1000 \times 125 \approx 332 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

**Step 8:** Check minimum: $A_{st,\text{min}} = 0.12 \times 1000 \times 150 / 100 = 180$ mm². ✓

**Step 9:** Bar selection: **Provide 10 mm @ 220 mm c/c** ($A_{st,prov} = 357$ mm²). Maximum spacing allowed = min(3 × 125, 300) = 300 mm ✓. Max bar dia ≤ D/8 = 18.75 mm ✓.

**Provide main steel: 10 mm Φ @ 220 mm c/c** at the bottom, along the short span.

**Distribution steel:** $A_{sd} = 0.12 \times 1000 \times 150 / 100 = 180$ mm². **Provide 8 mm @ 250 mm c/c** transversely.

**(b) Deflection, Shear & Development Length Checks:**

**Deflection check (cl. 23.2):**
- $p_t = (332 / (1000 \times 125)) \times 100 = 0.266\%$
- For $p_t = 0.266\%$, IS 456 Fig. 4 gives $\alpha_s \approx 1.20$ (interpolated).
- Allowable $L/d = 1.20 \times 20 = 24.0$
- Actual $L/d = 3125 / 125 = 25.0$

Since 25.0 > 24.0, **slightly exceeds the limit**. **Revise depth: try $D = 160$ mm, $d = 135$ mm**.
- Recompute: $w_D = 0.16 \times 25 = 4.0$ kN/m²; total = 8.0; $w_u = 12.0$ kN/m².
- $M_u = 12.0 \times 3.135^2 / 8 = 14.74$ kN·m/m.
- $A_{st} = 320$ mm². Provide 10 mm @ 240 mm c/c. Actual $L/d = 3135/135 = 23.2$ < 24.0 ✓.

**Shear check:**
- $V_u = w_u \cdot l_{ex} / 2 = 12.0 \times 3.135 / 2 = 18.81$ kN/m.
- $\tau_v = 18.81 \times 10^3 / (1000 \times 135) = 0.139$ N/mm².
- From Table 19 (M20, $p_t = 0.237\%$): $\tau_c \approx 0.37$ N/mm².
- $\tau_v < \tau_c$ ✓ — **No shear reinforcement required.**

**Development length:**
- $L_d$ required = $\phi\,\sigma_s / (4\,\tau_{bd}) = 10 \times 415 / (4 \times 1.4) = 741$ mm (for M20, $\tau_{bd} = 1.4$ N/mm²).
- $L_0 = \max(12\phi,\ d) = \max(120,\ 135) = 135$ mm. So $L_d \geq 135$ mm ✓.
- Available straight length at simple support = clear span / 2 = 1500 mm > 741 mm ✓.

**Detailing:** Provide **alternate bars curtailed at L/4 from the support face**. Anchorage by 180° hook at simple support for any curtailment point.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
#### `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Module 2 Choice Question]`

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the IS 456:2000 provisions for the design of a one-way slab with respect to: (i) effective span, (ii) depth of slab for deflection control, and (iii) cover requirements. (7 marks)**

**(b) A continuous one-way slab is continuous over 3 equal spans of 3.5 m each. The slab carries a live load of 4 kN/m². Assuming the slab thickness as 180 mm, design the slab for the interior span using M25 concrete and Fe 500 steel. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) IS 456:2000 Provisions:**

**(i) Effective Span (cl. 22.2):**

The effective span of a slab shall be the **lesser** of:
- (a) Distance between the **centers of the bearings**, OR
- (b) **Clear span + effective depth ($d$)**

This applies to slabs supported on **walls, beams, or columns**.

**(ii) Depth of Slab for Deflection Control (cl. 23.2.1):**

The vertical deflection limits may be assumed to be satisfied if the span-to-effective depth ratio is not greater than the values given in Table 26 of IS 456, multiplied by a modification factor $\alpha_s$ from Fig. 4 of IS 456.

| Support Type | Basic L/d (Fe 415) |
|--------------|---------------------|
| Simply supported | 20 |
| Continuous | 26 |
| Cantilever | 7 |

The modification factor $\alpha_s$ depends on:
- Percentage of tension steel ($p_t = A_{st}/(b\,d) \times 100$)
- Span type and support condition

**(iii) Cover Requirements (cl. 26.4):**

| Exposure | Nominal Cover (mm) |
|----------|---------------------|
| Mild | 20 |
| Moderate | 30 |
| Severe | 45 |
| Very Severe | 50 |
| Extreme | 75 |

For slabs, **minimum cover** to the outermost reinforcement = above values. Cover is measured from the concrete surface to the outermost bar surface.

**(b) Design of Continuous Slab (Interior Span, M25, Fe 500):**

**Step 1:** Assume $D = 180$ mm, $d = 180 - 20 - 0.5 \times 12 = 154$ mm (12 mm bar assumed, mild exposure).

**Step 2:** Effective span (continuous, equal spans): $l_{ex} = 3.5 + 0.154 = 3.654$ m (c/c of beams = 3.5 + 0.20/2 + 0.20/2 = 3.7 m; take **lesser** = 3.654 m).

**Step 3:** Loads:
- DL = 0.18 × 25 = 4.5 kN/m²
- LL = 4.0 kN/m²
- Total w = 8.5 kN/m²
- $w_u$ = 1.5 × 8.5 = 12.75 kN/m²

**Step 4:** For interior span of continuous slab (Table 12, IS 456):
- Mid-span positive moment coefficient: 1/12 (for interior span of continuous beam, three-span continuous)
- Support negative moment: -1/10 (at interior support)
- (Some textbooks use 1/10 mid-span for end spans; KTU typically follows SP-16.)

Using $M_{u,\text{pos}} = w_u l^2/12$ and $M_{u,\text{neg}} = w_u l^2/10$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,\text{pos}} &= \frac{12.75 \times 3.654^2}{12} = 14.19 \text{ kN·m/m} \\[4pt]
M_{u,\text{neg}} &= \frac{12.75 \times 3.654^2}{10} = 17.03 \text{ kN·m/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Governing moment: $M_u = 17.03$ kN·m/m.

**Step 6:** $M_{u,\text{lim}}$ for M25, Fe 500 = $0.133 \times f_{ck} b d^2$ (use **0.133** for Fe 500):
$$
M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.133 \times 25 \times 1000 \times 154^2 \times 10^{-6} = 78.85 \text{ kN·m} \gg 17.03 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 7:** $A_{st}$ at interior support:
$$
A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 25}{500}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 17.03 \times 10^6}{25 \times 1000 \times 154^2}}\right] \times 1000 \times 154 \approx 320 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

$A_{st,\text{min}} = 0.12 \times 1000 \times 180 / 100 = 216$ mm² ✓

**Step 8:** **Provide 12 mm @ 350 mm c/c** at the bottom for positive moment at midspan (top in negative moment zones).

Curtailment: 50% of bottom bars may extend into the support by L/4. The remaining bottom bars should be carried to the support for anchorage.

**Final detailing:** **12 mm @ 350 mm c/c at bottom (main steel), 12 mm @ 350 mm c/c at top over supports (negative moment), and 8 mm @ 250 mm c/c distribution steel.**

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students LOSE marks in one-way slab problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the floor finish in dead load** — this is the #1 error. Always include 1.0–1.5 kN/m².
> 2. **Using gross depth D** instead of effective depth **d** when computing moment capacity. The KTU evaluator WILL check this.
> 3. **Wrong sign on the moment coefficient** — students sometimes use $1/8$ for continuous slabs. Table 12 is the authority.
> 4. **Spacings above 300 mm** — IS 456 cl. 26.5.2.2 says max 3d **or** 300 mm. KTU expects the **lesser** value.
> 5. **Neglecting deflection check (L/d)** — students compute $A_{st}$ but skip the L/d verification. This costs **at least 1 to 2 marks**.
> 6. **Missing $A_{st,min}$ check** — for slabs with low steel, this controls. **Always compare**.
> 7. **Wrong cover (45 mm in severe exposure)** — students blindly use 20 mm. **Read the problem statement**.
> 8. **No hooks at simple supports** when curtailed bars don't have full development length.
> 9. **Confusing the load on a 1 m strip** — total factored load is applied on a **1 m wide** strip; never on the full slab area.
> 10. **Forgetting partial safety factor 1.5 on loads** — this is the most fundamental and most frequently missed.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition Criterion:** Slab is one-way when **Ly/Lx ≥ 2**, irrespective of the absolute support conditions.
- **Design Strip Width:** Always consider a **1 m wide strip** of slab for analysis — the design is **per metre width**.
- **Limiting Moment of Resistance (Fe 415, M20):** $M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.138\,f_{ck}\,b\,d^2$. This number **0.138** is a permanent part of your toolkit.
- **Partial Safety Factor for Loads:** Always **1.5** for both DL and LL in LSM (Table 18, IS 456).
- **Minimum Tension Steel:** $0.12\,bD/100$ (Fe 415); $0.15\,bD/100$ (mild steel).
- **Maximum Bar Spacing:** min(3d, 300 mm) for main bars; min(5d, 450 mm) for distribution steel.
- **Maximum Bar Diameter:** $\phi \leq D/8$ (cl. 26.5.2.2).
- **Bending Moment Coefficients:** For simply supported use $w_u L^2/8$. For continuous slabs, use **Table 12 of IS 456**.
- **Deflection Control (cl. 23.2):** Basic $L/d$ is **20 (simply supported)** and **26 (continuous)**, modified by $\alpha_s$ from Fig. 4.
- **Effective Span (cl. 22.2):** $l_{ex} = \min(\text{clear span} + d,\ \text{c/c of supports})$.
- **Cover (cl. 26.4):** **20 mm** for mild exposure, **30 mm** for moderate, **45 mm** for severe.
- **Shear in Slabs:** Slabs rarely need shear reinforcement; always check $\tau_v$ against $\tau_c$ (Table 19) for completeness.
- **Distribution Steel (cl. 26.5.3.2):** $0.12\,A_g/100$ (Fe 415); placed **perpendicular to the main steel**.
- **Curtailment:** Up to **50% of bars** can be curtailed at **L/4** from the support face in simply supported slabs.
- **Detailing Rules:** Hooks at simple supports, lap lengths at proper positions, spacers to maintain cover.
- **Order of Checks in Exam:** Compute $A_{st}$ → check $A_{st,\text{min}}$ → check spacing → check max bar dia → check L/d → check shear → check $L_d$.
- **Standard Materials:** Always use **M20 + Fe 415** unless the problem specifies otherwise (KTU default).
- **Concrete Density:** Always **25 kN/m³** for RCC.
- **Curtailment & Anchorage:** Even if bars are curtailed, **anchorage length** must be ≥ $L_d / 3$ beyond the curtailment point.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always write down the **list of IS 456 clauses** you are referring to (e.g., cl. 22.2, 23.2, 26.5.2.1). Examiners reward students who cite the code properly. Also, never forget to draw a **neat cross-section and plan** in your answer — even a rough sketch carries **2 to 3 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles, Theory and Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fundamental Definition

The **Basic Principles, Theory and Design** of reinforced concrete structures form the philosophical and mathematical foundation upon which all subsequent structural design is built. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (aligned with **IS 456:2000** and **NEP 2020** outcome-based pedagogy), this topic encompasses the codified design philosophies, the underlying assumptions of bending theory, the characterization of material strengths, and the analytical procedures that transform a structural concept into a safe, serviceable, and durable engineered member.

In formal terms, reinforced concrete design theory is the body of engineering principles that governs the **safe sizing, detailing, and proportioning** of structural members (beams, slabs, columns, and footings) composed of concrete (which is strong in compression but weak in tension) and steel reinforcement (which is strong in tension), working together as a composite material to resist applied loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic sits at the heart of the *Limit State Method (LSM)* — the **only method prescribed for design** under the KTU 2024 Scheme. The older *Working Stress Method (WSM)* is included purely for conceptual contrast and examination of historical context.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bundled Reeds"

Imagine you are trying to break a single dry twig. It snaps almost instantly because all the fibres fail together. Now imagine a **bundle of twigs tied together at intervals** — even if you break several individual twigs, the bundle as a whole continues to support the load. This is precisely how reinforced concrete behaves:

- **Concrete** = the dry twigs. It is **strong in compression** (squashing) but **brittle in tension** (pulling/bending). On its own, a concrete beam under bending will crack and fail on the tension face almost immediately.
- **Steel bars (rebar)** = the rope that ties the bundle. Steel is **strong in tension**. When embedded in the tension zone of the concrete, the steel carries the tensile stresses that concrete cannot sustain.
- **The bond** between steel and concrete (chemical adhesion + mechanical grip of deformed bars) is the "rope" that ensures both materials deflect together as a single composite section.

When a load is applied to a beam, the top fibres get **compressed** (concrete's strength) and the bottom fibres go into **tension** (steel's strength). The genius of reinforced concrete design is recognizing this complementarity and exploiting it.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Weightage (Typical KTU Pattern):**
> - Direct questions on assumptions, design philosophies, and partial safety factors: **3–5 marks** (Part A)
> - Numerical problems on moment of resistance / lever arm / neutral axis: **7–10 marks** (Part B)
> - This topic is **prerequisite** to all subsequent modules (Design of Beams, Slabs, Columns, Footings).

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Design Metrics

The following material partial safety factors ($\gamma_m$) are codified in **IS 456:2000 (Clause 36.4.1)** and are the bedrock values used throughout KTU 2024 Scheme calculations:

- **$\gamma_c$ for concrete** = **1.50** (partial safety factor for material strength of concrete)
- **$\gamma_s$ for steel** = **1.15** (partial safety factor for material strength of steel)
- **Design strength of concrete:** $f_d = 0.45 \, f_{ck}$ (where $f_{ck}$ is characteristic compressive cylinder strength at 28 days)
- **Design strength of steel:** $f_{yd} = f_y / 1.15$ (where $f_y$ is the characteristic yield strength of steel, typically **500 MPa** for Fe500 grade used in KTU design problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** All design strengths in calculations must use the **partial safety factor** values. Using $f_{ck}$ or $f_y$ directly without dividing by $\gamma_m$ is a common error that costs significant marks.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Philosophies of Design

### A. Working Stress Method (WSM) — Historical Background

- **Philosophy:** The structure is designed so that the stresses induced under **service loads** (working loads) remain within the **elastic limits / permissible stresses** of the materials.
- **Safety factor approach:** A single, large factor of safety (typically **3.0 for concrete** and **1.78 for steel** under flexure) is applied to the material strength to obtain permissible stresses.
- **Elastic theory:** Assumes a perfectly **linear-elastic** stress-strain relationship, governed by Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$).
- **Key limitation:** It does not account for the actual **ultimate behaviour** of the structure, nor does it give a realistic estimate of the **safety margin** against collapse.

### B. Limit State Method (LSM) — Current KTU Standard

The **Limit State Method** is the philosophy prescribed by **IS 456:2000** and is the **mandatory** approach for all KTU 2024 Scheme design problems. The structure is designed to satisfy **all relevant limit states**, which are states beyond which the structure becomes unfit for its intended use.

A structure may become unfit due to one of the following:

- **Local damage**
- **Discomfort to the occupants**
- **Failure leading to collapse**

## 2.2 Classification of Limit States (IS 456:2000, Clause 3.3)

There are two principal categories of limit states:

### I. Limit State of Collapse
This considers the **safety** of the structure against collapse under the most adverse combination of design loads.

- The structure is designed to ensure that the design strength is **at least equal to** the design action effect.
- The design action effect is computed using the **design loads** (factored loads).
- The design strength is computed using the **partial safety factors for materials** ($\gamma_m$).

### II. Limit State of Serviceability
This considers the **functional performance** of the structure under service loads, ensuring comfort, appearance, and durability.

Key serviceability checks include:
- **Deflection** (Clause 23.2 of IS 456)
- **Cracking** (Clause 43 of IS 456)
- **Vibration** (Clause 23.3 of IS 456)
- **Durability** (Clause 8 of IS 456)
- **Fire resistance** (Clause 21 of IS 456)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A common mistake is to confuse "service loads" with "design loads." In KTU numerical problems, you must clearly state which load combination is being used in each calculation step. The **Partial Safety Factor for Loads ($\gamma_f$)** is applied to loads, while the **Partial Safety Factor for Materials ($\gamma_m$)** is applied to material strengths.

## 2.3 Load Combinations (Partial Safety Factors for Loads, $\gamma_f$)

Per **IS 456:2000, Table 18**, the partial safety factors for loads are:

- **$\gamma_f$ for Dead Load (DL):** **1.5** (or 1.2 when DL acts to reduce the effect of another load)
- **$\gamma_f$ for Imposed Load (LL):** **1.5**
- **$\gamma_f$ for Wind Load (WL):** **1.2**

The most common KTU design load combination is:

$$
\text{Design Load} = 1.5 \, (\text{DL} + \text{LL})
$$

## 2.4 Assumptions in the Theory of Bending for Reinforced Concrete (IS 456:2000, Clause 22.3)

The behaviour of a reinforced concrete section under flexure (bending) is governed by the following assumptions, which form the theoretical bedrock for all subsequent design calculations:

1. **Plane sections remain plane after bending.** This means that strain is **linearly proportional** to the distance from the neutral axis.
2. **Perfect bond** exists between concrete and steel reinforcement, ensuring that they deform together as a single composite section.
3. **Tensile strength of concrete is ignored** in the analysis, as concrete is assumed to have cracked in the tension zone.
4. **Concrete is assumed to behave elastically in compression** up to a certain limit (specifically, the maximum compressive strain at the outermost compression fibre is taken as **0.0035** for flexural design).
5. **The stress-strain relationship for steel** is taken as an idealized elasto-plastic curve, where the stress remains constant at the design yield strength $f_{yd}$ once the yield strain is exceeded.
6. **The stress block in the concrete compression zone** can be simplified into an **equivalent rectangular stress block** (rectangular parabolic distribution) for design simplicity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Note:** These assumptions are valid for **under-reinforced sections** where steel yields before concrete crushes — the **desirable mode of failure** because it gives visible warning (large deflections and cracks) before collapse.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes all essential formulas required for solving KTU 2024 Scheme problems on this topic. The notation $\vert \cdot \vert$ is used to denote "magnitude of" (to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax).

| **#** | **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula** | **Standard Unit** | **Remarks / KTU Note** |
| :-: | :- | :- | :- | :- |
| 1 | Design strength of concrete | $f_d = 0.45 \, f_{ck} / 1.50$ | MPa (N/mm²) | $f_{ck}$ is characteristic cube strength at 28 days |
| 2 | Design strength of steel | $f_{yd} = f_y / 1.15$ | MPa (N/mm²) | For Fe500: $f_{yd} = 500/1.15 = 434.78$ MPa |
| 3 | Maximum compressive strain in concrete | $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$ | dimensionless | Used for ultimate flexural design |
| 4 | Modular ratio (WSM only) | $m = 280 / (3 \, \sigma_{cbc})$ | dimensionless | Not used in LSM (KTU 2024) |
| 5 | Lever arm distance | $z = d \, (1 - k/3)$ | mm | $k$ = neutral axis depth factor |
| 6 | Moment of resistance (singly reinforced, balanced) | $M_{u,\lim} = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, \frac{x_{u,\max}}{d} \, (1 - 0.42 \, x_{u,\max}/d) \, b \, d^2$ | N·mm | $x_{u,\max}/d = 0.48$ for Fe500 |
| 7 | Simplified form of $M_{u,\lim}$ for Fe500 | $M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$ | N·mm | Very high-frequency in KTU numericals |
| 8 | Area of steel for singly reinforced section | $A_{st} = \frac{M_u}{0.87 \, f_y \, z}$ | mm² | $z = d(1 - 0.42 \cdot x_u/d)$ for Fe500 |
| 9 | Neutral axis depth factor | $k = x_u / d$ | dimensionless | $k_{u,\max} = 0.48$ for Fe500 |
| 10 | Force equilibrium (singly reinforced) | $C = T \Rightarrow 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}$ | N | Fundamental equilibrium equation |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles covered in this topic are not merely academic — they form the **design backbone** of every major civil engineering project in Kerala, including:

- **Residential and commercial buildings** (KTU-affiliated curricula often use G+3 building projects for design exercises)
- **Bridges and flyovers** (where limit state of collapse for moving vehicular loads is critical)
- **Water-retaining structures** (where serviceability limit state of cracking is the controlling criterion)
- **Industrial structures** (where the limit state of vibration and fatigue becomes significant)
- **Coastal structures in Kerala** (where the durability limit state, including resistance to chloride attack, is paramount)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why WSM is still mentioned in KTU 2024:** While LSM is mandatory for new designs, WSM appears in some **serviceability-based preliminary checks** and in the **assessment of existing structures** built under older codes. KTU examiners test this for conceptual clarity, not for active design.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Equivalent Rectangular Stress Block (Whitney Stress Block)

### Background
The actual stress distribution in the concrete compression zone of a reinforced concrete beam at the limit state of collapse is **parabolic** (a curve). For design simplicity, IS 456:2000 (Clause 22.4.1) and **ACI 318** permit this curve to be replaced by an **equivalent uniform rectangular stress block** of intensity $0.446 \, f_{ck}$ acting over a depth of $0.42 \, x_u$ from the extreme compression fibre.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Consider the actual stress-strain curve of concrete.**

At ultimate limit state, the maximum compressive strain at the extreme compression fibre is:

$$
\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035
$$

The strain varies **linearly** across the depth (from plane sections assumption). So at the neutral axis (depth $x_u$), strain is zero; at depth $y$ below the top fibre, strain is:

$$
\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_{cu} \cdot \frac{(x_u - y)}{x_u}
$$

**Step 2: The stress corresponding to this strain.**

Using the parabolic stress-strain relationship for concrete (per IS 456):

$$
f_c(y) = 0.446 \, f_{ck} \cdot \left[ 2 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_0} - \left( \frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_0} \right)^2 \right]
$$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 0.002$ is the strain at peak stress.

**Step 3: Compute the total compressive force (C).**

Integrate the stress over the compression zone area:

$$
C = b \int_{0}^{x_u} f_c(y) \, dy
$$

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify.**

After performing the integration and using the standard result from limit state analysis:

$$
C = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u
$$

**Step 5: Locate the centroid of the rectangular stress block.**

The equivalent rectangular block has:
- **Depth** = $0.42 \, x_u$
- **Intensity** = $0.446 \, f_{ck}$ (effectively $0.36 \, f_{ck}/0.42 \approx 0.857 \cdot 0.42 \cdot f_{ck}$)

This means the total force $C = 0.446 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot (0.42 \, x_u) = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u$. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** The factor **0.36** in the compression force equation is the product of **$0.446 \, f_{ck}$** (stress block intensity) and **$0.42 \, x_u$** (stress block depth), divided by 1.0 (no additional factor) — but commonly cited with the 0.36 already applied. This is the **single most-tested** constant in KTU Module 2 design problems.

## 3.2 Derivation: Lever Arm (z) for Singly Reinforced Section

### Setup
For a singly reinforced rectangular beam with width $b$ and effective depth $d$, the compression force $C$ acts at a distance equal to the lever arm $z$ from the centroid of the tension reinforcement.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Establish the geometry of the stress block.**

The rectangular stress block has depth $0.42 \, x_u$ measured from the top fibre.

**Step 2: Locate the centroid of this block.**

The centroid of a uniform rectangle of depth $0.42 \, x_u$ lies at half its depth from the top:

$$
\text{Distance from top} = \frac{0.42 \, x_u}{2} = 0.21 \, x_u
$$

**Step 3: Compute the lever arm $z$.**

The lever arm is the distance from the centroid of tension steel to the centroid of the compression block:

$$
z = d - 0.21 \, x_u
$$

**Step 4: Express in terms of neutral axis depth factor $k = x_u/d$.**

$$
z = d - 0.21 \, k \, d = d \, (1 - 0.21 \, k)
$$

**Step 5: Substitute the balanced section value $k = 0.48$ for Fe500.**

$$
z = d \, (1 - 0.21 \times 0.48) = d \, (1 - 0.1008) = 0.8992 \, d \approx 0.9 \, d
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Practice:** In the simplified design formula $M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$, the lever arm is taken as $z = 0.9 \, d$ (for Fe500 with $k_{u,\max} = 0.48$). Always verify whether your KTU problem data uses Fe415 or Fe500 to know which $k_{u,\max}$ applies.

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Limiting Moment of Resistance ($M_{u,\lim}$)

### Condition
The section is **balanced** when the neutral axis depth is at its maximum permitted value, i.e., $x_u = x_{u,\max} = k_{u,\max} \cdot d$.

For **Fe500** grade steel: $k_{u,\max} = 0.48$.
For **Fe415** grade steel: $k_{u,\max} = 0.48$ (same as Fe500 for IS 456 limit state).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Apply force equilibrium $C = T$.**

$$
0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_{u,\max} = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st,\lim}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the moment of resistance.**

$$
M_{u,\lim} = C \cdot z = (0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_{u,\max}) \cdot (d - 0.42 \, x_{u,\max})
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $x_{u,\max} = 0.48 \, d$ for Fe500.**

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \cdot (0.48 \, d) \cdot (d - 0.42 \times 0.48 \, d)
$$

**Step 4: Simplify the inner bracket.**

$$
d - 0.42 \times 0.48 \, d = d - 0.2016 \, d = 0.7984 \, d
$$

**Step 5: Multiply all terms.**

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.36 \times 0.48 \times 0.7984 \times f_{ck} \, b \, d^2
$$

**Step 6: Compute the numerical constant.**

$$
0.36 \times 0.48 = 0.1728
$$
$$
0.1728 \times 0.7984 = 0.138 \, (\text{rounded to 3 decimal places})
$$

**Step 7: Final formula.**

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For Fe415 grade steel**, the same formula $M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$ applies because IS 456 specifies the same $k_{u,\max} = 0.48$ for both Fe415 and Fe500 under LSM. The difference appears in the **area of steel** formula, where the stress block factor 0.87 and yield strength $f_y$ differ.

## 3.4 Derivation: Area of Tension Steel ($A_{st}$) for Singly Reinforced Section

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: From force equilibrium $C = T$.**

$$
0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $A_{st}$.**

$$
A_{st} = \frac{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u}{0.87 \, f_y}
$$

**Step 3: Use the moment equation to find $x_u$.**

From $M_u = C \cdot z = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u \cdot (d - 0.42 \, x_u)$:

$$
M_u = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u \, d \cdot (1 - 0.42 \, x_u / d)
$$

**Step 4: Define $k = x_u/d$, giving $x_u = kd$.**

$$
M_u = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2 \, k \, (1 - 0.42 \, k)
$$

**Step 5: Solve the quadratic in $k$.**

Expanding and rearranging:

$$
0.1512 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2 \, k^2 - 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2 \, k + M_u = 0
$$

Dividing through by $f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$:

$$
0.1512 \, k^2 - 0.36 \, k + \frac{M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2} = 0
$$

This quadratic can be solved for $k$ using the standard formula. Once $k$ is known, $x_u$ follows, and $A_{st}$ can be computed from Step 2.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Shortcut for Numericals:** When the given moment $M_u \le M_{u,\lim}$, the section is **under-reinforced** (desirable), and you can directly use:
> - $A_{st} = \frac{M_u}{0.87 \, f_y \cdot z}$
> - where $z = d(1 - 0.42 \cdot k)$, with $k$ found from the quadratic above.
> - Or, use the iterative approach: assume $z = 0.9 \, d$ as a first guess, compute $A_{st}$, then refine $z$ from equilibrium.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Section Type Identification

| **Section Type** | **Steel Ratio $p_t = A_{st} / (b \cdot d)$** | **Failure Mode** | **KTU Recommendation** |
| :- | :- | :- | :- |
| Under-reinforced | $p_t < p_{t,\lim}$ | Steel yields **first** (ductile) | **Preferred design choice** — gives warning before failure |
| Balanced | $p_t = p_{t,\lim}$ | Steel yields **and** concrete crushes simultaneously | **Upper limit** for KTU design — never exceed this |
| Over-reinforced | $p_t > p_{t,\lim}$ | Concrete crushes **first** (brittle) | **Forbidden** in KTU 2024 design — failure is sudden |

The **limiting steel ratio** for Fe500 is:

$$
p_{t,\lim} = \frac{0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot 0.48}{0.87 \cdot f_y} = \frac{0.1728 \cdot f_{ck}}{0.87 \cdot f_y}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Mistake:** A "balanced section" is **not the same** as a "doubly reinforced section." A balanced section has the **maximum permissible** amount of tension steel in a singly reinforced beam, while a doubly reinforced section adds compression steel to handle moments **exceeding** $M_{u,\lim}$.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Limit State Method Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Service Loads] --> B[Compute Design Loads]
    B --> C[Apply Partial Safety Factors gamma_f]
    C --> D[Compute Design Moment Mu and Design Shear Vu]
    D --> E{Is Mu less than or equal to Mu_lim?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Design as Singly Reinforced Section]
    E -- No --> G[Design as Doubly Reinforced Section]
    F --> H[Compute Area of Steel Ast]
    H --> I[Check Serviceability: Deflection and Cracking]
    I --> J[Provide Detailing: Spacing, Cover, Anchorage]
    J --> K[End: Final Design]
    G --> L[Compute Additional Compression Steel Asc]
    L --> I
    I --> M{Serviceability Satisfied?}
    M -- No --> N[Revise Section Dimensions]
    N --> D
    M -- Yes --> K
```

**Explanation of the flowchart:**

- The design process begins with **service loads** (Dead Load + Live Load).
- These are **factored** using $\gamma_f = 1.5$ to obtain **design loads** per IS 456:2000.
- The **design moment** ($M_u$) is compared against the **limiting moment of resistance** ($M_{u,\lim}$).
- If $M_u \le M_{u,\lim}$, a **singly reinforced section** suffices.
- If $M_u > M_{u,\lim}$, a **doubly reinforced section** is required (covered in Module 3).
- **Serviceability checks** (deflection, cracking) are always performed, even after the collapse check.
- The design is **iterative** — if serviceability fails, dimensions are revised.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stress and Strain Distribution at Limit State of Collapse

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Beam Cross Section] --> B[Compression Zone: Top Fibres]
    A --> C[Neutral Axis: depth xu from top]
    A --> D[Tension Zone: Bottom Fibres]
    B --> E[Stress Block: 0.446 fck over 0.42 xu]
    C --> F[Strain: 0.0035 at top, 0 at neutral axis]
    D --> G[Steel Stress: 0.87 fy at yield]
    E --> H[Compression Force C]
    G --> I[Tension Force T]
    H --> J[Equilibrium: C equals T]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Resultant Moment Mu equals C times z]
```

**Explanation of the block diagram:**

- The beam cross-section is divided into three zones: **compression** (top), **neutral axis** (middle), and **tension** (bottom).
- The **concrete stress block** at the limit state is rectangular, with intensity **$0.446 \, f_{ck}$** and depth **$0.42 \, x_u$**.
- The **strain** varies linearly from **$0.0035$** at the top to **zero** at the neutral axis.
- The **steel** at the bottom has reached its **yield stress** ($0.87 \, f_y$ in design calculations).
- **Equilibrium** of forces ($C = T$) gives the moment of resistance $M_u = C \cdot z$.

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison: WSM vs LSM Philosophies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WSM[Working Stress Method]
        A1[Service Loads] --> A2[Compute Working Stresses]
        A2 --> A3[Compare with Permissible Stresses]
        A3 --> A4{Permissible Satisfied?}
        A4 -- Yes --> A5[Safe Design]
        A4 -- No --> A6[Revise Section]
    end
    subgraph LSM[Limit State Method]
        B1[Service Loads] --> B2[Apply gamma_f equals 1.5]
        B2 --> B3[Compute Design Loads]
        B3 --> B4[Compute Design Strength using gamma_m]
        B4 --> B5[Check Collapse Limit State]
        B5 --> B6[Check Serviceability Limit State]
        B6 --> B7[Detailing and Final Design]
    end
```

**Explanation:**

- **WSM** uses a **single** safety check (stress under service load < permissible stress).
- **LSM** uses **two** checks: (1) collapse limit state (using design loads and design strengths), and (2) serviceability limit state (deflection, cracking).
- The **partial safety factors** in LSM ($\gamma_f$ for loads, $\gamma_m$ for materials) replace the single factor of safety in WSM.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Note:** All schematics above are functional block diagrams. For physical drawings of beam cross-sections showing reinforcement, refer to standard textbook figures (e.g., the rectangular stress block and strain diagram). Mermaid is used here to depict the **logical flow** of the design methodology, which is the primary KTU examination expectation.
