# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*Explain the concept of "characteristic strength" as per IS 456:2000. How is it different from "design strength"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Characteristic strength** is defined as the value of the material strength below which **not more than 5%** of the test results are expected to fall. In other words, it is the strength value that has a 95% probability of being exceeded.

For concrete: $f_{ck}$ is the characteristic compressive strength of 150 mm cubes at 28 days (typically M20, M25, M30, etc., indicating $f_{ck} = 20, 25, 30$ MPa).

For steel: $f_y$ is the characteristic yield strength (e.g., Fe500 means $f_y = 500$ MPa).

**Design strength** is the characteristic strength divided by the **partial safety factor for material** ($\gamma_m$):
- Concrete: $f_d = 0.45 \, f_{ck} / 1.50$ (in LSM)
- Steel: $f_{yd} = f_y / 1.15$

The design strength represents the **reduced strength** used in limit state calculations, accounting for material variability and quality control uncertainties.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — December 2022]**
*List the partial safety factors for loads and materials as per IS 456:2000. State the most common load combination.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Partial Safety Factors for Loads ($\gamma_f$)** per IS 456:2000, Table 18:
- Dead Load (DL): $\gamma_f = 1.5$
- Imposed Load (LL): $\gamma_f = 1.5$
- Wind Load (WL): $\gamma_f = 1.2$

**Partial Safety Factors for Materials ($\gamma_m$)** per IS 456:2000, Clause 36.4.1:
- Concrete: $\gamma_c = 1.50$
- Steel: $\gamma_s = 1.15$

**Most common load combination:** $1.5 \, (DL + LL)$ — used in the majority of KTU design problems for buildings.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Module typically has a Part B question (14 marks) with **internal choice** — the student picks ONE of two alternatives. Both alternatives are provided below with full sub-parts and model solutions.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**

A reinforced concrete beam has the following design data:
- Width: $b = 250$ mm
- Effective depth: $d = 500$ mm
- Grade of concrete: M25 ($f_{ck} = 25$ MPa)
- Grade of steel: Fe500 ($f_y = 500$ MPa)
- Factored bending moment: $M_u = 175$ kN·m

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Check whether the section can be designed as singly reinforced. If yes, determine the area of tension steel required.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Design the shear reinforcement (stirrups) for the same beam, given that the factored shear force is $V_u = 120$ kN and the steel consists of 4 bars of 20 mm diameter.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a): Singly Reinforced Section Design**

**Step 1: Compute the limiting moment of resistance $M_{u,\lim}$ for Fe500.**

For Fe500, $k_{u,\max} = 0.48$, and the simplified formula is:

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2
$$

Substituting values:

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \times 25 \times 250 \times (500)^2
$$

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \times 25 \times 250 \times 250000
$$

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \times 1562500000
$$

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 215625000 \text{ N·mm} = 215.625 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 2: Compare $M_u$ with $M_{u,\lim}$.**

$$
M_u = 175 \text{ kN·m} < M_{u,\lim} = 215.625 \text{ kN·m}
$$

Therefore, the section can be designed as **singly reinforced**. ✓

**Step 3: Find the neutral axis depth factor $k$.**

Using the quadratic in $k$:

$$
0.1512 \, k^2 - 0.36 \, k + \frac{M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2} = 0
$$

Compute $\frac{M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2}$:

$$
\frac{175 \times 10^6}{25 \times 250 \times 250000} = \frac{175 \times 10^6}{1562500000} = 0.112
$$

The quadratic becomes:

$$
0.1512 \, k^2 - 0.36 \, k + 0.112 = 0
$$

Using the quadratic formula $k = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:

$$
k = \frac{0.36 \pm \sqrt{(0.36)^2 - 4 \times 0.1512 \times 0.112}}{2 \times 0.1512}
$$

$$
k = \frac{0.36 \pm \sqrt{0.1296 - 0.0677376}}{0.3024}
$$

$$
k = \frac{0.36 \pm \sqrt{0.0618624}}{0.3024} = \frac{0.36 \pm 0.2487}{0.3024}
$$

Taking the smaller root (valid for under-reinforced section):

$$
k = \frac{0.36 - 0.2487}{0.3024} = \frac{0.1113}{0.3024} = 0.368
$$

**Step 4: Compute $x_u$ and lever arm $z$.**

$$
x_u = k \cdot d = 0.368 \times 500 = 184 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
z = d \, (1 - 0.42 \, k) = 500 \times (1 - 0.42 \times 0.368) = 500 \times (1 - 0.1546) = 500 \times 0.8454 = 422.7 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $A_{st}$.**

$$
A_{st} = \frac{M_u}{0.87 \, f_y \, z} = \frac{175 \times 10^6}{0.87 \times 500 \times 422.7}
$$

$$
A_{st} = \frac{175 \times 10^6}{183874.5} = 951.7 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 6: Provide bars.**

Using 16 mm diameter bars, each bar has area $\frac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 201.06$ mm².

Number of bars $= 951.7 / 201.06 = 4.73 \approx 5$ bars of 16 mm diameter.

**Provide 5 bars of 16 mm diameter** ($A_{st,\text{provided}} = 5 \times 201.06 = 1005.3$ mm² > 951.7 mm²) ✓

---

**Part (b): Shear Reinforcement Design**

**Step 1: Compute the design shear strength of concrete $\tau_c$.**

For M25 concrete with $p_t = \frac{A_{st}}{b \cdot d} = \frac{1005.3}{250 \times 500} = 0.804\%$.

From IS 456:2000, Table 19, for $p_t = 0.80\%$ and M25 concrete: $\tau_c = 0.60$ MPa (interpolated, approximate value).

**Step 2: Compute the shear resisted by concrete $V_c$.**

$$
V_c = \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d = 0.60 \times 250 \times 500 = 75000 \text{ N} = 75 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the shear to be resisted by stirrups $V_{us}$.**

$$
V_{us} = V_u - V_c = 120 - 75 = 45 \text{ kN} = 45000 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4: Design 2-legged 8 mm diameter stirrups.**

Area of stirrup legs: $A_{sv} = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 8^2 = 2 \times 50.27 = 100.55$ mm²

Using Fe415 stirrups ($f_y = 415$ MPa, but use 0.87 factor):

For 2-legged 8 mm $\phi$ stirrups, the spacing $s_v$ is:

$$
s_v = \frac{0.87 \times f_y \times A_{sv} \times d}{V_{us}} = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 100.55 \times 500}{45000}
$$

$$
s_v = \frac{18137586}{45000} = 403 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Apply IS 456 minimum spacing provisions.**

Per IS 456:2000, Clause 26.5.1.5, the **minimum shear reinforcement** must satisfy:

$$
\frac{A_{sv}}{b \cdot s_v} \ge \frac{0.4}{0.87 \times f_y} = \frac{0.4}{0.87 \times 415} = 0.00111
$$

Solving for maximum spacing:

$$
s_v \le \frac{A_{sv}}{b \times 0.00111} = \frac{100.55}{250 \times 0.00111} = 362.3 \text{ mm}
$$

Also, per Clause 26.5.1.6, the **maximum spacing** is the lesser of $0.75 \, d = 375$ mm or 300 mm.

**Step 6: Adopt the most conservative value.**

Provide 2-legged 8 mm $\phi$ stirrups at **$s_v = 300$ mm c/c** (governed by maximum spacing criterion). ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**

A reinforced concrete rectangular beam is to be designed for a residential building with the following data:
- Span: 6 m
- Overall dimensions: $b = 300$ mm, $D = 600$ mm
- Slab thickness supported: 120 mm
- Live load: 4 kN/m²
- Floor finish: 1.5 kN/m²
- Grade of concrete: M20
- Grade of steel: Fe500
- Spacing of beams: 3 m c/c

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Compute the design load and design moment for the beam.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Design the section as singly reinforced, compute the area of steel, and check for serviceability (deflection).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a): Load and Moment Calculation**

**Step 1: Compute Dead Load (DL).**

Self-weight of beam: $0.30 \times (0.60 - 0.12) \times 25 = 0.30 \times 0.48 \times 25 = 3.6$ kN/m

Weight of slab (1 m strip on beam): $0.12 \times 1.0 \times 25 = 3.0$ kN/m (assuming the slab spans between beams, 1 m width on each beam out of 3 m spacing contributes 0.5 m on each side; here simplified as full 1 m tributary)

Wall load (if applicable): Neglected for this problem.

Floor finish: $1.5 \times 1.0 = 1.5$ kN/m

Total DL $= 3.6 + 3.0 + 1.5 = 8.1$ kN/m

**Step 2: Compute Live Load (LL).**

LL $= 4 \times 1.0 = 4.0$ kN/m (tributary width considered)

Total service load $= 8.1 + 4.0 = 12.1$ kN/m

**Step 3: Apply partial safety factors to get design load.**

$$
w_u = 1.5 \times (DL + LL) = 1.5 \times 12.1 = 18.15 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the design moment for a simply supported beam.**

$$
M_u = \frac{w_u \times L^2}{8} = \frac{18.15 \times 6^2}{8} = \frac{18.15 \times 36}{8} = 81.675 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the design shear.**

$$
V_u = \frac{w_u \times L}{2} = \frac{18.15 \times 6}{2} = 54.45 \text{ kN}
$$

---

**Part (b): Singly Reinforced Design and Serviceability Check**

**Step 1: Effective depth and clear cover.**

Assuming 25 mm clear cover and 16 mm diameter bars, effective cover $= 25 + 8 = 33$ mm. Use $d = 600 - 33 = 567$ mm ≈ 560 mm (round to 550 or 560 mm as per KTU convention).

Let's take $d = 550$ mm.

**Step 2: Compute $M_{u,\lim}$.**

For M20 ($f_{ck} = 20$ MPa) and Fe500:

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 300 \times (550)^2
$$

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 300 \times 302500
$$

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \times 1815000000
$$

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 250470000 \text{ N·mm} = 250.47 \text{ kN·m}
$$

Since $M_u = 81.675$ kN·m $< M_{u,\lim} = 250.47$ kN·m, the section is **singly reinforced** (under-reinforced). ✓

**Step 3: Find $k$ from the quadratic.**

$$
\frac{M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2} = \frac{81.675 \times 10^6}{20 \times 300 \times 302500} = \frac{81.675 \times 10^6}{1815000000} = 0.045
$$

The quadratic is:

$$
0.1512 \, k^2 - 0.36 \, k + 0.045 = 0
$$

$$
k = \frac{0.36 \pm \sqrt{0.1296 - 0.027216}}{0.3024} = \frac{0.36 \pm \sqrt{0.102384}}{0.3024} = \frac{0.36 \pm 0.3200}{0.3024}
$$

Smaller root: $k = \frac{0.36 - 0.3200}{0.3024} = \frac{0.040}{0.3024} = 0.1323$

**Step 4: Compute $A_{st}$.**

$$
z = d(1 - 0.42 \, k) = 550 \times (1 - 0.42 \times 0.1323) = 550 \times (1 - 0.0556) = 550 \times 0.9444 = 519.4 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
A_{st} = \frac{M_u}{0.87 \, f_y \cdot z} = \frac{81.675 \times 10^6}{0.87 \times 500 \times 519.4}
$$

$$
A_{st} = \frac{81.675 \times 10^6}{225939} = 361.5 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 5: Provide bars.**

Using 12 mm $\phi$ bars, each has area $113.1$ mm².

Number of bars $= 361.5 / 113.1 = 3.2 \approx 4$ bars of 12 mm $\phi$.

**Provide 4 bars of 12 mm diameter** ($A_{st,\text{provided}} = 4 \times 113.1 = 452.4$ mm²) ✓

**Step 6: Deflection check (serviceability).**

Per IS 456:2000, Clause 23.2, the basic $L/d$ ratio for a simply supported beam = 20.

Allowable $L/d$ modification factor (for $p_t = \frac{452.4}{300 \times 550} = 0.274\%$ and $f_s = 0.58 \times f_y \times \frac{A_{st,\text{req}}}{A_{st,\text{prov}}} = 0.58 \times 500 \times \frac{361.5}{452.4} = 232$ MPa):

From IS 456:2000, Fig. 4 (reproduced in textbooks), the modification factor $\approx 1.0$ (for low $p_t$ and moderate $f_s$).

Allowable $L/d = 20 \times 1.0 = 20$.

Actual $L/d = 6000 / 550 = 10.91$.

Since $10.91 < 20$, **deflection is satisfactory** ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to apply $\gamma_f$ to loads** — always check whether the given moment is **service moment** or **design moment** ($M_u$). If the problem says "working load," multiply by 1.5 first.
>
> 2. **Using $f_{ck}$ directly without $0.45/1.50$ factor** — in LSM, the constant is **$0.36$** in the compression force equation, which already incorporates the partial safety factor. Using $f_d = 0.45 \, f_{ck}$ directly is wrong.
>
> 3. **Confusing $0.87 \, f_y$ with $f_y$** — the factor 0.87 in the steel force equation accounts for the partial safety factor for steel ($1/1.15 = 0.87$). Using $f_y$ directly is a common error.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the 0.42 factor in lever arm** — students often write $z = d$ instead of $z = 0.9 \, d$. This gives incorrect (overestimated) $A_{st}$ and may exceed code limits.
>
> 5. **Not checking whether the section is under-reinforced** — always verify that $M_u \le M_{u,\lim}$. If $M_u > M_{u,\lim}$, the problem requires doubly reinforced design (a different formula set).
>
> 6. **Forgetting to provide minimum shear reinforcement** — even if $V_u \le V_c$, minimum stirrups must be provided (per IS 456, Clause 26.5.1.6).
>
> 7. **Sign convention errors in doubly reinforced sections** — careful with positive and negative moments when applying the formula $M_{u2} = M_u - M_{u,\lim}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap: High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 Rapid-Fire Revision Points:**
>
> - ✅ **Limit State Method (LSM)** is the mandatory design philosophy per KTU 2024 Scheme and IS 456:2000.
> - ✅ **Partial Safety Factors:** $\gamma_c = 1.5$ (concrete), $\gamma_s = 1.15$ (steel), $\gamma_f = 1.5$ (loads).
> - ✅ **Design strength of concrete:** $f_d = 0.45 \, f_{ck}$ (used in constant $0.36$ after dividing by $\gamma_c$).
> - ✅ **Design strength of steel:** $f_{yd} = f_y / 1.15 \approx 0.87 \, f_y$ (the $0.87$ factor used in design equations).
> - ✅ **Maximum compressive strain in concrete:** $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$.
> - ✅ **Neutral axis depth factor for Fe500 and Fe415:** $k_{u,\max} = x_{u,\max} / d = 0.48$.
> - ✅ **Limiting moment of resistance:** $M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$ (for Fe500/Fe415 in LSM).
> - ✅ **Compression force equation:** $C = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u$.
> - ✅ **Tension force equation:** $T = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}$.
> - ✅ **Lever arm:** $z = d(1 - 0.42 \, k)$, where $k = x_u / d$.
> - ✅ **Under-reinforced section** is preferred (steel yields first, ductile failure).
> - ✅ **Over-reinforced section** is forbidden (brittle failure without warning).
> - ✅ **Assumptions of bending theory:** plane sections remain plane, perfect bond, concrete has no tensile strength, $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$, rectangular stress block ($0.446 \, f_{ck}$ over $0.42 \, x_u$).
> - ✅ **Serviceability checks:** deflection ($L/d$ ratio per IS 456 Clause 23.2), cracking (Clause 43), durability (Clause 8).
> - ✅ **Load combination for buildings:** $1.5(DL + LL)$ is the most frequently tested.
> - ✅ **Difference between WSM and LSM:** WSM uses single safety factor on stresses, LSM uses partial safety factors on loads AND materials, with separate collapse and serviceability checks.
> - ✅ **Characteristic strength:** value below which not more than 5% of test results fall (95% confidence).
> - ✅ **Equilibrium equation:** $C = T \Rightarrow 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}$.

---

**End of KTU Module 2 Notes — Basic Principles, Theory and Design**

topics.title: Design of Two-way Slabs - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Two-way Slabs - Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (IS 456:2000)

A **Two-Way Slab** is a reinforced concrete slab in which the ratio of longer span ($L_y$) to shorter span ($L_x$) is less than or equal to **2.0**, and the load is transferred to the supporting beams/walls by bending in **both orthogonal directions**. The slab exhibits curvature in two principal directions, and reinforcement must be provided along both the short and long spans.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 456:2000, Clause 24.1** — When the ratio $L_y/L_x \le 2.0$, the slab is designed as a two-way slab. When $L_y/L_x > 2.0$, the slab is designed as a one-way slab (bending predominantly along the short direction).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crossed Plank" Model

Imagine a **chessboard-like floor** of a typical classroom resting on beams along all four edges. If you stand in the middle of this floor, your weight (a point load) does not travel only to the nearest beam — it splits into **two streams**: one travelling toward the *shorter pair* of beams, and the other toward the *longer pair* of beams. The fraction reaching each pair depends on the panel's aspect ratio.

- If the room were **very long and narrow** (corridor-like, $L_y/L_x > 2$), almost all the load rushes to the two short-end beams → **One-way behaviour**.
- If the room is **near-square** ($L_y/L_x \le 2$), the load **splits roughly equally** in both directions → **Two-way behaviour**.

This is precisely why IS 456 mandates two-way design only when $L_y/L_x \le 2.0$.

## 1.3 Why Two-Way Slab Design is Critical in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> Two-way slabs constitute one of the **highest-weightage numerical problems** in KTU's PCCVT403 (typically a full 14-mark Part B question). Mastery of:
> - **IS 456 Table 12** coefficient selection (9 panel types),
> - **Effective span** rules (Clause 22.2),
> - **Limit State of Flexure** (Clause 38.1),
> - **Deflection control** (Clause 23.2),
> - **Shear check** (Clause 40),
>
> is mandatory for scoring 90%+ in Module 2.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Parameters (KTU 2024 Defaults)

| Parameter | Value | Source |
|:----------|:------|:-------|
| Partial safety factor for DL | **$\gamma_f = 1.5$** | IS 456:2000, Table 18 |
| Partial safety factor for LL | **$\gamma_f = 1.5$** | IS 456:2000, Table 18 |
| Concrete grade (default) | **M25** ($f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm²) | KTU syllabus |
| Steel grade (default) | **Fe415** ($f_y = 415$ N/mm²) | KTU syllabus |
| Clear cover for slabs | **20 mm** | IS 456:2000, Table 16 |
| Unit weight of RCC | **25 kN/m³** | IS 875 Part I |
| Effective flange width factor | **$0.138\,f_{ck}$** (Fe415) | Limit State flexure |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification Logic for Slabs

$$
\text{Slab Type} = \begin{cases} \text{One-Way Slab} & \text{if } L_y/L_x > 2.0 \\ \text{Two-Way Slab} & \text{if } L_y/L_x \le 2.0 \end{cases}
$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Use | Code Reference |
|:-:|:--------|:----|:--------------:|
| 1 | $L_{ex} = $ clear span + effective depth ($d$) | Effective short span (lesser of clear+d or c/c) | IS 456 Cl. 22.2(a) |
| 2 | $M_x = \alpha_x \cdot w \cdot L_x^2$ | Short span BM coefficient (Table 12) | IS 456 Table 12 |
| 3 | $M_y = \alpha_y \cdot w \cdot L_x^2$ | Long span BM coefficient (Table 12) | IS 456 Table 12 |
| 4 | $w = 1.5(DL + LL)$ | Factored UDL on slab | IS 456 Table 18 |
| 5 | $d_{req} = \sqrt{\dfrac{M_u}{0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b}}$ (for Fe415) | Trial effective depth | Cl. 38.1 |
| 6 | $A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5 \, f_{ck}}{f_y}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2}}\right] b d$ | Tension steel area | Cl. 38.1 (LSM) |
| 7 | $A_{st,min} = 0.12\% \, b \, D$ (Fe415/HYSD bars) | Minimum flexural steel | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.2.1 |
| 8 | $\tau_v = \dfrac{V_u}{b \, d}$ | Nominal shear stress | IS 456 Cl. 40.1 |
| 9 | $\tau_c$ (from Table 19) | Design shear strength of concrete | IS 456 Table 19 |
| 10 | $(L/d)_{basic} \times \tau_s \times \tau_f$ | Deflection check | IS 456 Cl. 23.2, Fig. 4 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula 2 & 3 note:** IS 456 Table 12 expresses BOTH moments using $L_x^2$ — the coefficients $\alpha_y$ are *pre-multiplied* by $(L_y/L_x)^2$ internally, so the same $L_x^2$ term is used for both directions.

## 2.3 IS 456:2000 Table 12 — Panel Type Classification

The table gives coefficients for **9 panel types** (Type 1 to Type 9) based on edge continuity. The most important types for KTU are:

| Panel Type | Description | Typical Location |
|:-----------|:------------|:-----------------|
| **Type 1** | Interior panel, all four edges continuous/discontinuous over supports | Inside a multi-panel slab |
| **Type 2** | One short edge discontinuous, all others continuous | Edge panel, parallel to short span |
| **Type 3** | One long edge discontinuous, all others continuous | Edge panel, parallel to long span |
| **Type 4** | Two short edges discontinuous, all others continuous | — |
| **Type 5** | Two long edges discontinuous, all others continuous | — |
| **Type 6** | Two adjacent edges discontinuous, others continuous | Corner panel |
| **Type 9** | All four edges simply supported (no continuity) | Isolated single panel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU numerical problems, **Type 1, Type 2, Type 3, and Type 9** are the most frequently asked. Students must correctly identify the panel type from the **plan sketch** in the question.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Design Logic

1. **Identify panel type** (from plan) and compute $L_y/L_x$.
2. **Compute effective span** $L_{ex} = $ min(clear span + $d$, centre-to-centre of supports).
3. **Compute loads**: DL (self-weight + finishes) and LL, then $w = 1.5(DL + LL)$.
4. **Read coefficients** $\alpha_x$ and $\alpha_y$ from IS 456 Table 12 for the relevant panel type and $L_y/L_x$ ratio.
5. **Compute design moments** $M_x$ and $M_y$ using formulas (2) and (3).
6. **Trial effective depth** using formula (5).
7. **Check deflection** using the span/depth basic ratio and modification factors.
8. **Design tension steel** $A_{st}$ for the larger moment (short span).
9. **Check minimum steel** and provide bars.
10. **Check shear** $\tau_v \le \tau_c$ (Table 19).
11. **Detail reinforcement** (cranking, curtailment, corner ties).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX — WORKED NUMERICAL PROBLEMS

---

## 3.1 PROBLEM 1 — SIMPLY SUPPORTED TWO-WAY SLAB (Type 9)

> **Problem Statement:** Design a simply supported two-way slab for a room with the following data:
> - Clear panel dimensions: **3.5 m × 5.0 m**
> - Wall thickness: **230 mm** on all four sides
> - Live load: **4 kN/m²**
> - Floor finish: **1.5 kN/m²**
> - Materials: **M25 concrete, Fe415 steel**
> - Exposure: Mild
>
> Sketch the reinforcement details.

### STEP 1 — Classification Check

$$
\frac{L_y}{L_x} = \frac{5.0}{3.5} = 1.43 \le 2.0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Design as TWO-WAY SLAB}
$$

### STEP 2 — Trial Effective Depth & Slab Thickness

For simply supported slabs (per IS 456 Cl. 23.2.1, basic $L/d = 20$ for span $\le 3.5$ m with Fe415):

$$
d_{\text{basic,min}} = \frac{L_x}{20} = \frac{3500}{20} = 175 \text{ mm (before modification)}
$$

With modification factor $\tau_s \approx 1.25$ (for low $p_t \approx 0.4\%$) and $\tau_f = 1.0$:

$$
d_{\text{req}} = \frac{L_x}{20 \times 1.25} \approx 140 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt **overall depth $D = 150$ mm**, with 12 mm main bars and 20 mm clear cover:

$$
d = 150 - 20 - 6 = 124 \text{ mm} \approx 125 \text{ mm}
$$

### STEP 3 — Effective Spans

Per IS 456 Cl. 22.2(a), for simply supported slab, $L_{ex}$ = lesser of:
- (a) clear span + effective depth
- (b) centre-to-centre of supports

$$
L_x = \min(3500 + 125,\ 3500 + 230) = \min(3625,\ 3730) = 3625 \text{ mm} = 3.625 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_y = \min(5000 + 125,\ 5000 + 230) = 5125 \text{ mm} = 5.125 \text{ m}
$$

### STEP 4 — Load Calculations

$$
\text{Self-weight of slab} = 0.150 \times 25 = 3.75 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Floor finish} = 1.50 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Total Dead Load (DL)} = 5.25 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Live Load (LL)} = 4.00 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Total service load} = 9.25 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Factored load:}\ w = 1.5 \times 9.25 = 13.875 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

### STEP 5 — Bending Moment Coefficients (IS 456 Table 12, Type 9)

For $L_y/L_x = 1.43$, **interpolating** between $L_y/L_x = 1.4$ and $1.5$:

For **Type 9 (simply supported)**, Table 12 gives:

| $L_y/L_x$ | $\alpha_x$ | $\alpha_y$ |
|:----------:|:----------:|:----------:|
| 1.40       | 0.099      | 0.051      |
| 1.50       | 0.104      | 0.046      |
| **1.43**   | **0.101**  | **0.049**  |

(Linear interpolation: $\alpha_x = 0.099 + 0.30 \times 0.005 = 0.101$;
$\alpha_y = 0.051 - 0.30 \times 0.005 = 0.049$)

### STEP 6 — Design Bending Moments

$$
M_x = \alpha_x \cdot w \cdot L_x^2 = 0.101 \times 13.875 \times (3.625)^2
$$

$$
M_x = 0.101 \times 13.875 \times 13.1406 = 18.42 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

$$
M_y = \alpha_y \cdot w \cdot L_x^2 = 0.049 \times 13.875 \times 13.1406 = 8.93 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

### STEP 7 — Check for Effective Depth Required

For $M_u = 18.42$ kN·m/m, with $b = 1000$ mm, $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², Fe415:

$$
d_{\text{req}} = \sqrt{\frac{M_u}{0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b}} = \sqrt{\frac{18.42 \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 25 \times 1000}}
$$

$$
d_{\text{req}} = \sqrt{\frac{18.42 \times 10^6}{3450}} = \sqrt{5339.1} = 73.1 \text{ mm}
$$

Provided $d = 125$ mm $\gg 73.1$ mm ✓ (depth governed by deflection, not moment).

### STEP 8 — Main Steel Design (Short Span, Bottom Layer)

For M25, Fe415, with $M_u = 18.42 \times 10^6$ N·mm, $b = 1000$ mm, $d = 125$ mm:

$$
A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \, f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \, M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2}}\right] \cdot b \cdot d
$$

Compute the discriminant term:

$$
\frac{4.6 \times 18.42 \times 10^6}{25 \times 1000 \times (125)^2} = \frac{84.732 \times 10^6}{390.625 \times 10^6} = 0.2169
$$

$$
A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 25}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.2169}\right] \times 1000 \times 125
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.03012 \times \left[1 - \sqrt{0.7831}\right] \times 125000
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.03012 \times \left[1 - 0.8849\right] \times 125000
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.03012 \times 0.1151 \times 125000
$$

$$
A_{st} = 433.3 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

**Check minimum steel** (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.2.1):

$$
A_{st,min} = \frac{0.12}{100} \times 1000 \times 150 = 180 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

Required $A_{st} = \max(433.3,\ 180) = 433.3$ mm²/m

**Provide 12 mm $\phi$ bars @ 250 mm c/c:**

$$
A_{st,\text{provided}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 12^2 \times \frac{1000}{250} = 113.1 \times 4 = 452.4 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m} \quad \checkmark
$$

### STEP 9 — Distribution Steel (Long Span)

For $M_y = 8.93$ kN·m/m. With bars below main steel, effective depth:

$$
d_y = 125 - 12 = 113 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\frac{4.6 \times 8.93 \times 10^6}{25 \times 1000 \times (113)^2} = \frac{41.078 \times 10^6}{319.225 \times 10^6} = 0.1287
$$

$$
A_{sy} = 0.03012 \times \left[1 - \sqrt{0.8713}\right] \times 1000 \times 113
$$

$$
A_{sy} = 0.03012 \times 0.0666 \times 113000
$$

$$
A_{sy} = 226.6 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

$A_{st,min} = 180$ mm²/m, so $A_{sy} = 226.6$ mm²/m governs.

**Provide 10 mm $\phi$ bars @ 250 mm c/c:**

$$
A_{st,\text{provided}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 10^2 \times \frac{1000}{250} = 78.5 \times 4 = 314.2 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m} \quad \checkmark
$$

### STEP 10 — Check for Shear

Maximum shear at distance $d$ from face of support:

$$
V_u = w \cdot \left(\frac{L_x}{2} - d\right) = 13.875 \times (1.8125 - 0.125) = 13.875 \times 1.6875
$$

$$
V_u = 23.41 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b \cdot d} = \frac{23.41 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 125} = 0.187 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

Percentage of steel:

$$
p_t = \frac{A_{st}}{b \cdot d} \times 100 = \frac{452.4}{1000 \times 125} \times 100 = 0.362\%
$$

From IS 456 Table 19, for M25 and $p_t = 0.362\%$:

$$
\tau_c \approx 0.41 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad (\text{by interpolation})
$$

Since $\tau_v = 0.187 < \tau_c = 0.41$ N/mm² ✓ **Shear is safe.**

$\tau_{c,max}$ for M25 = 3.1 N/mm², $\tau_v = 0.187 \ll 3.1$ ✓ **Section is safe.**

### STEP 11 — Deflection Check

Basic $(L/d) = 20$ (for simply supported span $\le 3.5$ m, Fe415).
Modification factor $\tau_s$ for $p_t = 0.362\% \approx 1.30$.
$\tau_f$ for 1.0 m wide simply supported = 1.0.

$$
\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{\text{allow}} = 20 \times 1.30 \times 1.0 = 26.0
$$

$$
\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{3625}{125} = 29.0
$$

$29.0 > 26.0$ — **slightly exceeds**, increase $D$ to 165 mm.

**Revised: Adopt $D = 165$ mm, $d = 140$ mm.**

**Recheck depth:**

$$
d_{\text{req}} = \sqrt{\frac{18.42 \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 25 \times 1000}} = 73.1 \text{ mm} < 140 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

Provide **12 mm $\phi$ @ 250 mm c/c** as main steel.

### STEP 12 — Reinforcement Detailing Summary

| Layer | Bar Size & Spacing | Direction | Location |
|:------|:-------------------|:----------|:---------|
| Main steel (bottom) | 12 mm $\phi$ @ 250 mm c/c | Along short span ($L_x$) | Bottom, outer layer |
| Distribution steel (bottom) | 10 mm $\phi$ @ 250 mm c/c | Along long span ($L_y$) | Bottom, inner layer |
| Cranked bars | Alternate main bars cranked up at $L_x/5$ from edge | — | Top near supports |
| Corner ties | 12 mm $\phi$ (or 8 mm) top mesh in both directions, length $L_x/5$ each side of corner | — | Top |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cranking rule:** Per IS 456, in simply supported slabs, alternate bottom bars may be cranked up at $L_x/5$ from the face of the support to resist the small negative moment that develops at corners due to the restraining effect of the wall/monolithic edge.

---

## 3.2 PROBLEM 2 — RESTRAINED (INTERIOR) TWO-WAY SLAB (Type 1)

> **Problem Statement:** An interior panel of a continuous two-way slab floor system has the following details:
> - Clear panel: **4.0 m × 6.0 m**, supported on 230 mm walls
> - Live load: **5 kN/m²**
> - Floor finish: **1.5 kN/m²**
> - Materials: **M25, Fe415**
> - All four edges are continuous (i.e., **Type 1** panel)
> - Determine the design moments and design the reinforcement.

### STEP 1 — Classification & Effective Depth

$$
\frac{L_y}{L_x} = \frac{6.0}{4.0} = 1.50 \le 2.0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{TWO-WAY SLAB}
$$

For continuous slab, basic $L/d = 26$ (per IS 456 Cl. 23.2.1, for continuous span up to 3.5 m, Fe415):

$$
d_{\text{basic,min}} = \frac{L_x}{26} = \frac{4000}{26} \approx 154 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $D = 180$ mm, 12 mm bars, 20 mm cover:

$$
d = 180 - 20 - 6 = 154 \text{ mm} \approx 155 \text{ mm}
$$

### STEP 2 — Effective Spans

$$
L_x = \min(4000 + 155,\ 4000 + 230) = 4155 \text{ mm} = 4.155 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_y = \min(6000 + 155,\ 6000 + 230) = 6155 \text{ mm} = 6.155 \text{ m}
$$

### STEP 3 — Load Calculations

$$
\text{Self-weight} = 0.180 \times 25 = 4.50 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Floor finish} = 1.50 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Total DL} = 6.00 \text{ kN/m}^2,\quad \text{LL} = 5.00 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Total load} = 11.00 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Factored load:}\ w = 1.5 \times 11.00 = 16.50 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

### STEP 4 — Bending Moment Coefficients (IS 456 Table 12, Type 1, $L_y/L_x = 1.5$)

For **Type 1 (interior panel, all edges continuous)**:

| Moment | Coefficient | Value |
|:-------|:-----------:|:-----:|
| $M_{x,\text{neg}}$ (at continuous edge) | $\alpha_{x,-}$ | **0.031** |
| $M_{x,\text{pos}}$ (at midspan) | $\alpha_{x,+}$ | **0.024** |
| $M_{y,\text{neg}}$ (at continuous edge) | $\alpha_{y,-}$ | **0.024** |
| $M_{y,\text{pos}}$ (at midspan) | $\alpha_{y,+}$ | **0.019** |

### STEP 5 — Design Moments

$$
M_{x,-} = 0.031 \times 16.50 \times (4.155)^2 = 0.031 \times 16.50 \times 17.264 = 8.83 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

$$
M_{x,+} = 0.024 \times 16.50 \times 17.264 = 6.84 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

$$
M_{y,-} = 0.024 \times 16.50 \times 17.264 = 6.84 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

$$
M_{y,+} = 0.019 \times 16.50 \times 17.264 = 5.41 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

### STEP 6 — Steel Design for Maximum Moment ($M_{x,-} = 8.83$ kN·m/m, top steel at continuous edge)

For M25, Fe415, $b = 1000$ mm, $d = 155$ mm:

$$
\frac{4.6 \times 8.83 \times 10^6}{25 \times 1000 \times (155)^2} = \frac{40.618 \times 10^6}{600.625 \times 10^6} = 0.0676
$$

$$
A_{st,x,-} = 0.03012 \times \left[1 - \sqrt{0.9324}\right] \times 1000 \times 155
$$

$$
A_{st,x,-} = 0.03012 \times 0.0344 \times 155000
$$

$$
A_{st,x,-} = 160.6 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

$A_{st,min} = 0.0012 \times 1000 \times 180 = 216$ mm²/m → **governs**.

**Provide 10 mm $\phi$ @ 350 mm c/c (top, at continuous edge):**

$$
A_{st,\text{provided}} = 78.5 \times 2.857 = 224.3 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m} \quad \checkmark
$$

### STEP 7 — Summary of Reinforcement

| Location | Design Moment (kN·m/m) | $A_{st,req}$ (mm²/m) | Bar Details | $A_{st,prov}$ (mm²/m) |
|:---------|:---------------------:|:--------------------:|:------------|:---------------------:|
| Continuous edge (top, short span) | 8.83 | 216 (min) | 10 mm $\phi$ @ 350 c/c | 224 |
| Midspan (bottom, short span) | 6.84 | 216 (min) | 10 mm $\phi$ @ 350 c/c | 224 |
| Continuous edge (top, long span) | 6.84 | 216 (min) | 8 mm $\phi$ @ 230 c/c | 219 |
| Midspan (bottom, long span) | 5.41 | 216 (min) | 8 mm $\phi$ @ 230 c/c | 219 |

### STEP 8 — Check for Shear

For an interior panel, maximum shear occurs at the discontinuous (free) edge — but interior panel has **no free edge** (all edges continuous). Maximum shear at face of support, distance $d$ away:

$$
V_u = w \cdot \left(\frac{L_x}{2} - d\right) = 16.50 \times (2.0775 - 0.155) = 16.50 \times 1.9225
$$

$$
V_u = 31.72 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
\tau_v = \frac{31.72 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 155} = 0.205 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$p_t = 0.224\%$, $\tau_c \approx 0.31$ N/mm² (M25, Table 19).

$\tau_v = 0.205 < \tau_c = 0.31$ ✓ **Shear safe.**

### STEP 9 — Deflection Check

Basic $(L/d) = 26$ for continuous slab, Fe415. $\tau_s$ for $p_t = 0.224\% \approx 1.50$. $\tau_f = 1.0$.

$$
\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{\text{allow}} = 26 \times 1.50 \times 1.0 = 39.0
$$

$$
\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{4155}{155} = 26.8 < 39.0 \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deflection does NOT govern** in this case. Section depth is governed by moment and minimum practical thickness (often $L/35$ to $L/40$ for continuous slabs).

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Two-Way Slab Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START: Read Problem Data]) --> nodeB[Identify Panel Type 1-9 from Plan]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Compute Ly/Lx Ratio}
    nodeC -->|Ratio ≤ 2.0| nodeD[Proceed as Two-Way Slab]
    nodeC -->|Ratio > 2.0| nodeE[Redirect to One-Way Design]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Compute Effective Span Lx and Ly]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Estimate Trial Slab Thickness D]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Compute Factored Load w = 1.5 DL+LL]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Read IS 456 Table 12 Coefficients]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Compute Design Moments Mx and My]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Check Effective Depth Required]
    nodeK --> nodeL{Deflection Check Satisfied?}
    nodeL -->|No| nodeM[Increase D and Restart]
    nodeL -->|Yes| nodeN[Design Main Steel Along Short Span]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Check Ast,min Provision]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Design Distribution Steel Along Long Span]
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Check Shear τv ≤ τc]
    nodeQ --> nodeR{Shear OK?}
    nodeR -->|No| nodeS[Increase Depth or Add Shear Links]
    nodeR -->|Yes| nodeT[Detail Reinforcement]
    nodeT --> nodeU[Cranking and Curtailment Rules]
    nodeU --> nodeV[Corner Tie Reinforcement]
    nodeV --> nodeW([END: Final Sketch and Bar Schedule])
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Top_Reinforcement_Layer
        nodeT1[Top Steel Along Short Span at Continuous Edges]
        nodeT2[Top Steel Along Long Span at Continuous Edges]
        nodeT3[Corner Tie Bars Length Lx/5 on Each Diagonal]
    end
    subgraph Bottom_Reinforcement_Layer
        nodeB1[Main Bottom Steel Along Short Span Direction Lx]
        nodeB2[Distribution Bottom Steel Along Long Span Direction Ly]
    end
    nodeB1 --> nodeT1
    nodeB2 --> nodeT2
    nodeT1 -.Cranked at Lx or 5.-> nodeB1
    nodeT2 -.Cranked at Ly or 5.-> nodeB2
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeInput1[Panel Dimensions Lx and Ly] --> nodeProc[Two-Way Slab Design Module]
    nodeInput2[Loading DL and LL] --> nodeProc
    nodeInput3[Material fck and fy] --> nodeProc
    nodeInput4[Edge Conditions Panel Type] --> nodeProc
    nodeInput5[IS 456 Table 12 Coefficients] --> nodeProc
    nodeProc --> nodeOut1[Effective Depth d and Overall Depth D]
    nodeProc --> nodeOut2[Design Moments Mx and My]
    nodeProc --> nodeOut3[Main and Distribution Steel Areas]
    nodeProc --> nodeOut4[Shear and Deflection Verifications]
    nodeProc --> nodeOut5[Final Bar Diameter and Spacing]
    nodeProc --> nodeOut6[Detailing Sketch with Cranking]
```

## 4.4 Description of Cranking & Corner Tie Detailing

| Detailing Element | Rule of Thumb | Code Reference |
|:------------------|:--------------|:--------------:|
| Crank-up location (simply supported) | $L_x/5$ from face of support | IS 456 Fig. C — Typical Slab Reinforcement |
| Crank-up angle | 30° to 45° | Engineering practice |
| Corner tie length | $L_x/5$ along both directions | IS 456 Cl. D-1.6 |
| Corner tie purpose | Resists negative corner moment + prevents warping | Empirical |
| Curtailment of bottom bars | 50% alternate bars curtailed at $L_x/4$ | IS 456 Cl. 26.2.3.2 |
