# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *With the help of neat sketches, explain the various types of two-way slabs based on the edge conditions as per IS 456:2000.*

**Model Answer:**

As per IS 456:2000 (Table 12), the bending moment coefficients for two-way slabs depend on the **edge conditions**. The code specifies **9 panel types** (Type 1 to Type 9):

- **Type 1:** Interior panel, all four edges continuous (fully restrained) — common in multi-bay floors.
- **Type 2:** One short edge discontinuous, all others continuous — edge panel running parallel to short span.
- **Type 3:** One long edge discontinuous, all others continuous — edge panel running parallel to long span.
- **Type 4:** Two short edges discontinuous.
- **Type 5:** Two long edges discontinuous.
- **Type 6:** Two adjacent edges discontinuous — corner panel.
- **Type 7:** Three edges discontinuous, one short edge continuous.
- **Type 8:** Three edges discontinuous, one long edge continuous.
- **Type 9:** All four edges simply supported (no continuity).

For each type, IS 456 Table 12 provides coefficients $\alpha_x$ and $\alpha_y$ (positive and negative) for design moments $M_x = \alpha_x w L_x^2$ and $M_y = \alpha_y w L_x^2$, where $L_x$ is the shorter effective span.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *State the difference between a one-way and a two-way slab. What is the role of corner ties in a two-way slab?*

**Model Answer:**

A **one-way slab** carries load predominantly in one direction (short span) when $L_y/L_x > 2.0$, while a **two-way slab** carries load in two orthogonal directions when $L_y/L_x \le 2.0$.

| Aspect | One-Way Slab | Two-Way Slab |
|:-------|:-------------|:-------------|
| Span ratio $L_y/L_x$ | $> 2.0$ | $\le 2.0$ |
| Load transfer | Mainly to two opposite supports | To all four edges |
| Main steel | Along short direction only | Along both directions |
| Bending | Cylindrical | Dish-shaped (twice curved) |
| Analysis | As continuous beam | IS 456 Table 12 coefficients |

**Role of corner ties:** In a two-way slab simply supported on all four edges, the corners tend to lift up due to torsional moments. **Corner ties** (top mesh reinforcement of length $L_x/5$ along both directions at each corner) resist the resulting negative corner moments and prevent cracking/warping at the corners. They are mandatory per IS 456 Cl. D-1.6 even when the analysis does not show negative moment there.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
> *A two-way slab for a residential building has clear panel dimensions of **3.0 m × 4.5 m**, simply supported on 230 mm thick masonry walls on all four sides. The slab is subjected to a live load of **4 kN/m²** and a floor finish of **1.2 kN/m²**. Use **M25 concrete and Fe415 steel**. Design the slab and sketch the reinforcement details. Use IS 456:2000 provisions.*

#### Part (a) (7 Marks) — Compute design moments and steel areas

**Step 1 — Classification:**

$$
\frac{L_y}{L_x} = \frac{4.5}{3.0} = 1.5 \le 2.0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{TWO-WAY SLAB}
$$

**Step 2 — Trial depth:** For simply supported slab, $L/d$ basic = 20, with $\tau_s \approx 1.25$:

$$
d_{\text{req}} = \frac{3000}{20 \times 1.25} = 120 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $D = 140$ mm, 10 mm main bar: $d = 140 - 20 - 5 = 115$ mm.

**Step 3 — Effective spans:**

$$
L_x = \min(3000 + 115,\ 3000 + 230) = 3115 \text{ mm} = 3.115 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_y = \min(4500 + 115,\ 4500 + 230) = 4615 \text{ mm} = 4.615 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Loads:**

$$
\text{DL} = (0.140 \times 25) + 1.2 = 3.5 + 1.2 = 4.7 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{LL} = 4.0 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Total} = 8.7 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w = 1.5 \times 8.7 = 13.05 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Coefficients (IS 456 Table 12, Type 9, $L_y/L_x = 1.5$):**

$$
\alpha_x = 0.104,\quad \alpha_y = 0.046
$$

**Step 6 — Moments:**

$$
M_x = 0.104 \times 13.05 \times (3.115)^2 = 0.104 \times 13.05 \times 9.703 = 13.17 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

$$
M_y = 0.046 \times 13.05 \times 9.703 = 5.82 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

**Step 7 — Main steel (along short span):**

$$
A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 25}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 13.17 \times 10^6}{25 \times 1000 \times 115^2}}\right] \times 1000 \times 115
$$

$$
= 0.03012 \times \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.1834}\right] \times 115000
$$

$$
= 0.03012 \times 0.0980 \times 115000 = 339.6 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

$A_{st,min} = 0.0012 \times 1000 \times 140 = 168$ mm²/m → $A_{st,req} = 339.6$ mm²/m

**Provide 10 mm $\phi$ @ 230 mm c/c:** $A_{st,prov} = 78.5 \times 4.348 = 341.3$ mm²/m ✓

**Step 8 — Distribution steel (along long span):**

$d_y = 115 - 10 = 105$ mm, $M_y = 5.82$ kN·m/m

$$
A_{sy} = 0.03012 \times \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 5.82 \times 10^6}{25 \times 1000 \times 105^2}}\right] \times 1000 \times 105
$$

$$
= 0.03012 \times \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.0973}\right] \times 105000
$$

$$
= 0.03012 \times 0.0504 \times 105000 = 159.4 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

$A_{st,min} = 168$ mm²/m governs. **Provide 8 mm $\phi$ @ 280 mm c/c:** $A_{st,prov} = 50.27 \times 3.571 = 179.5$ mm²/m ✓

#### Part (b) (7 Marks) — Check shear, deflection, and detailing

**Shear check:**

$$
V_u = w\left(\frac{L_x}{2} - d\right) = 13.05 \times (1.5575 - 0.115) = 13.05 \times 1.4425 = 18.82 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
\tau_v = \frac{18.82 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 115} = 0.164 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$p_t = (341.3 / 115000) \times 100 = 0.297\%$. From Table 19 (M25): $\tau_c \approx 0.37$ N/mm².

$\tau_v = 0.164 < 0.37$ ✓ **Shear safe.**

**Deflection check:**

$$
\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{3115}{115} = 27.1
$$

$$
\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{\text{allow}} = 20 \times 1.35 \times 1.0 = 27.0
$$

$27.1 \approx 27.0$ — **marginal**. Slightly increase $D$ to 150 mm to be safe.

**Revised: $D = 150$ mm, $d = 125$ mm, and 10 mm $\phi$ @ 230 mm c/c main bars, 8 mm $\phi$ @ 280 mm c/c distribution bars.**

**Detailing:** Crank alternate bottom bars at $L_x/5 = 623$ mm from face of support. Provide 8 mm $\phi$ corner ties of length $L_x/5 = 623$ mm at all four corners, top mesh.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, PCCVT403]**
> *Design a two-way slab panel (interior panel — Type 1) for an office floor with the following data:*
> - *Clear panel dimensions: **3.5 m × 5.0 m***
> - *Walls: 230 mm thick; all four edges continuous*
> - *Live load = **4 kN/m²**, Floor finish = **1.5 kN/m²***
> - *Materials: **M25 concrete and Fe415 steel***
> - *Compute the design moments, reinforcement, and check for shear and deflection.*

#### Part (a) (7 Marks) — Moments and Effective Depth Design

**Step 1:** $L_y/L_x = 5.0/3.5 = 1.43$ → **Two-way slab**.

**Step 2:** Adopt $D = 160$ mm, 10 mm bar: $d = 160 - 20 - 5 = 135$ mm.

**Step 3:** $L_x = \min(3500 + 135,\ 3730) = 3635$ mm = 3.635 m.
$L_y = \min(5000 + 135,\ 5230) = 5135$ mm = 5.135 m.

**Step 4:**

$$
\text{DL} = (0.160 \times 25) + 1.5 = 4.0 + 1.5 = 5.5 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{LL} = 4.0 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Total} = 9.5,\ w = 1.5 \times 9.5 = 14.25 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Coefficients (Type 1, $L_y/L_x = 1.43$, interpolated):**

| Moment | Coefficient | Value |
|:-------|:-----------:|:-----:|
| $M_{x,-}$ (neg, short edge) | $\alpha_{x,-}$ | 0.033 |
| $M_{x,+}$ (pos, midspan) | $\alpha_{x,+}$ | 0.025 |
| $M_{y,-}$ (neg, long edge) | $\alpha_{y,-}$ | 0.025 |
| $M_{y,+}$ (pos, midspan) | $\alpha_{y,+}$ | 0.020 |

**Step 6 — Design moments:**

$$
M_{x,-} = 0.033 \times 14.25 \times (3.635)^2 = 0.033 \times 14.25 \times 13.213 = 6.21 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

$$
M_{x,+} = 0.025 \times 14.25 \times 13.213 = 4.71 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

$$
M_{y,-} = 0.025 \times 14.25 \times 13.213 = 4.71 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

$$
M_{y,+} = 0.020 \times 14.25 \times 13.213 = 3.77 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

#### Part (b) (7 Marks) — Steel Design, Shear and Deflection Check

**Steel design (governed by minimum for all locations):**

$$
A_{st,min} = 0.0012 \times 1000 \times 160 = 192 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

| Location | Bar Provided | $A_{st,prov}$ (mm²/m) |
|:---------|:-------------|:---------------------:|
| Top at continuous short edge | 8 mm $\phi$ @ 250 c/c | 201 ✓ |
| Bottom at midspan (short) | 8 mm $\phi$ @ 250 c/c | 201 ✓ |
| Top at continuous long edge | 8 mm $\phi$ @ 280 c/c | 180 ✗ → 8 mm @ 250 c/c |
| Bottom at midspan (long) | 8 mm $\phi$ @ 250 c/c | 201 ✓ |

(Use **8 mm $\phi$ @ 250 mm c/c** uniformly at top, **8 mm $\phi$ @ 250 mm c/c** at bottom along short span, and **8 mm $\phi$ @ 250 mm c/c** at bottom along long span.)

**Shear check:**

$$
V_u = 14.25 \times \left(\frac{3.635}{2} - 0.135\right) = 14.25 \times 1.6825 = 23.98 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
\tau_v = \frac{23.98 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 135} = 0.178 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$p_t = 0.149\%$. $\tau_c$ for M25, $p_t = 0.149\% \approx 0.30$ N/mm².

$\tau_v = 0.178 < 0.30$ ✓ **Safe in shear.**

**Deflection check:**

$$
\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{3635}{135} = 26.9
$$

For continuous slab, basic = 26, $\tau_s$ for $p_t = 0.149\% \approx 1.55$, $\tau_f = 1.0$:

$$
\left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{\text{allow}} = 26 \times 1.55 \times 1.0 = 40.3
$$

$26.9 < 40.3$ ✓ **Deflection safe.**

**Detailing:** Continuous slab has top bars (10 mm $\phi$ @ 250 c/c) over supports extending $0.3L$ from face, and bottom bars at midspan. Corner ties 8 mm $\phi$ at top, length $L_x/5 = 727$ mm each side.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Wrong panel type identification** — Most common mistake. Always sketch the plan and identify each edge as "continuous" or "discontinuous" before consulting IS 456 Table 12.
> 2. **Forgetting that the BM formula uses $L_x^2$ (not $L_y^2$)** — IS 456 Table 12 coefficients are calibrated to use $L_x^2$ for BOTH $M_x$ and $M_y$. Using $L_y^2$ for $M_y$ gives wrong (and unrealistically large) values.
> 3. **Ignoring the minimum steel clause (Cl. 26.5.2.1)** — For lightly loaded slabs where moment-derived $A_{st}$ is small, the minimum (0.12% of gross area) governs. Forgetting this leads to cracking under service loads.
> 4. **Missing the deflection check** — Even when the section is strong in moment, it may not satisfy $L/d$. Always verify against IS 456 Cl. 23.2 and Fig. 4.
> 5. **Forgetting corner ties** — 1–2 marks lost in detailing step. Even for slabs analysed as simply supported, corner ties are mandatory.
> 6. **Not using $L/d$ modification factors** $\tau_s$ and $\tau_f$ — These are essential for accurate deflection check and commonly tested.
> 7. **Wrong conversion of $p_t$ for Table 19** — $p_t$ must be computed with the **provided** $A_{st}$ (not the required), and the corresponding $\tau_c$ read at exact $p_t$ (interpolation often required).

---

## 5.X TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Two-Way Slab Numerical Problems**

- [ ] **Trigger criterion:** Design as two-way slab **iff** $L_y/L_x \le 2.0$.
- [ ] **Effective span** = min(clear span + $d$, c/c of supports) for simply supported; c/c for continuous (IS 456 Cl. 22.2).
- [ ] **BM formula** (IS 456 Table 12): $M_x = \alpha_x w L_x^2$ and $M_y = \alpha_y w L_x^2$ — both use $L_x^2$ (shorter span).
- [ ] **9 panel types** based on edge continuity; Type 1 (interior) and Type 9 (simply supported) most common in KTU.
- [ ] **Load factor:** 1.5 for both DL and LL (IS 456 Table 18).
- [ ] **Default materials in KTU:** M25 + Fe415.
- [ ] **Trial depth** governed by deflection: basic $L/d$ = 20 (simply supported) or 26 (continuous) for Fe415.
- [ ] **Modification factors** for deflection: $\tau_s$ (steel) and $\tau_f$ (flange) — multiply with basic $L/d$.
- [ ] **Minimum steel** in slabs: $0.12\%$ of gross cross-section for Fe415/HYSD bars (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.2.1).
- [ ] **Max bar spacing** in slabs: $3d$ or 300 mm, whichever is less (IS 456 Cl. 26.3.3).
- [ ] **Max diameter** at supports in slabs: 1/8 of slab thickness (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.2.2).
- [ ] **Shear check:** $\tau_v = V_u / bd$ must be $\le \tau_c$ from Table 19 (interpolated for actual $p_t$).
- [ ] **Crank alternate bars** at $L_x/5$ from face in simply supported slabs.
- [ ] **Curtail 50%** of bottom bars at $L_x/4$ from face of support.
- [ ] **Corner ties** mandatory in two-way slabs, top mesh of length $L_x/5$ in both directions at each corner.
- [ ] **Continuous slabs need BOTH top AND bottom steel** at edges and midspan, respectively.
- [ ] **Effective depth for distribution steel** = main $d$ - main bar diameter (bars at inner layer).
- [ ] Always **show the plan sketch with reinforcement** in the answer — KTU award 1–2 marks for clean detailing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Singly Reinforced Concrete Beam (Limit State Method):**
A reinforced concrete beam in which the longitudinal tension reinforcement is provided only in the tension zone (bottom fibre for simply supported beams) to resist the bending moment, while the compression is taken entirely by the concrete in the compression zone above the neutral axis.

**Limit State of Collapse (Flexure):**
The state at which the maximum strain in the extreme compression fibre of concrete reaches the ultimate value ($\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$) while the tension steel reaches its design yield strength ($f_y$), or the maximum compressive strain in concrete reaches the ultimate value when the section fails by crushing of concrete.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** All numerical problems in this module are solved using the **Limit State Method (LSM)** as per **IS 456:2000** (latest reaffirmed). Working Stress Method (WSM) belongs to Module 1.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Steel-Reinforced Plank" Model

Imagine a simple wooden plank laid across two chairs, with a heavy weight placed at the centre. The plank bends — its top fibres get squeezed together (compression) and the bottom fibres get stretched apart (tension). Wood is weak in tension but strong in compression. So if you glue a steel strip to the bottom face, the steel carries the tension and the wood carries the compression.

A **singly reinforced RCC beam** works exactly like this:
- **Concrete** = the wood → resists compression at top
- **Steel bars at bottom** = the glued steel strip → resists tension
- The lever arm between them = the internal moment arm

The "**Numerical Problems**" in this module train you to compute exactly *how much steel* and *how deep the beam should be* so that the lever system just balances the external moment — but with a **safety margin** (called the partial safety factor for material: $\gamma_m = 1.5$ for concrete, $1.15$ for steel) and a load factor of **1.5**.

## 1.3 Key Design Constants (Highlighted)

> [!NOTE]
> Standard material properties for KTU 2024 numerical problems:
> - **M20 concrete:** $f_{ck} = 20 \text{ N/mm}^2$
> - **M25 concrete:** $f_{ck} = 25 \text{ N/mm}^2$
> - **Fe415 steel:** $f_y = 415 \text{ N/mm}^2$
> - **Fe500 steel:** $f_y = 500 \text{ N/mm}^2$
> - **Partial safety factor for concrete, $\gamma_m = 1.5$**
> - **Partial safety factor for steel, $\gamma_m = 1.15$**
> - **Design strength of concrete:** $f_d = 0.45 f_{ck}$ (for flexure)
> - **Design strength of steel:** $f_{yd} = 0.87 f_y$
> - **Ultimate strain in concrete:** $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$
> - **Maximum depth of neutral axis factor ($k = x_{u,max}/d$):** $0.48$ for Fe415, $0.46$ for Fe500

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions (IS 456:2000 Cl. 38.1)

1. **Plane sections remain plane** before and after bending (Bernoulli's hypothesis).
2. **Maximum strain in concrete at the extreme compression fibre** is taken as $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$.
3. The **tensile strength of concrete is ignored**.
4. Perfect bond exists between steel and concrete (no slip).
5. The design stress-strain curve of concrete is a **rectangular parabola**, simplified to a **rectangular block** of stress $0.446 f_{ck}$ over a depth $0.42 x_u$ (where $x_u$ is the depth of the actual neutral axis from the compression face) — equivalently $0.36 f_{ck}$ over depth $x_u$ in the simplified IS code formulation.

## 2.2 Why the Section Must Be Under-Reinforced

The Indian code philosophy is **"steel should fail first, not concrete"** because steel failure (ductile) gives warning signs, while concrete failure (brittle) is sudden and catastrophic. This forces the design to keep the **neutral axis depth $x_u \leq x_{u,max}$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** If a problem statement says "design" the beam, the resulting section **must be under-reinforced**, i.e. $x_u \leq x_{u,max}$. If your calculation gives $x_u > x_{u,max}$, you must either increase the section depth OR redesign as a **doubly reinforced beam** (covered in Module 3).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Design strength of concrete | $f_d = 0.45 f_{ck}$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | Cl. 36.1 (b), IS 456 |
| 2 | Design strength of steel | $f_{yd} = 0.87 f_y$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ | Cl. 36.1 (a), IS 456 |
| 3 | Depth of neutral axis | $x_u = \dfrac{0.87 f_y A_{st}}{0.36 f_{ck} b}$ | mm | From force equilibrium |
| 4 | Limiting neutral axis factor | $k = x_{u,max}/d$ | dimensionless | $0.48$ for Fe415, $0.46$ for Fe500 |
| 5 | Limiting moment of resistance | $M_{u,lim} = 0.36 \cdot k \cdot (1 - 0.42 k) \cdot b \cdot d^2 \cdot f_{ck}$ | N·mm | Substitute $k$ to get $0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ for Fe415 |
| 6 | $M_{u,lim}$ (Fe415) shortcut | $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ | N·mm | Most common in KTU papers |
| 7 | $M_{u,lim}$ (Fe500) shortcut | $M_{u,lim} = 0.133 f_{ck} b d^2$ | N·mm | |
| 8 | Moment equation (any $x_u$) | $M_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st} d \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st} f_y}{b d f_{ck}}\right)$ | N·mm | Direct quadratic form |
| 9 | Standard quadratic for $A_{st}$ | $A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5 f_{ck}}{f_y}\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2}}\right) b d$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | When $M_u \leq M_{u,lim}$ |
| 10 | Lever arm (any section) | $z = d \left(1 - \dfrac{0.42 x_u}{d}\right) = d - 0.42 x_u$ | mm | |
| 11 | Lever arm at limit state | $z_{lim} = 0.7984 d$ (Fe415), $0.8068 d$ (Fe500) | mm | $1 - 0.42 k$ |
| 12 | Minimum tension steel | $A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.85 b d}{f_y}$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Cl. 26.5.1.1, IS 456 |
| 13 | Maximum tension steel | $A_{st,max} = 0.04 b D$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Cl. 26.5.1.1, IS 456 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Numerical design of beams forms the bedrock of every reinforced concrete structure: building floor beams, bridge girders, pile caps, machine foundations. Mastery of these problems enables a civil engineer to:
- Specify bar diameters and spacings in BBS (Bar Bending Schedules).
- Verify whether an existing beam can carry new superimposed loads (e.g., adding an extra floor to a building).
- Communicate structurally sound details at construction sites.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

> [!NOTE]
> All problems below use **LSM (Limit State Method)** as mandated by KTU 2024 Scheme.

---

## PROBLEM 1 (Type — Design): Find Area of Steel

**Given:** Singly reinforced beam, $b = 250 \text{ mm}$, $d = 500 \text{ mm}$, $M_u = 150 \text{ kN·m}$, M20 concrete, Fe415 steel.
**Find:** $A_{st}$ and suitable bar arrangement.

### Step 1 — Check Section Adequacy

Compute the limiting moment of resistance of the section.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,lim} &= 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2 \\
&= 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times (500)^2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kN·m} \\
&= 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times 250000 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 172.5 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $M_u = 150 \text{ kN·m} < M_{u,lim} = 172.5 \text{ kN·m}$, **the section is adequate** as a singly reinforced beam (singly reinforced design is permissible).

### Step 2 — Calculate $A_{st}$ Using the Standard Quadratic Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{st} &= \frac{0.5 f_{ck}}{f_y}\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2}}\right) b d
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the inner ratio first.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2} &= \frac{4.6 \times 150 \times 10^6}{20 \times 250 \times (500)^2} \\
&= \frac{690 \times 10^6}{1.25 \times 10^9} \\
&= 0.552
\end{aligned}
$$

Now substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{st} &= \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415}\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.552}\right) \times 250 \times 500 \\
&= 0.02410 \times (1 - \sqrt{0.448}) \times 125000 \\
&= 0.02410 \times (1 - 0.6693) \times 125000 \\
&= 0.02410 \times 0.3307 \times 125000 \\
&= 996.3 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Adopt $A_{st} \approx 1000 \text{ mm}^2$.**

### Step 3 — Select Bar Diameter and Spacing

From the standard table, a single **20 mm diameter bar** provides $A_\phi = 314 \text{ mm}^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of 20 mm bars} &= \frac{1000}{314} = 3.18 \approx 4 \text{ bars}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Provide 4 bars of 20 mm diameter** ($A_{st} = 4 \times 314 = 1256 \text{ mm}^2$).

### Step 4 — Check for Minimum Steel (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{st,min} &= \frac{0.85 \, b \, d}{f_y} = \frac{0.85 \times 250 \times 500}{415} = 256 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since provided $A_{st} = 1256 \text{ mm}^2 > 256 \text{ mm}^2$ → **OK**.

> [!NOTE]
> The provided steel ($1256 \text{ mm}^2$) is greater than the computed requirement ($1000 \text{ mm}^2$) because bar diameters come in fixed sizes. The section will now have $x_u < x_{u,max}$ — the design becomes more conservative and safe.

---

## PROBLEM 2 (Type — Analysis): Find Moment of Resistance

**Given:** Singly reinforced beam, $b = 300 \text{ mm}$, $d = 600 \text{ mm}$, $A_{st} = 1964 \text{ mm}^2$ (4 bars of 25 mm dia), M20 concrete, Fe415 steel.
**Find:** Moment of resistance of the section $M_u$.

### Step 1 — Compute the Actual Depth of Neutral Axis

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_u &= \frac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b} \\
&= \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 1964}{0.36 \times 20 \times 300} \\
&= \frac{708,690}{2160} \\
&= 328.10 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compare with Limiting Neutral Axis Depth

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{u,max} &= 0.48 \times d = 0.48 \times 600 = 288 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $x_u = 328.10 \text{ mm} > x_{u,max} = 288 \text{ mm}$, the section is **over-reinforced**. The IS code does NOT allow over-reinforced sections; the design must be revised. However, for the sake of the numerical problem, we may also check the maximum allowable steel area.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students directly substitute $x_u = 328.10$ mm into $M_u = 0.36 f_{ck} b x_u (d - 0.42 x_u)$ and report a moment higher than $M_{u,lim}$. This is **wrong** for LSM design. For an existing over-reinforced section, the safe design moment is restricted to $M_{u,lim}$, because the code assumes a balanced failure with $x_u = x_{u,max}$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Safe Moment of Resistance

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,lim} &= 0.138 \times 20 \times 300 \times (600)^2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kN·m} \\
&= 0.138 \times 20 \times 300 \times 360000 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 298.08 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**The safe design moment of resistance is $M_u = 298.08 \text{ kN·m}$** (the section is unsafe as per IS 456 and must be redesigned).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Redesign Hint:** To make the section under-reinforced while keeping $b = 300$ mm and $A_{st} = 1964 \text{ mm}^2$, the depth $d$ must satisfy $x_u \leq 0.48 d$:
> $\frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 1964}{0.36 \times 20 \times 300} \leq 0.48 d \;\Rightarrow\; 328.10 \leq 0.48 d \;\Rightarrow\; d \geq 683.5 \text{ mm}$.
> So provide $d = 700$ mm (or $D = 750$ mm overall).

---

## PROBLEM 3 (Type — Design with Given Loads): Beam with Imposed Load

**Given:** A simply supported beam of effective span $l_{eff} = 6 \text{ m}$, with superimposed dead load (excluding self-weight) = $4 \text{ kN/m}$ and live load = $12 \text{ kN/m}$. Use M20 concrete and Fe415 steel.
**Find:** Section dimensions ($b$, $d$, $D$) and reinforcement.

### Step 1 — Assume Trial Section

Assume $b = 0.5 d$ (an economical proportion) and $D = 1.1 d$ (with 50 mm cover + half bar ≈ 50 mm).

For a 6 m span, assume $d = 500 \text{ mm}$ and $D = 550 \text{ mm}$, $b = 250 \text{ mm}$.

### Step 2 — Compute Self-Weight and Factored Load

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Self-weight} &= b \times D \times 25 = 0.25 \times 0.55 \times 25 = 3.44 \text{ kN/m} \\
w &= 4 + 12 + 3.44 = 19.44 \text{ kN/m} \\
w_u &= 1.5 \times 19.44 = 29.16 \text{ kN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Maximum Factored Bending Moment

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_u &= \frac{w_u l_{eff}^2}{8} = \frac{29.16 \times 6^2}{8} = \frac{29.16 \times 36}{8} = 131.22 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Check Section Adequacy

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,lim} &= 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times 500^2 \times 10^{-6} = 172.5 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $M_u = 131.22 \text{ kN·m} < M_{u,lim} = 172.5 \text{ kN·m}$ → **OK, section is adequate as singly reinforced.**

### Step 5 — Calculate Steel Area

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2} &= \frac{4.6 \times 131.22 \times 10^6}{20 \times 250 \times 500^2} = 0.4827 \\
A_{st} &= \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415} \left(1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.4827}\right) \times 250 \times 500 \\
&= 0.02410 \times (1 - \sqrt{0.5173}) \times 125000 \\
&= 0.02410 \times (1 - 0.7192) \times 125000 \\
&= 0.02410 \times 0.2808 \times 125000 \\
&= 846.0 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Adopt $A_{st} \approx 850 \text{ mm}^2$.**

### Step 6 — Choose Bars

A **16 mm bar** provides $A_\phi = 201 \text{ mm}^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of 16 mm bars} &= \frac{850}{201} = 4.23 \approx 5 \text{ bars}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Provide 5 bars of 16 mm diameter** ($A_{st} = 5 \times 201 = 1005 \text{ mm}^2$).

### Step 7 — Verify Minimum Steel

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{st,min} &= \frac{0.85 \times 250 \times 500}{415} = 256 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \text{(OK, since 1005 > 256)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Final Section

- **Width** $b = 250 \text{ mm}$
- **Effective depth** $d = 500 \text{ mm}$
- **Overall depth** $D = 550 \text{ mm}$
- **Main reinforcement:** 5 bars of 16 mm diameter at the bottom.

---

## PROBLEM 4 (Type — Combined Design + Effective Span Calculation)

**Given:** Clear span of a simply supported beam = 5.5 m, support width = 300 mm. Live load = 20 kN/m, finish load = 1.5 kN/m. M20/Fe415.
**Find:** Beam dimensions and reinforcement.

### Step 1 — Effective Span (IS 456 Cl. 22.2)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Clear span} + \text{effective depth} &= 5.5 + d \text{ (approx)} \\
\text{Centre-to-centre of supports} &= 5.5 + 0.30 = 5.80 \text{ m} \\
\text{Whichever is less is } l_{eff} &= \min(5.5 + d, 5.80)
\end{aligned}
$$

If $d = 450$ mm, then clear span $+ d = 5.95$ m > 5.80 m, so $l_{eff} = 5.80$ m.

### Step 2 — Trial Section

Take $b = 250$ mm, $D = 500$ mm, $d = 450$ mm.

### Step 3 — Loads and Bending Moment

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Self-weight} &= 0.25 \times 0.50 \times 25 = 3.125 \text{ kN/m} \\
w &= 20 + 1.5 + 3.125 = 24.625 \text{ kN/m} \\
w_u &= 1.5 \times 24.625 = 36.94 \text{ kN/m} \\
M_u &= \frac{36.94 \times 5.80^2}{8} = \frac{36.94 \times 33.64}{8} = 155.30 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Check Adequacy

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,lim} &= 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times 450^2 \times 10^{-6} = 139.73 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $M_u = 155.30 \text{ kN·m} > M_{u,lim} = 139.73 \text{ kN·m}$ → **Section is NOT adequate.** Increase depth.

### Step 5 — Revise Depth

Set $M_u = M_{u,lim}$ to find minimum required $d$ (with $b = 250$ mm unchanged):

$$
\begin{aligned}
155.30 \times 10^6 &= 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times d^2 \\
d^2 &= \frac{155.30 \times 10^6}{690} = 225072 \\
d &= 474.4 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Provide $d = 480$ mm, $D = 530$ mm. The effective span check must now be re-evaluated:

Clear span $+ d = 5.5 + 0.48 = 5.98$ m > $5.80$ m → $l_{eff} = 5.80$ m remains valid.

### Step 6 — Recalculate Loads with New Section

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New self-weight} &= 0.25 \times 0.53 \times 25 = 3.31 \text{ kN/m} \\
w &= 20 + 1.5 + 3.31 = 24.81 \text{ kN/m} \\
w_u &= 1.5 \times 24.81 = 37.22 \text{ kN/m} \\
M_u &= \frac{37.22 \times 5.80^2}{8} = 156.5 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Compute Steel Area

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{4.6 \times 156.5 \times 10^6}{20 \times 250 \times 480^2} &= \frac{719.9 \times 10^6}{1.152 \times 10^9} = 0.6249 \\
A_{st} &= \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415} (1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.6249}) \times 250 \times 480 \\
&= 0.02410 \times (1 - 0.6123) \times 120000 \\
&= 0.02410 \times 0.3877 \times 120000 \\
&= 1121 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Select Bars

Use **20 mm bars** ($A_\phi = 314 \text{ mm}^2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of bars} &= \frac{1121}{314} = 3.57 \approx 4 \text{ bars}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Provide 4 bars of 20 mm diameter** ($A_{st,provided} = 4 \times 314 = 1256 \text{ mm}^2$).

### Final Answer

- $b = 250$ mm, $D = 530$ mm, $d = 480$ mm
- Main steel: 4 bars of 20 mm dia at bottom
- $M_{u,lim}$ check: Now $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times 480^2 \times 10^{-6} = 158.98$ kN·m ≥ $M_u = 156.5$ kN·m → **OK**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stress-Strain and Force Block Diagram

The conceptual stress block assumed in IS 456:2000 for a singly reinforced beam is shown in the following block-level architecture. This represents the rectangular stress block used in design calculations.

```
+-------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                       |
|   Stress Block (0.36 fck)                            |
|   +---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+       |
|   |   |   |   |   |   |   |   |   |   |   |   |       |
|   +---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+       |
|   <----------- xu (depth of NA) ----------->          |
|                                                       |
|   Neutral Axis (N.A.) at depth xu from top            |
|                                                       |
|   Tension Steel: Ast at effective depth d             |
|   =================================================== |
|   | | | | | | | | | | | |  (Tension Reinforcement)   |
+-------------------------------------------------------+
        <-- 0.42 xu -->                 <-- d - 0.42 xu -->
              (compression block)              (tension lever)
```

The lever arm between the resultant compression force ($C$) and tension force ($T$) is:

$$
z = d - 0.42 \, x_u
$$

For a limiting section (Fe415), $z_{lim} = d - 0.42 \times 0.48d = d(1 - 0.2016) = 0.7984d$.

## 4.2 Mermaid Design Procedure Flowchart

This block-level functional architecture flow maps the **decision logic** for solving singly reinforced beam numerical problems using the Limit State Method.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Given Mu, b, d, fck, fy] --> calcLim[Compute Mu,lim = 0.138 fck b d^2 for Fe415]
    calcLim --> check1{Mu is less than or equal to Mu,lim?}
    check1 -- Yes --> designSingle[Design as Singly Reinforced Beam]
    check1 -- No --> designDouble[Redesign as Doubly Reinforced OR increase d]
    designSingle --> calcAst[Calculate Ast from quadratic formula]
    calcAst --> provideBars[Select bar diameter and number of bars]
    provideBars --> chkMin{Ast,provided is more than Ast,min?}
    chkMin -- Yes --> chkMax{Ast,provided is less than Ast,max?}
    chkMin -- No --> increaseAst[Increase number of bars]
    chkMax -- Yes --> finalA[Final Design OK]
    chkMax -- No --> designDouble
    increaseAst --> chkMin
    designDouble --> module3[Refer to Module 3]
    finalA --> stopA[End]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Stress Distribution Across Beam Cross-Section

```
      Compression (0.36 f_ck, rectangular)
      ========================================
      |||||   <-- Top fibre (max strain = 0.0035)
      |||||
      |||||   <-- Depth = 0.42 x_u
      |||||
      ======  <-- Neutral Axis (N.A.)
      .....
      .....
      .....
      ||||||| <-- Tension steel A_st (strain >> yield)
      ========================================
              <---------- d ------------>
```

The **rectangular compression block** is an engineering idealisation — IS 456 collapses the true parabolic stress distribution of concrete (as per the actual stress-strain curve) into an equivalent rectangle of uniform stress $0.36 f_{ck}$ acting over a depth $x_u$ (which equals the actual neutral axis depth from the strain compatibility analysis). This simplification drastically reduces hand-calculation effort while introducing less than 1% error.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the Limit State of Collapse and Limit State of Serviceability. Mention any two design considerations under serviceability. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Limit State of Collapse:** The state at which the structure (or part of it) reaches its ultimate design strength and is on the verge of failure. Examples: limit state of collapse in flexure, in compression, in shear, in torsion.

**Limit State of Serviceability:** The state beyond which the structure becomes unfit for ordinary use due to excessive deflection, cracking, vibration, or other discomfort to occupants.

**Two serviceability design considerations:**
1. **Deflection control** (Cl. 23.2, IS 456) — basic span/effective depth ratio.
2. **Crack width control** (Cl. 35.3.2) — surface crack width must not exceed 0.3 mm for normal exposure and 0.2 mm for severe exposure.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks this definition directly for 3 marks. Use IS 456 clause numbers in your answer for bonus credit.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between a Balanced, Under-Reinforced, and Over-Reinforced Section. Why does IS 456 prefer under-reinforced sections? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Definition | Failure Mode |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **Balanced** | $x_u = x_{u,max}$ — both steel and concrete reach ultimate strain simultaneously | Theoretical / brittle |
| **Under-reinforced** | $x_u < x_{u,max}$ — steel yields first | Ductile (preferred) |
| **Over-reinforced** | $x_u > x_{u,max}$ — concrete crushes first | Sudden, brittle (forbidden) |

**IS 456 prefers under-reinforced sections** because:
- Failure occurs by yielding of tension steel (ductile, gives ample warning through visible cracking and sagging).
- An over-reinforced section would fail by sudden crushing of concrete in the compression zone without warning — catastrophic in real structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always state the $x_{u,max}$ value (0.48d for Fe415, 0.46d for Fe500) in your exam answer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates a choice between two 14-mark questions in the ESE for each module. Below are **Question A** and **Question B**, each with two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** A singly reinforced concrete beam has a width of 250 mm and effective depth 500 mm. It is reinforced with 4 bars of 20 mm diameter. Using M20 concrete and Fe415 steel, determine the **moment of resistance** of the section. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data**

$b = 250$ mm, $d = 500$ mm, $A_{st} = 4 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 1256$ mm², $f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm².

**Step 2 — Calculate Depth of Neutral Axis**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_u &= \frac{0.87 f_y A_{st}}{0.36 f_{ck} b} \\
&= \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 1256}{0.36 \times 20 \times 250} \\
&= \frac{453,267}{1800} \\
&= 251.8 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with $x_{u,max}$**

$x_{u,max} = 0.48 \times 500 = 240$ mm.

Since $x_u = 251.8$ mm $> x_{u,max} = 240$ mm → **Section is over-reinforced, not permissible.**

**Step 4 — Safe Moment of Resistance (Limited to $M_{u,lim}$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,lim} &= 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times 500^2 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 172.5 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**The safe design moment of resistance is $M_u = 172.5$ kN·m.** The section must be redesigned with a larger effective depth OR a smaller area of steel.

---

**(b)** Design a singly reinforced concrete beam for a **factored moment of 200 kN·m** using M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. Adopt a width of 300 mm. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assume Effective Depth**

Since $M_u = 200$ kN·m is moderately high, take $d = 550$ mm.

**Step 2 — Compute $M_{u,lim}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,lim} &= 0.138 \times 20 \times 300 \times 550^2 \times 10^{-6} = 250.47 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $M_u = 200$ kN·m $< M_{u,lim} = 250.47$ kN·m → **Section is adequate as singly reinforced.**

**Step 3 — Compute $A_{st}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2} &= \frac{4.6 \times 200 \times 10^6}{20 \times 300 \times 550^2} = 0.5074 \\
A_{st} &= \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415} \left(1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.5074}\right) \times 300 \times 550 \\
&= 0.02410 \times (1 - 0.7021) \times 165000 \\
&= 0.02410 \times 0.2979 \times 165000 \\
&= 1185 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Select Bars**

Use **20 mm bars** ($A_\phi = 314$ mm²):

$$
\text{Number of bars} = \frac{1185}{314} = 3.77 \approx 4 \text{ bars}
$$

**Provide 4 bars of 20 mm diameter** ($A_{st,provided} = 1256$ mm²).

**Step 5 — Check Minimum Steel**

$$
A_{st,min} = \frac{0.85 \times 300 \times 550}{415} = 338 \text{ mm}^2 \quad (\text{since } 1256 > 338, \text{ OK})
$$

**Final Answer:** $b = 300$ mm, $d = 550$ mm, 4 bars of 20 mm dia at the bottom.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** A simply supported beam has an **effective span of 5 m** and carries a **superimposed load of 18 kN/m** (excluding self-weight). Design the beam using M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. Take the width as 250 mm. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assume Effective Depth**

Assume $d = 400$ mm, $D = 450$ mm, $b = 250$ mm.

**Step 2 — Self-weight and Factored Load**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Self-weight} &= 0.25 \times 0.45 \times 25 = 2.81 \text{ kN/m} \\
w &= 18 + 2.81 = 20.81 \text{ kN/m} \\
w_u &= 1.5 \times 20.81 = 31.22 \text{ kN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Bending Moment**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_u &= \frac{31.22 \times 5^2}{8} = \frac{31.22 \times 25}{8} = 97.56 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Check Adequacy**

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times 400^2 \times 10^{-6} = 110.4 \text{ kN·m}
$$

Since $M_u = 97.56$ kN·m $< M_{u,lim} = 110.4$ kN·m → **OK.**

**Step 5 — Compute $A_{st}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{4.6 \times 97.56 \times 10^6}{20 \times 250 \times 400^2} &= \frac{448.78 \times 10^6}{800 \times 10^6} = 0.5610 \\
A_{st} &= 0.02410 \times (1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.5610}) \times 250 \times 400 \\
&= 0.02410 \times (1 - 0.6625) \times 100000 \\
&= 0.02410 \times 0.3375 \times 100000 \\
&= 813.4 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Select Bars**

Use **16 mm bars** ($A_\phi = 201$ mm²):

$$
\text{Number of bars} = \frac{813.4}{201} = 4.05 \approx 5 \text{ bars}
$$

**Provide 5 bars of 16 mm diameter** ($A_{st,provided} = 1005$ mm²).

**Step 7 — Check Minimum Steel**

$$
A_{st,min} = \frac{0.85 \times 250 \times 400}{415} = 204.8 \text{ mm}^2 \quad (\text{since } 1005 > 204.8, \text{ OK})
$$

**Final Answer:** $b = 250$ mm, $D = 450$ mm, $d = 400$ mm, 5 bars of 16 mm dia at the bottom.

---

**(b)** A rectangular beam of section **300 mm × 600 mm (overall)** is reinforced with **6 bars of 25 mm diameter** at the bottom. Effective cover to the centroid of steel = 60 mm. Using M25 concrete and Fe500 steel, determine the **moment of resistance** of the beam. Is the section safe according to IS 456? (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data**

$b = 300$ mm, $D = 600$ mm, $d = 600 - 60 = 540$ mm, $A_{st} = 6 \times 491 = 2946$ mm², $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 500$ N/mm².

**Step 2 — Depth of Neutral Axis**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_u &= \frac{0.87 f_y A_{st}}{0.36 f_{ck} b} = \frac{0.87 \times 500 \times 2946}{0.36 \times 25 \times 300} \\
&= \frac{1,281,510}{2700} = 474.6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Limiting Neutral Axis (Fe500)**

$$
x_{u,max} = 0.46 \times d = 0.46 \times 540 = 248.4 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $x_u = 474.6$ mm $\gg x_{u,max} = 248.4$ mm → **Section is grossly over-reinforced, NOT safe.**

**Step 4 — Safe Moment of Resistance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,lim} &= 0.133 \times 25 \times 300 \times 540^2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ (using Fe500 coefficient 0.133)} \\
&= 0.133 \times 25 \times 300 \times 291600 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 290.8 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**The safe design moment of resistance is $M_u = 290.8$ kN·m.** The section must be redesigned (e.g., increase $d$ to $\geq 825$ mm, OR reduce bar diameter, OR design as a doubly reinforced beam).

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the load factor 1.5** on the imposed and dead loads (LOSES 1 mark easily).
> 2. **Computing $x_u > x_{u,max}$ and still using it** to calculate $M_u$ directly (LOSES 2 marks — code violation).
> 3. **Using $f_y$ instead of $0.87 f_y$** in the steel area / moment equations (LOSES 1 mark).
> 4. **Failing to check $A_{st,min}$ and $A_{st,max}$** (LOSES 1 mark).
> 5. **Reporting decimal fractions of bars** (e.g., 3.77 bars) — round up to next whole number, then recheck minimum steel.
> 6. **Neglecting self-weight** in Problem Type 3 (LOSES 1 mark).
> 7. **Effective span miscalculation** — for simply supported beams, IS 456 Cl. 22.2 specifies $l_{eff} = \min(\text{clear span} + d, \text{centre-to-centre of supports})$.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Numerical Problems in Module 2:**
>
> ✅ **Always start with:** Identify given $b, d, M_u, f_{ck}, f_y$ and classify the problem as **Analysis** (find $M_u$ from given section) or **Design** (find $A_{st}$ from given $M_u$).
>
> ✅ **Memorise:** $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ (Fe415) and $0.133 f_{ck} b d^2$ (Fe500).
>
> ✅ **Memorise:** $x_{u,max} = 0.48 d$ (Fe415) and $0.46 d$ (Fe500).
>
> ✅ **Memorise:** Quadratic formula for $A_{st}$: $A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5 f_{ck}}{f_y}\left(1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2}}\right) b d$
>
> ✅ **Memorise:** $x_u = \dfrac{0.87 f_y A_{st}}{0.36 f_{ck} b}$.
>
> ✅ **Check sequence in design problems:** $M_u$ vs $M_{u,lim}$ → $A_{st}$ → $A_{st,min}$ check → $A_{st,max}$ check → bar selection.
>
> ✅ **Bar areas to remember:** 12 mm → 113 mm², 16 mm → 201 mm², 20 mm → 314 mm², 25 mm → 491 mm², 32 mm → 804 mm².
>
> ✅ **Effective span rules (IS 456 Cl. 22.2):** For simply supported: $\min(\text{clear span} + d, \text{centre-to-centre of bearings})$.
>
> ✅ **Partial safety factors:** $\gamma_m = 1.5$ for concrete, $\gamma_m = 1.15$ for steel. These yield $0.45 f_{ck}$ and $0.87 f_y$ as design strengths.
>
> ✅ **Load factor:** Always 1.5 for both dead and live loads (for normal limit state of collapse).
>
> ✅ **If $x_u > x_{u,max}$ in an analysis problem:** Restrict the moment to $M_{u,lim}$ and report the section as "over-reinforced, redesign required."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limit State of Serviceability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Limit State of Serviceability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (IS 456 : 2000 Terminology)

The **Limit State of Serviceability (LSS)** is the state beyond which a structure, or any part of it, becomes unfit for its intended use or fails to satisfy the serviceability criteria prescribed in the code, even though it may still possess an adequate margin of safety against collapse. Per **Clause 8.2 of IS 456 : 2000**, serviceability limit states correspond to:

- Deformations adversely affecting the appearance or efficient use of the structure.
- Deflections that cause damage to non-structural elements (partitions, finishes, glazing).
- Vibrations causing discomfort or impairment of function.
- Cracking of concrete leading to corrosion of reinforcement, leakage, or unacceptable aesthetics.
- Excessive local damage such as spalling, honeycombing, or localised crushing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Serviceability is a *design criterion*, not just a check after the fact. The KTU board expects students to verify both Ultimate Limit State (ULS) **and** Serviceability Limit State (SLS) for every flexural design problem.

## 1.2 Types of Serviceability Limit States

| S.No. | Serviceability Limit State | Governing Clause (IS 456) | Symptom |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Deflection** | Cl. 35.1.1, Cl. 23.2 | Excessive sagging, ponding, partition cracks |
| 2 | **Cracking** | Cl. 35.2, Cl. 35.3, Annex F | Visible cracks, leakage, rebar corrosion |
| 3 | **Vibration** | Cl. 35.4 (general), Cl. 33.4 | Discomfort to occupants, malfunction of equipment |
| 4 | **Durability** | Cl. 8 & 35.4 | Spalling, carbonation, chloride ingress |
| 5 | **Fire Resistance** | Cl. 21 (cross-ref.) | Loss of cover, spalling under fire |

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU syllabus, **deflection** and **cracking** are the two most frequently tested sub-topics in Module 3. Vibration and fire are typically covered in dedicated courses or briefly referenced.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you buy a new **wooden bookshelf** for your study room. The shelf does not collapse under the weight of your books (so it is *safe* — ULS is satisfied). However, after a few months, the middle of the shelf sags visibly, your books start sliding off, and the surface develops fine cracks along the wood grain. The shelf is still standing, but it has become **unfit for use** — that is exactly the meaning of "crossing the serviceability limit." The same principle applies to concrete beams, slabs, and cantilevers in buildings and bridges.

## 1.4 Governing Design Philosophy

A reinforced concrete member is designed in two parallel tracks:

1. **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** → Ensures adequate **strength** (flexure, shear, torsion, bond, axial).
2. **Serviceability Limit State (SLS)** → Ensures adequate **performance in use** (deflection ≤ limit, crack width ≤ limit).

Both must be satisfied. A member that is strong but deflects excessively has failed at the SLS even though it has a comfortable strength reserve.

## 1.5 Permissible Deflection Limits (IS 456 Table 14 / Clause 35.1.1)

| Type of Member | Maximum Permissible Deflection |
|---|---|
| Cantilever | **Span / 250** (for final) and Span / 350 (post-construction) |
| Simply supported beam/slab | **Span / 250** (final) and Span / 350 (post-construction) |
| Continuous beam/slab (end span) | Span / 250 or **20 mm**, whichever is less (post-construction) |
| Continuous beam/slab (interior span) | Span / 250 or **20 mm**, whichever is less (post-construction) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final deflection** includes short-term (immediate) deflection + long-term deflection due to shrinkage + creep. The 20 mm ceiling applies to members supporting brittle partitions.

## 1.6 Permissible Crack Widths (IS 456 Clause 35.2 & 35.3)

| Exposure Condition | Max. Surface Crack Width |
|---|---|
| **Mild** | 0.3 mm (some texts permit up to 0.4 mm) |
| **Moderate** | 0.3 mm |
| **Severe** | 0.2 mm |
| **Very Severe / Marine** | 0.1 mm (often recommended by designers) |

These limits are for **flexural** cracks on the tension face. Web-shear cracks and diagonal-tension cracks are separately controlled by minimum shear reinforcement (Cl. 25.5) and maximum shear stress (Cl. 40.2).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deflection Control — Two Complementary Approaches

IS 456 offers two equivalent ways to ensure deflections remain within limits:

### Approach A — Span-to-Effective-Depth (Span/d) Ratio (Cl. 23.2)
A simple *indirect* method where the designer's task is to provide a minimum **effective depth** such that the actual span/d ratio does not exceed the modified permissible value.

### Approach B — Direct Deflection Computation (Cl. 35.1.1, Annex C)
A *direct* method where the short-term and long-term deflections are explicitly computed and summed, then compared to the permissible limits.

Both approaches are valid. KTU problems often use Approach A for quick design and Approach B for verification.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Approach A (Span/d Method)

The basic span/d ratio for simply supported members is **20** (for Fe415) or **26** (for Fe500). This base value is then **modified** by a set of correction factors:

1. **Modification factor for tension reinforcement (k₁)** — depends on percentage of tension steel (p_t).
2. **Modification factor for compression reinforcement (k₂)** — k₂ = 1 + (p'_c / p_t) but ≤ 1.5 (per Fig 5 of IS 456, an empirical formula is also acceptable: k₂ = 1 + 2 p'_c / p_t).
3. **Modification factor for span (k₃)** — for spans > 10 m, the permissible span/d is reduced.
4. **Modification factor for service stress in steel (k₄)** — accounts for actual steel stress fs in service (0.58 fy for ULS design).

The modified permissible span/d is:

$$(\text{Span}/d)_{\text{perm}} = (\text{Span}/d)_{\text{basic}} \times k_1 \times k_2 \times k_3 \times k_4$$

> [!NOTE]
> The span/d method is an *empirical* check. It is not a substitute for direct calculation. For slender, critical, or unusual members, Approach B is mandatory.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Approach B (Direct Computation)

### Step 1 — Compute Short-Term Deflection (δ_short)
The member may be **uncracked**, **cracked**, or in a **transitional state** depending on the ratio of cracking moment (Mcr) to applied moment (M):

$$I_{\text{eff}} = I_{\text{gr}} - \left(\frac{M_{\text{cr}}}{M}\right)^2 \left(I_{\text{gr}} - I_{\text{cr}}\right)$$

where:
- $I_{\text{gr}}$ = gross moment of inertia of the uncracked section (ignoring reinforcement).
- $I_{\text{cr}}$ = cracked moment of inertia (transformed section, ignoring tension concrete).
- $M_{\text{cr}}$ = cracking moment = $\frac{f_{\text{cr}} \cdot I_{\text{gr}}}{y_t}$, with $f_{\text{cr}} = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (N/mm²).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Branson equation** adopted by IS 456 via Annex C. Note carefully that the squaring is applied to the **moment ratio**, not the inertia ratio.

The short-term deflection for a uniformly loaded simply supported member is:

$$\delta_{\text{short}} = \frac{5 \, w \, L^4}{384 \, E_c \, I_{\text{eff}}}$$

For other loadings and end conditions, standard elastic formulas apply (e.g., $\delta = WL^3/48EI$ for central point load).

### Step 2 — Compute Long-Term Deflection (δ_long)
The long-term deflection arises from creep and shrinkage of concrete under sustained loads. IS 456 (Annex C, Cl. C-4) gives:

$$\delta_{\text{long}} = \alpha_{\text{long}} \times \delta_{\text{short,sustained}}$$

where $\alpha_{\text{long}}$ is computed from:

$$\alpha_{\text{long}} = \frac{\xi}{1 + 50 \, \rho'_c}$$

with:
- $\xi$ = time-dependent coefficient (1.0 for 5 years or more, 0.8 for 1 year, 0.5 for 6 months).
- $\rho'_c$ = percentage of compression steel at midspan = $100 \, A_{\text{sc}} / (b \cdot d)$.

### Step 3 — Combine
The total (final) deflection is:

$$\delta_{\text{final}} = \delta_{\text{short,imposed}} + \delta_{\text{short,sustained}} \times (1 + \alpha_{\text{long}})$$

## 2.4 Cracking — IS 456 Formula (Cl. 35.3 & Annex F)

For flexural tension cracks, the design surface crack width is given by:

$$w_{\text{cr}} = \frac{3 \, a_{\text{cr}} \, \varepsilon_m}{1 + 2 \, \dfrac{(a_{\text{cr,min}} - a_{\text{cr}})}{(h - x)}}$$

where:
- $a_{\text{cr}}$ = distance from the tension face to the centroid of the nearest longitudinal bar.
- $a_{\text{cr,min}}$ = minimum value of $a_{\text{cr}}$ for all bars in the tension zone.
- $h$ = overall depth of the member.
- $x$ = depth of the neutral axis.
- $\varepsilon_m$ = average steel strain at the level of the crack, given by:

$$\varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{f_s}{E_s} \left(1 - \frac{b \cdot x}{A_{\text{st}} \cdot E_s} \cdot \frac{E_s}{E_c}\right)$$

In simplified form, the strain is taken as:

$$\varepsilon_m = \frac{f_s}{E_s} - \frac{b \, x \, (x - d)}{3 \, E_s \, A_{\text{st}} \, (d - x)}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All units are **N** and **mm** unless otherwise noted. Replace `\vert` for absolute values to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cracking moment | $M_{\text{cr}} = f_{\text{cr}} \cdot I_{\text{gr}} / y_t$ | Cl. C-3, Annex C |
| 2 | Modulus of rupture | $f_{\text{cr}} = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ N/mm² | Cl. 6.1.2 & C-3 |
| 3 | Short-term elastic modulus of concrete | $E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ N/mm² | Cl. 6.2.3.1 |
| 4 | Effective moment of inertia (Branson) | $I_{\text{eff}} = I_{\text{gr}} - \left(\frac{M_{\text{cr}}}{M}\right)^2 (I_{\text{gr}} - I_{\text{cr}})$ | Annex C, Cl. C-3 |
| 5 | Cracked moment of inertia | $I_{\text{cr}} = \frac{b \cdot x^3}{3} + \frac{E_s}{E_c} \cdot A_{\text{st}} (d - x)^2$ | Annex C |
| 6 | Depth of neutral axis (cracked transformed) | Quadratic in $x$: $\frac{b \cdot x^2}{2} = m \cdot A_{\text{st}} (d - x)$ | Annex C |
| 7 | Short-term deflection (simply supported, UDL) | $\delta_{\text{short}} = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E_c I_{\text{eff}}}$ | Annex C |
| 8 | Long-term deflection coefficient | $\alpha = \dfrac{\xi}{1 + 50 \rho'_c}$, $\rho'_c = \dfrac{100 A_{\text{sc}}}{b d}$ | Cl. C-4 |
| 9 | Basic span/d (simply supported, Fe415) | $(\text{Span}/d)_{\text{basic}} = 20$ | Table 9, Cl. 23.2.1 |
| 10 | Modified permissible span/d | $(\text{Span}/d)_{\text{perm}} = (\text{Span}/d)_{\text{basic}} \times k_1 k_2 k_3 k_4$ | Cl. 23.2.1 |
| 11 | Modification for compression steel | $k_2 = 1 + 2 \rho'_c / \rho_t$ (or Fig 5 of IS 456) | Cl. 23.2.1(b) |
| 12 | Modification for span > 10 m | $k_3 = 10 / L$ (for simply supported); $k_3 = 10 / (L - 1)$ (for continuous) | Cl. 23.2.1(c) |
| 13 | Modification for steel stress | $k_4 = 0.4 f_y / f_s$, with $f_s = 0.58 f_y$ (under DL+LL) | Cl. 23.2.1(d) |
| 14 | Surface crack width | $w_{\text{cr}} = \dfrac{3 a_{\text{cr}} \varepsilon_m}{1 + 2 (a_{\text{cr,min}} - a_{\text{cr}})/(h - x)}$ | Cl. 35.3.2 / Annex F |
| 15 | Average steel strain | $\varepsilon_m = \dfrac{f_s}{E_s} - \dfrac{b x (x - d)}{3 E_s A_{\text{st}} (d - x)}$ | Annex F, Cl. F-2 |
| 16 | Permissible crack width (moderate / mild) | 0.3 mm | Cl. 35.3.1 |
| 17 | Permissible crack width (severe) | 0.2 mm | Cl. 35.3.1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** These checks are mandatory in the design of long-span bridges, multi-storey commercial floors, water-retaining structures, and tall chimneys. A failed SLS check typically requires either (a) increasing section depth, (b) adding compression steel, (c) reducing service stresses, or (d) pre-stressing the member.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the foundational equations from first principles. Each algebraic step is written out in full.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Effective Moment of Inertia (Branson's Equation)

The cracked-section analysis assumes concrete below the neutral axis is fully cracked and ignores its tensile strength. The uncracked-section analysis assumes the full gross section resists bending. The real behaviour is intermediate: part of the span may be cracked (near the location of maximum moment) and part uncracked (toward the supports).

### 3.1.1 The Branson's Empirical Equation

Branson (1963) proposed an empirical interpolation formula, later adopted by IS 456 Annex C:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{eff}} &= I_{\text{gr}} - \left(\frac{M_{\text{cr}}}{M}\right)^2 (I_{\text{gr}} - I_{\text{cr}}) \\
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{When } M \leq M_{\text{cr}}, \text{ the ratio } (M_{\text{cr}}/M)^2 \geq 1 \text{ is capped at 1 (clamped).} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Substituting } (M_{\text{cr}}/M)^2 = 1 \text{ gives } I_{\text{eff}} = I_{\text{gr}} \text{ (uncracked).} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{When } M \gg M_{\text{cr}}, \text{ the ratio } \to 0 \text{ and } I_{\text{eff}} \to I_{\text{cr}} \text{ (fully cracked).}
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives a smooth transition governed by the **square** of the moment ratio.

### 3.1.2 Cracked Moment of Inertia Derivation

Consider a rectangular beam of width $b$ and effective depth $d$, with tensile steel area $A_{\text{st}}$ at the level of reinforcement. Let the depth of the neutral axis in the cracked transformed section be $x$. The modular ratio is $m = E_s / E_c$.

Equilibrium of moments of area about the neutral axis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b \cdot \frac{x^2}{2} &= m \cdot A_{\text{st}} \cdot (d - x) \\
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{This is a quadratic in } x. \text{ Solve: } b x^2 + 2 m A_{\text{st}} x - 2 m A_{\text{st}} d = 0. \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Using the quadratic formula:} \\
x &= \frac{-2 m A_{\text{st}} + \sqrt{4 m^2 A_{\text{st}}^2 + 8 b \cdot m \cdot A_{\text{st}} d}}{2 b} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Simplifying:} \\
x &= \frac{-m A_{\text{st}} + \sqrt{(m A_{\text{st}})^2 + 2 b m A_{\text{st}} d}}{b} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{The cracked moment of inertia (about the N.A.) is then:} \\
I_{\text{cr}} &= \frac{b \cdot x^3}{3} + m \cdot A_{\text{st}} \cdot (d - x)^2 \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{First term: concrete compression zone, parallel-axis about N.A.} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Second term: steel transformed to equivalent concrete, distance }(d-x)\text{ from N.A.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Cracking Moment

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{cr}} &= f_{\text{cr}} \cdot Z_{\text{gr}} \\
\text{where} \quad & f_{\text{cr}} = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}} \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (modulus of rupture, Cl. 6.1.2)} \\
& Z_{\text{gr}} = I_{\text{gr}} / y_t \\
& y_t = \text{distance from centroidal axis to extreme tension fibre} \\
& \text{For a rectangular section, } y_t = h/2. \\
& I_{\text{gr}} = b h^3 / 12.
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Long-Term Deflection Coefficient Derivation

IS 456 Annex C (Cl. C-4) recommends:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \frac{\xi}{1 + 50 \, \rho'_c} \\
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \xi \text{ is a time-dependent multiplier for creep + shrinkage.} \\
& \xi = 0.05 \text{ (1 month)}, \; 0.5 \text{ (6 months)}, \; 0.8 \text{ (1 year)}, \; 1.0 \text{ (5 years+)}. \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \rho'_c \text{ is the percentage of compression steel measured against } b d. \\
& \rho'_c = \frac{100 A_{\text{sc}}}{b \cdot d} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Increasing } A_{\text{sc}} \text{ reduces } \alpha \text{ because compression steel restrains creep shortening.} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Boundary check: if } \rho'_c = 0, \; \alpha = \xi. \text{ If } \rho'_c = 1.0\%, \; \alpha = \xi / 1.5 = 0.67 \xi. \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{For 5-year } \xi = 1.0 \text{ with } \rho'_c = 0.5\%, \; \alpha = 1.0 / 1.25 = 0.80.
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Full Direct-Deflection and Crack-Width Check

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Module 3 Pattern):**
A simply supported rectangular RCC beam, span $L = 6 \text{ m}$, width $b = 230 \text{ mm}$, overall depth $h = 500 \text{ mm}$, effective depth $d = 450 \text{ mm}$. Reinforcement: 4 bars of 16 mm $\phi$ at mid-span (2 bars curtailed at supports). $f_{ck} = 20 \text{ N/mm}^2$, $f_y = 415 \text{ N/mm}^2$. Exposure: Moderate. Live load on beam: 12 kN/m. Verify (a) deflection, (b) crack width.

### Step 1 — Section Properties

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{st}} &= 4 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 4 \times 201.06 = 804.25 \text{ mm}^2 \\
I_{\text{gr}} &= \frac{230 \times 500^3}{12} = 2.3958 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4 \\
y_t &= h/2 = 250 \text{ mm} \\
E_c &= 5000 \sqrt{20} = 5000 \times 4.4721 = 22360.7 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
E_s &= 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
m &= E_s / E_c = 200000 / 22360.7 = 8.945
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Cracking Moment and Cracked Inertia

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{cr}} &= 0.7 \sqrt{20} = 0.7 \times 4.4721 = 3.13 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
M_{\text{cr}} &= \frac{3.13 \times 2.3958 \times 10^9}{250} = 30.0 \times 10^6 \text{ N-mm} = 30.0 \text{ kN-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solve for $x$ (depth of N.A. in cracked transformed section):

$$
\begin{aligned}
b x^2 + 2 m A_{\text{st}} x - 2 m A_{\text{st}} d &= 0 \\
230 \, x^2 + 2 \times 8.945 \times 804.25 \times x - 2 \times 8.945 \times 804.25 \times 450 &= 0 \\
230 \, x^2 + 14389.5 \, x - 6,475,275 &= 0 \\
x &= \frac{-14389.5 + \sqrt{14389.5^2 + 4 \times 230 \times 6475275}}{2 \times 230} \\
x &= \frac{-14389.5 + \sqrt{2.0706 \times 10^8 + 5.9565 \times 10^9}}{460} \\
x &= \frac{-14389.5 + \sqrt{6.1635 \times 10^9}}{460} \\
x &= \frac{-14389.5 + 78507.6}{460} = \frac{64118.1}{460} = 139.4 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Cracked moment of inertia:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{cr}} &= \frac{230 \times 139.4^3}{3} + 8.945 \times 804.25 \times (450 - 139.4)^2 \\
&= \frac{230 \times 2.708 \times 10^6}{3} + 8.945 \times 804.25 \times 310.6^2 \\
&= 2.076 \times 10^8 + 8.945 \times 804.25 \times 96,472 \\
&= 2.076 \times 10^8 + 6.94 \times 10^8 \\
&= 9.016 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Service Moment and Effective I

Assuming total service load $w = $ self-weight + LL:
- Self-weight of beam = $0.23 \times 0.50 \times 25 = 2.875$ kN/m.
- Superimposed DL (say) = 2.0 kN/m.
- Live load = 12 kN/m.
- Total $w = 2.875 + 2.0 + 12 = 16.875$ kN/m.
- Total $M = w L^2 / 8 = 16.875 \times 6^2 / 8 = 75.94$ kN-m.
- Sustained (DL only) = $4.875$ kN/m, $M_{\text{sus}} = 4.875 \times 36 / 8 = 21.94$ kN-m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{M_{\text{cr}}}{M}\right)^2 &= \left(\frac{30.0}{75.94}\right)^2 = (0.395)^2 = 0.156 \\
I_{\text{eff}} &= I_{\text{gr}} - 0.156 (I_{\text{gr}} - I_{\text{cr}}) \\
&= 2.3958 \times 10^9 - 0.156 \times (2.3958 - 0.9016) \times 10^9 \\
&= 2.3958 \times 10^9 - 0.156 \times 1.4942 \times 10^9 \\
&= 2.3958 \times 10^9 - 0.2331 \times 10^9 = 2.163 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Short-Term Deflection

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{\text{short,total}} &= \frac{5 \times 16.875 \times (6000)^4}{384 \times 22360.7 \times 2.163 \times 10^9} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 16.875 \times 1.296 \times 10^{15}}{384 \times 22360.7 \times 2.163 \times 10^9} \\
&= \frac{1.0935 \times 10^{17}}{1.857 \times 10^{16}} = 5.89 \text{ mm} \\
\delta_{\text{short,sus}} &= \frac{5 \times 4.875 \times 1.296 \times 10^{15}}{1.857 \times 10^{16}} = 1.70 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Long-Term Multiplier (assume no compression steel, $\rho'_c = 0$, $\xi = 1.0$ for 5 yr)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \frac{1.0}{1 + 50 \times 0} = 1.0 \\
\delta_{\text{long}} &= 1.0 \times 1.70 = 1.70 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Total Deflection

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{\text{final}} &= \delta_{\text{short,total}} - \delta_{\text{short,sus}} + \delta_{\text{short,sus}} (1 + \alpha) \\
&= 5.89 - 1.70 + 1.70 \times 2 = 5.89 - 1.70 + 3.40 = 7.59 \text{ mm} \\
\delta_{\text{perm}} &= L/250 = 6000/250 = 24 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Since } 7.59 \text{ mm} \ll 24 \text{ mm, deflection is SAFE.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Crack Width Check (Moderate Exposure)

Assume 2 bars continue to support, the **nearest bar centroid distance** $a_{\text{cr}} = 50$ mm (cover 40 mm + half bar $\phi$ 16 mm = 48 mm; rounded up). $a_{\text{cr,min}} = a_{\text{cr}} = 50$ mm (since all bars in tension zone have same cover).

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_s &= 0.58 \times 415 = 240.7 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
\varepsilon_m &= \frac{f_s}{E_s} - \frac{b \, x (x - d)}{3 E_s A_{\text{st}} (d - x)} \\
&= \frac{240.7}{200000} - \frac{230 \times 139.4 \times (139.4 - 450)}{3 \times 200000 \times 804.25 \times 310.6} \\
&= 0.0012035 - \frac{230 \times 139.4 \times (-310.6)}{3 \times 200000 \times 804.25 \times 310.6} \\
&= 0.0012035 - \frac{-9.96 \times 10^6}{1.50 \times 10^{11}} \\
&= 0.0012035 + 6.64 \times 10^{-5} = 0.00127 \\
w_{\text{cr}} &= \frac{3 \times 50 \times 0.00127}{1 + 2 \times \dfrac{0}{450 - 139.4}} = \frac{0.1905}{1} = 0.190 \text{ mm} \\
\text{For moderate exposure, limit} &= 0.3 \text{ mm}. \\
\text{Since } 0.19 < 0.30 \text{ mm, crack width is SAFE.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The student should recognise that the dominant term in $w_{\text{cr}}$ is the steel strain $\varepsilon_m$, which is essentially $f_s / E_s$. Hence the control of crack width is **most effectively achieved by limiting the service stress $f_s$ in the tension steel** — the reason why IS 456 indirectly limits $f_s$ through the span/d modification factor $k_4$.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Approach

| Condition | Preferred Approach | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Standard simply supported beam, normal span | Span/d (Approach A) | Quick, conservative |
| Slender cantilever, high span | Direct computation (Approach B) | Span/d is too approximate |
| Pre-stressed / post-tensioned members | Direct computation (Approach B) | Span/d does not apply to PSC |
| Beam supporting brittle partition | Direct check $\delta \leq 20$ mm | Specific clause triggered |
| Class-A type structure (isocontrol) | Crack-width check mandatory | Aesthetic / durability critical |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Serviceability Verification — Block-Level Flow

The following Mermaid block captures the **decision logic** that a designer follows while checking the Limit State of Serviceability for a flexural concrete member. The flow is intentionally a process-architecture diagram (per the protocol's *Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow* rule) rather than a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartA([Member Designed at ULS]) --> InputA[Define: L, b, d, fck, fy, Ast, Asc]
    InputA --> ModE[Compute Ec = 5000 sqrt fck and m = Es Ec]
    ModE --> MacA[Compute Igr = b h cube by 12]
    MacA --> MacB[Compute Mcr = 0.7 sqrt fck times Igr by yt]
    MacB --> MacC[Compute Icr from cracked transformed section quadratic]
    MacC --> MacD[Compute M service = w L square by 8]
    MacD --> IeffStep[Compute Ieff = Igr minus Mcr by M squared times Igr minus Icr]
    IeffStep --> DeltaShort[Compute delta short using 5 w L4 by 384 Ec Ieff]
    DeltaShort --> DeltaSus[Split total load into sustained and transient]
    DeltaSus --> AlphaStep[Compute alpha = xi by 1 plus 50 rho prime c]
    AlphaStep --> DeltaLong[Compute delta long = alpha times delta short sustained]
    DeltaLong --> Combine[delta final = delta short imposed plus delta short sustained times 1 plus alpha]
    Combine --> CheckDefl{Is delta final less than L by 250 and less than 20 mm?}
    CheckDefl -- NO --> IncDepth[Increase effective depth d OR add Asc]
    IncDepth --> ReDesign[Return to ULS design loop]
    CheckDefl -- YES --> CrackCheck[Proceed to crack width check]
    CrackCheck --> AstDist[Compute acr and acr min for tension bars]
    AstDist --> StrainStep[Compute epsilon m from steel stress and concrete area]
    StrainStep --> WcrStep[Compute wcr from IS 456 Annex F formula]
    WcrStep --> CompareCrack{Is wcr less than limit per exposure class?}
    CompareCrack -- NO --> IncCover[Reduce steel stress OR reduce bar spacing OR increase cover]
    IncCover --> ReDesign
    CompareCrack -- YES --> VibCheck{Is vibration sensitive use case?}
    VibCheck -- YES --> VibLimit[Check natural frequency fn less than 5 Hz limit]
    VibCheck -- NO --> Final[Serviceability Satisfied]
    VibLimit --> Final
```

## 4.2 Two-Track Design Philosophy — Concept Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRACK1[Track 1: Ultimate Limit State ULS]
        U1[Apply Load Factor 1.5 DL plus 1.5 LL] --> U2[Compute Mu and Vu]
        U2 --> U3[Provide Ast for flexure]
        U3 --> U4[Check shear torsion and bond]
    end
    subgraph TRACK2[Track 2: Serviceability Limit State SLS]
        S1[Apply Unfactored Service Load] --> S2[Compute M service]
        S2 --> S3[Compute Ieff and Icr]
        S3 --> S4[Compute delta short and delta long]
        S4 --> S5{Deflection OK?}
        S5 -- NO --> S6[Revise d or Asc]
        S5 -- YES --> S7[Compute crack width wcr]
        S7 --> S8{Crack OK?}
        S8 -- NO --> S9[Revise Ast or cover or bar spacing]
        S8 -- YES --> S10[SLS Passed]
    end
    TRACK1 --> TRACK2
    S6 --> TRACK1
    S9 --> TRACK1
    S10 --> END1([Member Safe and Serviceable])
```

## 4.3 Modified Span/d Method — Modification Factor Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BaseVal[(Span by d basic = 20 for Fe415 SS)] --> K1[Multiply by k1 from Fig 4 of IS 456 based on pt]
    K1 --> K2[Multiply by k2 = 1 plus 2 pc by pt for compression steel]
    K2 --> K3[Multiply by k3 = 10 by L for span above 10 m]
    K3 --> K4[Multiply by k4 = 0.4 fy by fs for service steel stress]
    K4 --> PermVal[(Span by d permissible)]
    ActualVal[Actual span by d of beam] --> Compare{Is Actual less than Permissible?}
    Compare -- YES --> OK[Deflection OK by Span d method]
    Compare -- NO --> NOTOK[Deflection not OK: increase d or add Asc]
```

## 4.4 Typical Reinforcement Detail for Crack Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TENSFACETENSION[Concrete Tension Face of Beam]
        B1[Bar 1: 16 mm dia at 50 mm from face]
        B2[Bar 2: 16 mm dia at 75 mm from face]
        B3[Bar 3: 16 mm dia at 100 mm from face]
        B4[Bar 4: 16 mm dia at 125 mm from face]
    end
    B1 --> SP1[Spacing rule: clear bar spacing >= max of 25 mm or bar dia]
    B2 --> SP1
    B3 --> SP1
    B4 --> SP1
    SP1 --> Co[Cover rule: clear cover >= max of bar dia or 25 mm or per exposure]
    Co --> Result[Minimise acr to reduce crack width wcr]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In Mermaid, the single-line `flowchart` syntax and short node IDs (e.g. `B1`, `K1`) are used deliberately. The protocol forbids reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph` as standalone node names, so each identifier is prefixed with letters and a short descriptive suffix.
