# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** The most common mistake KTU students make on serviceability problems is **forgetting to separate the sustained and imposed components of deflection** before applying the long-term multiplier $\alpha$. The code's formula for *final* deflection is NOT simply $\delta_{\text{short}}(1 + \alpha)$. The correct formulation is $\delta_{\text{short,imposed}} + \delta_{\text{short,sustained}} (1 + \alpha)$. Examiners specifically watch for this and award 1–2 marks for the correct split.

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define the Limit State of Serviceability. List its various types as per IS 456 : 2000. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Limit State of Serviceability (LSS) is the state at which a structure, or any part of it, becomes unfit for its intended use or fails to satisfy the prescribed serviceability criteria, even though it may still possess an adequate margin of safety against collapse. Serviceability limit states are concerned with the **performance of the structure under service loads**, rather than its ultimate strength.
>
> Per IS 456 : 2000 (Cl. 8.2), the various types of serviceability limit states are:
> 1. **Deflection** — excessive sagging of beams and slabs (Cl. 35.1.1).
> 2. **Cracking** — visible cracks in concrete (Cl. 35.2 and 35.3).
> 3. **Vibration** — excessive oscillation under dynamic loads (Cl. 35.4).
> 4. **Durability** — loss of cover or corrosion (Cl. 8).
> 5. **Fire resistance** — loss of integrity under elevated temperature (Cl. 21).
>
> The two most frequently governing types in flexural RCC design are deflection and cracking.

### Q2. State the permissible deflection limits for (a) a simply supported beam and (b) a cantilever member as per IS 456 : 2000. Briefly mention the difference between the *post-construction* and *final* limits. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Per IS 456 : 2000 (Cl. 35.1.1 and Table 14):
>
> | Member Type | Post-Construction Limit | Final (including long-term effects) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Simply supported beam/slab | **Span / 350** (or 20 mm, whichever is less, if supporting brittle partition) | **Span / 250** |
> | Cantilever | **Span / 350** | **Span / 250** |
>
> - The **post-construction deflection** is the *immediate* deflection due to loads applied after the installation of non-structural elements (partitions, finishes). It is limited to a tighter value to prevent damage to these brittle components.
> - The **final deflection** is the *total* deflection after all long-term effects (creep, shrinkage) have developed, and is allowed a more relaxed value.
> - For continuous members (end span and interior span), the 20 mm ceiling on post-construction deflection is mandatory if the member supports partitions that can be damaged by even small movements.

---

## Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks each)

### **Question 1A** (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

A simply supported rectangular RCC beam has the following data:
- Effective span $L = 7$ m
- Cross-section: $b = 250$ mm, $d = 525$ mm, $h = 575$ mm
- Tension reinforcement: 5 bars of 20 mm $\phi$ ($A_{\text{st}} = 1570$ mm²)
- $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 500$ N/mm²
- Live load on beam = 15 kN/m, Dead load (excluding self-weight) = 3 kN/m
- Exposure: Moderate
- No compression steel is provided at midspan.

**(a) Verify the deflection of the beam by the direct computation method as per IS 456 : 2000.** **(7 marks)**
**(b) Check the crack width at the tension face. State whether the section is safe in cracking.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q1A

##### Part (a) — Deflection Verification (7 marks)

**Step 1: Material and Section Properties**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_c &= 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}} = 5000 \sqrt{25} = 25000 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
E_s &= 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
m &= E_s / E_c = 200000 / 25000 = 8.0 \\
I_{\text{gr}} &= b h^3 / 12 = 250 \times 575^3 / 12 = 3.962 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4 \\
y_t &= h / 2 = 287.5 \text{ mm} \\
f_{\text{cr}} &= 0.7 \sqrt{25} = 3.5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
M_{\text{cr}} &= f_{\text{cr}} \cdot I_{\text{gr}} / y_t = 3.5 \times 3.962 \times 10^9 / 287.5 = 48.23 \text{ kN-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Cracked Section Properties**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b x^2 + 2 m A_{\text{st}} x - 2 m A_{\text{st}} d &= 0 \\
250 x^2 + 2 \times 8 \times 1570 \times x - 2 \times 8 \times 1570 \times 525 &= 0 \\
250 x^2 + 25120 x - 1.319 \times 10^7 &= 0 \\
x &= \frac{-25120 + \sqrt{25120^2 + 4 \times 250 \times 1.319 \times 10^7}}{500} \\
x &= \frac{-25120 + \sqrt{6.31 \times 10^8 + 1.319 \times 10^{10}}}{500} \\
x &= \frac{-25120 + 119630}{500} = 189.0 \text{ mm} \\
I_{\text{cr}} &= \frac{250 \times 189^3}{3} + 8 \times 1570 \times (525 - 189)^2 \\
&= 5.62 \times 10^8 + 1.298 \times 10^9 = 1.86 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Service Moments**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Self-weight} &= 0.25 \times 0.575 \times 25 = 3.59 \text{ kN/m} \\
\text{Total DL} &= 3.0 + 3.59 = 6.59 \text{ kN/m} \\
\text{LL} &= 15 \text{ kN/m} \\
\text{Total } w &= 6.59 + 15 = 21.59 \text{ kN/m} \\
M_{\text{total}} &= 21.59 \times 7^2 / 8 = 132.27 \text{ kN-m} \\
M_{\text{sus}} &= 6.59 \times 7^2 / 8 = 40.37 \text{ kN-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Effective Moment of Inertia**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{M_{\text{cr}}}{M_{\text{total}}}\right)^2 &= \left(\frac{48.23}{132.27}\right)^2 = 0.133 \\
I_{\text{eff}} &= 3.962 \times 10^9 - 0.133 \times (3.962 - 1.86) \times 10^9 \\
&= 3.962 \times 10^9 - 0.279 \times 10^9 = 3.683 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Short-Term Deflection**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{\text{short,total}} &= \frac{5 \times 21.59 \times (7000)^4}{384 \times 25000 \times 3.683 \times 10^9} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 21.59 \times 2.401 \times 10^{15}}{384 \times 25000 \times 3.683 \times 10^9} \\
&= \frac{2.592 \times 10^{17}}{3.535 \times 10^{16}} = 7.33 \text{ mm} \\
\delta_{\text{short,sus}} &= \frac{5 \times 6.59 \times 2.401 \times 10^{15}}{3.535 \times 10^{16}} = 2.24 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Long-Term Multiplier (no compression steel, $\xi = 1.0$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= 1.0 / (1 + 0) = 1.0 \\
\delta_{\text{long}} &= 1.0 \times 2.24 = 2.24 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Final Deflection and Check**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{\text{final}} &= (7.33 - 2.24) + 2.24 \times (1 + 1.0) = 5.09 + 4.48 = 9.57 \text{ mm} \\
\delta_{\text{perm}} &= L / 250 = 7000 / 250 = 28 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Since } 9.57 \text{ mm} \ll 28 \text{ mm, the beam is SAFE in deflection.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

##### Part (b) — Crack Width Verification (7 marks)

**Step 1: Service Steel Stress**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_s &= 0.58 f_y = 0.58 \times 500 = 290 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Bar Geometry and $a_{\text{cr}}$**

Assuming clear cover = 40 mm and 16 mm $\phi$ stirrups, the centroidal distance of outermost bar from the tension face:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{\text{cr}} &= 40 + 8 \text{ (half of stirrup)} + 10 \text{ (half of 20 mm bar)} = 58 \text{ mm} \\
a_{\text{cr,min}} &= a_{\text{cr}} = 58 \text{ mm (all bars identical in arrangement)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Average Steel Strain (Annex F)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_m &= \frac{f_s}{E_s} - \frac{b \cdot x \cdot (x - d)}{3 \cdot E_s \cdot A_{\text{st}} \cdot (d - x)} \\
&= \frac{290}{200000} - \frac{250 \times 189 \times (189 - 525)}{3 \times 200000 \times 1570 \times (525 - 189)} \\
&= 0.00145 - \frac{250 \times 189 \times (-336)}{3 \times 200000 \times 1570 \times 336} \\
&= 0.00145 + \frac{1.5876 \times 10^7}{3.166 \times 10^{11}} \\
&= 0.00145 + 5.01 \times 10^{-5} = 0.00150
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Surface Crack Width**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{\text{cr}} &= \frac{3 \times a_{\text{cr}} \times \varepsilon_m}{1 + 2 \times \dfrac{(a_{\text{cr,min}} - a_{\text{cr}})}{h - x}} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 58 \times 0.00150}{1 + 0} = 0.261 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Comparison**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Permissible } w_{\text{cr}} \text{ for moderate exposure} &= 0.30 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Actual } w_{\text{cr}} = 0.261 \text{ mm} < 0.30 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Hence, the section is SAFE in cracking.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question 1B** (Internal Choice — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

A continuous RCC beam over three equal spans of 5 m each is 300 mm wide and 600 mm deep (effective). It is reinforced with 0.85% tension steel and 0.30% compression steel at the support section. M25 concrete and Fe415 steel are used.

**(a) Determine the modified permissible span-to-effective-depth ratio using IS 456 modification factors.** **(7 marks)**
**(b) If the actual span/d provided is 14, comment on whether the section is safe in deflection. What remedial measure would you suggest if the check fails?** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q1B

##### Part (a) — Modified Permissible Span/d (7 marks)

**Step 1: Basic Span/d for Continuous Beam with Fe415**

For a continuous beam, IS 456 Table 9 gives:

$$
(\text{Span}/d)_{\text{basic}} = 26 \text{ (continuous, for Fe415)}
$$

**Step 2: Modification Factors**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 \text{ (from Fig 4, IS 456 for } p_t &= 0.85\% \text{ in continuous beam)} \\
&\approx 1.20 \text{ (read from chart)} \\
k_2 &= 1 + 2 \, p'_c / p_t = 1 + 2 \times 0.30 / 0.85 = 1 + 0.706 = 1.706
\end{aligned}
$$

Per IS 456, $k_2$ is **capped at 1.5**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_2 &= 1.5 \\
k_3 \text{ (for span L = 5 m < 10 m)} &= 1.0 \\
k_4 &= 0.4 f_y / f_s \\
\text{For service stress, } f_s &= 0.58 \times 415 = 240.7 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
k_4 &= 0.4 \times 415 / 240.7 = 0.690
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Modified Permissible Span/d**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\text{Span}/d)_{\text{perm}} &= 26 \times 1.20 \times 1.5 \times 1.0 \times 0.690 \\
&= 32.29
\end{aligned}
$$

##### Part (b) — Comparison and Remedial Action (7 marks)

**Step 1: Comparison**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual span}/d &= 14 \\
\text{Permissible span}/d &= 32.29 \\
\text{Since } 14 < 32.29, \text{ the section is SAFE in deflection by the span}/d\text{ method.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Discussion**

> The section has substantial reserve in deflection capacity — the actual span/d is less than half the permissible value. This means the designer could have economised on either (a) the overall depth $h$ of the beam, (b) the tension steel area, or (c) the compression steel area. In practice, a small reserve in span/d is desirable because the span/d method is empirical and may not capture all boundary conditions.

**Step 3: If the Check Had Failed, Remedial Measures**

- **Option 1 — Increase effective depth $d$** by increasing overall depth $h$ while keeping cover and bar diameter the same. This is the most direct method.
- **Option 2 — Add compression steel** $A_{\text{sc}}$ at the support section. This increases $k_2$ (up to the cap of 1.5) and also reduces long-term deflection.
- **Option 3 — Use higher grade of concrete** (e.g., M30 instead of M25). This increases $E_c$ and reduces deflection, and also improves the cracking behaviour.
- **Option 4 — Pre-stress the member**. For very long spans, partial pre-stressing can drastically reduce both deflection and crack width.
- **Option 5 — Camber the member** at the time of construction by an amount equal to the expected long-term deflection. This does not reduce the deflection but counteracts its visible effect.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — $k_2$ cap and continuous vs. simply supported basic value:** Students frequently forget (a) that $k_2$ is capped at 1.5, and (b) that the *basic* span/d for a continuous member is **26**, not 20. Mixing these up can cause a 2–3 mark deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two-track design philosophy:** Every flexural RCC member must satisfy **both** ULS (strength) and SLS (serviceability). SLS is *not* an afterthought.
- **Effective moment of inertia (Branson):** $I_{\text{eff}} = I_{\text{gr}} - (M_{\text{cr}}/M)^2 (I_{\text{gr}} - I_{\text{cr}})$. The squaring is on the *moment ratio*, not the inertia ratio.
- **Cracking moment** uses $f_{\text{cr}} = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ and $M_{\text{cr}} = f_{\text{cr}} Z_{\text{gr}}$.
- **Long-term deflection multiplier** $\alpha = \xi / (1 + 50 \rho'_c)$ depends on time ($\xi$) and compression steel ($\rho'_c$).
- **Final deflection formula** (very commonly misapplied): $\delta_{\text{final}} = \delta_{\text{short,imposed}} + \delta_{\text{short,sustained}} (1 + \alpha)$. *Do not* simply multiply the total short-term deflection by $(1 + \alpha)$.
- **Permissible limits:** Span/250 (final) and Span/350 or 20 mm (post-construction) — the 20 mm ceiling applies if the member supports brittle partitions.
- **Span/d method:** Basic value is 20 (simply supported, Fe415), 26 (continuous, Fe415), 23 (simply supported, Fe500), 30 (continuous, Fe500).
- **Modification factors $k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4$** are read from IS 456 Fig 4 / Fig 5 / Table 10; remember the cap of 1.5 on $k_2$ and the limit of 1.0 on $k_3$ for spans ≤ 10 m.
- **Crack width formula** (Cl. 35.3, Annex F): $w_{\text{cr}} = 3 a_{\text{cr}} \varepsilon_m / [1 + 2 (a_{\text{cr,min}} - a_{\text{cr}})/(h - x)]$. Dominated by $a_{\text{cr}}$ and $\varepsilon_m$.
- **Permissible crack width:** 0.3 mm for moderate, 0.2 mm for severe, 0.1 mm for very severe exposure.
- **Effective ways to control crack width:** reduce $f_s$ in service (use partial pre-stressing or increase Ast at the limit-state section), reduce bar spacing, increase cover (carefully), or use smaller-diameter bars.
- **Effective ways to control deflection:** increase $d$, add compression steel, use higher grade of concrete, or pre-stress.
- **KTU-specific reminder:** The University exam often asks the **direct computation method** in Part (b) and the **span/d method** in Part (a) of a 14-mark question. Practice both, and present numerical answers with **units** in every line — examiners deduct marks for missing units in intermediate steps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compression Members - Introduction to IS Code Provisions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compression Members - Introduction to IS Code Provisions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (IS 456:2000 Terminology)

A **Compression Member** is a structural element (column, strut, pedestal, or wall) whose primary internal force is an **axial compressive force** with or without associated bending moments and shears. According to **IS 456:2000**, columns are vertical compression members whose effective length exceeds **3 times the least lateral dimension** (Cl. 25.1.1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedestal vs. Column (IS 456:2000 — Cl. 25.1.1)**
> A **pedestal** is a vertical compression member whose **effective length ≤ 3 times the least lateral dimension**. Pedestals are designed as plain concrete members (no lateral tie requirements of Cl. 26.5.3 apply). Once `L_eff / D > 3`, the member is treated as a **column** and must satisfy all tie and slenderness provisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Compression Member Classification — Loading Type**
> 1. **Axially Loaded Columns** — Load passes through the centroid of the section (`e = 0`).
> 2. **Uniaxially Eccentrically Loaded Columns** — Load has eccentricity in **one** principal direction.
> 3. **Biaxially Eccentrically Loaded Columns** — Load has eccentricity in **both** principal directions (corner columns).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Standing Athlete

Imagine a **standing athlete** holding a heavy barbell. If the barbell sits directly on the head (axial load), the body is compressed uniformly — the spine bears pure compression. If the athlete tilts slightly to one side (small eccentricity), the spine now experiences **compression + bending**, just like an eccentrically loaded column. If the athlete is very tall and thin, even a small tilt causes a dangerous wobble (buckling tendency — slenderness effect). IS Code provisions are essentially the **engineering rulebook** to ensure the "athlete" does not buckle or fail under these varied loadings.

## 1.3 Why IS Code Provisions Matter

IS 456:2000 prescribes **minimum safety margins** against three failure modes:

| Failure Mode | IS Code Reference | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Material crushing | Cl. 39.1 (Design strength limits) | Sudden concrete failure |
| Buckling instability | Cl. 25.3 (Slenderness limits) | Sudden lateral collapse |
| Lateral-tie failure | Cl. 26.5.3.2 (Tie diameter & pitch) | Bar buckling, brittle failure |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Concepts from IS 456:2000

### A. Effective Length (`L_eff`)

The effective length of a compression member is the **distance between successive points of contraflexure** of the buckled shape. IS 456:2000 (Cl. 25.2.1) prescribes reduction factors `k` to be applied to the **unsupported length `L`**:

$$L_{eff} = k \cdot L$$

where `k` depends on the **end conditions** (rotational and translational restraint).

### B. Slenderness Ratio (`λ`)

$$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{D} \quad \text{(for rectangular sections)}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{D} \quad \text{(for circular sections, D = diameter)}$$

**Classification (Cl. 25.1.1):**
- **Short Column** → `λ ≤ 12` → Failure by **material crushing**.
- **Long/Slender Column** → `λ > 12` → Failure by **buckling + crushing**, strength reduction required.

### C. Effective Slenderness Ratio

For rectangular sections, the slenderness ratio is computed **about both axes**:

$$\lambda_x = \frac{L_{ex}}{D_x}, \quad \lambda_y = \frac{L_{ey}}{D_y}$$

The **governing slenderness** is the **larger** of `λ_x` and `λ_y`.

### D. Minimum Eccentricity (Cl. 25.4)

Real columns always have some unintended constructional eccentricity. IS Code mandates a **minimum design eccentricity**:

$$e_{x,min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30} \geq 20 \text{ mm}$$

$$e_{y,min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{B}{30} \geq 20 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Even a purely **"axially loaded"** column must be designed for a bending moment `M = P \times e_{min}` due to the code-prescribed minimum eccentricity.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the complete IS 456:2000 formula reference for compression member design — **memorize this table** for KTU ESE.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Limit | IS Code Clause |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective Length | $L_{eff} = k \cdot L$ | Cl. 25.2.1 |
| 2 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = L_{eff} / D$ | Cl. 25.1.1 |
| 3 | Short Column Limit | $\lambda \leq 12$ | Cl. 25.1.1 |
| 4 | Beam Slenderness Limit | $\lambda \leq 1.2 \cdot D/B \cdot D/B$ (≤ 60) | Cl. 25.3 |
| 5 | Min. Eccentricity (X) | $e_{x,min} = L/500 + D/30 \geq 20$ mm | Cl. 25.4 |
| 6 | Min. Eccentricity (Y) | $e_{y,min} = L/500 + B/30 \geq 20$ mm | Cl. 25.4 |
| 7 | Design Strength (Concrete) | $\sigma_{cc} = 0.45 f_{ck} \cdot C_{r}$ (Rectangular) | Cl. 36.4.1 |
| 8 | Column Factor (Rectangular) | $C_{r} = 1.0$ | Table 14 |
| 9 | Column Factor (Circular) | $C_{c} = 0.80$ | Table 14 |
| 10 | Reduction Factor | From Table 16 (function of `λ`, `f_y`) | Cl. 39.3.1 |
| 11 | Min. Longitudinal Steel | $0.008 \cdot A_g$ | Cl. 26.5.3.1 |
| 12 | Max. Longitudinal Steel | $0.04 \cdot A_g$ (lap splices) | Cl. 26.5.3.1 |
| 13 | Min. Tie Diameter | $\max(\phi_{main}/4, \ 6 \text{ mm})$ | Cl. 26.5.3.2 |
| 14 | Max. Tie Pitch | $\min(D, \ 16\phi_{main}, \ 300 \text{ mm})$ | Cl. 26.5.3.2 |
| 15 | Min. Cover | $40$ mm (column) | Cl. 26.4.1 |
| 16 | Min. No. of Bars (Rect.) | 4 (rectangular), 6 (circular) | Cl. 26.5.3.1 |
| 17 | Max. Permissible Load | $P_u \leq 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ | Cl. 39.3 |

## 2.3 Effective Length Reduction Factor `k` (Cl. 25.2.1)

| End Condition | Sketch | k |
|---|---|---|
| Both ends held in position but not restrained against rotation |  | **1.00 L** |
| One end fixed (in position & rotation), other end held in position but not restrained |  | **0.80 L** |
| Both ends held in position and one end restrained against rotation |  | **0.80 L** |
| Both ends held in position and restrained against rotation |  | **0.65 L** |
| One end held in position and restrained, other end not held in position |  | **1.20 L** |
| Both ends not held in position, one end restrained |  | **1.50 L** |
| Both ends not held in position, not restrained |  | **2.00 L** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reality Check:** In RC buildings, columns are typically part of rigid frames — the **recommended value is `L_eff = 0.65 L`** for braced frames (cl. 25.2.1, footnote). For unbraced frames, use `1.0 L` to `1.2 L`. Always check both axes.

## 2.4 Reduction Factor `C_r` from Table 16 (IS 456:2000)

| Slenderness `λ` | `f_y = 415` MPa | `f_y = 500` MPa |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | 1.00 | 1.00 |
| 10 | 0.98 | 0.97 |
| 15 | 0.95 | 0.93 |
| 20 | 0.91 | 0.89 |
| 25 | 0.86 | 0.83 |
| 30 | 0.81 | 0.77 |
| 35 | 0.75 | 0.71 |
| 40 | 0.69 | 0.63 |
| 45 | 0.62 | 0.55 |
| 50 | 0.55 | 0.48 |
| 55 | 0.48 | 0.40 |
| 60 | 0.41 | 0.32 |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of These Provisions

- **Building design**: Determines column cross-section and reinforcement before detailed analysis.
- **Preliminary sizing**: Prevents non-iterative design by giving upper-bound slenderness check.
- **Safety verification**: Ensures stability against second-order (P-Δ) effects, which are implicitly captured in the reduction factor `C_r`.
- **Detailing compliance**: The 4%/0.8% rule prevents **brittle failure** by ensuring adequate ductility.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Minimum Eccentricity (Conceptual Basis)

> [!NOTE]
> IS 456:2000 **does not derive** `e_{min}` from first principles; rather, it is an **empirical safety limit** accounting for:
> (a) Constructional imperfection (out-of-plumb of up to `L/500`),
> (b) Discontinuity of concrete (`D/30` represents one-third of the lateral dimension).

**Empirical Form:**

$$e_{min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30} \quad \text{(IS 456:2000, Cl. 25.4)}$$

The lower bound of **20 mm** ensures practical minimum even for very short and stocky columns.

## 3.2 Analytical Proof — Slenderness Limit `λ ≤ 12`

**Conceptual basis:** When `λ` is small, the column fails by **material crushing** (concrete reaches its ultimate strain `0.0035` before buckling). When `λ` exceeds a threshold, **Euler buckling** initiates before material crushing, requiring strength reduction.

**Euler Critical Load (pinned-pinned):**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_{eff}^2}$$

**Concrete crushing load:**

$$P_{crush} = 0.45 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$$

**Setting** $P_{cr} = P_{crush}$ and solving for the **transition slenderness** yields `λ ≈ 12` for typical RC sections — which is the IS Code limit. For `λ ≤ 12`, the column is treated as **short** (no buckling reduction).

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Min. Eccentricity Computation

**Given:** A rectangular column of size `300 mm × 450 mm`, unsupported length `L = 4.0 m`. Both ends held in position, one end restrained against rotation. Determine the **minimum design eccentricities**.

### Step 1 — Compute Effective Length

Both ends held in position, one end restrained against rotation:

$$L_{eff} = 0.80 \cdot L = 0.80 \cdot 4000 = 3200 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2 — Slenderness Ratios About Both Axes

**About X-axis** (bending about major axis, `D_x = 450 mm`):

$$\lambda_x = \frac{L_{eff}}{D_x} = \frac{3200}{450} = 7.11 < 12 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Short column about X}$$

**About Y-axis** (bending about minor axis, `D_y = 300 mm`):

$$\lambda_y = \frac{L_{eff}}{D_y} = \frac{3200}{300} = 10.67 < 12 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Short column about Y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both slenderness ratios are < 12 → **Short column**; **no buckling reduction** (`C_r = 1.0`).

### Step 3 — Minimum Eccentricity About X-axis

Here `D` in Cl. 25.4 refers to the **dimension parallel to bending direction**. For bending about the major axis, the dimension **perpendicular to bending** is the lateral dimension governing instability, but conventionally the IS Code uses:

$$e_{x,min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30} \geq 20 \text{ mm}$$

Using `D = 450 mm` (column depth parallel to X):

$$e_{x,min} = \frac{4000}{500} + \frac{450}{30} = 8.0 + 15.0 = 23.0 \text{ mm} \quad (\geq 20 \text{ mm} \ \checkmark)$$

### Step 4 — Minimum Eccentricity About Y-axis

Using `B = 300 mm` (column width parallel to Y):

$$e_{y,min} = \frac{4000}{500} + \frac{300}{30} = 8.0 + 10.0 = 18.0 \text{ mm}$$

Since `18 mm < 20 mm`, the code limit governs:

$$e_{y,min} = 20 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5 — Design Bending Moments from Min. Eccentricity

For an axial load `P_u = 1500 kN`:

$$M_{ux} = P_u \cdot e_{x,min} = 1500 \cdot 0.023 = 34.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{uy} = P_u \cdot e_{y,min} = 1500 \cdot 0.020 = 30.0 \text{ kN·m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always compute **both** `e_{x,min}` and `e_{y,min}`. A common mistake is omitting one direction, leading to **failure in biaxial bending check** and loss of 3–4 marks.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Flow

```
START
  │
  ▼
Compute L_eff (Cl. 25.2.1)
  │
  ▼
Compute λ_x = L_eff / D_x and λ_y = L_eff / D_y
  │
  ▼
Governing λ = max(λ_x, λ_y)
  │
  ├── λ ≤ 12 ────────────────► SHORT COLUMN
  │                              • Full σ_cc = 0.45 f_ck
  │                              • C_r = 1.0
  │                              • Apply e_min clause
  │
  └── λ > 12 ────────────────► SLENDER COLUMN
                                 • σ_cc = 0.45 f_ck × C_r
                                 • C_r from Table 16
                                 • Mandatory e_min
END
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IS 456 Compression Member Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input: Column Geometry, Loads, f_ck, f_y] --> B1[Step 1: Compute Unsupported Length L]
    B1 --> B2[Step 2: Identify End Conditions]
    B2 --> B3[Step 3: Select k from Cl 25.2.1]
    B3 --> C1[Step 4: Compute L_eff = k x L]
    C1 --> D1[Step 5: Compute lambda_x and lambda_y]
    D1 --> E1{Step 6: lambda max less than or equal to 12?}
    E1 -->|Yes| F1[Step 7A: Short Column]
    E1 -->|No| F2[Step 7B: Slender Column]
    F2 --> G1[Step 8: Read C_r from Table 16]
    G1 --> H1[Step 9: Compute min eccentricity]
    F1 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Step 10: Compute M_u from P_u and e_min]
    I1 --> J1[Step 11: Apply Reduction Factor to sigma_cc]
    J1 --> K1[Step 12: Check Reinforcement Limits 0.8 to 4 percent]
    K1 --> L1[Step 13: Detailing: Ties and Cover]
    L1 --> M0[Output: Final Design]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — End Conditions & Effective Length Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[End Condition 1: Both ends hinged, free to rotate, no sway] --> B1[k = 1.00]
    A2[End Condition 2: One end fixed, other hinged, no sway] --> B2[k = 0.80]
    A3[End Condition 3: Both ends fixed against rotation, no sway] --> B3[k = 0.65]
    A4[End Condition 4: One end fixed, other free, sway permitted] --> B4[k = 1.20]
    A5[End Condition 5: Both ends fixed against rotation, sway permitted] --> B5[k = 1.00]
    A6[End Condition 6: Both ends free, fully unbraced] --> B6[k = 2.00]
    B1 --> C0[Effective Length L_eff computed]
    B2 --> C0
    B3 --> C0
    B4 --> C0
    B5 --> C0
    B6 --> C0
    C0 --> D0[Slenderness lambda = L_eff over D]
    D0 --> E0{lambda over 12?}
    E0 -->|Yes| F0[Apply Reduction Factor C_r]
    E0 -->|No| G0[No Reduction C_r = 1.00]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Compression Members

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Compression Member] --> S1{L_eff over D less than or equal to 3}
    S1 -->|Yes| S2[Pedestal - Plain Concrete Allowed]
    S1 -->|No| S3[Column - Reinforced]
    S3 --> T0{Type of Loading}
    T0 --> T1[Axially Loaded Pure Compression]
    T0 --> T2[Uniaxially Loaded plus M_x or M_y]
    T0 --> T3[Biaxially Loaded plus M_x and M_y]
    T3 --> U0[Designed using SP 16 Charts or Interaction Equations]
    T2 --> V0{lambda over 12}
    V0 -->|Yes| V1[Slender Column with e_min in one direction]
    V0 -->|No| V2[Short Column with e_min in one direction]
    T1 --> W0[Designed by Cl 39.3 with min eccentricity clause]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Slenderness Ratio" of a compression member as per IS 456:2000. What is the limiting value to classify a column as short?**

**Model Answer:**

The slenderness ratio of a compression member is the **ratio of effective length to the least lateral dimension** of the cross-section:

$$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{D_{min}}$$

As per **Cl. 25.1.1 of IS 456:2000**, a compression member is classified as a **short column** if its slenderness ratio `λ ≤ 12`. For `λ > 12`, it is treated as a **slender column**, requiring a strength reduction factor.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the IS 456:2000 provisions for minimum and maximum longitudinal reinforcement in a reinforced concrete column.**

**Model Answer:**

As per **Cl. 26.5.3.1 of IS 456:2000**:
- The cross-sectional area of longitudinal reinforcement shall be **not less than 0.8% of the gross cross-sectional area** of the column.
- It shall be **not more than 4% of the gross cross-sectional area**. If lap splices are used and the bars are not staggered, the maximum may be 4% (laps not used in plastic hinge zones in seismic zones).
- The minimum number of longitudinal bars is **4 for rectangular columns** and **6 for circular columns**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full Question with Model Solution

**[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Internal Choice | 14 Marks]**

> **(a)** Explain the IS 456:2000 provisions for the **minimum eccentricity** to be considered in the design of axially loaded short columns. Why is this provision mandatory even when the column is "axially" loaded? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A rectangular RC column of size `400 mm × 600 mm` has an unsupported length of `5.0 m`. The column is effectively held in position at both ends and restrained against rotation at one end. Compute the **minimum design eccentricities** in both directions. If the column carries an axial working load of `1800 kN` (assuming `1.5` load factor), determine the **design bending moments** in both directions. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Minimum Eccentricity Provisions (Cl. 25.4 of IS 456:2000):**

$$e_{x,min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30} \geq 20 \text{ mm}$$

$$e_{y,min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{B}{30} \geq 20 \text{ mm}$$

**Why is it mandatory for "axially loaded" columns?** [3 Marks each point]

1. **Constructional Imperfection:** Real columns are never perfectly vertical; out-of-plumb errors of `L/500` are practically unavoidable. This translates to a real eccentricity of `L/500`.

2. **Material Non-Uniformity:** Concrete has inherent heterogeneity. The term `D/30` accounts for non-uniform strain distribution and partial discontinuity in the cross-section.

3. **Safety Margin:** It transforms a theoretically pure axial column into a column subjected to a **small but real moment** `M = P × e_{min}`, ensuring the column has flexural capacity even under perfect axial load assumptions.

4. **Lower Bound of 20 mm:** The absolute minimum of 20 mm ensures even very short, very stocky columns (e.g., pedestals) have a design eccentricity.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- Column size: `B = 400 mm, D = 600 mm`
- Unsupported length: `L = 5000 mm`
- End condition: Both ends held in position, one end restrained against rotation → `k = 0.80`
- Axial working load: `W = 1800 kN`, Load factor `= 1.5`

**Step 1 — Effective Length**

$$L_{eff} = 0.80 \times 5000 = 4000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Slenderness Ratios (Classification Check)**

$$\lambda_x = \frac{L_{eff}}{D} = \frac{4000}{600} = 6.67 \quad (< 12 \ \checkmark)$$

$$\lambda_y = \frac{L_{eff}}{B} = \frac{4000}{400} = 10.00 \quad (< 12 \ \checkmark)$$

Both axes qualify as **short column**. No reduction factor required.

**Step 3 — Minimum Eccentricities**

$$e_{x,min} = \frac{5000}{500} + \frac{600}{30} = 10.0 + 20.0 = 30.0 \text{ mm} \quad (\geq 20 \text{ mm} \ \checkmark)$$

$$e_{y,min} = \frac{5000}{500} + \frac{400}{30} = 10.0 + 13.33 = 23.33 \text{ mm} \quad (\geq 20 \text{ mm} \ \checkmark)$$

**Step 4 — Factored Axial Load**

$$P_u = 1.5 \times 1800 = 2700 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Design Bending Moments**

$$M_{ux} = P_u \cdot e_{x,min} = 2700 \times 0.030 = 81.0 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{uy} = P_u \cdot e_{y,min} = 2700 \times 0.0233 = 63.0 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Final Answer:** `M_{ux} = 81.0 kN·m` and `M_{uy} = 63.0 kN·m`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to check the 20 mm lower bound:** Many students compute `e_{y,min}` as `18 mm` and stop there, losing 1 mark. Always check `≥ 20 mm`.
> 2. **Confusing D and B in eccentricity formula:** Use the dimension **parallel to the bending direction** as `D` (e.g., for `M_x`, use depth `D`; for `M_y`, use width `B`).
> 3. **Missing the load factor:** KTU ESE requires working load × 1.5 to be computed. Many forget this and lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Neglecting classification check:** A long column would need `C_r` from Table 16 applied to `e_{min}` design — but this case is short, so no reduction. Always state the classification explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Internal Choice | 14 Marks]**

> **(a)** With the help of IS 456:2000, explain the classification of compression members based on **slenderness ratio**. State the slenderness limits for columns and beams. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A circular RC column of diameter `450 mm` and unsupported length `6.0 m` is held in position and restrained against rotation at both ends. Using `f_{ck} = 25 MPa` and `f_y = 415 MPa`, determine the **reduction factor `C_r`** and the **permissible axial load capacity** of the column. Gross area `A_g = \pi/4 \times 450^2 = 159{,}043 \text{ mm}^2`, assume `2%` longitudinal steel. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Classification Based on Slenderness (Cl. 25.1.1):**

| Type | Slenderness Ratio | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Pedestal | $L_{eff}/D \leq 3$ | Material crushing (plain concrete) |
| Short Column | $\lambda = L_{eff}/D \leq 12$ | Material crushing |
| Long/Slender Column | $\lambda = L_{eff}/D > 12$ | Buckling + crushing (reduced strength) |

**Slenderness Limits (Cl. 25.3):**

For a beam (compression flange) where `$D$` = depth, `$B$` = width of compression flange:

$$\frac{L_{eff}}{D} \leq 1.2 \cdot \frac{D}{B} \quad \text{but} \quad \frac{L_{eff}}{D} \leq 60$$

> **Pedestal:** Designed as plain concrete, no lateral tie rules apply.
> **Short Column:** Designed per Cl. 39 of IS 456. Full `σ_cc = 0.45 f_ck` permissible. No reduction.
> **Long Column:** Reduction factor `C_r` from Table 16 is applied to `σ_cc` before computing load capacity.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- Diameter `D = 450 mm`, `L = 6000 mm`
- Both ends held in position and restrained → `k = 0.65`
- `f_{ck} = 25 MPa`, `f_y = 415 MPa`
- `A_g = 159{,}043 \text{ mm}^2`, Steel = 2% × `A_g = 0.02 × 159043 = 3180.86 \text{ mm}^2`

**Step 1 — Effective Length**

$$L_{eff} = 0.65 \times 6000 = 3900 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Slenderness Ratio**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{D} = \frac{3900}{450} = 8.67 \quad (\leq 12 \ \Rightarrow \text{Short column})$$

Wait — re-evaluating with the 0.65 factor:

$$\lambda = 8.67 < 12 \Rightarrow C_r = 1.00 \text{ (from Table 16)}$$

> **If** the column were unbraced with `k = 1.0`, then `L_{eff} = 6000 mm`, `λ = 13.33`, and from Table 16 (linear interpolation between `λ = 10` and `λ = 15` for `f_y = 415`): `C_r ≈ 0.97`.

**Step 3 — Permissible Axial Load (Cl. 39.3 — Short Column with Helical Ties)**

For circular column with helical reinforcement, the column factor is `0.80` × effective enhancement.

Using the **Cl. 39.3 formula:**

$$P_{u,max} = 0.4 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c + 0.67 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc}$$

where `$A_c = A_g - A_{sc} = 159043 - 3180.86 = 155862 \text{ mm}^2$`.

$$P_{u,max} = (0.4)(25)(155862) + (0.67)(415)(3180.86)$$

$$P_{u,max} = 1{,}558{,}620 + 882{,}475 = 2{,}441{,}095 \text{ N} \approx 2441 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Concluding Statement**

Since the slenderness ratio is less than 12 (short column), the full `C_r = 1.0` is applicable. If the slenderness had exceeded 12, the permissible load would be multiplied by the reduction factor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mistakes in Q.B**
> 1. **Using wrong `k` value:** Students often pick `k = 1.0` (for "both ends hinged") even when the problem states "restrained against rotation". The correct value is `0.65`. **1–2 marks lost.**
> 2. **Forgetting the column factor:** Circular columns use a column factor of `0.80` in `σ_cc` calculation (different from rectangular which uses `1.0`).
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract steel area:** `A_c = A_g - A_{sc}`. Many students use `A_g` directly, which overestimates capacity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: IS Code Provisions for Compression Members**

- [x] **Slenderness Ratio `λ = L_eff / D`** — Limit is `12` for short column classification.
- [x] **Effective Length `L_eff = k × L`** — `k` depends on end restraint (from 0.65 to 2.00 per Cl. 25.2.1).
- [x] **Minimum Eccentricity** — `e_min = L/500 + D/30 ≥ 20 mm` in **both** directions. Applies to ALL columns, even "axial" ones.
- [x] **Pedestal vs Column** — Pedestal: `L_eff / D ≤ 3` (plain concrete OK); Column: `> 3` (must follow Cl. 26.5.3 detailing).
- [x] **Longitudinal Steel** — Min `0.8% A_g`, Max `4% A_g` (Cl. 26.5.3.1). Min 4 bars (rectangular) or 6 (circular).
- [x] **Lateral Ties** — Diameter ≥ `max(φ_main/4, 6 mm)`; Pitch ≤ `min(D, 16φ_main, 300 mm)` (Cl. 26.5.3.2).
- [x] **Reduction Factor `C_r`** — Apply to `σ_cc` for slender columns (Cl. 39.3.1, Table 16). Linear interpolation permitted.
- [x] **Column Factor** — `1.0` (rectangular) and `0.80` (circular with helical/spiral).
- [x] **Max Permissible Axial Load** — `0.4 f_ck A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}` (Cl. 39.3).
- [x] **Min Cover** — `40 mm` for columns (Cl. 26.4.1).
- [x] **Slenderness for Beams** — `L_eff/D ≤ 1.2 × D/B`, but `L_eff/D ≤ 60` (Cl. 25.3).
- [x] **Always design for biaxial bending** — `M_{ux}` AND `M_{uy}` must be computed using respective `e_{min}`.

> **Final Mantra for KTU ESE:** *"Every column is a beam-column, every axial load has a minimum eccentricity, and every slenderness ratio must be checked on **both** axes."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definitions, Classifications, Guidelines and Assumptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Definitions, Classifications, Guidelines and Assumptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: Torsion in Reinforced Concrete Structures

In the context of **Limit State Design of Reinforced Concrete (RCC) beams**, **Torsion** is defined as the *twisting moment* (denoted as $T$ or $T_u$) acting about the longitudinal axis of a structural member, producing **diagonal tension cracks** and **shear stresses** that are distinct from those caused by transverse shear. As per **IS 456:2000 (Clause 41)**, a beam is considered to be subjected to significant torsion when the **torsional moment** is greater than **0.2 times the flexural moment** at the critical section.

Mathematically, the **torsional moment of resistance** required at a section is expressed as:

$$T_u = \sum \left( M_{T,\text{left}} + M_{T,\text{right}} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

where $M_{T,\text{left}}$ and $M_{T,\text{right}}$ are the fixed-end moments due to torsion at the supports.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 deals with the **Design of RCC Beams for Combined Bending, Shear, and Torsion**. The design philosophy uses the **Equivalent Shear (V_e)** and **Equivalent Moment (M_e)** concept to handle these combined effects under the **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)**.

## 1.2 Key Terminology — The Vocabulary of Torsion

Before classification, mastering the formal terms used by IS 456 and KTU examiners is mandatory:

| Term | Definition (KTU/IS 456 Standard) |
|---|---|
| **Torsion ($T$)** | Twisting moment causing rotation about the beam's longitudinal centroidal axis. |
| **Equilibrium Torsion** | Torsion required to maintain *static equilibrium* of the structure (e.g., edge beams supporting spandrel panels). |
| **Compatibility Torsion** | Torsion induced by *deformation compatibility* between connected members (e.g., secondary beams framing into main beams). |
| **Shear Centre** | The point on a cross-section through which the applied transverse load must pass to avoid twisting. |
| **Centre of Twist** | The point on a cross-section that remains stationary when the section twists. |
| **Warping** | Out-of-plane deformation of the cross-section under torsion (negligible for compact solid sections). |
| **St. Venant Torsion (Pure Torsion)** | Torsion resisted purely by *circulating shear stresses* on a solid section. |
| **Skew Cracking** | Diagonal cracks at approximately **45°** to the longitudinal axis caused by principal tensile stresses. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Wrench and the Bolt

Imagine tightening a **nut with a wrench**. Your hand applies a twisting force along the length of the wrench handle. The nut, just like an RCC beam:

- **Resists by shear stress flowing around its circumference** (just like a thin-walled tube resists torsion through circulating shear).
- **Develops diagonal cracks at 45°** if the torque exceeds its capacity (like concrete cracking along principal tensile planes).
- **Needs both longitudinal bars (like the bolt's shank)** and **lateral stirrups (like the nut's threads)** to resist the twisting.

In RCC, concrete is weak in tension, so we use:
- **Transverse closed stirrups** to resist the diagonal tension.
- **Longitudinal bars** distributed around the perimeter to resist the axial component of the diagonal compressive struts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Torsion is *not* an independent force. It always **travels with bending and shear** in real structures. You almost never design for "pure torsion" in practice — it is always **Bending + Shear + Torsion** acting together.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (IS 456:2000)

For torsion design, the following standard values are mandated by **IS 456:2000** (also referenced by KTU 2024 scheme):

- **Concrete density (for self-weight):** $\gamma_c = \mathbf{25\ kN/m^3}$
- **Steel yield strength (Fe 415):** $f_y = \mathbf{415\ N/mm^2}$
- **Partial safety factor for concrete:** $\gamma_m = \mathbf{1.5}$
- **Partial safety factor for steel:** $\gamma_m = \mathbf{1.15}$
- **Strut inclination angle:** $\theta = \mathbf{45°}$ (default per IS 456 Clause 41.4.1)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Torsion — The "Why" Behind the Code

### 2.1.1 Equilibrium Torsion (Primary Torsion)

This torsion is **statically determinate** and is required to maintain the equilibrium of the structure. The structure will collapse if this torsion is not resisted by the member.

**Examples:** 
- Spandrel beams (perimeter beams) supporting masonry walls on one side.
- Edge beams of a helical staircase.
- Beams supporting curved/canopy loads eccentric to the beam centroid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **equilibrium torsion**, the magnitude of the twisting moment is **independent** of the beam's torsional stiffness. The design engineer **must** design the member to resist the full calculated torsional moment.

### 2.1.2 Compatibility Torsion (Secondary Torsion)

This torsion arises because adjacent structural members (e.g., a secondary beam framing into a main beam) must undergo **compatible deformations** at the joint. The magnitude depends on the **relative stiffness** of the connecting members.

**Example:** A secondary beam framing into a main beam. The main beam twists due to the compatibility of rotations at the joint.

> [!NOTE]
> For **compatibility torsion**, IS 456 (Clause 41.1 Note) allows the designer to **ignore the torsion** if the structural integrity is maintained and adequate crack control is provided. This is a common KTU theory question.

## 2.2 Classification of Torsional Cracking (Failure Modes)

Research (Hsu, 1968) has identified three distinct types of cracks caused by torsion in RCC beams:

1. **Skew-Bending Cracks:** Form on the *tension face* of the beam due to combined bending + torsion. They start at the tension face and propagate upward at an angle.
2. **Skew-Shear Cracks:** Form on the *web* (side face) of the beam due to pure torsion or torsion + shear. They form a continuous helical pattern around the beam.
3. **Spalling Cracks:** Form at the *compression face* due to severe torsion. They spall the concrete cover.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common 3-mark question asks students to draw or describe the **crack pattern** of a beam subjected to pure torsion. The correct answer is a **continuous spiral/helical crack** at 45° wrapping around all four faces.

## 2.3 IS 456:2000 — Code-Based Classification of Sections for Torsion

The Indian Standard classifies sections into three categories based on dimensions:

| Section Type | Condition (IS 456) | Torsional Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Compact Section** | $\frac{D}{b} \leq 4$ | Full torsion design as per IS 456 |
| **Moderately Slender** | $4 < \frac{D}{b} \leq 10$ | Full design, but with reduced efficiency |
| **Slender Section** | $\frac{D}{b} > 10$ | **Neglect torsion** per IS 456 Clause 41.1 Note |

## 2.4 The "How" — Code Philosophy in 5 Steps

The IS 456 design philosophy for combined bending, shear, and torsion operates on the **Space Truss Analogy** + **Equivalent Shear/Moment** concept:

- **Step 1:** Compute the *factored* (ultimate) loads: $M_u$, $V_u$, $T_u$.
- **Step 2:** Calculate **Equivalent Shear**: $V_e = V_u + \frac{1.6 \, T_u}{b}$
- **Step 3:** Calculate **Torsional Moment Component**: $M_t = \frac{T_u \left(1 + \frac{D}{b}\right)}{1.7}$
- **Step 4:** Form **Equivalent Bending Moments**: $M_{e1} = M_u + M_t$, $M_{e2} = M_u - M_t$
- **Step 5:** Design **stirrups** for combined shear + torsion, and **longitudinal steel** for the equivalent bending moment.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU Module 3 numerical problems. Memorize these formulas.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | IS 456 Clause |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_e = V_u + \dfrac{1.6 \, T_u}{b}$ | Equivalent shear due to torsion | Cl. 41.3.1 |
| 2 | $M_t = \dfrac{T_u \left(1 + \dfrac{D}{b}\right)}{1.7}$ | Additional moment due to torsion | Cl. 41.4.2 |
| 3 | $M_{e1} = M_u + M_t$ | Equivalent moment (top fibre) | Cl. 41.4.2 |
| 4 | $M_{e2} = M_u - M_t$ | Equivalent moment (bottom fibre) | Cl. 41.4.2 |
| 5 | $\tau_{ve} = \dfrac{V_e}{b \, d}$ | Nominal equivalent shear stress | Cl. 40.1 |
| 6 | $A_{sv}/s_v = \dfrac{V_u \, s}{0.87 \, f_y \, d} + \dfrac{T_u}{1.6 \, b_1 \, d_1 \, f_y}$ | Total transverse reinforcement (two-legged + extra legs) | Cl. 41.4.3 |
| 7 | $A_{sl} = \dfrac{A_{st}}{2} + \dfrac{T_u \, (perimeter)}{1.6 \, b_1 \, d_1 \, f_y \, s_v}$ | Longitudinal torsional reinforcement | Cl. B-6.6.3 (SP-16) |
| 8 | $\tau_{c,\max}$ from IS 456 Table 20 | Maximum permissible shear stress for given $p_t$ | Cl. 40.2.3 |
| 9 | $\dfrac{D}{b} \leq 4$ | Limit for full torsional design | Cl. 41.1 |
| 10 | $b_1 = b - 2c$, $d_1 = d - 2c$ | Centre-to-centre dimensions of stirrup ($c = $ cover) | Cl. B-6.6.3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Absolute value note:** In all table rows above, the **vertical bar** symbol has been replaced with **\vert** or implicitly the parenthesis notation. For example, $\vert M_u \vert$ should be read as the **magnitude** of the bending moment.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The torsion design methodology described above is essential in:

- **Spandrel beams** of multi-storey buildings supporting brick infill walls.
- **Curved beam bridges** and **helicoidal (spiral) staircases**.
- **Beams supporting off-centre loading** (e.g., crane beams, balcony slabs).
- **Transfer girders** in high-rise buildings where the load path is eccentric.
- **Edge beams of flat slabs** where the column strip twists due to punching shear interaction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equivalent Shear ($V_e$) from Torsion

The **IS 456 equivalent shear formula** is derived from the principle of **superposition of shear stresses**. Consider a rectangular beam of width $b$ and overall depth $D$ subjected to transverse shear $V_u$ and torsion $T_u$.

**Step 1:** The maximum transverse shear stress due to $V_u$ alone (per IS 456 Cl. 40.1) is:

$$\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b \, d}$$

**Step 2:** The maximum torsional shear stress on a rectangular section (per St. Venant's elastic theory of torsion) is:

$$\tau_t = \frac{T_u}{\alpha \, b^2 \, d}$$

where $\alpha$ is a shape-dependent coefficient. IS 456 simplifies this using the **equivalent thin-walled tube concept** (Saint-Venant-Bredt theory):

$$\tau_t = \frac{T_u}{1.6 \, b \, D \, \text{(shape factor)}}$$

**Step 3:** IS 456 empirically combines both into an **equivalent shear stress** as:

$$\tau_{ve} = \frac{V_e}{b \, d} = \frac{V_u}{b \, d} + \frac{1.6 \, T_u}{b^2 \, d}$$

**Step 4:** Multiplying numerator and denominator by $b \, d$:

$$V_e = V_u + \frac{1.6 \, T_u}{b}$$

This is the **fundamental torsion-shear equivalence** used in every KTU design problem.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Torsional Moment Component ($M_t$)

The **additional moment $M_t$** represents the flexural effect caused by the eccentric shear flow on the side faces of the beam.

**Step 1:** Consider the side face of width $D$. The shear flow $q$ on the side face due to torsion is:

$$q = \frac{T_u}{2 \, A_o}$$

where $A_o$ is the area enclosed by the centreline of the shear flow path.

**Step 2:** For a rectangular section, $A_o = b \cdot D$ (approximation per IS 456), so:

$$q = \frac{T_u}{2 \, b \, D}$$

**Step 3:** The shear flow acts on the side face over its full height $D$, creating a **flexural moment** about the horizontal centroidal axis of the section:

$$M_t = q \cdot D \cdot \frac{D}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1.7}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $q$:

$$M_t = \frac{T_u \cdot D^2}{4 \, b \, D} \cdot \frac{1}{1.7} = \frac{T_u \left(1 + \frac{D}{b}\right)}{1.7}$$

The factor $\left(1 + \frac{D}{b}\right)$ accounts for the **combined effect of side and top/bottom faces**, and $1.7$ is an empirical reduction factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical insight:** The $1.7$ denominator in the IS 456 formula is a **partial safety factor equivalent** calibrated against experimental data (from Lehigh University tests). It is **not** a theoretical constant — this is a frequently asked *theoretical* question in KTU exams.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Transverse Reinforcement ($A_{sv}/s_v$)

The transverse reinforcement is designed to resist **both** the equivalent shear $V_e$ and the **diagonal tension due to torsion**.

**Step 1:** The shear force resisted by stirrups alone (per IS 456 Cl. 40.4):

$$V_{us} = \frac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{sv} \, d}{s_v}$$

**Step 2:** For combined shear and torsion, IS 456 specifies that the stirrups must resist:

$$V_{us} = V_e - \tau_c \, b \, d$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$\frac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{sv} \, d}{s_v} = V_e - \tau_c \, b \, d$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $A_{sv}/s_v$ (per Cl. 41.4.3):

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \frac{V_e - \tau_c \, b \, d}{0.87 \, f_y \, d}$$

**Step 5:** Expressing in terms of $V_u$ and $T_u$ explicitly:

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \frac{V_u - \tau_c \, b \, d}{0.87 \, f_y \, d} + \frac{T_u}{1.6 \, b_1 \, d_1 \, f_y}$$

where the second term is the **extra reinforcement** required for torsion, and $b_1, d_1$ are the **centreline dimensions** of the stirrup.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Longitudinal Torsional Reinforcement ($A_{sl}$)

The longitudinal bars are designed to resist the **axial component of the diagonal compressive strut** in the space truss model.

**Step 1:** The longitudinal force per unit length in the perimeter of the equivalent tube is:

$$N = \frac{T_u}{2 \, A_o} = \frac{T_u}{2 \, b_1 \, d_1}$$

**Step 2:** The total longitudinal force around the perimeter (per Cl. B-6.6.3 of SP-16):

$$F_L = N \cdot (2 b_1 + 2 d_1) = \frac{T_u \cdot (b_1 + d_1)}{b_1 \, d_1}$$

**Step 3:** Dividing by the yield strength gives the **additional longitudinal area**:

$$A_{sl} = \frac{F_L}{0.87 \, f_y} = \frac{T_u (b_1 + d_1)}{0.87 \, f_y \, b_1 \, d_1 \, s_v}$$

**Step 4:** The **total longitudinal steel** is then:

$$A_{st} = A_{st,\text{bending}} + A_{sl,\text{side\,face}} + A_{sl,\text{corner}}$$

A common simplified form (per IS 456 Cl. B-6.6.3) is:

$$A_{sl} = \frac{T_u \cdot (perimeter)}{1.6 \, b_1 \, d_1 \, f_y \cdot s_v}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Assumptions of Torsion Design (IS 456)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **direct KTU frequently-asked question**. Memorize all 7 assumptions verbatim for the 14-mark question.

| # | Assumption | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Thin-walled tube analogy** | Concrete outside stirrups spalls off; only the concrete *inside* the stirrup is effective. |
| 2 | **Strut inclination = 45°** | Diagonal cracks form at 45° to the longitudinal axis. |
| 3 | **Tension-softened concrete** | Concrete resists diagonal *compression* only, not tension (cracked stage). |
| 4 | **Stirrups yield in tension** | Closely spaced stirrups reach yield before crushing of concrete struts. |
| 5 | **Longitudinal bars yield in tension** | Distributed around the perimeter to balance the diagonal compression. |
| 6 | **Compatibility of strains** | Longitudinal strain = vertical strain (strain compatibility truss). |
| 7 | **Torsion always acts with shear** | Pure torsion design is rarely used; shear-torsion interaction is the default. |

## 3.6 Worked Example: Computing Equivalent Shear (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A rectangular RCC beam ($b = 300$ mm, $D = 600$ mm) is subjected at the critical section to:
- Factored bending moment: $M_u = 250$ kN·m
- Factored shear force: $V_u = 180$ kN
- Factored torsional moment: $T_u = 45$ kN·m
- Effective cover: 50 mm
- Concrete: M25, Steel: Fe 415

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute effective depth:

$$d = D - \text{cover} - \frac{\phi}{2} = 600 - 50 - 10 = 540\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2:** Equivalent shear (Cl. 41.3.1):

$$V_e = V_u + \frac{1.6 \, T_u}{b} = 180 \times 10^3 + \frac{1.6 \times 45 \times 10^6}{300}$$

$$V_e = 180{,}000 + 240{,}000 = 420{,}000\ \text{N} = 420\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 3:** Torsional moment component (Cl. 41.4.2):

$$M_t = \frac{T_u \left(1 + \frac{D}{b}\right)}{1.7} = \frac{45 \times 10^6 \left(1 + \frac{600}{300}\right)}{1.7}$$

$$M_t = \frac{45 \times 10^6 \times 3}{1.7} = 79.41 \times 10^6\ \text{N·mm} = 79.41\ \text{kN·m}$$

**Step 4:** Equivalent bending moments:

$$M_{e1} = M_u + M_t = 250 + 79.41 = 329.41\ \text{kN·m}$$

$$M_{e2} = M_u - M_t = 250 - 79.41 = 170.59\ \text{kN·m}$$

**Step 5:** Nominal equivalent shear stress:

$$\tau_{ve} = \frac{V_e}{b \, d} = \frac{420 \times 10^3}{300 \times 540} = 2.59\ \text{N/mm}^2$$

This value is then compared with $\tau_{c,\max}$ from IS 456 Table 20 to verify section adequacy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
