# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
**(a) Equilibrium Torsion (1½ marks)**
**(b) Compatibility Torsion (1½ marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Equilibrium Torsion:** Equilibrium torsion (also called *primary* or *statically determinate* torsion) is the twisting moment that is required to maintain the **static equilibrium** of the structure. If the beam does not resist this torsion, the structure will collapse. 
*Example:* The twisting of a **spandrel beam** supporting a masonry wall on one side. The wall load creates an eccentric thrust that must be resisted as torsion to maintain equilibrium.

**(b) Compatibility Torsion:** Compatibility torsion (also called *secondary* or *statically indeterminate* torsion) is the twisting moment that arises because of the **compatibility of deformations** at a joint between connected members. 
*Example:* A **secondary beam framing into a main beam** causes the main beam to twist because both must rotate by the same angle at the joint. The IS 456 allows this torsion to be **neglected** in design if structural integrity is maintained.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**List and briefly explain any three assumptions made in the design of RCC beams for torsion as per IS 456:2000. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three key assumptions of torsion design per IS 456 are:

1. **Thin-Walled Tube Analogy:** The concrete outside the closed stirrups is neglected after cracking; only the concrete enclosed by the stirrup (the "tube") resists the torsion through circulating shear flow.

2. **Diagonal Concrete Struts at 45°:** Concrete resists only *diagonal compression* along a 45° spiral, not tension. The diagonal tension is entirely resisted by the steel stirrups and longitudinal bars.

3. **Torsion Always Accompanies Shear:** The code assumes torsion and shear act simultaneously. Pure torsion is rarely designed; the interaction is handled via the equivalent shear $V_e$ concept (Cl. 41.3.1).

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Paper]`

**A rectangular RCC beam of width $b = 300$ mm and overall depth $D = 600$ mm is subjected at the critical section (near the support) to the following factored loads:**
- Factored bending moment: $M_u = 180$ kN·m
- Factored shear force: $V_u = 200$ kN
- Factored torsional moment: $T_u = 30$ kN·m

**Effective cover = 50 mm. Concrete: M25. Steel: Fe 415. Use Fe 415 stirrups of 8 mm diameter.**

**Calculate:**
**(a) The equivalent shear $V_e$ and equivalent bending moments $M_{e1}, M_{e2}$. (7 marks)**
**(b) The nominal equivalent shear stress $\tau_{ve}$ and check whether the section is adequate using IS 456 Table 20. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Equivalent Shear and Equivalent Moments (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $b = 300$ mm, $D = 600$ mm
- $M_u = 180$ kN·m = $180 \times 10^6$ N·mm
- $V_u = 200$ kN = $200 \times 10^3$ N
- $T_u = 30$ kN·m = $30 \times 10^6$ N·mm

**Step 1: Effective depth (d) — (1 mark)**

$$d = D - \text{cover} - \frac{\phi_{\text{stirrup}}}{2} = 600 - 50 - 4 = 546\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2: Equivalent shear $V_e$ (per IS 456 Cl. 41.3.1) — (3 marks)**

$$V_e = V_u + \frac{1.6 \, T_u}{b} = 200 \times 10^3 + \frac{1.6 \times 30 \times 10^6}{300}$$

$$V_e = 200{,}000 + 160{,}000 = 360{,}000\ \text{N} = 360\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 3: Torsional moment component $M_t$ (per IS 456 Cl. 41.4.2) — (2 marks)**

$$M_t = \frac{T_u \left(1 + \frac{D}{b}\right)}{1.7} = \frac{30 \times 10^6 \left(1 + \frac{600}{300}\right)}{1.7}$$

$$M_t = \frac{30 \times 10^6 \times 3}{1.7} = 52.94 \times 10^6\ \text{N·mm} = 52.94\ \text{kN·m}$$

**Step 4: Equivalent bending moments (1 mark)**

$$M_{e1} = M_u + M_t = 180 + 52.94 = 232.94\ \text{kN·m}$$

$$M_{e2} = M_u - M_t = 180 - 52.94 = 127.06\ \text{kN·m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For section design, the **higher equivalent moment** ($M_{e1} = 232.94$ kN·m) is used to design the **tension reinforcement at the bottom**, while $M_{e2}$ is used to design **top reinforcement**. If $M_{e2}$ comes out negative, it indicates a **reversal of moment** and requires **doubly reinforced section design**.

---

**Part (b) — Nominal Equivalent Shear Stress and Section Adequacy (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Nominal equivalent shear stress — (2 marks)**

$$\tau_{ve} = \frac{V_e}{b \, d} = \frac{360 \times 10^3}{300 \times 546} = 2.198\ \text{N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Limiting shear stress from IS 456 Table 20 — (2 marks)**

For M25 concrete and assuming 0.5% tension steel (typical):
- $\tau_{c,\max} = 2.8\ \text{N/mm}^2$ (for $p_t = 0.5\%$)

**Step 3: Section adequacy check — (1 mark)**

$$\tau_{ve} = 2.198\ \text{N/mm}^2 < \tau_{c,\max} = 2.8\ \text{N/mm}^2$$

∴ **The section is ADEQUATE** in shear + torsion.

**Step 4: Calculate $p_t$ (percentage of longitudinal steel) — (2 marks)**

The required longitudinal steel for $M_{e1} = 232.94$ kN·m using limit state method:

$$p_{t,\text{req}} = \frac{A_{st,\text{req}}}{b \, d} \times 100 \approx 0.62\%$$

Since this is greater than the assumed 0.5%, we use $\tau_c$ for $p_t = 0.75\% \Rightarrow \tau_{c,\max} = 3.1\ \text{N/mm}^2$. Section is still adequate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mistakes students make:
> 1. **Forgetting the $1.6$ factor** in the equivalent shear formula — this is the #1 reason for lost marks.
> 2. **Using $D$ instead of $d$** in the $\tau_{ve}$ calculation.
> 3. **Not computing $M_t$ separately** and applying it correctly to find $M_{e1}$ and $M_{e2}$.
> 4. **Failing to verify section adequacy** by comparing $\tau_{ve}$ with $\tau_{c,\max}$ from Table 20 — examiners specifically test this verification step.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Model Paper]`

**(a) Explain the space truss analogy used in the design of RCC beams for torsion. Illustrate with a neat sketch. (7 marks)**
**(b) Differentiate between equilibrium torsion and compatibility torsion. Under what circumstances, as per IS 456, can the compatibility torsion be neglected in design? (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Space Truss Analogy (7 marks)**

The **space truss analogy** (also called the *skew-bending model* or *thin-walled tube model*) is the theoretical basis for the design of RCC beams subjected to torsion. It is derived from Hsu's research (1968) and adopted by IS 456.

**Key Concepts (3 marks):**

1. **Equivalent Thin-Walled Tube:** After diagonal cracking, the concrete outside the closed stirrups spalls off. The remaining concrete inside the stirrup behaves as a *thin-walled closed tube* of effective wall thickness $t_e$.

2. **45° Diagonal Compression Struts:** The concrete tube resists torsion by developing *diagonal compression struts* spiralling around the tube at an angle of 45° to the longitudinal axis.

3. **Tension Ties in Steel:** The diagonal compression in concrete must be balanced by:
   - **Transverse tension ties** (the *legs of the closed stirrups*) — resist the tangential component.
   - **Longitudinal tension ties** (the *corner bars and side face bars*) — resist the axial component of the diagonal flow.

**Sketch Description (2 marks):** The model is best understood as a 3D truss where:
- Top and bottom chord bars = longitudinal steel.
- Vertical (or angled) members = stirrup legs.
- Diagonals at 45° = concrete struts.

**Equilibrium Equations (2 marks):** The shear flow $q$ around the perimeter is given by Bredt's formula:

$$q = \frac{T_u}{2 \, A_o}$$

where $A_o$ is the area enclosed by the centreline of the tube. The total longitudinal force is then:

$$F_L = q \cdot \text{perimeter} = \frac{T_u \cdot (2 b_1 + 2 d_1)}{2 \, b_1 \, d_1} = \frac{T_u (b_1 + d_1)}{b_1 \, d_1}$$

This is the basis of the **$A_{sl}$** formula in IS 456.

---

**Part (b) — Equilibrium vs Compatibility Torsion (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Equilibrium Torsion | Compatibility Torsion |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Required for static equilibrium | Arises from joint deformation compatibility |
| **Magnitude** | Independent of beam stiffness | Depends on relative member stiffness |
| **Criticality** | Must be fully designed | May be neglected in many cases |
| **Example** | Spandrel beam supporting wall | Secondary beam framing into main beam |
| **IS 456 Reference** | Cl. 41.1 (general) | Cl. 41.1 Note (special provision) |

**Difference Explanation (4 marks):**

- **Equilibrium torsion** is a *primary* internal force. The structure will collapse if the beam does not resist it. The magnitude is computed from equilibrium equations and is unaffected by cracking or change in stiffness.
- **Compatibility torsion** is a *secondary* internal force arising from the requirement that connected members rotate by the same angle at a joint. If the supporting beam cracks and loses torsional stiffness, the torsion reduces — leading to force redistribution.

**Circumstances for Neglecting Compatibility Torsion (3 marks):**

As per **IS 456:2000, Clause 41.1 Note**:
> "In structures where torsion is required to maintain equilibrium, the effect of torsion shall be considered in design. In structures where torsion is not required for equilibrium, but where it is induced by continuity of the member with adjacent members, the resulting torsional moment may be reduced by redistribution of forces to adjacent members, provided the structural integrity is maintained."

In practice, compatibility torsion can be **neglected** when:
1. The torsional stiffness of the supporting member is low (i.e., it cracks easily).
2. The adjacent members have adequate reserve strength to absorb the redistributed moment.
3. The structural integrity of the slab/beam system is not compromised.
4. Crack widths are controlled by adequate side-face reinforcement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** When neglecting compatibility torsion, students often forget to **still provide minimum side-face reinforcement** ($0.1\% \, bD$) to control diagonal cracks. Examiners specifically deduct marks for this omission.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Torsion Definitions, Classifications, Guidelines & Assumptions**

### Definitions to Memorize
- [ ] Torsion = Twisting moment about the beam's longitudinal centroidal axis.
- [ ] **Equilibrium Torsion** = Statically determinate; required for stability.
- [ ] **Compatibility Torsion** = Statically indeterminate; can be neglected in many cases.
- [ ] **Equivalent Shear ($V_e$)** = Total shear accounting for transverse + torsional effects.
- [ ] **Equivalent Moment ($M_t$)** = Additional flexural component from torsional shear flow.
- [ ] **$M_{e1}, M_{e2}$** = Effective bending moments at top and bottom fibres due to combined effects.

### Classifications to Remember
- [ ] Torsion is classified into **Equilibrium** and **Compatibility** (origin-based).
- [ ] Cracking is classified as **Skew-Bending, Skew-Shear, and Spalling** (failure-mode based).
- [ ] Sections are classified as **Compact ($D/b \le 4$)**, **Moderate ($4 < D/b \le 10$)**, and **Slender ($D/b > 10$)** (geometry-based).

### The 7 Key Assumptions (IS 456 Design)
- [ ] Thin-walled tube analogy
- [ ] 45° diagonal concrete struts
- [ ] Tension-softened concrete (no tension in concrete)
- [ ] Stirrups yield in tension
- [ ] Longitudinal bars yield in tension
- [ ] Strain compatibility truss model
- [ ] Torsion always combined with shear

### Critical Formulas to Memorize
- [ ] $V_e = V_u + \dfrac{1.6 \, T_u}{b}$ — (Cl. 41.3.1)
- [ ] $M_t = \dfrac{T_u (1 + D/b)}{1.7}$ — (Cl. 41.4.2)
- [ ] $\tau_{ve} = \dfrac{V_e}{b \, d}$ — (Cl. 40.1)
- [ ] Section adequacy check: $\tau_{ve} \le \tau_{c,\max}$ (Table 20)

### IS 456 Clauses to Quote in Answers
- [ ] **Cl. 41.1** — Torsion design scope and limits on $D/b$.
- [ ] **Cl. 41.3.1** — Equivalent shear formula.
- [ ] **Cl. 41.4.2** — Equivalent moment formula.
- [ ] **Cl. 41.4.3** — Transverse reinforcement.
- [ ] **Cl. B-6.6.3 of SP-16** — Longitudinal torsional reinforcement.

### Common Mistakes to Avoid in Exam
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting the **$1.6$** factor in $V_e$.
- [ ] ❌ Using **$D$** instead of **$d$** in $\tau_{ve}$.
- [ ] ❌ Not verifying section adequacy with $\tau_{c,\max}$.
- [ ] ❌ Neglecting the check for $D/b \le 4$.
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting to provide side-face reinforcement (min $0.1\% \, bD$).

### High-Yield Keywords for 14-Mark Answers
- [ ] "**Thin-walled tube analogy**" — must mention in every answer.
- [ ] "**Space truss model**" — alternative phrasing.
- [ ] "**45° diagonal concrete struts**" — always state this.
- [ ] "**Bredt's formula**" — credit-grabber for derivation answers.
- [ ] "**Partial safety factors $\gamma_c = 1.5$, $\gamma_s = 1.15$**" — show your code awareness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modes of Failure and Analysis of Short Axially Loaded Compression Members under Axial Load with Uniaxial and Biaxial Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modes of Failure and Analysis of Short Axially Loaded Compression Members under Axial Load with Uniaxial and Biaxial Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Short Axially Loaded Compression Member?

A **compression member** is a structural element that primarily carries axial compressive forces, often combined with bending moments. In reinforced concrete (RC) design, these are the building blocks of every multi-storey structure — columns of buildings, piers of bridges, and struts of trusses all fall into this category.

A **short column** (as per IS 456:2000, Clause 25.1.2) is one whose **effective length (L_e)** relative to its **least lateral dimension (D or b)** does **not exceed 12** for a member unsupported against lateral buckling. For a column to be classified as a "short column":

$$\frac{L_{e}}{D} \le 12 \quad \text{(for circular or rectangular columns)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Short"?** Because in short columns, failure is governed by **material strength (crushing of concrete and yielding of steel)** — *not* by elastic instability (buckling). Slenderness-induced buckling is the hallmark of *long/slender* columns, which belong to a different design philosophy beyond this module.

## 1.2 Modes of Failure — The Big Picture

The term "modes of failure" refers to **the distinct ways a column can collapse** under combined loading. For a short RC column under combined axial load and bending, the failure can broadly be classified by the **relative magnitude of axial load (P) versus bending moment (M)**:

| Loading Regime | Dominant Action | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Pure Axial (P only) | Pure compression | **Axial crushing** — uniform compressive failure across the section |
| Large P, Small e | Compression dominant | **Compression failure** — concrete crushes on the more compressed side; tension steel may or may not yield |
| Small P, Large e | Tension dominant | **Tension failure** — tension steel yields first, then concrete crushes |
| Balanced | P = P_b | **Balanced failure** — simultaneous yielding of tension steel and crushing of concrete |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Balanced Failure** is the transition point. It is the most "ductile" mode and acts as the reference condition for all interaction-based design. The Indian Standard IS 456:2000 uses this philosophy to generate **interaction diagrams (P–M curves)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaning Pole" Picture

Imagine you are holding a **wooden pole vertically** and pressing down on its top (this is **pure axial load**). The pole crushes evenly at the bottom. Easy to predict.

Now imagine someone places a **heavy bag on one side** of the pole's top. The bag tries to bend the pole, but the pole is still being pushed down. Now you have a **combination** of compression *and* bending. The pole no longer fails uniformly — one side is squeezed hard (compression) while the other side may actually stretch (tension). If the bag is light compared to the downward push, the pole is mostly crushed; if the bag is huge, the bending dominates and the pole might "snap" on the tension side.

**Biaxial bending** is the same situation, but the bag is hanging off a *corner* — the column is being pushed down *and* twisted about *both* horizontal axes (X and Y). This is what corner columns of buildings experience due to wind from two directions or earthquake shaking.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Examples of Biaxial Bending**:
> - **Corner columns** of buildings (subject to wind/seismic moments on both principal axes).
> - **Bridge piers** struck by traffic from any direction.
> - **Sign posts or lamp posts** with heavy fixtures mounted asymmetrically.

## 1.4 IS 456:2000 Minimum Eccentricity Provision

Even when a column is *nominally* subjected to pure axial load, IS 456:2000 (Clause 25.4) mandates a **minimum design eccentricity**:

$$e_{x,min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30} \ge 20 \text{ mm}$$

$$e_{y,min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{b}{30} \ge 20 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why minimum eccentricity?** Construction is never perfect — columns are not cast exactly vertical, and loads are not applied exactly at the centroid. The code forces designers to consider this **unavoidable imperfection** by designing for a small bending moment, even when the user requests "pure" axial design.

## 1.5 Key Definitions for KTU Board

- **Axial Load (P_u)**: Factored compressive force acting along the longitudinal axis of the column.
- **Uniaxial Bending**: Bending about **one** principal axis (X-X or Y-Y) only.
- **Biaxial Bending**: Simultaneous bending about **both** principal axes (X-X and Y-Y).
- **Interaction Diagram / Chart**: A graphical (or analytical) relationship between axial load capacity (P_u) and moment capacity (M_u) of a given cross-section, used as a design aid.
- **SP-16**: "Design Aids for IS 456" — the **bible** of Indian RC design charts, published by the Bureau of Indian Standards.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Modes of Failure — Detailed Mechanics

A short axially loaded RC column can fail in any of the following modes, depending on the **eccentricity (e = M/P)** of the load:

### Mode 1: Pure Axial Compression (e = 0)
- The entire cross-section is under uniform compression.
- Failure occurs when concrete reaches its ultimate compressive strain (0.0035) on all sides.
- All longitudinal steel is in compression.
- **Theoretical capacity** (without any safety factor on strength reduction):

$$P_{u0} = 0.45 f_{ck} A_c + 0.75 f_y A_{sc}$$

- **IS 456:2000 design capacity** (Clause 26.3.1, for columns with minimum eccentricity):

$$P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$$

where:
- $f_{ck}$ = characteristic compressive strength of concrete (N/mm²)
- $f_y$ = characteristic yield strength of steel (N/mm²)
- $A_c$ = net area of concrete (gross area minus steel area) = $A_g - A_{sc}$
- $A_{sc}$ = total area of longitudinal steel

### Mode 2: Compression Failure (Large Axial Load, Small Eccentricity)
- The concrete on the **more compressed face** reaches its ultimate strain (0.0035) **first**.
- The strain in the steel on the **tension face** is **less than the yield strain** ($f_s < f_y$).
- Failure is **sudden and brittle** — concrete crushes before steel can yield.
- This is the case when $e < e_b$ (eccentricity less than balanced eccentricity).

### Mode 3: Tension Failure (Small Axial Load, Large Eccentricity)
- The steel on the **tension face** yields **first** (reaches $f_y$).
- As the load increases, the **neutral axis migrates** towards the compression face.
- Finally, concrete on the **compression face** crushes at strain 0.0035.
- Failure is **ductile** — there is visible cracking and warning before collapse.
- This is the case when $e > e_b$ (eccentricity greater than balanced eccentricity).

### Mode 4: Balanced Failure (e = e_b)
- **Simultaneous** yielding of tension steel and crushing of compression concrete.
- The neutral axis depth is $x_u = x_{u,max} = 0.0035 \cdot d / (0.0055 + 0.67 f_y/E_s)$.
- This is the **transition point** between compression and tension failure modes.

## 2.2 Assumptions in IS 456:2000 Limit State Design (Clause 38.1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These assumptions govern **every** calculation in this module — commit them to memory.

1. **Plane sections remain plane** after bending (Bernoulli's hypothesis).
2. Maximum strain in concrete at the outermost compression fibre = **0.0035** (in flexure) — but for members subjected to combined axial load and bending, IS 456 uses a **parabolic-rectangular stress block** for $f_{ck} \le$ **35 MPa** (Clause 38.1, Fig. 21).
3. Maximum usable strain at the extreme concrete compression fibre = **0.0035** for $f_{ck} \le$ **35 MPa**, and reduces linearly to **0.002** at **M 100** grade.
4. Tensile strength of concrete is **ignored** (only steel resists tension).
5. The stress-strain relationship of steel is **idealized** as a horizontal plateau at $f_y$ after yield (elastic-perfectly plastic), with $E_s$ = **2 × 10⁵ N/mm²**.
6. The stress block parameters: for $f_{ck} \le$ **35 MPa**, the rectangular stress block has **constant stress = 0.446 $f_{ck}$** over a depth of **0.434 $x_u$** from the compression face.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | IS Code Clause |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ | Axial capacity with min. eccentricity | 26.3.1 |
| 2 | $e_{x,min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30} \ge 20$ mm | Min. eccentricity about X-axis | 25.4 |
| 3 | $e_{y,min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{b}{30} \ge 20$ mm | Min. eccentricity about Y-axis | 25.4 |
| 4 | $P_u \le 0.45 f_{ck} A_c + 0.75 f_y A_{sc}$ (no safety factor) | Squash load capacity | 26.3.1 |
| 5 | $p = \frac{A_{sc}}{A_g} \times 100$ | Percentage of steel (0.8% to 6%) | 26.5.3.1 |
| 6 | $\frac{P_u}{f_{ck} b D}, \frac{M_u}{f_{ck} b D^2}$ | Non-dimensional parameters (SP-16 axes) | SP-16 |
| 7 | $\frac{1}{P_u} = \frac{1}{P_{ux}} + \frac{1}{P_{uy}} - \frac{1}{P_{u0}}$ | **Bresler's Reciprocal Load Equation** for biaxial bending | 25.4.3 (concept) |
| 8 | $P_{ux} = P_{u} \cdot (1 + \alpha_x)$ and $P_{uy} = P_{u} \cdot (1 + \alpha_y)$ | Uniaxial capacities for Bresler's method | - |
| 9 | $\alpha = \frac{P_{u0} - P_{u}}{P_{u}}$ | Excentricity parameter in Bresler's method | - |
| 10 | $M_{ux} = P_{ux} \cdot e_{y}$ and $M_{uy} = P_{uy} \cdot e_{x}$ | Biaxial moments from Bresler | - |
| 11 | $P_u = \text{Case A or B, IS 456 Annex G}$ | Simplified IS 456 biaxial bending check | Annex G |
| 12 | For high eccentricity: $M_{ux} = M_u \cdot (1 + \beta)$ and $M_{uy} = M_u \cdot \beta$ | IS 456 Annex G – Case A | Annex G |
| 13 | $M_{uy} = M_{ux} \cdot \frac{b}{D}$ | Case B (corner column) of IS 456 Annex G | Annex G |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize formulas 1, 2, 3, 7, 11, 12, and 13** — these are the high-frequency formulas tested in KTU ESE.

## 2.4 SP-16 Design Charts — How to Read Them

**SP-16 charts** (Design Aids for IS 456) provide pre-calculated interaction curves for rectangular columns with various reinforcement layouts (equal reinforcement on two opposite faces, equal on all four faces, etc.).

**How SP-16 axes work:**

- The **X-axis** is a non-dimensional moment parameter: $\frac{M_u}{f_{ck} b D^2}$ (or $\frac{M}{f_{ck} b D^2}$ for working stress).
- The **Y-axis** is a non-dimensional axial load parameter: $\frac{P_u}{f_{ck} b D}$ (or $\frac{P}{f_{ck} b D}$).
- Each curve corresponds to a specific $p/f_{ck}$ ratio (where $p$ = percentage of steel).
- The curves are valid for a particular reinforcement layout (e.g., "equal bars on 2 sides" vs. "equal bars on 4 sides" vs. "equal bars on all 4 sides with corner bars").

**Design procedure using SP-16 (uniaxial bending):**

1. Calculate the non-dimensional parameters $\frac{P_u}{f_{ck} b D}$ and $\frac{M_u}{f_{ck} b D^2}$.
2. Enter the appropriate chart with $P_u/(f_{ck} b D)$ on Y-axis.
3. Move horizontally to the appropriate $p/f_{ck}$ curve.
4. Read off $\frac{M_u}{f_{ck} b D^2}$.
5. If the actual moment is **less than** the chart's moment capacity, the section is **safe**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The analysis methods discussed in this module are routinely used in:
- **Building frame design** (RC frames under gravity + lateral loads).
- **Bridge pier analysis** (combined dead load + live load + wind/earthquake).
- **Industrial structures** (columns supporting cranes on multiple axes).
- **Audit/retrofit assessments** of existing columns to determine reserve capacity.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pure Axial Capacity Equation (IS 456, Cl. 26.3.1)

**Given**: A short column with gross area $A_g$, steel area $A_{sc}$, concrete area $A_c = A_g - A_{sc}$, grades $f_{ck}$ and $f_y$.

**Step 1**: Under pure axial compression, both materials develop uniform stress. At ultimate limit state, the design strengths are:
- Concrete in compression: $0.4 f_{ck}$ (partial safety factor $\gamma_m = 1.5$ applied to $0.67 f_{ck}$ characteristic strength).
- Steel in compression: $0.67 f_y$ (partial safety factor $\gamma_m = 1.15$).

**Step 2**: The total axial capacity is the sum of capacities from each material:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_u &= (\text{Stress in concrete}) \times (\text{Concrete area}) + (\text{Stress in steel}) \times (\text{Steel area}) \\
P_u &= (0.4 f_{ck}) \cdot A_c + (0.67 f_y) \cdot A_{sc}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3**: Substituting $A_c = A_g - A_{sc}$:

$$P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} (A_g - A_{sc}) + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$$

**Step 4**: For convenience, the equation is often written as:

$$\boxed{P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The coefficient **0.4** (not 0.67/1.5 = 0.447) is conservative because the IS code accounts for additional uncertainties such as accidental eccentricity, slenderness, and material variability.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Balanced Section Parameters

For a **rectangular section** with width $b$, effective depth $d$, $d'$ = cover to centroid of compression steel, area of tension steel $A_{st}$, and area of compression steel $A_{sc}$:

**Step 1**: At balanced failure, the strain in the extreme concrete compression fibre = 0.0035, and strain in the tension steel = $f_y / E_s$.

**Step 2**: By similar triangles (strain compatibility):

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{x_{u,b}}{d} &= \frac{0.0035}{0.0035 + \frac{f_y}{E_s}} \\
x_{u,b} &= \frac{0.0035 \cdot d}{0.0035 + \frac{f_y}{2 \times 10^5}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3**: For $f_y = 415$ N/mm² (most common in India):

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{u,b} &= \frac{0.0035 \cdot d}{0.0035 + \frac{415}{200000}} \\
x_{u,b} &= \frac{0.0035 \cdot d}{0.0035 + 0.002075} \\
x_{u,b} &= \frac{0.0035 \cdot d}{0.005575} \\
x_{u,b} &= 0.628 d
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4**: For Fe 500 steel:

$$x_{u,b} = \frac{0.0035 \cdot d}{0.0035 + 0.0025} = 0.583 d$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Bresler's Reciprocal Load Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **guaranteed KTU exam question**. The Bresler equation is the cornerstone of biaxial bending design in RC columns.

**Scenario**: A rectangular column is subjected to a factored axial load $P_u$ and biaxial moments $M_{ux}$ and $M_{uy}$ about the X and Y axes respectively, with eccentricities $e_x = M_{ux}/P_u$ and $e_y = M_{uy}/P_u$.

**Step 1: Define the three characteristic loads**:
- $P_{u0}$ = Pure axial load capacity (squash load) when the column carries only axial load (no moment).
- $P_{ux}$ = Uniaxial load capacity when the column carries axial load + moment $M_{ux}$ about the X-axis only (eccentricity in the Y direction).
- $P_{uy}$ = Uniaxial load capacity when the column carries axial load + moment $M_{uy}$ about the Y-axis only (eccentricity in the X direction).

**Step 2: Bresler's Assumption**:
Bresler (1960) proposed that the failure surface of an RC column under biaxial bending can be approximated by the **reciprocal load equation**:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{P_u} = \frac{1}{P_{ux}} + \frac{1}{P_{uy}} - \frac{1}{P_{u0}}}$$

**Step 3: Origin of the Equation**:
This equation is empirically derived from the observation that when $P_{ux} = P_{uy} = P_{u0}$, the column is under pure axial load. The equation degenerates to $\frac{1}{P_u} = \frac{1}{P_{u0}} + \frac{1}{P_{u0}} - \frac{1}{P_{u0}} = \frac{1}{P_{u0}}$, satisfying the boundary condition.

**Step 4: When the Biaxial Moment is small** (i.e., $P_{u0} \gg P_{ux}$ and $P_{u0} \gg P_{uy}$), the term $\frac{1}{P_{u0}}$ becomes negligible, and we recover the simple uniaxial design equations. This is the **Bresler's simplified form**:

$$\frac{1}{P_u} \approx \frac{1}{P_{ux}} + \frac{1}{P_{uy}}$$

**Step 5: Useful rearrangement — using the parameter $\alpha$**:
Define $\alpha_x = \frac{P_{u0} - P_{ux}}{P_{ux}}$ and $\alpha_y = \frac{P_{u0} - P_{uy}}{P_{uy}}$.

Then the moment capacities for biaxial bending become:

$$M_{ux} = P_u \cdot e_y \quad \text{and} \quad M_{uy} = P_u \cdot e_x$$

**Step 6: The Extended Bresler Equation** (in terms of $P_u$ alone):

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{P_u} &= \frac{1}{P_{ux}} + \frac{1}{P_{uy}} - \frac{1}{P_{u0}} \\
\Rightarrow P_{ux} \cdot P_{uy} \cdot P_{u0} &= P_u \cdot P_{uy} \cdot P_{u0} + P_u \cdot P_{ux} \cdot P_{u0} - P_u \cdot P_{ux} \cdot P_{uy} \\
\Rightarrow P_{ux} \cdot P_{uy} \cdot P_{u0} &= P_u \left[ P_{uy} P_{u0} + P_{ux} P_{u0} - P_{ux} P_{uy} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for $P_u$:

$$\boxed{P_u = \frac{P_{ux} \cdot P_{uy} \cdot P_{u0}}{P_{uy} P_{u0} + P_{ux} P_{u0} - P_{ux} P_{uy}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity of Bresler's Equation**: It is **valid for small eccentricities** ($e/D < 0.3$ to $0.4$). For larger eccentricities, IS 456 recommends a more rigorous analysis using strain compatibility or finite element methods.

## 3.4 IS 456:2000 Simplified Biaxial Bending Procedure (Annex G)

The IS code provides a simplified design check for biaxial bending for **rectangular columns with rectangular reinforcement layout** subjected to axial load $P_u$ and biaxial moments $M_{ux}$ and $M_{uy}$:

**Case A**: Uniaxial moment capacity $M_{u2}$ about the axis of bending (i.e., the axis **parallel** to the smaller dimension $b$ — see Fig. K of IS 456). Then:

$$M_{ux} = M_{u} \cdot (1 + \beta) \quad \text{and} \quad M_{uy} = M_{u} \cdot \beta$$

where:
- $\beta = \frac{0.5 + \frac{e_x}{D}}{1 + \frac{e_x}{D}}$ (a function of $e_x$)
- OR (alternative form): $\beta = 0.67 + 1.0 \cdot (e_y / D)$, depending on code edition.

**Case B**: For corner columns and other situations where both moments are significant:

$$M_{uy} = M_{ux} \cdot \frac{b}{D}$$

This simplified check is approximate but extremely useful for routine design verification.

## 3.5 Numerical Example — Uniaxial Bending Capacity

**Given**: Rectangular column 300 mm × 450 mm, $f_{ck}$ = 25 N/mm², $f_y$ = 415 N/mm², $p$ = 2% steel, effective cover = 60 mm, $d = 450 - 60 = 390$ mm, $d' = 60$ mm.

**Step 1**: Areas
- $A_g = 300 \times 450 = 135000$ mm²
- $A_{sc} = 0.02 \times 135000 = 2700$ mm²

**Step 2**: Pure axial capacity
$$\begin{aligned}
A_c &= 135000 - 2700 = 132300 \text{ mm}^2 \\
P_{u0} &= 0.4 \times 25 \times 132300 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 2700 \\
&= 1323000 + 750585 = 2073585 \text{ N} \approx 2073.6 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3**: Minimum eccentricity (Clause 25.4, assuming L = 3 m unsupported length, $L_e = 3$ m = 3000 mm, $D = 450$ mm):
$$\begin{aligned}
e_{x,min} &= \frac{3000}{500} + \frac{450}{30} = 6 + 15 = 21 \text{ mm} \ge 20 \text{ mm} \checkmark \\
\therefore e_{min} &= 21 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4**: Moment corresponding to minimum eccentricity
$$M_{u,min} = P_u \cdot e_{min} = 2073.6 \times 0.021 = 43.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

This minimum moment **must be designed for** in addition to any external applied moments — a frequently missed KTU pitfall.

## 3.6 Numerical Example — Bresler's Biaxial Bending

**Given**: A 400 mm × 600 mm column (with $D = 600$ mm parallel to $M_{ux}$, $b = 400$ mm parallel to $M_{uy}$), $f_{ck}$ = 30 N/mm², $f_y$ = 500 N/mm², $p$ = 1.5%, $A_{sc} = 1.5\% \times 400 \times 600 = 3600$ mm².

**Applied loads**: $P_u = 1800$ kN, $M_{ux} = 120$ kN·m, $M_{uy} = 80$ kN·m.

**Step 1**: Calculate $P_{u0}$ (squash load):
$$\begin{aligned}
A_c &= 240000 - 3600 = 236400 \text{ mm}^2 \\
P_{u0} &= 0.4 \times 30 \times 236400 + 0.67 \times 500 \times 3600 \\
&= 2836800 + 1206000 = 4042800 \text{ N} = 4042.8 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2**: Calculate eccentricities:
$$\begin{aligned}
e_x &= \frac{M_{uy}}{P_u} = \frac{80}{1800} = 0.0444 \text{ m} = 44.4 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(measured in x-direction)} \\
e_y &= \frac{M_{ux}}{P_u} = \frac{120}{1800} = 0.0667 \text{ m} = 66.7 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(measured in y-direction)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3**: Using SP-16 charts (or interaction equations), find $P_{ux}$ (load capacity for uniaxial bending about X-axis with $e_y = 66.7$ mm) and $P_{uy}$ (load capacity for uniaxial bending about Y-axis with $e_x = 44.4$ mm).

Assuming (from SP-16 charts): $P_{ux} = 2200$ kN, $P_{uy} = 2500$ kN.

**Step 4**: Apply Bresler's equation:
$$\begin{aligned}
P_{u,predicted} &= \frac{P_{ux} \cdot P_{uy} \cdot P_{u0}}{P_{uy} P_{u0} + P_{ux} P_{u0} - P_{ux} P_{uy}} \\
&= \frac{2200 \times 2500 \times 4042.8}{2500 \times 4042.8 + 2200 \times 4042.8 - 2200 \times 2500} \\
&= \frac{22235400000}{10107000 + 8894160 - 5500000} \\
&= \frac{22235400000}{13501160} \\
&\approx 1647 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5**: Compare with applied $P_u = 1800$ kN.

Since $P_{u,predicted} (1647 \text{ kN}) < P_{u,applied} (1800 \text{ kN})$, the section is **NOT safe**. We must increase the section size, increase the steel area, or use a higher concrete grade.

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Column Failure Modes (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Short RC Column under Combined Loading] --> B{Determine Eccentricity e = M/P}
    B -->|e = 0| C[Mode 1: Pure Axial Crushing]
    B -->|e < eb| D[Mode 2: Compression Failure]
    B -->|e = eb| E[Mode 3: Balanced Failure]
    B -->|e > eb| F[Mode 4: Tension Failure]

    C --> C1[Concrete crushes uniformly]
    C --> C2[All steel in compression]
    C --> C3[Sudden brittle failure]

    D --> D1[Concrete crushes on more compressed face]
    D --> D2[Tension steel does NOT yield]
    D --> D3[Sudden brittle failure]

    E --> E1[Tension steel yields]
    E --> E2[Concrete crushes simultaneously]
    E --> E3[Ductile transition point]

    F --> F1[Tension steel yields first]
    F --> F2[NA moves toward compression face]
    F --> F3[Concrete crushes after steel yields]
    F --> F4[Gradual ductile failure]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Pu, Mu, b, D, fck, fy] --> B[Compute non-dimensional parameters]
    B --> B1[Pu / fck × b × D]
    B --> B2[Mu / fck × b × D²]
    A --> C[Assume p percentage of steel]
    C --> D[Enter SP-16 Chart with p/fck]
    D --> E[Read Mu capacity from chart]
    E --> F{Applied Mu ≤ Chart Mu?}
    F -->|YES| G[Section is SAFE]
    F -->|NO| H[Increase section OR increase p]

    J --> K[End]
```

## 4.4 Design Flowchart — Biaxial Bending Column (Bresler's Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Pu, Mux, Muy, b, D, fck, fy] --> B[Calculate Pu0 squash load]
    B --> B1[Pu0 = 0.4 fck Ac + 0.67 fy Asc]

    A --> C[Calculate ex = Muy/Pu]
    A --> D[Calculate ey = Mux/Pu]

    C --> E[Find Pux from SP-16 uniaxial chart with ey]
    D --> F[Find Puy from SP-16 uniaxial chart with ex]

    E --> G[Apply Bresler Reciprocal Equation]
    F --> G
    B1 --> G
    G --> G1[1/Pu_pred = 1/Pux + 1/Puy − 1/Pu0]

    G1 --> H{Pu_pred ≥ Pu applied?}
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. (a)** Distinguish between the **three modes of failure** of a short RC column under combined axial load and uniaxial bending. Support your answer with neat sketches of the strain profiles.

**Model Answer**:
A short RC column under combined axial load and uniaxial bending can fail in three distinct modes depending on the eccentricity ($e = M/P$):

1. **Compression Failure** (small eccentricity, $e < e_b$): The concrete on the more compressed face reaches its ultimate strain of **0.0035** before the tension steel yields. The failure is sudden and brittle. The neutral axis depth is large ($x_u > x_{u,b}$), and the strain in tension steel is below $f_y/E_s$.

2. **Balanced Failure** ($e = e_b$): Tension steel just yields ($f_s = f_y$) **simultaneously** with concrete reaching its ultimate compressive strain. The neutral axis is at $x_{u,b} = 0.628 d$ for Fe 415 steel.

3. **Tension Failure** (large eccentricity, $e > e_b$): Tension steel yields first, and the neutral axis migrates towards the compression face as load increases. Concrete on the compression face eventually crushes. Failure is gradual and ductile, with visible cracking as a warning sign.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q2. (a)** State and explain **Bresler's Reciprocal Load Equation** for the design of short RC columns under biaxial bending. Mention its limitations.

**Model Answer**:
Bresler's equation (1960) gives the **load capacity $P_u$** of a rectangular column under biaxial bending and axial load as:

$$\frac{1}{P_u} = \frac{1}{P_{ux}} + \frac{1}{P_{uy}} - \frac{1}{P_{u0}}$$

where:
- $P_{u0}$ = squash load (pure axial capacity, Clause 26.3.1 of IS 456).
- $P_{ux}$ = uniaxial load capacity under moment $M_x$ only.
- $P_{uy}$ = uniaxial load capacity under moment $M_y$ only.

**Procedure**: $P_{ux}$ and $P_{uy}$ are obtained from SP-16 design charts for the given eccentricities $e_y = M_x/P_u$ and $e_x = M_y/P_u$ respectively. The predicted $P_u$ from Bresler's equation is then compared with the applied $P_u$.

**Limitations**:
- Valid for **small eccentricities** ($e/D \le 0.3$ to $0.4$).
- Not reliable when one eccentricity is **very small** and the other is **large** (extreme biaxial cases).
- The term $1/P_{u0}$ is often **neglected** for design simplification.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**A short RC column 400 mm × 600 mm is subjected to a factored axial load of 1800 kN and a factored moment of 240 kN·m about the major axis (X-X). Use M 25 concrete and Fe 415 steel. The unsupported length is 3.0 m. Assume 2% longitudinal steel. Check the adequacy of the section using SP-16 design charts.**

**(a) Calculate the minimum eccentricity and corresponding moment (7 marks).**
**(b) Check the section's safety under combined axial load and uniaxial bending (7 marks).**

**Model Solution**:

**Part (a): Minimum Eccentricity (7 marks)**

Given: $b = 400$ mm, $D = 600$ mm, $L = 3.0$ m = 3000 mm, $L_e = 3000$ mm (assume braced), $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm², $p = 2\%$, $P_u = 1800$ kN, $M_u = 240$ kN·m.

**Step 1**: Calculate gross and steel areas.
$$A_g = 400 \times 600 = 240000 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$A_{sc} = 0.02 \times 240000 = 4800 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$A_c = 240000 - 4800 = 235200 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2**: Calculate squash load $P_{u0}$.
$$P_{u0} = 0.4 \times 25 \times 235200 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 4800$$
$$P_{u0} = 2352000 + 1335840 = 3687840 \text{ N} \approx 3687.84 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3**: Calculate minimum eccentricities (Clause 25.4).
$$e_{x,min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{D}{30} = \frac{3000}{500} + \frac{600}{30} = 6 + 20 = 26 \text{ mm} \ge 20 \text{ mm} \checkmark$$
$$e_{y,min} = \frac{L}{500} + \frac{b}{30} = \frac{3000}{500} + \frac{400}{30} = 6 + 13.33 = 19.33 \text{ mm} < 20 \text{ mm}$$
$$\therefore e_{y,min} = 20 \text{ mm} \text{ (minimum)}$$

**Step 4**: Minimum moments.
$$M_{x,min} = P_u \times e_{y,min} = 1800 \times 0.020 = 36 \text{ kN·m}$$
$$M_{y,min} = P_u \times e_{x,min} = 1800 \times 0.026 = 46.8 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 5**: Design moments to use.
$$M_{ux} = \max(M_{u,applied}, M_{x,min}) = \max(240, 36) = 240 \text{ kN·m (governs)}$$
$$M_{uy} = M_{y,min} = 46.8 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Part (b): Section Adequacy (7 marks)**

**Step 1**: Non-dimensional parameters (note: bending about major axis means $D$ is the relevant depth).
$$\frac{P_u}{f_{ck} b D} = \frac{1800 \times 10^3}{25 \times 400 \times 600} = \frac{1800000}{6000000} = 0.30$$

$$\frac{M_{ux}}{f_{ck} b D^2} = \frac{240 \times 10^6}{25 \times 400 \times 600^2} = \frac{240 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^9} = 0.0667$$

**Step 2**: $p/f_{ck}$ ratio.
$$\frac{p}{f_{ck}} = \frac{2.0}{25} = 0.08$$

**Step 3**: From SP-16 Chart 30 (Fe 415, equal bars on 4 sides, $d'/D = 0.10$), entering with $P_u/(f_{ck} b D) = 0.30$ and $p/f_{ck} = 0.08$:
$$\left( \frac{M_u}{f_{ck} b D^2} \right)_{chart} \approx 0.12$$

**Step 4**: The chart capacity in absolute terms:
$$M_{u,chart} = 0.12 \times 25 \times 400 \times 600^2 = 432 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 5**: Compare with applied.
$$M_{u,applied} = 240 \text{ kN·m} < M_{u,chart} = 432 \text{ kN·m} \checkmark$$

**The section is SAFE** under combined axial load and uniaxial bending. 

**Part (b) Continued — Lateral Ties Check** (sometimes asked for full marks):
Diameter of ties $\ge$ **largest bar diameter / 4** = 25/4 ≈ 6.25 mm. Use **8 mm $\phi$** ties. Spacing $\le$ **least lateral dimension** = 400 mm, $\le$ **16 × smallest main bar diameter** = 16 × 25 = 400 mm, $\le$ **300 mm**. Use **200 mm c/c** spacing for safety.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice for Question A**

**A corner column 450 mm × 450 mm carries a factored axial load of 1600 kN with factored moments $M_{ux} = 90$ kN·m and $M_{uy} = 60$ kN·m. Use M 30 concrete and Fe 500 steel. The column has 1.8% steel distributed equally on all four faces. Check the safety of the column under biaxial bending using Bresler's Reciprocal Load Equation. Squash load $P_{u0}$ = 2700 kN.**

**(a) Compute the uniaxial capacities $P_{ux}$ and $P_{uy}$ using SP-16 and Bresler's parameters (7 marks).**
**(b) Determine the safe load by Bresler's equation and compare with the applied load (7 marks).**

**Model Solution**:

**Part (a): Uniaxial Capacities (7 marks)**

Given: $b = D = 450$ mm, $P_u = 1600$ kN, $M_{ux} = 90$ kN·m, $M_{uy} = 60$ kN·m, $f_{ck} = 30$ N/mm², $f_y = 500$ N/mm², $p = 1.8\%$, $P_{u0} = 2700$ kN.

**Step 1**: Compute eccentricities.
$$e_x = \frac{M_{uy}}{P_u} = \frac{60}{1600} = 0.0375 \text{ m} = 37.5 \text{ mm}$$
$$e_y = \frac{M_{ux}}{P_u} = \frac{90}{1600} = 0.05625 \text{ m} = 56.25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2**: Non-dimensional eccentricities for SP-16.
$$\frac{e_x}{D} = \frac{37.5}{450} = 0.0833$$
$$\frac{e_y}{b} = \frac{56.25}{450} = 0.125$$

**Step 3**: Non-dimensional load parameter.
$$\frac{P_u}{f_{ck} b D} = \frac{1600 \times 10^3}{30 \times 450 \times 450} = \frac{1600000}{6075000} = 0.263$$

**Step 4**: Compute $p/f_{ck}$.
$$\frac{p}{f_{ck}} = \frac{1.8}{30} = 0.06$$

**Step 5**: From SP-16 chart for Fe 500 (Chart 44 in SP-16, equal bars on 4 sides), entering with $P_u/(f_{ck} b D) = 0.263$ and $p/f_{ck} = 0.06$:
- For uniaxial bending with $e_y/b = 0.125$ (i.e., $M_x$ alone): $P_{ux} \approx$ **2200 kN** (read from chart).
- For uniaxial bending with $e_x/D = 0.0833$ (i.e., $M_y$ alone): $P_{uy} \approx$ **2400 kN** (read from chart).

**Step 6**: Bresler's $\alpha$ parameters.
$$\alpha_x = \frac{P_{u0} - P_{ux}}{P_{ux}} = \frac{2700 - 2200}{2200} = \frac{500}{2200} = 0.227$$
$$\alpha_y = \frac{P_{u0} - P_{uy}}{P_{uy}} = \frac{2700 - 2400}{2400} = \frac{300}{2400} = 0.125$$

**Part (b): Bresler's Equation (7 marks)**

**Step 1**: Apply Bresler's reciprocal load equation.
$$\frac{1}{P_u} = \frac{1}{P_{ux}} + \frac{1}{P_{uy}} - \frac{1}{P_{u0}}$$
$$\frac{1}{P_u} = \frac{1}{2200} + \frac{1}{2400} - \frac{1}{2700}$$

**Step 2**: Convert to common denominator (LCM $\approx$ 1188000):
$$\frac{1}{P_u} = \frac{540}{1188000} + \frac{495}{1188000} - \frac{440}{1188000} = \frac{595}{1188000}$$

**Step 3**: Solve for $P_u$.
$$P_{u,Bresler} = \frac{1188000}{595} \approx 1996.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4**: Compare with applied.
$$P_{u,Bresler} = 1996.6 \text{ kN} > P_{u,applied} = 1600 \text{ kN} \checkmark$$

**The section is SAFE** under biaxial bending with a reserve capacity of $(1996.6 - 1600) = 396.6$ kN.

**Safety Factor Margin**: $\frac{1996.6 - 1600}{1600} \times 100\% = 24.8\%$ reserve — adequate for KTU Board standards.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Minimum Eccentricity** (Cl. 25.4): Many students design the column for the **applied moment only**, ignoring the additional $M_{min} = P_u \times e_{min}$. The IS Code **mandatorily** requires this check. **Loss: 2–3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Sign Convention for Bresler's Equation**: Mixing up which $P_{ux}$ corresponds to which axis. **Mnemonic**: $P_{ux}$ is the load capacity when the moment is about the X-axis (so eccentricity is in the Y-direction: $e_y = M_{ux}/P_u$). **Loss: 2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Using Working Loads Instead of Factored Loads**: IS 456 uses **Limit State Design (LSD)**. Always use **factored loads** ($1.5 D + 1.5 L$ or $1.2 D + 1.2 L + 1.2 W$ for combined loading) and **design strengths** (0.4 $f_{ck}$ and 0.67 $f_y$). **Loss: 2–3 marks**.
>
> 4. **Confusing Squash Load and Axial Capacity**: $P_{u0} = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ is the capacity **at zero eccentricity**. The actual capacity **decreases** as eccentricity increases (this is the whole point of the P-M interaction curve). **Loss: 1–2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Not Selecting the Correct SP-16 Chart**: SP-16 has separate charts for each $d'/D$ ratio, $p/f_{ck}$ ratio, and reinforcement layout. Using the wrong chart leads to incorrect capacity estimation. **Loss: 2–3 marks**.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the SP-16 Units**: SP-16 uses **$P_u$ in kN and moments in kN·m** in many editions, but **$f_{ck}$, $b$, $D$ in N/mm² and mm**. Mix-and-match errors are the #1 numerical mistake. **Loss: 1–2 marks**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ High-Yield Quick-Fire Summary

- **Short column** = $L_e/D \le 12$ (IS 456 Cl. 25.1.2). Slenderness-induced buckling is **NOT** the failure mode; material strength governs.
- **Four failure modes**: Pure axial, Compression failure ($e < e_b$), Balanced ($e = e_b$), Tension failure ($e > e_b$).
- **Balanced NA depth**: $x_{u,b} = 0.628d$ for Fe 415, $x_{u,b} = 0.583d$ for Fe 500.
- **Axial capacity (squash load)**: $P_{u0} = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$.
- **Min. eccentricity**: $e_{min} = L/500 + D/30 \ge 20$ mm (about both axes).
- **Min. steel**: 0.8% of gross area. **Max. steel**: 6% of gross area (Clause 26.5.3.1).
- **Min. column dimension**: 200 mm for cast-in-situ (Cl. 25.1.1, modified as per IS 456 commentary).
- **Bresler's equation** (biaxial bending): $\frac{1}{P_u} = \frac{1}{P_{ux}} + \frac{1}{P_{uy}} - \frac{1}{P_{u0}}$.
- **IS 456 Annex G**: Simplified check for rectangular columns with rectangular reinforcement layout.
- **SP-16 charts** are based on $P_u/(f_{ck} b D)$ vs $M_u/(f_{ck} b D^2)$ non-dimensional parameters.
- **Lateral ties**: 8 mm $\phi$ minimum, spacing $\le 400$ mm, $\le 16 \phi_{main}$, $\le$ least lateral dimension.

### 🎯 KTU Exam Strategy Checklist

- [ ] Always **start** with the **minimum eccentricity check** before the main design.
- [ ] **Identify** whether the column is braced or unbraced to compute $L_e$ correctly.
- [ ] **Use SP-16 charts** of the **correct grade of steel** (Fe 415, Fe 500) and **correct reinforcement layout**.
- [ ] **Apply Bresler's equation** only for **biaxial bending with small eccentricities** ($e/D < 0.3$).
- [ ] **For corner columns** with significant biaxial moments, **always** check IS 456 Annex G as a backup.
- [ ] **Mention** the type of failure (compression, balanced, or tension) — this shows conceptual clarity to the examiner.
- [ ] **Provide units** in every numerical step (mm, N/mm², kN, kN·m) — partial credit is generous when units are correct.
- [ ] **Draw a neat cross-section** with reinforcement layout, dimensions, and loading — visual presentation earns 1–2 bonus marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Short Columns under Axial Load with Uniaxial and Biaxial Bending - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Short Columns under Axial Load with Uniaxial and Biaxial Bending - Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — DESIGN OF SHORT COLUMNS UNDER AXIAL LOAD WITH UNIAXIAL & BIAXIAL BENDING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

A **short column** is a compression member whose effective length-to-least lateral dimension ratio ($\frac{l_{ex}}{D} \le 12$ for rectangular or $\frac{l_{ex}}{D} \le 4$ for circular) is small enough that **buckling may be ignored**, and the column fails purely by **material crushing** under combined axial compression and bending.

When a column is subjected to:
- An **axial compressive load** $P_u$ acting along the centroidal axis, AND
- **Bending moments** $M_{ux}$ (about the major axis) and/or $M_{uy}$ (about the minor axis) caused by end restraints, frame action, or eccentric loading,

the member is said to be subjected to **combined axial load and bending**. The design problem is to determine the cross-section dimensions ($b \times D$) and the area of longitudinal steel ($A_{sc}$) such that the section can safely resist the combined action of $P_u + M_u$.

- **Uniaxial Bending**: Bending occurs about one principal axis only ($M_{uy} = 0$).
- **Biaxial Bending**: Bending occurs simultaneously about both principal axes ($M_{ux} \ne 0$ and $M_{uy} \ne 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition**: Per **IS 456:2000, Cl. 25.1.1**, a compression member may be considered as a *short column* when the ratio of effective length to least lateral dimension is **less than 12**. The design must satisfy both the **axial load capacity** and the **bending capacity** simultaneously, using the **strain-compatibility** approach or the **SP-16 interaction diagrams**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Leaning Pencil" Test**
>
> Imagine a **chubby wooden pillar** holding a heavy ceiling. Now push the ceiling slightly off-centre (say, 5 cm to the right). The pillar will still carry the vertical weight, but it will also have to **bend** a little to absorb the sideways push. The fibres on the right side are crushed more, and the fibres on the left side are stretched less — but since the whole section is in compression, even the "stretched" side is still in compression.
>
> Now push the ceiling diagonally (both right AND forward). The pillar must bend in **two directions at once** — this is biaxial bending. The worst-case stress occurs in one of the four corners of the pillar, not at the centre.
>
> **Uniaxial bending** = pillar pushed along one axis (X or Y).
> **Biaxial bending** = pillar pushed along a diagonal — the corner is the most stressed point.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (IS 456:2000)

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Unit | IS Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Partial safety factor for concrete | $\gamma_m$ | **1.5** | Cl. 36.4.2.1 |
| Partial safety factor for steel | $\gamma_m$ | **1.15** | Cl. 36.4.2.1 |
| Minimum grade of concrete (column) | $f_{ck}$ | **M20** (preferred M25) | Cl. 6.1.2 |
| Maximum grade of concrete | $f_{ck}$ | **M60** | Table 2 |
| Minimum longitudinal steel | $p_{min}$ | **0.8 %** of gross $A_g$ | Cl. 26.5.3.1(a) |
| Maximum longitudinal steel | $p_{max}$ | **6.0 %** of gross $A_g$ (4 % lap zone) | Cl. 26.5.3.1(b) |
| Minimum bar diameter | $\phi$ | **12 mm** | Cl. 26.5.3.1(c) |
| Minimum clear cover | — | **40 mm** (for column) | Table 16 |
| Minimum eccentricity (for design) | $e_{x,min}$, $e_{y,min}$ | $\frac{l}{500} + \frac{D}{30} \ge 20$ mm | Cl. 25.4 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Strain Compatibility Approach

When a column section is subjected to combined axial load $P_u$ and uniaxial moment $M_{ux}$, the **resultant strain distribution across the section becomes linear** (Euler-Bernoulli assumption: plane sections remain plane). This linear strain distribution produces:

- A **uniform compressive strain** $\varepsilon_c$ (from axial component), and
- A **linear strain gradient** $\phi \cdot y$ (from bending component).

The total strain at any fibre at distance $y$ from the centroid is:

$$\varepsilon(y) = \varepsilon_c + \phi \cdot y$$

This linear strain profile, combined with the **stress-strain curves of concrete and steel**, generates a non-linear stress block. The internal stress resultants must satisfy:

- $\int \sigma_c \cdot dA + \sum \sigma_{si} \cdot A_{si} = P_u$
- $\int \sigma_c \cdot y \cdot dA + \sum \sigma_{si} \cdot y_i \cdot A_{si} = M_u$

> [!NOTE]
> Solving these two non-linear equations by hand is impractical for every problem. Therefore, **IS 456:2000 permits the use of SP-16 design charts** (also called *interaction diagrams*) which pre-compute the ($P_u/f_{ck} b D$, $M_u/f_{ck} b D^2$) pairs for various $p/f_{ck}$ ratios and $d'/D$ ratios.

### 2.2 The Three Design Equations of IS 456 (Cl. 39.3)

For a short column subjected to combined axial compression and uniaxial bending, IS 456 prescribes a **triple check**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equation Set (Three Mandatory Conditions)**
> 1. **Axial Capacity Condition** (Cl. 39.3, simplified):
>    $$P_u \le 0.4 \, f_{ck} \, A_c + 0.67 \, f_y \, A_{sc}$$
> 2. **Eccentricity Condition** (Cl. 39.3 IS 456):
>    $$P_u \left(1.05 - \frac{4.0}{f_{ck} + 6}\right) \le 0.4 \, f_{ck} \, A_c + 0.67 \, f_y \, A_{sc}$$
> 3. **Biaxial Interaction Condition** (Bresler's Equation, Cl. 39.4 of IS 456):
>    $$\left(\frac{M_{ux}}{M_{ux1}}\right)^{\alpha_n} + \left(\frac{M_{uy}}{M_{uy1}}\right)^{\alpha_n} \le 1.0$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Reference — Print This Table**

| # | Formula / Condition | Equation | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimum eccentricity | $e_{min} = \dfrac{l}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30} \ge 20$ mm | Applied in **both** directions |
| 2 | Axial capacity (concrete + steel) | $P_{u,0} = 0.4 \, f_{ck} \, A_c + 0.67 \, f_y \, A_{sc}$ | Pure axial capacity check |
| 3 | Reduced axial capacity (eccentricity) | $P_{u,red} = P_{u,0}\left(1.05 - \dfrac{4.0}{f_{ck}+6}\right)$ | For $e < 0.05D$ |
| 4 | Uniaxial interaction (SP-16) | $\dfrac{P_u}{P_{u,0}} \; \text{vs} \; \dfrac{M_u}{M_{u0}}$ | Use chart for $p = A_{sc}/bD$ |
| 5 | Bresler biaxial equation | $\left(\dfrac{M_{ux}}{M_{ux1}}\right)^{\alpha_n} + \left(\dfrac{M_{uy}}{M_{uy1}}\right)^{\alpha_n} \le 1$ | $M_{ux1}, M_{uy1}$ are uniaxial capacities |
| 6 | Exponent $\alpha_n$ (Bresler) | $\alpha_n = 0.67 + \dfrac{1.7}{P_u/P_{uz}} \le 1.7$ | $P_{uz} = $ max axial capacity |
| 7 | Equivalent uniaxial method (cl. 39.5.1) | $M_{ux,eq} = M_{ux} + \dfrac{h}{b} \cdot M_{uy} \cdot \dfrac{(1-\beta)}{\beta}$ | $\beta = 0.67$ for rectangular columns |
| 8 | Moment magnification (slender columns) | $M_{u,total} = M_u \cdot C_m \cdot \delta$ | **Not applicable** for short columns |
| 9 | Total steel range | $0.008 \, bD \le A_{sc} \le 0.06 \, bD$ | 4 % max in lap zone |
| 10 | Lateral tie diameter | $\phi_{tie} \ge \max\!\left(\dfrac{\phi_{main}}{4}, 6 \text{ mm}\right)$ | Cl. 26.5.3.2 |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Application of This Design Procedure**
> - **Corner Columns of Buildings**: Receive axial load from beams coming from **two perpendicular directions** → biaxial bending design is essential.
> - **Bridge Piers**: Subjected to vertical dead load + lateral wind/vehicle load + longitudinal braking force → multi-axis bending.
> - **Industrial RCC Chimneys & Silos**: Wind pressure causes bending about the windward-leeward axis.
> - **Earthquake-Resistant Frames**: Per IS 13920, beam-column joints experience reversal of moments about both axes.
> - **Offshore Structures**: Wave load + dead load = severe biaxial bending with high $P-\Delta$ effects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Bresler's Biaxial Interaction Equation

**Starting premise**: The ultimate strength of a column under combined biaxial bending can be expressed as an interaction surface in 3D space ($P_u, M_{ux}, M_{uy}$).

**Step 1 — Define the failure surface**:
At a constant axial load $P_u$, the section can resist moments $(M_{ux}, M_{uy})$ until a failure surface (typically a curve) is reached. The points $\left(M_{ux1}, 0\right)$ and $\left(0, M_{uy1}\right)$ are the **uniaxial moment capacities** at the same $P_u$.

**Step 2 — Bresler's empirical power law (1960)**:
Bresler proposed that this failure curve could be approximated by a power function:

$$\left(\frac{M_{ux}}{M_{ux1}}\right)^{\alpha_n} + \left(\frac{M_{uy}}{M_{uy1}}\right)^{\alpha_n} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Determination of $\alpha_n$**:
Bresler calibrated $\alpha_n$ against experimental data. He found that $\alpha_n$ depends on the **axial load ratio** $\dfrac{P_u}{P_{uz}}$ where $P_{uz} = 0.45 f_{ck} A_g + 0.75 f_y A_{sc}$ is the *pure axial capacity with zero eccentricity*.

$$\alpha_n = 0.67 + \frac{1.7}{\left(\dfrac{P_u}{P_{uz}}\right)} \le 1.7$$

**Step 4 — Bounding values**:
- When $P_u/P_{uz} \to 0$ (pure bending), $\alpha_n \to \infty$, giving a *square* failure envelope (conservative).
- When $P_u/P_{uz} \to 1$ (pure axial), $\alpha_n \to 0.67$, giving a *triangular* failure envelope.

**Step 5 — Design check (the governing condition)**:

$$\left(\frac{M_{ux}}{M_{ux1}}\right)^{\alpha_n} + \left(\frac{M_{uy}}{M_{uy1}}\right)^{\alpha_n} \le 1.0$$

> If the LHS is less than 1.0, the section is **safe**. If it exceeds 1.0, **increase the section or steel**.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example 1 — Uniaxial Bending Design (14-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern)**
>
> Design a short column to support an axial factored load of **$P_u = 1800$ kN** and a factored moment **$M_{ux} = 120$ kN·m** about the major axis. Use $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 500$ N/mm², effective length $l_{ex} = 3.0$ m (both directions). Assume the column is bent about the major axis (depth $D$). Also design lateral ties.

#### Step 1 — Assume Section Dimensions (Initial Trial)

For combined loading, start with an empirical size:

$$A_g \approx \frac{1.05 \, P_u}{0.45 \, f_{ck}} = \frac{1.05 \times 1800 \times 10^3}{0.45 \times 25} \times 10^{-2} \approx 168000 \text{ mm}^2$$

Try $b = 350$ mm, $D = 500$ mm. Then $A_g = 175000$ mm² (acceptable).

#### Step 2 — Minimum Eccentricity Check

$$e_{x,min} = \frac{l_{ex}}{500} + \frac{D}{30} = \frac{3000}{500} + \frac{500}{30} = 6 + 16.67 = 22.67 \text{ mm}$$

$$e_{y,min} = \frac{l_{ey}}{500} + \frac{b}{30} = \frac{3000}{500} + \frac{350}{30} = 6 + 11.67 = 17.67 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow \text{Use 20 mm}$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate Effective Eccentricity

Actual eccentricity:

$$e_x = \frac{M_{ux}}{P_u} = \frac{120 \times 10^6}{1800 \times 10^3} = 66.67 \text{ mm}$$

Since $e_x = 66.67$ mm $> e_{x,min} = 22.67$ mm ✓ → use $e_x = 66.67$ mm.

Also, $e_{x}/D = 66.67/500 = 0.133 > 0.05$ → **Section is in tension/transition zone** (not governed by pure axial check).

#### Step 4 — Assume Steel Percentage and Effective Cover

Assume $p = 2.0\%$, $d' = 50$ mm, so $d'/D = 50/500 = 0.10$.

Required steel: $A_{sc} = 0.02 \times 175000 = 3500$ mm².

Provide 8 bars of 25 mm $\phi$: $A_{sc,prov} = 8 \times 491 = 3928$ mm².

#### Step 5 — Read SP-16 Interaction Chart

For $p = 3928/175000 = 2.24\%$, $d'/D = 0.10$, $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa, $f_y = 500$ MPa:

From SP-16 Chart No. 33 (or 37 for $f_y = 500$):

$$\frac{P_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, D} = \frac{1800 \times 10^3}{25 \times 350 \times 500} = 0.411$$

$$\frac{M_{ux}}{f_{ck} \, b \, D^2} = \frac{120 \times 10^6}{25 \times 350 \times 500^2} = 0.0549$$

#### Step 6 — Check from Chart

On the SP-16 chart for $p = 2.0\%$ and $d'/D = 0.10$, the point $(0.411, 0.0549)$ lies **below the curve** → **Section is SAFE**.

#### Step 7 — Design of Lateral Ties

Per IS 456 Cl. 26.5.3.2:
- Diameter of tie: $\phi_{tie} \ge \max(25/4, 6) = \max(6.25, 6) = 8$ mm
- Spacing: $s \le \min(300 \text{ mm}, b, D, 16\phi_{main}) = \min(300, 350, 500, 400) = 300$ mm

**Provide 8 mm $\phi$ ties at 300 mm c/c spacing.**

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example 2 — Biaxial Bending (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement**
>
> A corner column of size $400 \times 600$ mm, reinforced with $A_{sc} = 4500$ mm² ($p = 1.875\%$), is subjected to $P_u = 2000$ kN, $M_{ux} = 100$ kN·m (about $D = 600$ mm axis), and $M_{uy} = 80$ kN·m (about $b = 400$ mm axis). $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa, $f_y = 500$ MPa. Check adequacy by Bresler's method.

#### Step 1 — Compute Uniaxial Moment Capacities

For uniaxial bending about **X-axis** ($M_{ux1}$ at same $P_u$):
Use SP-16 with $p = 1.875\%$, $d'/D = 50/600 = 0.083$:

For given $P_u / (f_{ck} b D) = 2000 \times 10^3 / (25 \times 400 \times 600) = 0.333$:

Reading SP-16: $M_{ux1} / (f_{ck} b D^2) = 0.105$

$$M_{ux1} = 0.105 \times 25 \times 400 \times 600^2 \times 10^{-6} = 378 \text{ kN·m}$$

For uniaxial bending about **Y-axis** ($M_{uy1}$ at same $P_u$):
Use SP-16 with $b' = 600$ mm, $D' = 400$ mm (axes swapped):

$$M_{uy1} / (f_{ck} b D^2) = 0.110 \;\; \text{(from chart for } p = 1.875\%, d'/D = 0.125)$$

$$M_{uy1} = 0.110 \times 25 \times 600 \times 400^2 \times 10^{-6} = 264 \text{ kN·m}$$

#### Step 2 — Calculate $P_{uz}$ and $\alpha_n$

$$P_{uz} = 0.45 f_{ck} A_g + 0.75 f_y A_{sc} = 0.45 \times 25 \times 400 \times 600 + 0.75 \times 500 \times 4500$$

$$P_{uz} = 2,700,000 + 1,687,500 = 4,387,500 \text{ N} = 4387.5 \text{ kN}$$

$$\frac{P_u}{P_{uz}} = \frac{2000}{4387.5} = 0.456$$

$$\alpha_n = 0.67 + \frac{1.7}{0.456} = 0.67 + 3.73 = 4.40 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{Use } \alpha_n = 1.7 \text{ (max)}$$

#### Step 3 — Apply Bresler's Equation

$$\left(\frac{M_{ux}}{M_{ux1}}\right)^{1.7} + \left(\frac{M_{uy}}{M_{uy1}}\right)^{1.7} \le 1.0$$

$$\left(\frac{100}{378}\right)^{1.7} + \left(\frac{80}{264}\right)^{1.7} \le 1.0$$

$$(0.2646)^{1.7} + (0.3030)^{1.7} = 0.1042 + 0.1314 = 0.2356 \le 1.0 \;\; \checkmark \textbf{ SAFE}$$

#### Step 4 — Equivalent Uniaxial Method (Cl. 39.5.1, IS 456)

Alternatively, the equivalent uniaxial moment about the major axis:

$$M_{ux,eq} = M_{ux} + \frac{D}{b} \cdot \frac{(1-\beta)}{\beta} \cdot M_{uy}, \quad \beta = 0.67$$

$$M_{ux,eq} = 100 + \frac{600}{400} \cdot \frac{0.33}{0.67} \cdot 80 = 100 + 1.5 \times 0.4925 \times 80 = 100 + 59.1 = 159.1 \text{ kN·m}$$

Check SP-16: For $P_u / (f_{ck} b D) = 0.333$ and $M_{ux,eq} / (f_{ck} b D^2) = 159.1 \times 10^6 / (25 \times 400 \times 600^2) = 0.0442$.

On the chart for $p = 1.875\%$, $d'/D = 0.083$, the point $(0.333, 0.0442)$ lies below the curve → **SAFE** ✓.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Pu, Mux, Muy, lex, ley, fck, fy] --> B[Step 1: Calculate e_min = L/500 + D/30]
    B --> C{Step 2: Actual eccentricity}
    C -->|ex = Mux/Pu| D[Step 3: Assume Section b x D]
    C -->|ey = Muy/Pu| D
    D --> E[Step 4: Check Min Steel 0.8% bD]
    E --> F[Step 5: Calculate Asc from SP-16 Chart]
    F --> G{Step 6: Biaxial Check}
    G -->|Bresler Equation| H[Calculate alpha_n and Mn1, Mx1]
    H --> I{LHS <= 1.0?}
    I -->|Yes| J[SAFE: Detail Ties and Laps]
    I -->|No| K[INCREASE b, D, or Asc]
    K --> D
    J --> L[Final Output: Design Summary]
```

### 4.2 SP-16 Interaction Diagram (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Pu / fck b D] -->|x-axis| Curve((SP-16 Interaction Curve))
    M[Mu / fck b D squared] -->|y-axis| Curve
    Curve -->|Point Above| FAIL[UNSAFE - Increase Steel]
    Curve -->|Point Below| OK[SAFE - Section OK]
    Curve -->|Point On| LIM[Limit State Design]
```

### 4.3 Force / Strain Distribution Schematic for Uniaxial Bending + Axial Load

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pure Axial Load] -->|Adds| C[Combined Strain]
    B[Pure Bending Moment] -->|Adds| C
    C --> D[Strain Diagram - Linear]
    D --> E[Stress Block - Non-Linear]
    E --> F[Resultant Pu and Mu]
    F --> G[Design by SP-16]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Section Schematic (Biaxial Bending — Corner Stress)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rectangular Section b x D] --> B[Centroid at C]
    B --> C[Corner 1 - Max Compression]
    B --> D[Corner 2 - Tension/Compression]
    B --> E[Corner 3 - Tension Side]
    B --> F[Corner 4 - Neutral Axis Region]
    C --> G[Critical corner governs design]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Use Bresler formula]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Define a short column as per IS 456:2000. Why is the design of short columns governed by material strength and not by buckling?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> As per IS 456:2000 Cl. 25.1.1, a column is classified as a *short column* when the ratio of effective length to the least lateral dimension is **less than 12** for a rectangular column (or less than 4 for a circular column). For such members, the slenderness ratio is small enough that **lateral buckling** does not occur, and failure is by **crushing of concrete and yielding of steel** under combined axial and bending stresses. The design is therefore governed by the **material strength** of concrete and steel, and the relevant design checks are those of **Cl. 39.3** (axial + bending), not Cl. 25.5 (slenderness/buckling). The design is performed using the strain compatibility approach or **SP-16 design charts**.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **What is minimum eccentricity? Why is it mandatory in column design even when the column is theoretically axially loaded?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Minimum eccentricity is the *assumed* unavoidable eccentricity to be considered in design to account for **constructional inaccuracies, end restraint effects, and unintended load misalignment**. As per IS 456:2000 Cl. 25.4, $e_{min} = \frac{l}{500} + \frac{D}{30} \ge 20$ mm. It is mandatory because a perfectly axially loaded column does not exist in practice — even minor imperfections or end moments introduce bending. Designing the column as purely axially loaded (with $e = 0$) is unsafe, as it ignores the **secondary bending stresses** that will develop in reality. The design moment is then taken as $M_u = P_u \times e_{min}$ in the direction under consideration.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — Uniaxial Bending Design**
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**
>
> **(a)** Design a short rectangular RCC column to carry an axial factored load $P_u = 1500$ kN with a factored moment $M_{ux} = 90$ kN·m about the major axis. Take effective length = 3.2 m, $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 500$ N/mm², $d' = 50$ mm. Adopt IS 456:2000 and SP-16. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A short column 300 mm × 450 mm is reinforced with 8 bars of 20 mm diameter. Compute the axial load capacity and uniaxial moment capacity about the major axis using the approximate method. $f_{ck} = 20$ MPa, $f_y = 415$ MPa, $d' = 50$ mm. **[7 marks]**

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Design Solution:**

**Step 1**: Minimum eccentricity $e_{min} = \frac{3200}{500} + \frac{D}{30} \ge 20$ mm.
Trial: assume $D = 500$ mm, then $e_{min} = 6.4 + 16.67 = 23.07$ mm.

**Step 2**: Required area:
$$A_g \approx \frac{1.05 \, P_u}{0.45 \, f_{ck}} = \frac{1.05 \times 1500 \times 10^3}{0.45 \times 25} = 140000 \text{ mm}^2$$

Adopt $b = 300$ mm, $D = 500$ mm → $A_g = 150000$ mm².

**Step 3**: Actual eccentricity $e_x = M_{ux}/P_u = 90 \times 10^6 / (1500 \times 10^3) = 60$ mm. Since $e_x = 60 > 23.07$ mm ✓, use $e_x = 60$ mm.

**Step 4**: Non-dimensional parameters:
$$\frac{P_u}{f_{ck} b D} = \frac{1500 \times 10^3}{25 \times 300 \times 500} = 0.40$$
$$\frac{M_{ux}}{f_{ck} b D^2} = \frac{90 \times 10^6}{25 \times 300 \times 500^2} = 0.048$$

**Step 5**: From SP-16 for $d'/D = 0.10$, the required $p \approx 1.4\%$.
$$A_{sc} = 0.014 \times 150000 = 2100 \text{ mm}^2$$
Provide 6 bars of 22 mm $\phi$ → $A_{sc,prov} = 2280$ mm². (Revise $p = 1.52\%$, re-check from chart — safe).

**Step 6**: Lateral ties: $\phi_{tie} = \max(22/4, 6) = 6$ mm (use 8 mm for safety). Spacing: $s \le \min(300, 300, 500, 16 \times 22 = 352) = 300$ mm.
**Provide 8 mm ties @ 300 mm c/c.**

**Part (b) — Axial + Moment Capacity (Approximate Method):**

Given: $b = 300$, $D = 450$, $A_{sc} = 8 \times 314 = 2512$ mm², $p = 2512/(300 \times 450) = 1.86\%$.

**Step 1**: Pure axial capacity:
$$P_{u,0} = 0.4 \times 20 \times (300 \times 450 - 2512) + 0.67 \times 415 \times 2512$$
$$P_{u,0} = 0.4 \times 20 \times 132488 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 2512$$
$$P_{u,0} = 1,059,904 + 698,432 = 1758.3 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2**: Uniaxial moment about major axis from SP-16: For $p = 1.86\%$, $d'/D = 50/450 = 0.11$:
At zero axial load, $M_{u0} \approx 0.135 \times f_{ck} b D^2 = 0.135 \times 20 \times 300 \times 450^2 \times 10^{-6} = 164$ kN·m.
At $P_u = P_{u,0}$ (full axial), $M_{ux} = 0$ (linear interpolation of chart gives moment at $0.5 P_{u0}$ as ~ 0.05 normalized).

**For design moment at working axial load** $P_u = 1000$ kN (assume):
$$\frac{P_u}{f_{ck} b D} = \frac{1000 \times 10^3}{20 \times 300 \times 450} = 0.370$$

From SP-16 chart for $p = 1.86\%, d'/D = 0.11$:
$$\frac{M_{ux}}{f_{ck} b D^2} \approx 0.092 \Rightarrow M_{ux} = 0.092 \times 20 \times 300 \times 450^2 \times 10^{-6} = 111.8 \text{ kN·m}$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Biaxial Bending**
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
>
> **(a)** Explain Bresler's formula for the design of a short column under biaxial bending. How is the exponent $\alpha_n$ evaluated? **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A short column 400 mm × 600 mm carries $P_u = 1800$ kN, $M_{ux} = 150$ kN·m, $M_{uy} = 120$ kN·m. Steel provided is $A_{sc} = 4000$ mm². Use Bresler's formula and SP-16 to check the adequacy. $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa, $f_y = 500$ MPa, $d' = 50$ mm. **[7 marks]**

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Theory (see Section 3.1 above for full derivation).** Key points to write:

- Bresler proposed a non-dimensional interaction equation:
$$\left(\frac{M_{ux}}{M_{ux1}}\right)^{\alpha_n} + \left(\frac{M_{uy}}{M_{uy1}}\right)^{\alpha_n} \le 1$$
- $M_{ux1}$ and $M_{uy1}$ are the uniaxial moment capacities at the same axial load $P_u$, read from SP-16 charts.
- Exponent $\alpha_n$ depends on the axial load ratio:
$$\alpha_n = 0.67 + \frac{1.7}{(P_u / P_{uz})} \le 1.7$$
where $P_{uz} = 0.45 f_{ck} A_g + 0.75 f_y A_{sc}$ is the zero-eccentricity axial capacity.
- If the LHS ≤ 1, the section is safe in biaxial bending.

**Part (b) — Numerical Check:**

**Step 1**: $A_g = 400 \times 600 = 240000$ mm², $p = 4000/240000 = 1.667\%$, $d'/D = 50/600 = 0.083$.

**Step 2**: Calculate $P_{uz}$:
$$P_{uz} = 0.45 \times 25 \times 240000 + 0.75 \times 500 \times 4000 = 2,700,000 + 1,500,000 = 4200 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3**: $\dfrac{P_u}{P_{uz}} = \dfrac{1800}{4200} = 0.4286$

$$\alpha_n = 0.67 + \frac{1.7}{0.4286} = 0.67 + 3.966 = 4.636 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \alpha_n = 1.7 \text{ (cap)}$$

**Step 4**: Non-dimensional axial parameter:
$$\frac{P_u}{f_{ck} b D} = \frac{1800 \times 10^3}{25 \times 400 \times 600} = 0.30$$

**Step 5**: Read uniaxial capacities from SP-16:
- For $p = 1.667\%, d'/D = 0.083$, at $P_u/(f_{ck} bD) = 0.30$: $M_{ux1}/(f_{ck} b D^2) \approx 0.082$
$$M_{ux1} = 0.082 \times 25 \times 400 \times 600^2 \times 10^{-6} = 295.2 \text{ kN·m}$$
- For bending about minor axis: $M_{uy1}/(f_{ck} b D^2) \approx 0.105$ (using $b' = 600, D' = 400$)
$$M_{uy1} = 0.105 \times 25 \times 600 \times 400^2 \times 10^{-6} = 252 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 6**: Apply Bresler:
$$\left(\frac{150}{295.2}\right)^{1.7} + \left(\frac{120}{252}\right)^{1.7} = (0.508)^{1.7} + (0.476)^{1.7}$$
$$= 0.323 + 0.290 = 0.613 \le 1.0 \;\; \checkmark \textbf{ SAFE}$$

**Conclusion**: The section is **safe** in biaxial bending with significant reserve capacity (~39%).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting minimum eccentricity** (IS 456 Cl. 25.4) → lose 2 marks in every problem.
> 2. **Using $P_{u,0}$ (zero eccentricity) instead of $P_{uz}$** in Bresler formula → wrong $\alpha_n$ and possibly unsafe design.
> 3. **Capping $\alpha_n$ at 1.7** — many students forget the upper limit, leading to wrongly small LHS values.
> 4. **Swapping $b$ and $D$** in SP-16 charts — remember that $D$ is the **depth in the direction of bending**.
> 5. **Ignoring 4 % steel limit in lap zone** — practical detailing marks deducted if maximum is 6 % but lap region exceeds 4 %.
> 6. **Tie spacing** not designed → lose easy 1–2 marks. Always design ties (diameter and spacing) explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 (Short Columns)**
>
> ✅ **Definition**: Short column → $l_{ex}/D \le 12$ (rectangular), $l_{ex}/D \le 4$ (circular). No slenderness check.
>
> ✅ **Three mandatory IS 456 checks**: (i) Pure axial capacity $0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$, (ii) Reduced axial capacity for small eccentricity, (iii) Bresler's biaxial interaction.
>
> ✅ **Minimum eccentricity** (in both directions): $e_{min} = l/500 + D/30 \ge 20$ mm.
>
> ✅ **Uniaxial design procedure**: Compute $P_u/(f_{ck} bD)$ and $M_u/(f_{ck} bD^2)$, enter **SP-16 chart** for chosen $p$ and $d'/D$, ensure point lies **below** the curve.
>
> ✅ **Biaxial design procedure (Bresler)**: Compute $M_{ux1}$ and $M_{uy1}$ from SP-16 at the design $P_u$. Compute $\alpha_n$ with the cap of 1.7. Verify LHS $\le 1.0$.
>
> ✅ **Steel limits**: $0.008 bD \le A_{sc} \le 0.06 bD$ (4 % in lap zone). Min bar $\phi = 12$ mm. Min clear cover = 40 mm.
>
> ✅ **Tie design**: $\phi_{tie} \ge \max(\phi_{main}/4, 6 \text{ mm})$. Spacing $\le \min(300 \text{ mm}, b, D, 16\phi_{main})$.
>
> ✅ **SP-16 chart selection**: Chart number depends on $f_{ck}$, $f_y$, and $d'/D$. For $f_y = 500$ MPa, $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa, $d'/D = 0.10$, refer to **Chart 33** (or 37 depending on edition).
>
> ✅ **Strain compatibility**: Assumed linear strain distribution; non-linear stress block; non-dimensional SP-16 charts avoid tedious hand integration.
>
> ✅ **Code references**: IS 456:2000 Cl. 25.1.1, 25.4, 39.3, 39.4, 39.5; SP-16 (1980) design charts.
>
> ✅ **Practical caveat**: Always use the SP-16 chart of the **correct $f_{ck}$ and $f_y$ combination**; interpolation between charts is **not permitted**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
