topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Module 3 Scope — Numerical Problem Landscape

Module 3 of **PCCVT403 – Design of Concrete Structures** under the KTU 2024 Scheme is built around three primary structural design elements:

1. **Staircases** (Dog-legged and Open-well)
2. **Columns** (Short axially loaded, with minimum eccentricity)
3. **Isolated Footings** (Axial and Eccentric loading)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Exam Pattern Reminder**
> Module 3 carries significant weightage in the End Semester Examination (ESE). Typical question allocation:
> - **Part A (3 marks):** Direct design formula or IS 456 clause-based question
> - **Part B (14 marks):** Full design numerical — staircase (14M) OR column + footing combined (14M split as 7+7)

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (IS 456:2000 & SP-16 Anchored)

### 1.2.1 Staircase
A **staircase** is a structural system comprising a series of steps (risers + treads) supported by inclined slabs (waist slab) or stringer beams, designed to transfer vertical loads from occupants and self-weight to the landing beams/floors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dog-legged Staircase:** A staircase with two flights running in opposite directions, separated by a half-space landing. This is the most common residential/commercial type.
>
> **Open-well Staircase:** A staircase with a central rectangular well, used when the floor-to-floor height is large (> 3.5 m) and three or more flights are needed.

### 1.2.2 Column
A **column** is a vertical compression member whose effective length exceeds 3 times the least lateral dimension, used primarily to transfer axial loads from beams/slabs to the foundation.

> [!NOTE]
> **IS 456:2000, Cl. 25.1.2 — Minimum Eccentricity:**
> Columns shall be designed for the **minimum eccentricity** equal to the unsupported length / 500 + lateral dimension / 30, subject to a minimum of 20 mm. This accounts for construction imperfections.

### 1.2.3 Isolated Footing
An **isolated footing (spread footing)** is a shallow foundation that transfers the load from a single column to the soil, distributing it over a sufficient area such that the **safe bearing capacity (SBC)** of the soil is not exceeded.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

| Structural Element | Real-World Analogy | Loading Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| **Staircase Waist Slab** | A tilted road bridge for people | Gravity pulls people down the slope → creates bending in the inclined slab |
| **Column** | A vertical pillar holding up a roof | All floor loads stack on top; buckling risk if slender |
| **Isolated Footing** | A snowshoe underfoot | Spreads the point load over a wide area to keep the ground from failing |

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Exam Defaults)

- **Concrete grade for design:** M20 (most common in problems), occasionally M25
- **Steel grade:** Fe415 (HYSD bars conforming to IS 1786)
- **Partial safety factor for concrete (γ_m for concrete):** **1.5**
- **Partial safety factor for steel (γ_m for steel):** **1.15**
- **Design bond stress (τ_bd) for M20 concrete with Fe415:** **1.2 N/mm²** (IS 456, Table 21, for plain bars in tension; 1.4 for deformed — but KTU typically uses 1.2 unless specified)
- **Clear cover:**
  - Slab/Staircase: **20 mm**
  - Column: **40 mm** (main reinforcement)
  - Footing: **50 mm** (bottom)
- **Unit weight of RCC:** **25 kN/m³**
- **Unit weight of PCC (plain concrete):** **24 kN/m³**
- **Live load on stairs (residential):** **3.0 kN/m²** (IS 875, Part 2, Table 2)
- **Live load on stairs (public):** **5.0 kN/m²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always re-read the problem statement. KTU numerical problems **explicitly state** the live load value, concrete grade, and steel grade. Never assume — use what is given.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design of Dog-Legged Staircase — Operational Logic Flow

The design of a dog-legged staircase proceeds through a deterministic sequence of steps:

1. **Geometry Fixation** — Rise (R), Tread (T), Number of steps per flight, Landing width
2. **Effective Span Determination** (IS 456, Cl. 33.1.a)
3. **Load Computation** — Dead Load (self-weight of waist slab, finishes) + Live Load
4. **Factored Load** — Multiply by **1.5** (Load factor for DL+LL)
5. **Bending Moment and Shear Force** — Treat as simply supported (one-way slab analysis)
6. **Effective Depth and Main Reinforcement** — Limit state design (LSM)
7. **Distribution Steel** — As per IS 456, Cl. 26.5.2.1
8. **Check for Deflection and Cracking** — Span/depth ratio

### 2.1.1 Effective Span for Staircase (IS 456, Cl. 33.1.a)

The effective span of a stair slab shall be taken as the **horizontal distance between centre-to-centre of supports**, OR the **clear horizontal span + width of landing**, whichever is **smaller**.

$$
L_{eff} = \min\left[\,c/c \text{ of supports},\;\; L_{clear} + \frac{b_{landing}}{2} + \frac{b_{landing}}{2}\,\right]
$$

### 2.1.2 Thickness of Waist Slab — Empirical Thumb Rule

$$
t_{waist} = \frac{L_{eff}}{20} \quad \text{to} \quad \frac{L_{eff}}{25}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU problems frequently pre-specify waist slab thickness. Use the given value directly.

### 2.2 Design of Axially Loaded Short Column — Theoretical Steps

1. **Compute Factored Axial Load:** $P_u = 1.5 \times (DL + LL)$
2. **Compute Minimum Eccentricity:** $e_{x,min} = \max\left( \dfrac{L_{ex}}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30},\; 20 \text{ mm} \right)$
3. **Assume Column Dimensions** (b × D)
4. **Compute Gross Area** $A_g$ and **Effective Cover** $d'$
5. **Apply IS 456, Cl. 39.3 (Short Axially Loaded Columns):**

$$
P_u = 0.4 \times f_{ck} \times A_c + 0.67 \times f_y \times A_{sc}
$$

where:
- $A_c = A_g - A_{sc}$ (net concrete area)
- $A_{sc}$ = area of compression steel

6. **Solve for $A_{sc}$**, provide minimum 0.8% of $A_g$ as per IS 456, Cl. 26.5.3.1.

> [!WARNING]
> For **helical/spiral columns**, the design strength increases by **5%**, but the lap length requirements differ. KTU problems predominantly use **tied columns**.

### 2.3 Design of Isolated Footing (Uniaxial Eccentric) — Theory

For an axially loaded isolated footing:

1. **Area of Footing:** $A_f = \dfrac{P}{SBC}$ (service load, unfactored)
2. **Factored Net Upward Pressure:** $w_u = \dfrac{1.5 \times P}{A_f}$
3. **Net Pressure** (factored, since footing self-weight + soil are balanced):

$$
p_u = \dfrac{P_u}{A_f}\quad \text{where } P_u \text{ excludes footing self-weight}
$$

4. **Bending Moment at Column Face:**

$$
M_u = \dfrac{p_u \times B \times (L - a)^2}{2}
$$

where L = footing length, a = column dimension along L.

5. **Effective Depth Required:**

$$
d = \sqrt{\dfrac{M_u}{0.138 \times f_{ck} \times b}} \quad (b = 1 \text{ m strip})
$$

6. **Area of Steel:**

$$
A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5 \times f_{ck}}{f_y}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6 \times M_u}{f_{ck} \times b \times d^2}}\right] \times b \times d
$$

7. **Check for One-Way Shear** at distance **d** from column face.
8. **Check for Two-Way (Punching) Shear** at $d/2$ from column face.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Governing Clause |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{eff,stair} = \min(c/c,\; L_{clear} + w_{landing})$ | Effective span of staircase | IS 456, Cl. 33.1.a |
| 2 | $w_u = 1.5 \times (DL + LL)$ | Factored load on waist slab | IS 456, Cl. 18 |
| 3 | $M_u = \dfrac{w_u \times L_{eff}^2}{8}$ | Factored BM (simply supported) | LSM |
| 4 | $A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.12}{100} \times b \times D$ | Min steel in slabs (Fe415) | IS 456, Cl. 26.5.2.1 |
| 5 | $A_{st,dist} = \dfrac{0.12}{100} \times A_g$ | Distribution steel in slabs | IS 456, Cl. 26.5.2.1 |
| 6 | $e_{x,min} = \max\left(\dfrac{L_{ex}}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30},\; 20\text{ mm}\right)$ | Min eccentricity (column) | IS 456, Cl. 25.4 |
| 7 | $P_u = 0.4 f_{ck} A_c + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ | Axial capacity (short tied column) | IS 456, Cl. 39.3 |
| 8 | $A_{sc,min} = 0.008 \times A_g$ | Min column steel (0.8%) | IS 456, Cl. 26.5.3.1 |
| 9 | $A_{sc,max} = 0.04 \times A_g$ | Max column steel (4%) | IS 456, Cl. 26.5.3.1 |
| 10 | $A_f = \dfrac{P}{SBC}$ | Area of footing (service load) | Foundation design |
| 11 | $M_u = \dfrac{p_u \cdot B \cdot (L-a)^2}{2}$ | BM in footing at column face | LSM |
| 12 | $\tau_c$ from IS 456, Table 19 | Shear strength of concrete | IS 456, Cl. 40.2.1 |
| 13 | $V_u = p_u \times B \times (L/2 - a/2 - d)$ | One-way shear check | IS 456, Cl. 34.2.4.1 |
| 14 | $V_p = p_u \times [A_f - (a+d)(b+d)]$ | Punching shear (two-way) | IS 456, Cl. 31.6 |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Solved Problem 1 — Design of Dog-Legged Staircase (KTU Pattern, 14-Mark Style)

### Problem Statement

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**
>
> Design a **dog-legged staircase** for a residential building (office type) with the following data:
> - Floor-to-floor height = **3.6 m**
> - Rise (R) = **150 mm**
> - Tread (T) = **270 mm**
> - Width of stair = **1.2 m**
> - Landing width = **1.2 m** (along direction of flight)
> - Live load = **3.0 kN/m²**
> - Finishes load = **1.0 kN/m²**
> - Concrete = **M20**, Steel = **Fe415**
> - Exposure = Mild
>
> Sketch the reinforcement details.

### Step 1: Geometry Fixation

Number of risers per flight = Total height / Rise

$$
n_{risers} = \frac{3.6}{0.150} = 24 \text{ risers (12 per flight for dog-legged)}
$$

Number of treads per flight = 12 − 1 = **11 treads** (one less than risers to account for landing)

### Step 2: Effective Span (IS 456, Cl. 33.1.a)

Horizontal length of one flight:

$$
L_{flight} = 11 \times 0.270 = 2.97 \text{ m}
$$

Clear span (centre-to-centre) consideration:

$$
L_{c/c} = L_{flight} + \text{landing width} = 2.97 + 1.20 = 4.17 \text{ m}
$$

Clear span + landing = $L_{flight} + w_{landing} = 2.97 + 1.20 = 4.17$ m

Therefore, **Effective Span $L_{eff}$ = 4.17 m** (the smaller of the two — they are equal in this case)

> [!NOTE]
> If the two values differ, IS 456 mandates the **smaller** one to be used. This is a frequent KTU trap question.

### Step 3: Thickness of Waist Slab

Using the thumb rule: $t = L_{eff}/25$ to $L_{eff}/30$

$$
t = \frac{4170}{25} = 166.8 \text{ mm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Take } t = 170 \text{ mm}
$$

Effective cover for slab = 20 mm, so effective depth $d = 170 - 20 = 150 \text{ mm}$

### Step 4: Load Calculation (per m² of plan area of waist slab)

**Inclined length** of waist slab (slant length of one flight):

$$
L_{slant} = \sqrt{L_{flight}^2 + H_{flight}^2} = \sqrt{2.97^2 + (12 \times 0.150)^2}
$$

$$
L_{slant} = \sqrt{8.8209 + 3.24} = \sqrt{12.0609} = 3.473 \text{ m}
$$

Self-weight of waist slab (per m² of plan area):

$$
SW = 25 \times 0.170 \times \frac{L_{slant}}{L_{flight}} = 25 \times 0.170 \times \frac{3.473}{2.97}
$$

$$
SW = 4.25 \times 1.169 = 4.97 \approx 5.0 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The multiplier $\dfrac{L_{slant}}{L_{flight}} = \sec\theta$ converts the inclined slab weight to equivalent horizontal load. This is **critical** in staircase design.

Adding finishes (1.0 kN/m²) and Live Load (3.0 kN/m²):

$$
DL = 5.0 + 1.0 = 6.0 \text{ kN/m}^2, \quad LL = 3.0 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

Total service load on plan area = $6.0 + 3.0 = 9.0$ kN/m²

Load per metre run of flight (width = 1.2 m):

$$
w = 9.0 \times 1.2 = 10.8 \text{ kN/m}
$$

### Step 5: Factored Load and Bending Moment

$$
w_u = 1.5 \times 10.8 = 16.2 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
M_u = \frac{w_u \times L_{eff}^2}{8} = \frac{16.2 \times 4.17^2}{8}
$$

$$
M_u = \frac{16.2 \times 17.3889}{8} = \frac{281.7}{8} = 35.21 \text{ kN·m}
$$

### Step 6: Effective Depth Check (Limiting $M_{u,lim}$)

For M20, Fe415: $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times f_{ck} \times b \times d^2$ (per metre width)

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 1000 \times 150^2 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 1000 \times 22500 \times 10^{-6} = 62.1 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

Since $M_u = 35.21 < 62.1$ kN·m, **no compression steel is needed.** ✓

### Step 7: Main Reinforcement (Tension Steel)

$$
A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times f_{ck}}{f_y}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times M_u}{f_{ck} \times b \times d^2}}\right] \times b \times d
$$

Substituting (in N, mm):

$M_u = 35.21 \times 10^6$ N·mm, $b = 1000$ mm, $d = 150$ mm:

$$
A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 35.21 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 150^2}}\right] \times 1000 \times 150
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{161.97 \times 10^6}{450 \times 10^6}}\right] \times 150000
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \times [1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.3599}] \times 150000
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \times [1 - 0.8014] \times 150000
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \times 0.1986 \times 150000 = 718.04 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Minimum steel check** (IS 456, Cl. 26.5.2.1, for Fe415 slabs):

$$
A_{st,min} = \frac{0.12}{100} \times 1000 \times 170 = 204 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$A_{st,calc} = 718 > 204$, so **provide 718 mm²**.

**Selection of bars:** 12 mm dia bars have area = 113 mm²

$$
\text{Spacing} = \frac{1000 \times 113}{718} = 157.4 \text{ mm c/c}
$$

**Provide 12 mm Φ @ 150 mm c/c** (Adopt lower spacing, slightly more steel — safe and standard).

**Distribution steel:**

$$
A_{st,dist} = \frac{0.12}{100} \times 1000 \times 170 = 204 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Provide **8 mm Φ @ 250 mm c/c** ($A_{st}$ provided = 1000 × 50.3/250 = 201 mm² ≈ 204 mm² ✓)

### Step 8: Summary of Staircase Design

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Waist slab thickness | 170 mm |
| Effective span | 4.17 m |
| Factored load | 16.2 kN/m |
| Factored BM | 35.21 kN·m |
| Main steel | 12 mm Φ @ 150 mm c/c (bottom) |
| Distribution steel | 8 mm Φ @ 250 mm c/c |
| Cover | 20 mm |

---

## 3.2 Solved Problem 2 — Axially Loaded Short Column with Footing (KTU Pattern, 14-Mark)

### Problem Statement

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
>
> Design a **short RC column** with an **isolated footing** to carry a service load of **1500 kN** (DL = 1000 kN, LL = 500 kN). The column is **3.5 m long** (unsupported), with one end fixed and the other hinged. SBC of soil = **200 kN/m²**. Use M20 and Fe415. Sketch details.

### Step 1: Column Design

**Trial dimensions:** Take column = **400 mm × 400 mm**

Effective length: $L_{ex} = 0.80 \times 3.5 = 2.80$ m (IS 456, Cl. 25.3, one end fixed, other hinged)

> [!NOTE]
> **Effective length factors (IS 456, Cl. 25.3):**
> - Both ends hinged: 1.0 L
> - One end fixed, other hinged: 0.80 L
> - Both ends fixed: 0.65 L
> - One end fixed, other free (cantilever): 2.0 L

**Minimum eccentricity (Cl. 25.4):**

$$
e_{x,min} = \max\left(\frac{L_{ex}}{500} + \frac{D}{30},\; 20 \text{ mm}\right) = \max\left(\frac{2800}{500} + \frac{400}{30},\; 20\right)
$$

$$
e_{x,min} = \max(5.6 + 13.33,\; 20) = \max(18.93,\; 20) = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

So $e_{x,min} = 20$ mm. Compare with $0.05D = 20$ mm — **same** ✓

**Slenderness ratio check (Cl. 25.1.2):**

$$
\frac{L_{ex}}{D} = \frac{2800}{400} = 7 < 12 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Short column} \checkmark
$$

**Factored Load:**

$$
P_u = 1.5 \times (1000 + 500) = 2250 \text{ kN}
$$

**Apply IS 456, Cl. 39.3** (Short Axially Loaded Tied Column):

$$
P_u = 0.4 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_c + 0.67 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc}
$$

with $A_c = A_g - A_{sc}$, $A_g = 400 \times 400 = 160{,}000$ mm²:

$$
2{,}250{,}000 = 0.4 \times 20 \times (160{,}000 - A_{sc}) + 0.67 \times 415 \times A_{sc}
$$

$$
2{,}250{,}000 = 8 \times (160{,}000 - A_{sc}) + 278.05 \times A_{sc}
$$

$$
2{,}250{,}000 = 1{,}280{,}000 - 8 A_{sc} + 278.05 A_{sc}
$$

$$
2{,}250{,}000 - 1{,}280{,}000 = 270.05 A_{sc}
$$

$$
970{,}000 = 270.05 A_{sc} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_{sc} = 3592 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Minimum steel check:**

$$
A_{sc,min} = 0.008 \times 160{,}000 = 1280 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$A_{sc,calc} = 3592 > 1280$ ✓

**Selection of bars:** Use 20 mm dia bars ($A_\phi = 314$ mm²)

$$
n = \frac{3592}{314} = 11.44 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Provide 12 bars of 20 mm Φ}
$$

$A_{sc,prov} = 12 \times 314 = 3768$ mm² ✓

**Lateral ties:** 8 mm Φ @ 300 mm c/c (IS 456, Cl. 26.5.3.2)

---

### Step 2: Footing Design

**Area of footing:**

$$
A_f = \frac{P_{service}}{SBC} = \frac{1500}{200} = 7.5 \text{ m}^2
$$

Take square footing: $L = B = \sqrt{7.5} = 2.738$ m ≈ **2.75 m**

**Factored Net Upward Pressure** (assuming footing self-weight is balanced by soil):

$$
p_u = \frac{P_u}{A_f} = \frac{2250}{2.75 \times 2.75} = \frac{2250}{7.5625} = 297.5 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Projection of footing (cantilever projection):**

$$
\text{Projection} = \frac{L - a}{2} = \frac{2750 - 400}{2} = 1175 \text{ mm}
$$

**Bending moment at column face** (critical section, Cl. 34.2.4.1):

$$
M_u = \frac{p_u \times B \times (L - a)^2}{2} = \frac{297.5 \times 2.75 \times (1.175)^2}{2}
$$

Wait — careful with units. Use $p_u$ in kN/m², B = 2.75 m, projection in m:

$$
M_u = \frac{297.5 \times 2.75 \times 1.175^2}{2} = \frac{297.5 \times 2.75 \times 1.381}{2}
$$

$$
M_u = \frac{1130.2}{2} = 565.1 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Effective depth required** (assuming b = 1 m strip, M20):

$$
d_{req} = \sqrt{\frac{M_u}{0.138 \times f_{ck} \times b}} = \sqrt{\frac{565.1 \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 20 \times 1000}}
$$

$$
d_{req} = \sqrt{\frac{565.1 \times 10^6}{2760}} = \sqrt{204{,}746} = 452.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt **overall depth D = 500 mm**, $d = 500 - 50 - \frac{12}{2} = 444$ mm (cover 50 mm, bar 12 mm)

Since $d_{prov} = 444 < 452.5$, **slightly increase D to 550 mm** → $d = 494$ mm ✓

**Area of steel** (per metre width):

$$
A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 565.1 \times 10^6}{20 \times 2750 \times 494^2}}\right] \times 2750 \times 494
$$

First, evaluate the discriminant term:

$$
\frac{4.6 \times 565.1 \times 10^6}{20 \times 2750 \times 494^2} = \frac{2{,}599.46 \times 10^6}{13.426 \times 10^9} = 0.1936
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \times [1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.1936}] \times 1{,}358{,}500
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \times [1 - 0.8982] \times 1{,}358{,}500
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \times 0.1018 \times 1{,}358{,}500 = 3332 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Minimum steel** (footings — IS 456, Cl. 26.5.2.1, but in footings, use 0.12% of gross cross-section OR 0.15% if Fe415 — IS 456, Cl. 34.3.1 for footings):

$$
A_{st,min} = \frac{0.12}{100} \times 2750 \times 550 = 1815 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$A_{st,calc} = 3332 > 1815$ ✓

**Spacing of 12 mm Φ bars** ($A_\phi = 113$ mm²):

$$
s = \frac{1000 \times 113}{3332/2.75} = \frac{1000 \times 113}{1211.6} = 93.2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Provide 12 mm Φ @ 90 mm c/c** (slightly closer, safer).

**One-way shear check** (at distance d = 494 mm from column face):

$$
V_u = p_u \times B \times \left(\frac{L - a}{2} - d\right) = 297.5 \times 2.75 \times (1.175 - 0.494)
$$

$$
V_u = 297.5 \times 2.75 \times 0.681 = 557.1 \text{ kN}
$$

Nominal shear stress:

$$
\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b \cdot d} = \frac{557.1 \times 10^3}{2750 \times 494} = 0.410 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

Permissible shear stress (M20, 0.25% steel, IS 456 Table 19):

$$
\tau_c \approx 0.36 + \frac{(0.48 - 0.36)}{(0.50 - 0.25)} \times (0.25 - 0.15) \approx 0.41 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$\tau_v \approx \tau_c$ — borderline safe. ✓ (with k = 1.3 for 550 mm depth, $\tau_{c,max}$ will be higher)

**Punching shear check** at $d/2$ from column face:

$$
V_p = p_u \times [A_f - (a+d)(b+d)] = 297.5 \times [7.5625 - (0.894)^2]
$$

$$
V_p = 297.5 \times [7.5625 - 0.7992] = 297.5 \times 6.7633 = 2012 \text{ kN}
$$

Punching shear capacity: $0.4 \times \tau_c \times$ perimeter × d. With $\tau_c = 0.41$:

$$
V_{p,cap} = 0.4 \times 0.41 \times 4 \times 894 \times 494 = 289{,}560 \text{ N} \approx 289.6 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> $V_p = 2012$ kN > $V_{p,cap}$ = 289.6 kN — **Punching shear fails!**

**Correction:** Increase footing depth.

Try D = 700 mm, d = 644 mm:

$$
V_{p,cap} = 0.4 \times 0.41 \times 4 \times 1044 \times 644 = 440{,}733 \text{ N} = 440.7 \text{ kN}
$$

Still fails. The issue: punching shear governs heavily.

**Try D = 1100 mm, d = 1044 mm:**

$$
V_{p,cap} = 0.4 \times 0.41 \times 4 \times 1444 \times 1044 = 989{,}030 \text{ N} \approx 989 \text{ kN} < 2012
$$

**Solution:** Provide **pedestal** (increase column size at base) or use **double-footing** with deeper section. For KTU, providing D = **1200 mm** (d = 1144 mm) and noting punching governs is standard:

$$
V_{p,cap} = 0.4 \times 0.41 \times 4 \times 1544 \times 1144 = 1{,}159{,}000 \text{ N} \approx 1159 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exam solutions, the convention is to **report the failure and propose a deeper footing** or **pedestal**, accepting the iteration as part of the design. Always show the iteration in your answer.

**Final adopted footing depth: D = 1200 mm**

---

### Step 3: Final Design Summary

| Component | Final Spec |
|---|---|
| **Column** | 400 mm × 400 mm |
| Main bars | 12 nos. of 20 mm Φ |
| Lateral ties | 8 mm Φ @ 300 mm c/c |
| Cover | 40 mm |
| **Footing** | 2.75 m × 2.75 m × 1.2 m deep |
| Main steel | 12 mm Φ @ 90 mm c/c (bottom, both ways) |
| Cover | 50 mm |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Flow — Staircase Design Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given R T floor height SBC etc] --> B[Compute Number of Risers and Treads]
    B --> C[Compute Effective Span IS 456 Cl 33 1 a]
    C --> D[Assume Waist Slab Thickness]
    D --> E[Compute Slant Length sec theta factor]
    E --> F[Compute Dead Load SW plus Finishes]
    F --> G[Add Live Load per IS 875]
    G --> H[Compute Factored Load wu equals 1.5 times w]
    H --> I[Compute Factored BM Mu equals wu L2 by 8]
    I --> J[Check Mu with Mu lim 0.138 fck b d2]
    J --> K{Mu less than Mu lim}
    K -->|Yes| L[Single reinforced design]
    K -->|No| M[Double reinforced design]
    L --> N[Compute Ast from design equation]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Check Ast against Ast min 0.12 percent]
    O --> P[Choose bar diameter and spacing]
    P --> Q[Check Distribution Steel 0.12 percent]
    Q --> R[Check Span by Depth ratio]
    R --> S[Provide Sketch with Main and Dist steel]
    S --> T[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Column + Footing Design Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Given Service Load P DL LL Unsupported Length SBC] --> P2[Assume Column Size b by D]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Effective Length IS 456 Cl 25 3]
    P3 --> P4[Check Slenderness Ratio less than 12 for Short]
    P4 --> P5[Compute Min Eccentricity IS 456 Cl 25 4]
    P5 --> P6[Compute Pu equals 1.5 times total]
    P6 --> P7[Apply Cl 39 3 Pu equals 0.4 fck Ac plus 0.67 fy Asc]
    P7 --> P8[Solve for Asc]
    P8 --> P9[Check Asc min 0.8 percent Ag]
    P9 --> P10[Choose bars and lateral ties]

    P10 --> F1[Footing: Af equals P service by SBC]
    F1 --> F2[Choose L equals B square footing]
    F2 --> F3[Compute pu equals Pu by Af]
    F3 --> F4[Compute Mu at column face]
    F4 --> F5[Assume depth D compute d]
    F5 --> F6[Compute Ast from design equation]
    F6 --> F7[Choose bars and spacing]
    F7 --> F8[Check One Way Shear at d from face]
    F8 --> F9[Check Two Way Punching Shear at d by 2]
    F9 --> F10{Both Shear Safe}
    F10 -->|Yes| F11[Finalise Design]
    F10 -->|No| F5
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
> *Explain the concept of minimum eccentricity in the design of axially loaded short columns. Why is it not zero even for perfectly axial loading?*

**Model Answer (3 key points, ~80–100 words):**

- IS 456, Cl. 25.4 mandates that all axially loaded short columns be designed for a **minimum eccentricity** to account for constructional imperfections, misalignment of loads, and unavoidable eccentricities.
- Formula: $e_{min} = \max\left(\dfrac{L_{ex}}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30},\; 20 \text{ mm}\right)$, but **not less than 0.05D**.
- This ensures the column is checked for combined axial + bending, mimicking realistic field conditions, and prevents brittle failure modes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Writing 0.05D as the only formula is **incomplete** for full 3 marks. The full Cl. 25.4 expression must be stated.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Differentiate between the effective span of a staircase (IS 456, Cl. 33.1.a) and that of a simply supported slab. State the formula.*

**Model Answer:**

- For a **staircase**: $L_{eff}$ = centre-to-centre of supports OR clear horizontal span + width of landing, **whichever is smaller**.
- For a **simply supported slab**: $L_{eff}$ = clear span + effective depth (L < 10 m case, with end bearings) or clear span only.
- The staircase formula accounts for landing continuity and the inclined nature of the slab.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module 3 Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Adapted]**
>
> Design a **dog-legged staircase** for a building with the following data:
> - Floor-to-floor height = **3.0 m**
> - Rise (R) = **160 mm**
> - Tread (T) = **250 mm**
> - Width of flight = **1.25 m**
> - Landing width = **1.25 m**
> - Live load = **5.0 kN/m²** (public building)
> - Finishes = **1.5 kN/m²**
> - M20 concrete, Fe415 steel, mild exposure
>
> **(a)** Compute the design loads and the bending moment. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Design the main and distribution reinforcement, with a neat sketch. (7 marks)

#### Solution to Question A (a) — Loads and BM (7 Marks)

**Step A.1: Geometry**

Number of risers per floor = $\frac{3.0}{0.160} = 18.75$ → adopt **20 risers** (10 per flight, dog-legged)

Adjust rise: $R = \frac{3.0}{20} = 0.150$ m = 150 mm (recomputed)

Treads per flight = 10 − 1 = **9 treads**

Horizontal length of one flight = $9 \times 0.250 = 2.25$ m

Effective span (c/c of supports assumed) = $2.25 + 1.25 = 3.50$ m

**Step A.2: Slant Length**

$$
L_{slant} = \sqrt{2.25^2 + (10 \times 0.150)^2} = \sqrt{5.0625 + 2.25} = \sqrt{7.3125} = 2.704 \text{ m}
$$

**Step A.3: Waist Slab Thickness**

$t = L_{eff}/25 = 3500/25 = 140$ mm → take **150 mm** (cover 20, d = 130 mm)

**Step A.4: Self-Weight of Waist Slab (per m² plan)**

$$
SW = 25 \times 0.150 \times \frac{2.704}{2.25} = 3.75 \times 1.202 = 4.51 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step A.5: Total Load**

DL = 4.51 + 1.5 = 6.01 kN/m²
LL = 5.0 kN/m²
Total = 11.01 kN/m²

Per m run of flight (1.25 m wide): $w = 11.01 \times 1.25 = 13.76$ kN/m

Factored: $w_u = 1.5 \times 13.76 = 20.64$ kN/m

**Step A.6: Bending Moment**

$$
M_u = \frac{20.64 \times 3.5^2}{8} = \frac{20.64 \times 12.25}{8} = 31.61 \text{ kN·m}
$$

#### Solution to Question A (b) — Reinforcement Design (7 Marks)

**Step B.1: $M_{u,lim}$ check**

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 1000 \times 130^2 \times 10^{-6} = 46.64 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$M_u = 31.61 < 46.64$ → **singly reinforced** ✓

**Step B.2: Steel Area**

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 31.61 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 130^2}}\right] \times 1000 \times 130
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \times [1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.4304}] \times 130000 = 0.02410 \times 0.2440 \times 130000 = 765 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step B.3: Bar Selection**

12 mm Φ ($A = 113$ mm²), Spacing = $\frac{1000 \times 113}{765} = 148$ mm

**Provide 12 mm Φ @ 140 mm c/c** ($A_{st,prov} = 807$ mm²) ✓

**Step B.4: Distribution Steel**

$A_{st,dist} = 0.12\% \times 1000 \times 150 = 180$ mm²

**Provide 8 mm Φ @ 250 mm c/c** ($A_{st,prov} = 201$ mm²) ✓

**Step B.5: Check Span/Depth**

Actual = $3500/130 = 26.9$. Allowable for simply supported slab = 20 (with 0.3% steel, IS 456, Fig. 4). **Modification factor** for 5 kN/m² = 1.0; modification for 150 mm width = 0.85.

Allowable = $20 \times 1.0 \times 0.85 = 17$

Actual > Allowable → **slightly deflects.** For KTU answer, state this and either increase depth to 180 mm or accept with crack control check.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students forget the **width modification factor (0.85)** for 150 mm wide stairs. The span/depth check is the most skipped step in KTU valuations, costing **1 mark easily**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
>
> An **isolated square footing** is to be designed for a 400 mm × 400 mm column carrying a service load of **1200 kN** (DL = 800 kN, LL = 400 kN). Unsupported column length = **3.0 m** (both ends fixed). SBC of soil = **180 kN/m²**. M20 concrete, Fe415 steel.
>
> **(a)** Design the column and find the steel required. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Design the footing and check for one-way and two-way shear. (7 marks)

#### Solution to Question B (a) — Column Design (7 Marks)

Assume column = **400 × 400 mm**

Effective length (both ends fixed): $L_{ex} = 0.65 \times 3.0 = 1.95$ m

Slenderness: $\frac{L_{ex}}{D} = \frac{1950}{400} = 4.875 < 12$ ✓ Short column

Min eccentricity:

$$
e_{min} = \max\left(\frac{1950}{500} + \frac{400}{30},\; 20\right) = \max(3.9 + 13.33,\; 20) = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.05D
$$

Factored load: $P_u = 1.5 \times 1200 = 1800$ kN

IS 456, Cl. 39.3:

$$
1{,}800{,}000 = 0.4 \times 20 \times (160{,}000 - A_{sc}) + 0.67 \times 415 \times A_{sc}
$$

$$
1{,}800{,}000 = 1{,}280{,}000 - 8 A_{sc} + 278.05 A_{sc}
$$

$$
A_{sc} = \frac{520{,}000}{270.05} = 1926 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Minimum steel: $0.008 \times 160{,}000 = 1280$ mm²

$A_{sc,calc} = 1926$ mm² > 1280 mm² ✓

Use 16 mm Φ bars ($A = 201$ mm²):

$$
n = \frac{1926}{201} = 9.58 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Provide 10 bars of 16 mm Φ}
$$

$A_{sc,prov} = 2010$ mm²

Lateral ties: 8 mm Φ @ 300 mm c/c

#### Solution to Question B (b) — Footing Design (7 Marks)

Area of footing: $A_f = \frac{1200}{180} = 6.667 \text{ m}^2$

Take **2.6 m × 2.6 m** ($A_f = 6.76$ m²)

Factored net pressure: $p_u = \frac{1800}{6.76} = 266.3$ kN/m²

Projection: $\frac{2.6 - 0.4}{2} = 1.1$ m

BM at column face:

$$
M_u = \frac{266.3 \times 2.6 \times 1.1^2}{2} = 418.5 \text{ kN·m}
$$

Required effective depth:

$$
d_{req} = \sqrt{\frac{418.5 \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 20 \times 2600}} = \sqrt{58{,}318} = 241.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt **D = 300 mm**, d = 300 − 50 − 6 = 244 mm (cover 50, half of 12 mm bar)

$A_{st}$ (per 2.6 m width):

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 418.5 \times 10^6}{20 \times 2600 \times 244^2}}\right] \times 2600 \times 244
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \times [1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.6208}] \times 634400
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \times 0.3831 \times 634400 = 5857 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Provide 12 mm Φ @ 100 mm c/c (both ways, bottom):
$A_{st,prov} = 1000 \times 113 / 100 = 1130$ mm² per metre = $1130 \times 2.6 = 2938$ mm²

> [!WARNING]
> **Mismatch:** Calculated $A_{st} = 5857$ mm² but provided 2938 mm² — **under-reinforced!** Increase bar diameter or reduce spacing.

Use 16 mm Φ @ 130 mm c/c:

$A_{st,prov} = \frac{1000 \times 201}{130} = 1546$ mm²/m = $1546 \times 2.6 = 4020$ mm² — still less.

Use 16 mm Φ @ 100 mm c/c → $A_{st,prov} = 2010 \times 2.6 = 5226$ mm² — still less than 5857.

**Adopt 20 mm Φ @ 130 mm c/c** ($A_\phi = 314$ mm²):

$A_{st,prov} = \frac{1000 \times 314}{130} \times 2.6 = 2415 \times 2.6 = 6279$ mm² ✓

**One-way shear check** at d = 244 mm from face:

$$
V_u = 266.3 \times 2.6 \times (1.1 - 0.244) = 266.3 \times 2.6 \times 0.856 = 592.6 \text{ kN}
$$

$\tau_v = \frac{592.6 \times 10^3}{2600 \times 244} = 0.934$ N/mm²

$p_t = \frac{6279}{2600 \times 300} \times 100 = 0.805\%$

From IS 456, Table 19, for M20, $p_t = 0.805\%$, $\tau_c = 0.57$ N/mm²

Since $\tau_v = 0.934 > 0.57$ — **One-way shear fails!** Increase depth to **D = 450 mm**, d = 394 mm.

Recheck with d = 394 mm:
$V_u = 266.3 \times 2.6 \times (1.1 - 0.394) = 266.3 \times 2.6 \times 0.706 = 488.8$ kN
$\tau_v = \frac{488.8 \times 10^3}{2600 \times 394} = 0.477$ N/mm²

$p_t = \frac{A_{st}}{2600 \times 450} \times 100$

For $A_{st}$: at d = 394 mm:

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 418.5 \times 10^6}{20 \times 2600 \times 394^2}}\right] \times 2600 \times 394
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \times [1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.2387}] \times 1024400
$$

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \times 0.1311 \times 1024400 = 3237 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$p_t = \frac{3237}{2600 \times 450} \times 100 = 0.277\%$

$\tau_c$ for M20, $p_t = 0.277\%$ ≈ 0.39 N/mm² (interpolated)

$\tau_v = 0.477 > 0.39$ — **still fails!** 

**Try D = 550 mm, d = 494 mm:**

$V_u = 266.3 \times 2.6 \times (1.1 - 0.494) = 266.3 \times 2.6 \times 0.606 = 419.6$ kN
$\tau_v = \frac{419.6 \times 10^3}{2600 \times 494} = 0.327$ N/mm²

$p_t = \frac{A_{st}}{2600 \times 550} \times 100$

$A_{st}$ at d = 494 mm:

$$
A_{st} = 0.02410 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 418.5 \times 10^6}{20 \times 2600 \times 494^2}}\right] \times 2600 \times 494
$$

$$
= 0.02410 \times [1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.1518}] \times 1284400 = 0.02410 \times 0.0795 \times 1284400 = 2461 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$p_t = \frac{2461}{2600 \times 550} \times 100 = 0.172\%$

$\tau_c$ for M20, $p_t = 0.172\% ≈ 0.28$ N/mm²

$\tau_v = 0.327 > 0.28$ — **still fails marginally!**

**Adopt D = 600 mm, d = 544 mm:**

$V_u = 266.3 \times 2.6 \times (1.1 - 0.544) = 266.3 \times 2.6 \times 0.556 = 384.9$ kN
$\tau_v = \frac{384.9 \times 10^3}{2600 \times 544} = 0.272$ N/mm²

$p_t = 0.16\%$, $\tau_c = 0.27$ N/mm²

$\tau_v \approx \tau_c$ → **Acceptable** with k = 1.3 correction for 600 mm depth:

$\tau_{c,max} = 0.27 \times 1.3 = 0.351 > 0.272$ ✓ **Safe**

**Two-way (punching) shear** at d/2 = 272 mm from face:

Punching perimeter: $(a + d)(b + d) = (400 + 544)^2 = 891{,}136$ mm² = 0.891 m²

$$
V_p = p_u \times [A_f - (a+d)(b+d)] = 266.3 \times [6.76 - 0.891] = 266.3 \times 5.869 = 1562.9 \text{ kN}
$$

Punching capacity: $0.4 \times \tau_c \times 4(a+d) \times d = 0.4 \times 0.27 \times 4 \times 944 \times 544 \times 10^{-3} = 221.6$ kN

$V_p = 1562.9 > 221.6$ — **Punching shear fails catastrophically!**

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Concept — Punching Shear Dominance:** For square footings on loose soil, punching shear almost always governs over one-way shear. The student must always check both, and most solutions will need **D = 1000 mm or more**, or a **pedestal**.

**Pedestal Solution:** Introduce a **400 × 400 → 800 × 800 pedestal** of height 600 mm, raising the effective column face for footing bending to 800 mm. Repeat calculation. This is the **KTU-recommended professional approach**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Most Common Mark Loss in Footing Design:**
> - Forgetting to multiply $p_u$ by both **B and the cantilever projection** in $M_u$ formula
> - Using **service load** instead of **factored load** in $M_u$ and $V_u$
> - Not iterating depth after shear failure
> - Reporting initial depth without re-checking after failure

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Staircase Design Checklist
- ✓ Always **convert inclined weight to horizontal** using $\sec\theta = L_{slant}/L_{flight}$
- ✓ **Effective span** = smaller of (c/c of supports) OR (clear span + landing width)
- ✓ Treat as **simply supported one-way slab**; $M_u = w_u L_{eff}^2/8$
- ✓ **Minimum steel** in slabs = 0.12% of gross cross-section (Fe415)
- ✓ **Distribution steel** = 0.12% gross cross-section, perpendicular to main steel
- ✓ **Span/depth ratio check** (Fig. 4 of IS 456) — use modification factors for width < 1 m

### Column Design Checklist
- ✓ Determine **effective length** from end conditions (Cl. 25.3)
- ✓ Check **slenderness** — short column if $L_{ex}/D \leq 12$ (rectangular)
- ✓ Compute **minimum eccentricity** from Cl. 25.4 — never less than 20 mm or 0.05D
- ✓ Use **Cl. 39.3** equation for short axially loaded tied columns
- ✓ $A_{sc,min} = 0.008 A_g$, $A_{sc,max} = 0.04 A_g$
- ✓ Lateral ties: ≤ 300 mm c/c, ≤ 16 × dia of main bar, ≤ column dim

### Footing Design Checklist
- ✓ **Area of footing** uses **service load and SBC** (NOT factored)
- ✓ **Net upward pressure** for design = factored load / footing area
- ✓ **BM critical section** = at the face of the column
- ✓ **One-way shear** at distance **d** from column face
- ✓ **Two-way (punching) shear** at **d/2** from column face — usually governs!
- ✓ **Iteration** of depth is **expected** — show the iterations in the answer
- ✓ **Use pedestal** when footing depth becomes uneconomical (> 1 m typically)

### Numerical Safety Margin
- Always **round up** bar spacing (e.g., calculated 148 mm → provide 140 mm c/c)
- Always **round up** footing dimensions (calculated 2.738 m → adopt 2.75 m)
- Always **adopt next standard depth** when depth is increased (D = 300, 350, 400, ... 50 mm increments)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, staircase design questions are **favourite ESE Part B questions**. Practice drawing the **sectional reinforcement detail** (cross-section showing main and distribution bars, cover, landing bars) — drawing the sketch **carries 2–3 marks** independently of calculation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Earthquake Resistant Design - Magnitude and Intensity of an Earthquake
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Earthquake Resistant Design - Magnitude and Intensity of an Earthquake

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 4 — Introduction to Earthquake Resistant Design: Magnitude and Intensity of an Earthquake

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Earthquake Resistant Design (ERD) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Earthquake Resistant Design (ERD)** is a specialized branch of structural engineering that deals with the analysis, proportioning, detailing, and construction of structures to **resist the lateral and vertical dynamic forces** generated by ground motion during an earthquake, ensuring **life safety, functional continuity, and controlled damage** in accordance with IS 1893 (Part 1) : 2016 and IS 13920 : 2016.

ERD is **NOT** about making a building earthquake-proof (an impossible goal) — it is about making a building **earthquake-resilient**, i.e., capable of withstanding seismic demands without collapse.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 🍃

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Bamboo Tree vs. The Rigid Concrete Wall Analogy**
> 
> Imagine you plant a **rigid concrete wall** in your garden and a **bamboo tree** next to it. When a strong wind blows:
> - The **concrete wall** resists by being **stiff and brittle** → it transfers enormous forces to its base and may **snap suddenly**.
> - The **bamboo tree** resists by being **flexible and ductile** → it **bends, oscillates, dissipates energy**, and **survives**.
> 
> Earthquake Resistant Design teaches us to design concrete buildings to behave like the **bamboo**, not the wall — through **ductile detailing** (confined concrete, closely-spaced stirrups, capacity-based design, strong-column/weak-beam philosophy).

### 1.3 Magnitude of an Earthquake — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnitude** is a **quantitative measure of the energy released at the seismic source (focus/hypocenter)** of an earthquake. It is a **single, unique number** that depends only on the earthquake's inherent energy and is **independent of the observer's location**.

It is an **instrumental measurement** obtained from seismographs.

**Standard Metrics:**
- **Units:** Dimensionless (a logarithmic scale).
- **Reference Scales:** Richter Local Magnitude ($M_L$), Body-wave Magnitude ($m_b$), Surface-wave Magnitude ($M_S$), Moment Magnitude ($M_w$).
- **Range:** Typically **$M = 0$ to $M = 9.5$** (largest recorded: 1960 Valdivia, Chile, $M_w = 9.5$).

### 1.4 Intensity of an Earthquake — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intensity** is a **qualitative (semi-quantitative) measure of the effects / severity of ground shaking** at a specific **location** on the Earth's surface. It is **NOT** a single number for an earthquake — **intensity varies from place to place**, decreasing generally with distance from the epicenter.

It is based on **observed damage to structures, human perception, and ground behavior** (largely a **subjective / empirical** scale).

**Standard Metrics:**
- **Common Scales:** Modified Mercalli Intensity (**MMI**, 12 levels, I to XII), MSK-64 (also 12 levels), JMA (Japan).
- **Isoseismals:** Contour lines connecting points of equal intensity.
- **Indian Context:** India uses **MSK-64** historically; **IS 1893** zoning maps are based on expected **intensity** (Zone II to V).

---

### 1.5 Quick Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | Magnitude | Intensity |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Quantitative (instrumental) | Qualitative / Empirical (observational) |
| **Measured by** | Seismograph | Human observation + damage survey |
| **Unique per event?** | **Yes** — single value | **No** — varies with location |
| **Based on** | Released seismic energy | Damage & human perception |
| **Scale type** | Logarithmic (open-ended) | Ordinal (e.g., 12 levels in MMI) |
| **Decreases with distance?** | No (property of source) | **Yes** (property of site) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Magnitude Scales — A Detailed Hierarchy

### 2.1.1 Richter Local Magnitude ($M_L$) — Charles F. Richter, 1935

Developed originally for **Southern California earthquakes** using a **Wood-Anderson torsion seismograph**. The formal definition:

> [!NOTE]
> **Richter's definition:** $M_L$ is the **logarithm (base 10) of the ratio** of the **maximum trace amplitude $A$** (in micrometers, $\mu m$) recorded by a standard Wood-Anderson seismograph at an **epicentral distance of 100 km**, to a **reference amplitude $A_0$** corresponding to a "zero magnitude" earthquake.

**Why logarithmic?** Because earthquake amplitudes span **8 to 10 orders of magnitude**, and a logarithmic scale conveniently compresses this enormous dynamic range.

### 2.1.2 Limitations of $M_L$ → Move to $M_w$

- **Saturation** for very large earthquakes ($M > 7$): surface-wave amplitudes do not grow proportionally.
- **Distance limitation**: only valid for Southern California-like attenuation.
- **Source physics ignored**: only measures amplitude, not rupture geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moment Magnitude ($M_w$)** by **Hanks & Kanamori (1979)** solved all these problems. It is based on the **seismic moment $M_0$**, which is the **fundamental physical measure** of an earthquake's size.

### 2.2 The Intensity Scales — Detailed Mechanics

### 2.2.1 Modified Mercalli Intensity (MMI) — 1931 (revised 1956)

12-level scale (Roman numerals **I → XII**), where:
- **I–IV:** Felt only by instruments/people at rest; negligible damage.
- **V–VII:** Felt by all; light to moderate damage.
- **VIII–XII:** Considerable to total destruction; landscape changes (landslides, fissures).

### 2.2.2 MSK-64 (Medvedev–Sponheuer–Karnik) Scale

A 12-level scale **widely used in India** and Europe. IS 1893 zoning was historically based on **MSK intensity**:
- **Zone II:** MSK V or less
- **Zone III:** MSK VI
- **Zone IV:** MSK VII
- **Zone V:** MSK VIII and above

### 2.3 Why Both Magnitude AND Intensity?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single earthquake has **one Magnitude** but **many Intensities** — engineers care about **Magnitude** (to size the event) and **Intensity** (to size the local ground motion for design). The **design philosophy** in IS 1893 uses **Zone Factor $Z$** (an intensity-based parameter), and the **spectral design** uses PGA / $S_a/g$ which are derived from magnitude–distance relationships.

---

## 2.4 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Meaning / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Richter Local Magnitude** | $M_L = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A}{A_0}\right)$ | $A$ = max amplitude on Wood-Anderson seismograph; $A_0$ = reference amplitude | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **Body-wave Magnitude** | $m_b = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A}{T}\right) + Q(h,\Delta)$ | $A/T$ = amplitude/period; $Q$ = depth-distance correction | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Surface-wave Magnitude** | $M_S = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A}{T}\right) + 1.66 \log_{10}\Delta + 3.3$ | $\Delta$ = epicentral distance in degrees | Dimensionless |
| 4 | **Seismic Moment** | $M_0 = \mu \cdot A_f \cdot \bar{d}$ | $\mu$ = shear modulus; $A_f$ = fault rupture area; $\bar{d}$ = avg. slip | N·m |
| 5 | **Moment Magnitude** | $M_w = \dfrac{2}{3}\log_{10}(M_0) - 6.07$ | $M_0$ in N·m; (if $M_0$ in dyne·cm, constant = **10.7**) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | **Gutenberg–Richter Energy Law** | $\log_{10} E = 1.5 M + 4.8$ | $E$ = radiated seismic energy | ergs |
| 7 | **Energy from Seismic Moment** | $E \approx \dfrac{M_0}{20{,}000}$ | Approximate radiated energy | ergs |
| 8 | **PGA vs. Intensity (rough)** | $\log_{10}(a) \approx \dfrac{I}{3} - 0.5$ | $a$ = PGA in cm/s²; $I$ = MMI intensity | — |
| 9 | **MSK Damage Class** | $\dfrac{I_{MSK}}{M_w} \approx$ empirical | See regional attenuation curves | — |
| 10 | **Arias Intensity** | $I_a = \dfrac{\pi}{2g}\int_0^{t_f} a^2(t)\, dt$ | Total energy per unit mass in a record | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** In structural design per IS 1893, **Intensity** drives the **Zone Factor $Z$** selection (0.10, 0.16, 0.24, 0.36 for Zones II, III, IV, V). The **Response Spectrum** (used to compute base shear $V_B = A_h \cdot W$) implicitly carries the magnitude-distance signature. A magnitude-only or intensity-only approach is **incomplete** — both are needed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Richter Scale Uses a Logarithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Show that an increase of **1 unit** in $M_L$ corresponds to a **10× increase** in ground amplitude and a **~31.6× increase** in radiated energy.

### Step 1 — Start with the definition

By Richter's definition, the local magnitude is the base-10 logarithm of the amplitude ratio:

$$M_L = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A}{A_0}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Take two earthquakes with amplitudes $A_1$ and $A_2$

Their magnitudes are:

$$M_1 = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_1}{A_0}\right), \qquad M_2 = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_0}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Subtract the two equations

Using the logarithm subtraction rule $\log a - \log b = \log(a/b)$:

$$M_2 - M_1 = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_0}\right) - \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_1}{A_0}\right)$$

$$M_2 - M_1 = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Interpret $\Delta M = 1$

If $M_2 - M_1 = 1$, then:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right) = 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = 10^{1} = 10$$

**Conclusion:** A difference of **1 magnitude unit** ⇒ **10× amplitude**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Magnitude ↔ Energy (Gutenberg–Richter Law)

### Step 1 — Gutenberg & Richter (1956) empirical relation

Based on global seismograph data, they fitted:

$$\log_{10} E \;(\text{in ergs}) = 1.5\, M + 4.8$$

### Step 2 — Convert to natural form (useful for energy ratios)

Let $E_1, E_2$ correspond to $M_1, M_2$. Then:

$$\log_{10} E_2 - \log_{10} E_1 = 1.5(M_2 - M_1)$$

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{E_2}{E_1}\right) = 1.5(M_2 - M_1)$$

### Step 3 — Raise to the power 10

$$\dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = 10^{\,1.5(M_2 - M_1)}$$

### Step 4 — Specialize for $\Delta M = 1$

$$\dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = 10^{1.5} \approx 31.62$$

**Conclusion:** A difference of **1 magnitude unit** ⇒ **~31.6× more radiated energy**. Therefore a magnitude 8 earthquake releases **~31.6× the energy of a magnitude 7**, and **~1000× the energy of a magnitude 6**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Moment Magnitude from Seismic Moment

### Step 1 — Definition of seismic moment

A fault rupture is modeled as a slip surface. The seismic moment is:

$$M_0 = \mu \cdot A_f \cdot \bar{d}$$

where $\mu$ = shear modulus (Pa), $A_f$ = rupture area (m²), $\bar{d}$ = average slip (m).

### Step 2 — Empirical scaling (Hanks & Kanamori, 1979)

For most earthquakes, the radiated energy $E$ is roughly proportional to $M_0 / 20{,}000$. Substituting into the Gutenberg–Richter relation and calibrating constants (using N·m for $M_0$), Hanks and Kanamori obtained:

$$M_w = \dfrac{2}{3}\,\log_{10}(M_0) - 6.07$$

### Step 3 — Verification for $M_0 = 10^{20}$ N·m (typical $M \approx 7$)

$$M_w = \dfrac{2}{3}\,(20) - 6.07 = 13.33 - 6.07 = 7.26 \;\; \checkmark$$

### Step 4 — Note on unit conversion

If $M_0$ is given in **dyne·cm** (1 N·m = $10^7$ dyne·cm), the constant changes:

$$M_w = \dfrac{2}{3}\,\log_{10}(M_0) - 10.7$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Magnitude vs. Intensity at a Glance

| Property | Magnitude | Intensity |
|---|---|---|
| **Symbolic form** | $M$, $M_L$, $M_w$ | $I$, MMI, MSK |
| **Type of scale** | Logarithmic, open-ended | Ordinal, finite (12 levels) |
| **Source / Receiver** | Source property | Receiver (site) property |
| **Measurement** | Seismograph (objective) | Observation + damage survey (semi-objective) |
| **Per earthquake** | One unique value | Many values (one per location) |
| **Areal pattern** | Point (focus) | Contour map (isoseismals) |
| **Saturation?** | $M_L$ saturates; $M_w$ does not | Saturation beyond ~XII |
| **Engineering use** | Hazard analysis, source modeling | Zoning maps, design ground motion |
| **Indian standard** | — | **IS 1893 Zoning** uses MSK |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Earthquake Energy Propagation Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps how energy travels from the seismic source to a structural design point — making clear the distinct roles of **Magnitude** (source-side) and **Intensity** (site-side).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Focus / Hypocenter] --> B[Seismic Source - Fault Rupture]
    B --> C[Seismic Moment M0 = mu x Af x d]
    C --> D[Moment Magnitude Mw Computation]
    D --> E[Source Spectrum Brune Model]
    E --> F[Path Attenuation Geometric + Anelastic]
    F --> G[Site Amplification Soil Effects]
    G --> H[Surface Ground Motion PGA / PGV / Sa]
    H --> I[Observed Damage and Human Perception]
    I --> J[Intensity I - MMI or MSK]
    H --> K[Response Spectrum at Site]
    K --> L[Design Base Shear VB = Ah x W]
    L --> M[IS 1893 Compliant RC Structure]
    subgraph "Source-Side Parameter"
        D
    end
    subgraph "Path-Side Parameter"
        F
    end
    subgraph "Site-Side Parameter"
        J
        K
    end
```

## 4.2 Magnitude vs. Intensity — Decision / Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[How do we describe the earthquake?] --> P1{Is it a source property or site property?}
    P1 -->|Source Property| Q1[Energy released at focus]
    P1 -->|Site Property| Q2[Shaking severity at a location]
    Q1 --> R1[Quantitative - Logarithmic]
    Q1 --> R2[Measured by Seismograph]
    Q1 --> R3[Single unique value]
    R1 --> S1[Richter ML]
    R1 --> S2[Moment Magnitude Mw]
    R1 --> S3[Surface Wave MS]
    Q2 --> T1[Qualitative - Ordinal 12 levels]
    Q2 --> T2[Based on observed damage]
    Q2 --> T3[Many values per event - one per site]
    T1 --> U1[MMI Modified Mercalli]
    T1 --> U2[MSK-64]
    T1 --> U3[JMA Japan]
```

## 4.3 Magnitude Scale Hierarchy — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Richter ML - 1935] -->|Saturation for M greater than 7| B[Body Wave mb and Surface Wave MS]
    B -->|Still saturate and are empirical| C[Moment Magnitude Mw - 1979]
    C -->|No saturation - physically rigorous| D[Modern Standard Mw]
    A --> E[Applicable only to local events]
    B --> F[Global but limited range]
    C --> G[Global - all magnitudes]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each box represents a magnitude scale; the arrows show the *evolution* from Richter's local scale → global body/surface wave scales → the modern Moment Magnitude. The top labels (Saturation / Applicability) indicate why newer scales replaced older ones.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]
**"Distinguish clearly between the 'Magnitude' and 'Intensity' of an earthquake. State at least four points of difference."**

#### Model Answer (with key points):
1. **Magnitude** measures the **energy released at the source (focus)**, while **Intensity** measures the **severity of shaking at a specific location** on the Earth's surface.
2. Magnitude is a **single value** per earthquake; Intensity **varies from place to place**, decreasing with distance from the epicenter.
3. Magnitude is **instrumental** (seismograph), **quantitative**, and **logarithmic** (e.g., Richter $M_L = \log_{10}(A/A_0)$). Intensity is **observational / empirical**, based on damage to structures and human perception, and is **ordinal** (e.g., MMI: I to XII).
4. Magnitude is used in **seismic hazard analysis** to size the source; Intensity is used in **zoning maps** (e.g., IS 1893 Zone II–V) to size the local design ground motion.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]
**"What is the Moment Magnitude Scale ($M_w$)? How does it overcome the limitations of the Richter Local Magnitude ($M_L$)?"**

#### Model Answer (with key points):
- **Definition:** $M_w = \dfrac{2}{3}\log_{10}(M_0) - 6.07$, where $M_0 = \mu \cdot A_f \cdot \bar{d}$ is the seismic moment.
- **Limitations of $M_L$ overcome:**
  1. **No saturation** — $M_w$ works for all magnitudes, including $M > 8$, while $M_L$ saturates around $M \approx 7$.
  2. **Physically based** — $M_w$ derives from rupture geometry (fault area, slip, rigidity), whereas $M_L$ is purely empirical.
  3. **Global applicability** — $M_L$ was calibrated for Southern California attenuation; $M_w$ has no regional calibration.
  4. **Better energy representation** — $M_w$ correlates more directly with total radiated energy via the Gutenberg–Richter law.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. **Internal choice** means the student picks **ONE** of two alternative questions. Solutions below show **complete, step-by-step working** with all marks distributed.

---

### ✅ Option A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> **(a)** Define the **Richter Local Magnitude ($M_L$)** and obtain the relationship showing that a unit increase in $M_L$ corresponds to a **10-fold increase** in recorded ground amplitude. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Using the **Gutenberg–Richter energy–magnitude relation**, compute the ratio of energy released by a **$M = 8$** earthquake to a **$M = 6$** earthquake. Comment on the structural design implications. (7 marks)

#### Solution (a) — Derivation (7 marks):

**Definition (2 marks):**
Richter (1935) defined the local magnitude as:

$$M_L = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A}{A_0}\right)$$

where $A$ = maximum trace amplitude (in $\mu m$) on a **standard Wood-Anderson seismograph** located **100 km** from the epicenter, and $A_0$ = amplitude of a reference zero-magnitude earthquake at the same distance.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider two earthquakes producing amplitudes $A_1$ and $A_2$:

$$M_1 = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_1}{A_0}\right), \qquad M_2 = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_0}\right)$$

Subtracting:

$$M_2 - M_1 = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_0}\right) - \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_1}{A_0}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$$

For a unit change, $M_2 - M_1 = 1$:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right) = 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = 10^{1} = 10 \quad \blacksquare$$

**Conclusion:** Each unit increase in $M_L$ ⇒ **10× increase in ground amplitude**.

#### Solution (b) — Energy Ratio & Implications (7 marks):

**Step 1 — Apply Gutenberg–Richter Law (2 marks):**
$$\log_{10} E = 1.5 M + 4.8$$

For $M_1 = 6$ and $M_2 = 8$:

$$\log_{10} E_1 = 1.5(6) + 4.8 = 13.8 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; E_1 = 10^{13.8} \text{ ergs}$$

$$\log_{10} E_2 = 1.5(8) + 4.8 = 16.8 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; E_2 = 10^{16.8} \text{ ergs}$$

**Step 2 — Compute ratio (2 marks):**

$$\dfrac{E_2}{E_1} = \dfrac{10^{16.8}}{10^{13.8}} = 10^{3} = 1000$$

**Cross-check with shortcut (2 marks):** $\Delta M = 2 \Rightarrow$ ratio = $10^{1.5 \times 2} = 10^3 = 1000$ ✓

**Structural Design Implications (1 mark):**
A magnitude 8 event releases **1000× the energy** of a magnitude 6 event. Hence small numerical increases in design magnitude demand **disproportionately large structural capacity upgrades** — justifying the **logarithmic design philosophy** (capacity design, ductile detailing per IS 13920) rather than linear scaling of member sizes.

---

### ✅ Option B — Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice]

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Modified Mercalli Intensity (MMI) Scale**. List any **six intensity levels** with brief descriptions. (7 marks)
> **(b)** The seismic moment of an earthquake is $M_0 = 2 \times 10^{20}$ N·m. Compute its **Moment Magnitude $M_w$**. If a city's design is based on **MSK Intensity VIII**, comment qualitatively on the expected structural damage at that intensity. (7 marks)

#### Solution (a) — MMI Scale (7 marks):

**Description (3 marks):**
The **Modified Mercalli Intensity (MMI) Scale** was developed by **Harry O. Wood and Frank Neumann (1931)** and is the standard intensity scale used in the United States and many engineering applications. It has **12 levels** designated by **Roman numerals I to XII**, based on **human perception of shaking**, **effects on structures**, and **changes to the natural landscape**.

**Sketch (2 marks):** (Conceptual — typically an isoseismal map with concentric contours of decreasing intensity around an epicenter, with each contour labeled by MMI level.)

**Six Intensity Levels (2 marks — any six):**

| Level | Description |
|---|---|
| **I** | Not felt except by very few under especially favorable conditions. |
| **III** | Felt quite noticeably indoors; hanging objects swing. |
| **V** | Felt by nearly everyone; some dishes/windows broken; pendulum clocks stop. |
| **VII** | Damage negligible in buildings of good design; slight to moderate in well-built ordinary structures; considerable in poorly built structures; felt by motorists. |
| **IX** | Damage considerable in specially designed structures; well-designed frame structures thrown out of plumb; great damage in substantial buildings, with partial collapse. |
| **XII** | Damage total; waves seen on ground surface; lines of sight and level distorted; objects thrown into the air. |

#### Solution (b) — Moment Magnitude Computation (7 marks):

**Step 1 — Write formula (1 mark):**
$$M_w = \dfrac{2}{3}\log_{10}(M_0) - 6.07, \quad M_0 \text{ in N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute (2 marks):**
$$M_w = \dfrac{2}{3}\log_{10}\!\left(2 \times 10^{20}\right) - 6.07$$

$$\log_{10}\!\left(2 \times 10^{20}\right) = \log_{10}(2) + 20 = 0.3010 + 20 = 20.3010$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate (2 marks):**
$$M_w = \dfrac{2}{3}(20.3010) - 6.07 = 13.5340 - 6.07 = 7.464 \approx 7.5$$

**Step 4 — MSK Intensity VIII — Expected Damage (2 marks):**
At **MSK VIII**, damage to **ordinary (non-engineered) masonry buildings** is **heavy to very heavy** (large cracks, partial collapse). **Well-engineered RC structures** with ductile detailing per **IS 13920** sustain **moderate damage** (hairline cracks, minor spalling) but **remain life-safe**. Reinforced buildings without ductile detailing may experience **column shear failure** — emphasizing the critical importance of IS 13920 compliance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $M_L$ saturation with $M_w$:** Students often state "$M_w$ also saturates" — INCORRECT. $M_w$ has **no saturation**. (Lose 2 marks)
> 2. **Wrong unit constant in $M_w$ formula:** If $M_0$ is in dyne·cm (CGS), the constant is **10.7**, not 6.07. Always check units first. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Treating Intensity as a single value:** Intensity is **location-dependent** — must draw **isoseismals** or state "decreases with distance from epicenter." (Lose 1 mark)
> 4. **Energy ratio mistake:** Energy ratio per magnitude step is **$\approx 31.6$, NOT 10**. Students confuse energy ratio with amplitude ratio. (Lose 2 marks)
> 5. **Mixing up IS 1893 Zone Factor $Z$ with Magnitude:** $Z$ is an **intensity-derived** parameter, not directly a magnitude. (Lose 1 mark)
> 6. **Missing ductile detailing link:** Always link **MSK VIII / Zone V** design to **IS 13920** provisions for full marks on design-implication sub-parts.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Magnitude** = source property (energy at focus) → **single value** → **logarithmic**, **instrumental**.
- ✅ **Intensity** = site property (effects at location) → **many values** → **ordinal (I–XII)**, **observational**.
- ✅ **Richter $M_L$** = $\log_{10}(A/A_0)$; saturates for $M > 7$.
- ✅ **Moment Magnitude $M_w = \dfrac{2}{3}\log_{10}(M_0) - 6.07$** (with $M_0$ in N·m); **no saturation**; modern global standard.
- ✅ **Seismic Moment** $M_0 = \mu \cdot A_f \cdot \bar{d}$ — fundamental physical measure.
- ✅ **Gutenberg–Richter Energy Law:** $\log_{10} E = 1.5 M + 4.8$ (ergs).
- ✅ **Key short-cuts:** $\Delta M = 1$ ⇒ **10× amplitude, ~31.6× energy**. $\Delta M = 2$ ⇒ **100× amplitude, ~1000× energy**.
- ✅ **Scales to know:** MMI (12 levels, US), MSK-64 (12 levels, India/Europe), JMA (Japan).
- ✅ **IS 1893** zoning uses **MSK intensity** → Zone Factor $Z$ (0.10, 0.16, 0.24, 0.36 for Zones II–V).
- ✅ **Always pair** Magnitude (source-side) with Intensity (site-side) for complete seismic design — never use either alone.
- ✅ **Design code linkage:** Earthquake Resistant Design in India → IS 1893 (loads) + IS 13920 (ductile detailing) + IS 4326 (masonry) + IS 13827 / 13828 (retrofitting).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seismic Effects on Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT403 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Seismic Effects on Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Seismic effects on structures** refer to the dynamic response of a building or structural system when subjected to ground accelerations caused by the propagation of seismic (earthquake) waves through the earth's crust. According to **IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016** — *Criteria for Earthquake Resistant Design of Structures*, a structure must be designed to resist the lateral inertial forces generated during an earthquake without collapse, while sustaining only repairable damage during moderate shaking.

The fundamental goal of seismic design is **life safety**, achieved through:

1. **Strength** — adequate capacity to resist induced lateral forces.
2. **Stiffness** — to limit inter-storey drift and non-structural damage.
3. **Ductility** — to undergo large inelastic deformations without collapse.

The seismic force on a structure is fundamentally derived from the equation of motion:

$$F(t) = m \cdot \ddot{u}_g(t) + c \cdot \dot{u}(t) + k \cdot u(t)$$

where $m$ is the mass, $c$ is the damping, $k$ is the stiffness, $\ddot{u}_g$ is the ground acceleration, and $u$ is the relative displacement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Seismic Effects on Structures covers the codal philosophy of IS 1893:2016, seismic zoning, design base shear computation using the **Equivalent Static Method**, vertical distribution of lateral forces, and the basic principles governing **ductile detailing** as per IS 13920:2016.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a tall glass of water sitting on a table. When you suddenly shake the table horizontally, the water inside sloshes back and forth — and in fact, the water's motion *amplifies* the table's motion. The taller the glass, the larger the sloshing amplitude. Now replace the glass with a building and the water with the building's mass: this is exactly what happens during an earthquake.

The ground lurches, but the **building's inertia resists** this motion, generating huge lateral forces near its base. A short, squat building (like a wide glass) is rigid and survives the shake easily. A tall, slender building (like a tall narrow glass) sways violently — sometimes at a "natural frequency" that exactly matches the earthquake's frequency, causing catastrophic resonance (think of the famous **Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse of 1940**, where wind-induced resonance destroyed the structure).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition Box:**
> - **Earthquake** = ground motion at the base.
> - **Building** = mass + spring + damper system.
> - **Damage** = depends on stiffness, mass, ductility, and how the ground shakes.

## 1.3 Important Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** | Physical constant |
| Seismic zone factor (Z) | **0.10 (Zone II) → 0.36 (Zone V)** | IS 1893:2016, Table 3 |
| Importance factor (I) | **1.0 – 1.8** | IS 1893:2016, Table 8 |
| Response reduction factor (R) | **3.0 (OMRF) – 5.0 (SMRF)** | IS 1893:2016, Table 9 |
| Site factor (SS, ST) | **1.0 – 1.67** | IS 1893:2016, Table 4 |
| Fundamental natural period (Ta) | **0.075 h^0.75** (RCC MRF) | IS 1893:2016, Cl. 7.6.2 |
| Critical damping for RCC | **5% of critical** | IS 1893:2016 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — The 7 Logical Pillars of Seismic Design

### Pillar 1: Seismic Zoning of India (IS 1893:2016, Cl. 6.4)

India is divided into **four seismic zones** (Zone II to Zone V), based on the intensity of past seismicity and tectonic features. Zone V is the most severe (highest seismic hazard), and Zone II is the lowest.

- The **Zone Factor (Z)** is a numerical coefficient representing the peak ground acceleration (PGA) for design.
- It is **NOT** the probability of occurrence of an earthquake — it represents the severity once it occurs.

### Pillar 2: Structural Classification — Moment-Resisting Frames

IS 1893 defines three categories based on lateral load resisting systems:

| Category | R-Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **OMRF** (Ordinary Moment Resisting Frame) | R = 3.0 | Designed for low ductility, no special detailing |
| **SMRF** (Special Moment Resisting Frame) | R = 5.0 | Designed for high ductility, follows IS 13920 detailing |
| **OMRF+Bracing** | R = 4.0 | Hybrid system |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A higher **R** means the structure is allowed to dissipate MORE energy through inelastic deformation. This *reduces* the design base shear, but **demands** superior ductile detailing.

### Pillar 3: Design Spectrum (IS 1893:2016, Cl. 6.4.2)

The **Spectral Acceleration Coefficient (Sa/g)** is a function of the fundamental natural period (T) of the structure. IS 1893:2016 provides a piecewise spectrum:

- For **rocky/hard soil** (SS = 1.0, ST = 1.0):
  - T = 0 to 0.40s: Sa/g = 1 + 15T (linear ramp, value 1.0 → 7.0)
  - T = 0.40 to 4.0s: Sa/g = 2.5 (constant plateau, capped at 2.5)
  - T > 4.0s: Sa/g = 1.0 / T

- For **soft soil**: a longer plateau (T between 0.55 to 4.0s) with peak at 2.5.

### Pillar 4: Fundamental Natural Period (Ta)

For a **RCC Moment-Resisting Frame with infill walls**, the empirical formula is:

$$T_a = \frac{0.075 \cdot h^{0.75}}{\sqrt{d}}$$

For a **RCC MRF without infill**:

$$T_a = 0.075 \cdot h^{0.75}$$

For a **Steel MRF**:

$$T_a = \frac{0.085 \cdot h^{0.75}}{\sqrt{d}}$$

where $h$ is the total building height (m), and $d$ is the base dimension (m) in the considered direction.

### Pillar 5: Design Base Shear (VB)

The single most important equation in seismic design:

$$V_B = A_h \cdot W$$

where:
- $A_h$ = Design horizontal acceleration spectrum value
- $W$ = Seismic weight of the building (full dead load + 25%–50% of live load)

$$A_h = \frac{Z}{2} \cdot \frac{I}{R} \cdot \frac{S_a}{g}$$

### Pillar 6: Vertical Distribution of Base Shear

The base shear $V_B$ is distributed to each floor as per the formula (IS 1893:2016, Cl. 7.7.1):

$$Q_i = V_B \cdot \frac{W_i \cdot h_i^2}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j \cdot h_j^2}$$

where:
- $Q_i$ = design lateral force at floor $i$
- $W_i$, $W_j$ = seismic weights at floors $i$ and $j$
- $h_i$, $h_j$ = heights of floors $i$ and $j$ from base

### Pillar 7: Ductile Detailing (IS 13920:2016)

For **SMRF systems**, the structure must satisfy:

- **Strong-column / weak-beam** design: $\sum M_c \geq 1.1 \cdot \sum M_b$ at a joint.
- **Minimum flexural reinforcement** in beams: top steel $\geq 0.385 f_{ct}/f_y \cdot b \cdot d$ (often simplified to a fraction of gross section).
- **Confinement** at beam-column joints: closely spaced closed stirrups and cross-ties within a length equal to $2d$ from the face of the column.
- **Lap splices** away from plastic hinge regions.
- **125% anchorage length** for bars through joints.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Coefficients |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_h = \dfrac{Z \cdot I \cdot S_a}{2 \cdot R \cdot g}$ | Design horizontal acceleration coefficient | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $V_B = A_h \cdot W$ | Total design base shear | kN |
| 3 | $W = \sum (D_L + 0.25 L_L)$ (for residential) | Seismic weight | kN |
| 4 | $T_a = \dfrac{0.075 h^{0.75}}{\sqrt{d}}$ | Empirical fundamental period (RCC MRF) | seconds |
| 5 | $Q_i = V_B \cdot \dfrac{W_i h_i^2}{\sum W_j h_j^2}$ | Floor-wise lateral force | kN |
| 6 | $S_a/g = 1 + 15T$ | For $0 \leq T \leq 0.40$s (rocky soil) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $S_a/g = 2.50$ | For $0.40 < T \leq 4.0$s (rocky soil) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $S_a/g = 1.0 / T$ | For $T > 4.0$s (rocky soil) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\sum M_c \geq 1.1 \cdot \sum M_b$ | Strong-column weak-beam check | kN·m |
| 10 | Drift = $\Delta / h$ | Inter-storey drift $\leq 0.004 h$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the operational backbone of every seismic design calculation in India. Whether you design a hospital in Zone IV (Kerala) or a school in Zone V (Bhuj), this same codal framework (IS 1893) governs. They are also the foundation of **performance-based design** and **capacity-based design** principles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Equation of Motion for a Single-Story Building

Consider a **single-storey, single-bay** building idealized as a mass $m$ supported by a massless column of stiffness $k$ and damping coefficient $c$. During an earthquake, the ground moves with displacement $u_g(t)$ and acceleration $\ddot{u}_g(t)$, while the mass experiences a relative displacement $u(t)$ from the ground.

### Step 1: Free Body Diagram and Force Balance

The forces acting on the mass are:
- Inertia force: $F_I = m \cdot (\ddot{u} + \ddot{u}_g)$ (mass times total acceleration)
- Damping force: $F_D = c \cdot \dot{u}$ (proportional to relative velocity)
- Spring force: $F_S = k \cdot u$ (proportional to relative displacement)

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law (Dynamic Equilibrium)

By D'Alembert's principle, sum of forces = mass × total acceleration, which yields the equation of motion:

$$m \cdot \ddot{u}(t) + c \cdot \dot{u}(t) + k \cdot u(t) = -m \cdot \ddot{u}_g(t)$$

### Step 3: Divide Throughout by Mass (m)

This yields the canonical form:

$$\ddot{u}(t) + 2 \zeta \omega_n \dot{u}(t) + \omega_n^2 \cdot u(t) = -\ddot{u}_g(t)$$

where:
- $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ is the **undamped natural frequency** (rad/s)
- $\zeta = c / (2 m \omega_n)$ is the **damping ratio** (typically 5% for RCC)

### Step 4: Natural Period Calculation

The natural period $T$ in seconds is:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$$

### Step 5: Pseudo-Static Base Shear (Response Spectrum Approach)

The maximum response of a single-degree-of-freedom system to ground acceleration $\ddot{u}_g(t)$ is captured by the **pseudo-spectral acceleration**:

$$S_a = \omega_n^2 \cdot u_{max}$$

The equivalent static lateral force on the mass is therefore:

$$V_B = m \cdot S_a = \frac{S_a}{g} \cdot m \cdot g = \frac{S_a}{g} \cdot W$$

Substituting the IS 1893 spectral coefficient definition:

$$V_B = A_h \cdot W = \left(\frac{Z}{2} \cdot \frac{I}{R} \cdot \frac{S_a}{g}\right) \cdot W$$

This is the **fundamental derivation** of the design base shear formula. It shows that the design force is the elastic spectral force scaled down by $1/R$ (ductility reduction) and $1/2$ (additional safety factor of 2 for conservatism in design).

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTUs Favourite Problem Type

**Problem:** A 5-storey RCC Special Moment Resisting Frame (SMRF) office building is to be constructed in **Kozhikode (Zone III)**. The building is 15 m tall with a base dimension of 20 m. Each floor has a seismic weight of 1000 kN. Determine:
1. The fundamental natural period.
2. The design spectral acceleration.
3. The total design base shear.
4. The lateral force on each floor.

**Given Data:**
- Zone: III → Z = 0.16
- Importance: Office → I = 1.2
- Frame type: SMRF → R = 5.0
- Soil type: Medium (assumed rocky) → SS = 1.0, ST = 1.0
- Heights (from base): Floor 1 = 3 m, Floor 2 = 6 m, Floor 3 = 9 m, Floor 4 = 12 m, Floor 5 = 15 m
- W_i = 1000 kN at each floor

### Solution — Step 1: Fundamental Natural Period

For SMRF with infill (RCC):

$$T_a = \frac{0.075 \cdot h^{0.75}}{\sqrt{d}} = \frac{0.075 \cdot (15)^{0.75}}{\sqrt{20}}$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$15^{0.75} = 15^{3/4} = (15^{0.5})^{1.5} = (3.873)^{1.5}$$

Using the identity $a^{1.5} = a \cdot \sqrt{a}$:

$$15^{0.75} = 15 \cdot \sqrt{15} = 15 \cdot 3.873 = 58.094$$

Therefore:

$$T_a = \frac{0.075 \cdot 58.094}{\sqrt{20}} = \frac{4.357}{4.472} = 0.974 \, \text{seconds}$$

### Solution — Step 2: Design Spectral Acceleration

Since T = 0.974 s falls in the plateau range (0.40 < T < 4.0 s) for rocky soil:

$$\frac{S_a}{g} = 2.50$$

### Solution — Step 3: Design Horizontal Acceleration Coefficient

$$A_h = \frac{Z}{2} \cdot \frac{I}{R} \cdot \frac{S_a}{g} = \frac{0.16}{2} \cdot \frac{1.2}{5.0} \cdot 2.50$$

Step-by-step:

$$A_h = 0.08 \cdot 0.24 \cdot 2.50 = 0.048$$

### Solution — Step 4: Total Design Base Shear

$$V_B = A_h \cdot W = 0.048 \cdot (5 \cdot 1000) = 0.048 \cdot 5000 = 240 \, \text{kN}$$

### Solution — Step 5: Vertical Distribution of Base Shear

Compute $W_i \cdot h_i^2$ for each floor:

| Floor i | W_i (kN) | h_i (m) | W_i × h_i² (kN·m²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1000 | 3 | 9,000 |
| 2 | 1000 | 6 | 36,000 |
| 3 | 1000 | 9 | 81,000 |
| 4 | 1000 | 12 | 144,000 |
| 5 | 1000 | 15 | 225,000 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **495,000** |

Now apply the formula:

$$Q_i = V_B \cdot \frac{W_i h_i^2}{\sum W_j h_j^2} = 240 \cdot \frac{W_i h_i^2}{495,000}$$

Computing each:

$$Q_1 = 240 \cdot \frac{9000}{495000} = 4.36 \, \text{kN}$$

$$Q_2 = 240 \cdot \frac{36000}{495000} = 17.45 \, \text{kN}$$

$$Q_3 = 240 \cdot \frac{81000}{495000} = 39.27 \, \text{kN}$$

$$Q_4 = 240 \cdot \frac{144000}{495000} = 69.82 \, \text{kN}$$

$$Q_5 = 240 \cdot \frac{225000}{495000} = 109.09 \, \text{kN}$$

**Verification:** $\sum Q_i = 4.36 + 17.45 + 39.27 + 69.82 + 109.09 = 239.99 \approx 240$ kN ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The top floor carries the highest force (109 kN) despite having the same weight — this is the fundamental nature of seismic loading. **Inertia increases with height** because the upper floors accumulate acceleration amplification.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: SMRF vs OMRF Comparison

| Parameter | OMRF (R = 3.0) | SMRF (R = 5.0) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Base shear | Higher (1.67×) | Lower | SMRF more economical in steel |
| Detailing | Simple, no special req. | Strict (IS 13920) | SMRF needs skilled labour |
| Ductility demand | Low | High | SMRF can undergo inelastic deformation |
| Damageability | High (brittle failure) | Low (plastic hinges) | SMRF survives major quakes |
| Application | Low-rise, Zone II–III | All heights, Zone III–V | SMRF preferred for hospitals, schools |
| Column-beam ratio | No requirement | $\sum M_c \geq 1.1 \sum M_b$ | Beam yields first in SMRF |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Equivalent Static Method Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Building Geometry Input] --> B[Determine Seismic Zone Z]
    B --> C[Identify Importance Factor I]
    C --> D[Select Structural System R]
    D --> E[Determine Soil Type SS ST]
    E --> F[Compute Natural Period Ta]
    F --> G{Locate T on Spectrum}
    G -->|T less than 0.40s| H1[Sa by g equals 1 plus 15T]
    G -->|0.40s to 4.0s| H2[Sa by g equals 2.50]
    G -->|T greater than 4.0s| H3[Sa by g equals 1.0 by T]
    H1 --> I[Compute Ah Coefficient]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Compute Seismic Weight W]
    J --> K[Compute Base Shear VB]
    K --> L[Distribute VB to Floors]
    L --> M[Apply Strong Column Weak Beam Check]
    M --> N[Check Interstorey Drift]
    N --> O[Design Beams Columns Joints]
    O --> P[Final Seismic Safe Structure]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — IS 1893 Design Response Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Period T in seconds] --> B[T less than 0.40s]
    A --> C[T 0.40 to 4.0s]
    A --> D[T greater than 4.0s]
    B --> E[Sa by g equals 1 plus 15T]
    C --> F[Sa by g equals 2.50 Constant Plateau]
    D --> G[Sa by g equals 1.0 by T Decay Branch]
    E --> H[Determine Ah]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Base Shear VB equals Ah times W]
    I --> J[Floor Forces Qi]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Hierarchy of Seismic Force Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Peak Ground Acceleration PGA] --> B[Multiply by Zone Factor Z]
    B --> C[Apply Spectral Amplification Sa by g]
    C --> D[Elastic Spectral Force]
    D --> E[Reduce by Importance I]
    E --> F[Reduce by Response R for Ductility]
    F --> G[Apply 0.5 Safety Factor]
    G --> H[Design Base Shear VB]
    H --> I[Distribute Vertically]
    I --> J[Floor Lateral Force Qi]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Strong Column Weak Beam Philosophy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Earthquake Excitation] --> B[Plastic Hinge Formation]
    B -->|Bad Design| C[Column Hinges Form First]
    B -->|Good Design| D[Beam Hinges Form First]
    C --> E[Storey Collapse Mechanism]
    D --> F[Beam Sway Mechanism]
    E --> G[Progressive Collapse]
    F --> H[Energy Dissipated Safely]
    G --> I[Structure Fails]
    H --> J[Structure Survives]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The "weak-beam, strong-column" design philosophy ensures that **beams yield first** (acting as structural fuses) before columns can fail. This is the cornerstone of ductile earthquake-resistant design.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]

**Define the term "Importance Factor (I)" as per IS 1893:2016. List its typical values for different categories of buildings.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Importance Factor (I)** is a multiplying factor applied to the design base shear to account for the functional use and hazard potential of a structure. Buildings of higher importance (hospitals, schools, emergency services) are designed for higher seismic forces to ensure they remain functional after an earthquake.

**Typical values (IS 1893:2016, Table 8):**

| Structure Category | I |
|---|---|
| Ordinary residential, commercial | 1.0 |
| Important service buildings (schools, offices) | 1.2 |
